⚡

Application Development Using Claude Code

The Professional Handbook
by Tri Doan
📖 30 Chapters 📄 ~384 Pages 📝 153,978 Words 📅 2026

Part I: Foundations

Chapter 1: The Claude Code Revolution¶ ~18 min read

You open a terminal. You type a short sentence. Thirty seconds later, your bug is fixed, a test is written, the code is linted, and a pull request is waiting for review. No file was opened manually. No stack trace was Googled. No documentation was consulted. You typed six words, and the machine handled the rest.

This is not a demo. This is Tuesday.

Welcome to Claude Code – and to a fundamentally different way of building software.

What Claude Code Actually Is¶

Let’s be precise, because the market is flooded with AI coding tools and most people conflate them.

Claude Code is an agentic AI coding system that runs in your terminal. It is not a chatbot with a code window. It is not an autocomplete engine. It is not a browser-based IDE wrapper. It is an autonomous agent that can read your files, edit your code, run shell commands, search the web, manage git, execute tests, and orchestrate multi-step workflows – all within a single conversation turn.

Here is what happens when you launch it:

bash
$ claude

You get a REPL. You type natural language. Claude Code reads your project, assembles a system prompt from your configuration files, and enters an agentic loop: it decides which tools to call, calls them, reads the results, decides what to do next, and keeps going until the task is complete. You do not tell it “now read the file” then “now edit line 47” then “now run the tests.” You say “fix the authentication bug” and it figures out the rest.

The distinction matters. Autocomplete tools (GitHub Copilot, Codeium) predict the next line. Chat tools (ChatGPT, Gemini) answer questions about code. Claude Code does the work. It is an agent, not an assistant.

Here is the tool set it operates with:

Tool What It Does
Read Read any file in your project
Edit Make precise string replacements in files
Write Create or overwrite entire files
Bash Execute any shell command
Glob Find files by pattern (e.g., **/*.py)
Grep Search file contents with regex
Agent Spawn a sub-agent for parallel work
WebFetch Retrieve and process web content
WebSearch Search the internet
NotebookEdit Edit Jupyter notebook cells

These are not decorative. In a single turn, Claude Code might call Grep to find all usages of a function, Read to understand the implementation, Edit to fix the bug, Bash to run pytest, read the test output, fix a second issue it discovered, run tests again, then Bash to create a git commit. That is six tool calls in one conversational turn, executed autonomously.

The Configuration Stack¶

What makes Claude Code qualitatively different from other AI coding tools is not the agent itself – it is the depth of configuration around the agent. Here is the full stack of configuration primitives:

Component File Purpose
CLAUDE.md CLAUDE.md in project root Project-specific instructions: coding standards, conventions, what to do and not do
Settings .claude/settings.json Permissions, hooks wiring, environment variables, model defaults
Rules .claude/rules/*.md Path-scoped coding standards (e.g., Python rules load only for .py files)
Skills .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md Packaged multi-step workflows triggered by /commands
Hooks .claude/hooks/*.sh Shell scripts that fire on lifecycle events (block commands, auto-lint, notify)
Agents .claude/agents/*.md Specialized subagent definitions with their own model, tools, and personality

Each of these is a separate chapter in Part II (Chapters 4-8). For now, the key insight is that professional usage is not about writing better prompts – it is about configuring this stack so that Claude Code already knows your standards, your workflows, and your guardrails before you type a single word.

Think of it like this: a pilot does not manually control every flap, throttle, and trim tab during a flight. They configure the autopilot, monitor the instruments, and intervene when judgment is needed. The configuration stack is your autopilot for AI-assisted development.

A Day in the Life: Before and After¶

To make this concrete, here is what a real engineering day looks like with and without professional Claude Code usage.

Without Claude Code (or with basic usage):

9:00  Read Slack, find bug report from QA
9:05  Open IDE, navigate to the file mentioned
9:15  Read the code, form a hypothesis
9:25  Add some print statements, run the app
9:35  Identify the bug
9:40  Write the fix
9:50  Write a test (maybe)
10:00 Run tests, fix a typo, run again
10:10 Stage, commit, push
10:15 Open browser, create PR
10:25 Realize you forgot to lint, push again
10:30 Done with ONE bug

With professional Claude Code (Level 4-5):

9:00  Read Slack, find bug report mentioning issue #142
9:01  Type: 142
9:03  PR is created with fix, test, and security review
9:03  Move to next task

That is not exaggeration. It is what happens when the configuration stack handles investigation, testing, linting, committing, and PR creation. You provide the judgment call (“yes, fix this issue”) and the system executes the entire workflow.

By 10:30, the basic user has fixed one bug. The professional user has fixed eight, reviewed a PR, and is scaffolding a new feature. Same engineer, same brain, same Claude Code. Different configuration.

The Paradigm Shift: From “Write Code” to “Engineer with AI”¶

There is a phrase gaining traction in the developer community: vibe coding. The original meaning – letting AI generate code while you “vibe” – is dangerously reductive. Most people who vibe code produce fragile, untested, poorly structured software. They are passengers.

This book teaches something different: vibe coding engineering. The engineer still architects, still makes design decisions, still owns the quality. But instead of typing every character, they configure an AI system to execute at professional standards. The work shifts from writing code to engineering the system that writes code.

Consider two engineers fixing the same bug.

Engineer A (basic prompting):

You: fix the bug in login.py where users can't reset their password

Claude: I'll take a look at login.py...
[reads file, makes a change]
Here's the fix. The issue was that the token validation...

Engineer A got a patch. Maybe it works. Maybe it introduced a regression. Maybe it missed similar bugs in three other files. There is no test, no lint check, no commit convention, no PR.

Engineer B (professional configuration):

Engineer B types the exact same six words. But their project has a CLAUDE.md with coding standards, a security.md rule that flags authentication changes for extra scrutiny, a PreToolUse hook that blocks dangerous shell commands, a debug workflow skill that enforces test-first fixing, and an auto-lint hook that formats code after every edit. Here is what actually happens:

  1. The auto-router skill detects “fix” + “bug” and routes to the debug workflow
  2. Claude Code reads login.py and searches for related files with Grep
  3. A failing test is written first, demonstrating the bug
  4. The minimal fix is implemented
  5. The auto-lint hook fires, running ruff check --fix and ruff format
  6. Tests are executed – the new test passes, existing tests still pass
  7. A commit is created: fix: correct password reset token validation
  8. A PR is drafted with a summary and linked issue reference

Same prompt. Radically different outcome. The difference is not Claude Code – it is the engineering around Claude Code.

What Separates Beginners from Professionals¶

After observing hundreds of engineers use Claude Code, a clear pattern emerges. Usage falls into five distinct levels, and most people never get past Level 2.

The 5 Levels of Claude Code Mastery¶

Level 1: Basic Prompting

You treat Claude Code like ChatGPT in a terminal. You ask questions, get answers. You paste error messages, get explanations. You say “write a function that does X,” copy the output into your editor, and test it manually.

You: How do I read a CSV file in Python?
Claude: Here's how to read a CSV file...

This works. It is also a waste of 95% of Claude Code’s capability. You are using a Formula 1 car to drive to the grocery store.

Level 2: Tool-Aware Usage

You understand that Claude Code can read files, edit files, and run commands. You point it at specific files and ask it to make changes. You let it run tests. You are starting to work with the agent rather than treating it as a Q&A system.

You: Read src/auth/login.py and fix the password reset bug on line 47

Claude: [reads file, edits line 47, runs tests]

Most tutorials teach you to this level. Most engineers stay here. The improvement over Level 1 is real but modest – you are still directing every step manually.

Level 3: Configuration Engineering

You create a CLAUDE.md file that tells Claude Code about your project: coding standards, preferred libraries, testing approach, commit conventions. You set up settings.json with permissions that allow safe commands and block dangerous ones. You write rules in .claude/rules/ that apply coding standards automatically when files of certain types are edited.

markdown
# CLAUDE.md
## Coding Standards
- Python: type hints on all functions, ruff for formatting
- Tests: pytest with fixtures, test-first for bugs
- Git: imperative mood commits, feat/fix/docs prefixes

Now every interaction with Claude Code follows your standards. You do not need to say “remember to add type hints” or “run the linter after editing.” The configuration handles it.

Level 4: Workflow Automation

You build skills that package multi-step workflows into single commands. /fix-issue 42 reads the GitHub issue, investigates the codebase, writes a failing test, implements the fix, runs the test suite, commits with the right message, and creates a PR. You build hooks that block destructive commands, scan for secrets before commits, and auto-format code on every save. Each workflow codifies your team’s best practices.

You: /fix-issue 42
[6 minutes later: issue investigated, test written, bug fixed, PR created]

Level 5: Architecture-Level AI Collaboration

You design intent routing systems that decode short prompts into full workflows. You configure specialized agents (security reviewer on Opus, code reviewer on Sonnet) that Claude Code spawns in parallel. You integrate MCP servers that extend Claude Code’s capabilities with custom tools. You build multi-agent architectures where different AI instances collaborate on different aspects of a task.

You: 42
[Claude Code decodes "42" as "fix GitHub issue #42", routes to fix-issue
 workflow, spawns debugger agent for investigation, spawns security-reviewer
 agent to check the fix, runs both in parallel, synthesizes results]

At Level 5, you are not using a tool. You are operating an engineering system that happens to be powered by AI.

A Concrete Level 3 Example¶

To illustrate the jump from Level 2 to Level 3, consider a specific scenario. You are writing a Python service and every time Claude Code generates code, you manually remind it to add type hints, use f-strings, and import from pathlib instead of os.path.

At Level 2, you type these reminders with every prompt:

You: Write a function to parse the config file. Remember to use
     type hints, pathlib, and f-strings.

At Level 3, you create a rule file:

markdown
# .claude/rules/python.md
---
paths:
  - "**/*.py"
---

# Python Rules
- Type hints on all function signatures
- f-strings over .format() or %
- pathlib over os.path
- Use dataclasses or Pydantic for data structures

Now you just type:

You: Write a function to parse the config file

The rule loads automatically when Claude Code touches a .py file. You never mention type hints again. Every Python file Claude Code generates follows your standards. The rule is checked into git, so your entire team gets the same behavior.

This single change – writing a 10-line rule file once – eliminates hundreds of repeated instructions over the lifetime of the project. That is the Level 3 multiplier.

The Level Distribution¶

Here is the uncomfortable truth about where most users sit:

Level Description % of Users Productivity Multiplier
1 Basic prompting 50% 1.5x
2 Tool-aware usage 35% 3x
3 Configuration engineering 10% 7x
4 Workflow automation 4% 15x
5 Architecture-level collaboration 1% 25x+

This book starts at Level 3 and takes you to Level 5. You already know how to ask Claude Code questions. Now you will learn how to engineer a system that executes at professional standards with minimal input.

Baby Usage vs. Professional Usage: A Concrete Comparison¶

Let’s make the difference visceral. Same task, two approaches.

The task: Your e-commerce application has a bug where discount codes are applied twice when the user clicks the “Apply” button rapidly. Issue #87 on GitHub.

Baby Usage (Level 1-2)¶

You: There's a bug in our checkout where discount codes get applied twice.
     Can you look at src/checkout/discount.py and fix it?

Claude: Let me read that file...
[reads file]
I see the issue. The apply_discount function doesn't check if a discount
has already been applied. Here's the fix:
[shows a code change]

You: Looks good, can you also write a test?

Claude: Sure, here's a test:
[shows a test]

You: Can you run the tests?

Claude: [runs pytest, one test fails]
It looks like there's an import error...

You: Fix the import

Claude: [fixes import, runs tests again, passes]

You: Ok, can you commit this?

Claude: [commits with "fix discount bug"]

Time: 8-12 minutes of back-and-forth. No standard commit message. No PR. No security check on the financial logic. No linting. You directed every single step.

Professional Usage (Level 4-5)¶

The project has: - CLAUDE.md with coding standards and test-first workflow - .claude/rules/python.md enforcing type hints and ruff formatting - .claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh preventing dangerous commands - .claude/hooks/auto-lint-python.sh running ruff after every edit - .claude/skills/fix-issue/SKILL.md defining the end-to-end fix workflow - .claude/agents/security-reviewer.md for financial code scrutiny

You: 87

One number. Here is what executes:

  1. Auto-router decodes “87” as a GitHub issue number, routes to fix-issue skill
  2. gh issue view 87 reads the full issue description and comments
  3. Grep searches for apply_discount, discount_code, related patterns
  4. Read opens src/checkout/discount.py and tests/test_discount.py
  5. A failing test is written: test_apply_discount_rapid_double_click_applies_once
  6. Bash(pytest tests/test_discount.py -k rapid) confirms the test fails
  7. The fix is implemented: an idempotency check using a set of applied codes
  8. Auto-lint hook fires: ruff check --fix and ruff format run automatically
  9. Bash(pytest tests/test_discount.py) – all tests pass
  10. Bash(pytest) – full suite passes
  11. The security-reviewer agent is spawned to check the financial logic
  12. git commit -m "fix: prevent duplicate discount application (fixes #87)"
  13. gh pr create with summary, linked issue, and test plan

Time: 90 seconds. You typed two characters.

The Economics: Why This Matters¶

Let’s talk numbers, because engineering time is expensive and AI tokens are cheap.

A senior engineer costs roughly $100/hour fully loaded. Claude Code with Opus costs approximately $15 per million input tokens and $75 per million output tokens. A typical complex task (reading files, making changes, running tests) consumes about 50K input tokens and 10K output tokens – roughly $1.50.

Metric Basic Usage Professional Usage
Time per bug fix 15 min 2 min
Steps you direct manually 6-8 0-1
Tests written Maybe, if you ask Always (enforced by skill)
Linting Manual Automatic (hook)
Commit quality Inconsistent Standardized (config)
Security check Never Automatic (agent)
Engineer cost (per fix) $25 $3.30
AI cost (per fix) $0.50 $1.50
Total cost $25.50 $4.80
Tasks per day 30 200+

The professional setup costs 3x more in AI tokens but saves 5x in engineer time. More importantly, the quality is higher because standards are enforced by configuration, not by memory.

Over a year, a team of five engineers saves roughly 4,000 hours. That is two full-time engineer-years freed up for architecture, design, and the work that actually requires human judgment.

The Compounding Effect¶

The economics get better over time, not worse. Here is why:

Week 1: You create CLAUDE.md and basic settings. Productivity gain is modest – maybe 2x. You are still learning the system.

Month 1: You have added rules for your primary languages, two or three hooks (auto-lint, destructive command blocking), and a skill for your most common workflow. Productivity jumps to 5-7x.

Month 3: Your configuration stack is mature. Skills cover all common workflows. Hooks enforce quality. Agents handle specialized reviews. You rarely type more than a sentence for any task. Productivity is 10-15x.

Month 6: New team members clone the repo and immediately get the full configuration stack. They start at Level 3 on day one. The entire team operates at 10x+ because the configuration is shared infrastructure, not individual knowledge.

This is the real power. Configuration is not a personal optimization – it is team infrastructure. A well-configured .claude/ directory committed to git is worth more than any individual engineer’s prompting skill.

Claude Code vs. the Competition¶

An honest comparison. No vendor cheerleading.

Feature Claude Code GitHub Copilot Cursor Windsurf Aider
Interface Terminal (CLI) IDE extension IDE (fork of VS Code) IDE (fork of VS Code) Terminal (CLI)
Agentic execution Full (reads, edits, runs commands, loops) Limited (chat + apply) Yes (Composer agent) Yes (Cascade agent) Partial (edit + run)
Tool system 10+ built-in tools N/A Built into IDE Built into IDE Edit + shell
Configuration depth CLAUDE.md, rules, skills, hooks, agents, settings .github/copilot-instructions.md .cursorrules .windsurfrules .aider.conf.yml
Custom workflows Skills (packaged multi-step workflows) None None None None
Automation hooks 7 lifecycle events, JSON protocol None None None None
Multi-agent Built-in Agent tool, parallel subagents None None None None
Model flexibility Anthropic, Bedrock, Vertex, custom GPT-4o/Claude (fixed) Multiple providers Multiple providers Multiple providers
Context window 200K tokens ~8K (inline), varies (chat) 200K+ 200K+ Varies by model
MCP support Native None Partial Partial None
Price API usage ($15/$75 per MTok Opus) or Max plan ($100-200/mo) $10-39/mo $20/mo $15/mo Free (pay for API)
Best for Power users, complex workflows, automation Quick inline suggestions Visual learners, IDE-centric IDE-centric, Cascade agent Simple terminal edit-and-run

Where Claude Code wins: Configuration depth, workflow automation, multi-agent architectures, terminal-native power users. No other tool has hooks, skills, custom agents, and intent routing.

Where others win: Copilot has the smoothest inline autocomplete. Cursor and Windsurf have polished visual interfaces with side-by-side diffs. If you never leave your IDE and want suggestions while typing, those tools have a better UX for that specific workflow.

The honest take: Claude Code is not for everyone. If you want a tool that suggests the next line while you type, use Copilot. If you want a polished IDE with AI features, use Cursor. If you want to engineer systems where AI executes complex workflows autonomously and you configure rather than direct – Claude Code is the only serious option.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

After working with hundreds of engineers adopting Claude Code, the same mistakes appear repeatedly. Knowing these upfront saves weeks of frustration.

Mistake 1: Treating It Like a Chatbot¶

The most common mistake. Engineers who used ChatGPT or Copilot Chat bring conversational habits: they ask questions, wait for answers, then ask follow-up questions. With Claude Code, this creates unnecessary back-and-forth.

Wrong:

You: What files handle authentication?
Claude: The auth files are in src/auth/...
You: Can you read login.py?
Claude: [reads file] Here's the content...
You: Can you find where the token is validated?
Claude: [searches] Line 47 has the validation...
You: Fix the validation bug there
Claude: [fixes it]

Right:

You: Fix the token validation bug in the auth system
Claude: [reads files, searches for validation, identifies bug, fixes it, runs tests]

Claude Code is an agent. Give it the goal and let it figure out the steps.

Mistake 2: Not Investing in Configuration¶

Engineers who skip CLAUDE.md and jump straight to prompting are repeating themselves constantly: “Use type hints.” “Run the linter.” “Follow our commit conventions.” “Write tests.” Every session. Every task.

Configuration is a one-time investment that pays compound interest. A 30-minute session creating CLAUDE.md, rules, and a few hooks saves 5-10 minutes per task, every task, forever.

Mistake 3: Over-Configuring on Day One¶

The opposite mistake. Some engineers spend a week building an elaborate configuration before they have used Claude Code for real work. They create 15 skills, 8 hooks, and a 200-line CLAUDE.md – then discover half of it is unnecessary and the other half is wrong.

Start with: 1. A minimal CLAUDE.md (under 50 lines) 2. Basic permissions in settings.json 3. One or two rules for your primary language

Iterate from there. Add a hook when you catch yourself manually running the linter. Add a skill when you notice a repeated multi-step workflow. Configuration should emerge from practice, not precede it.

Mistake 4: Not Using /clear Between Tasks¶

Context is finite. If you fix a bug, then review a PR, then write documentation – all in one session without clearing context – Claude Code is carrying 150K tokens of irrelevant auth code context while writing API docs. Quality degrades. Costs increase. Use /clear between unrelated tasks.

Mistake 5: Directing Every Step¶

If you find yourself typing “now do X” then “now do Y” then “now do Z,” you are working against the agent model. Claude Code is designed to determine the steps autonomously. Your job is to specify the outcome, not the process.

Directing: “Read the file, find the bug, write a test, fix the bug, run the test, commit.”

Engineering: “Fix the bug. Tests must pass.” (Your skill and hooks handle the rest.)

What You Will Build by the End of This Book¶

This is not a theoretical book. By the final chapter, you will have built and deployed:

  1. A complete professional configuration stack – CLAUDE.md, settings, rules, hooks, and skills that enforce your team’s standards automatically (Chapters 4-8)

  2. Custom workflow skills – /fix-issue, /deploy-check, /security-audit and others tailored to your project (Chapter 7)

  3. Automation hooks – Pre-commit secret scanning, auto-linting, destructive command blocking, desktop notifications (Chapter 8)

  4. Custom agents – A security reviewer that checks auth changes, a code reviewer that enforces patterns, a debugger that follows your investigation playbook (Chapter 21)

  5. An intent routing system – Type a number and it fixes the issue. Type a filename and it reviews the code. Type “deploy” and it validates readiness (Chapter 21)

  6. A full-stack web application – Built entirely through Claude Code, from scaffold to production, demonstrating every technique in the book (Chapter 17)

  7. A production SaaS – The capstone case study: planning, building, testing, and deploying a real application using professional Claude Code workflows (Chapter 25)

Every configuration, skill, hook, and agent in this book is production-tested. They come from real engineering workflows, not contrived examples.

Prerequisites¶

This book assumes you have:

  • Terminal comfort. You use the command line daily. You know what cd, ls, grep, and git do without looking them up. You do not need to be a bash wizard – Chapter 10 covers advanced shell operations – but the terminal should not intimidate you.

  • One programming language. Most examples use Python and TypeScript, but the concepts apply to any language. If you can read a function, follow a conditional, and understand what a test does, you have enough.

  • Git basics. You understand commits, branches, push, pull, and pull requests. You do not need to know git rebase --interactive or git bisect (though Claude Code does).

  • An API key. You need an Anthropic API key, or access through AWS Bedrock or Google Vertex AI. Chapter 2 covers setup in detail.

You do not need prior experience with AI coding tools. You do not need to know prompt engineering. You do not need a computer science degree. If you build software professionally and want to get dramatically better at it, this book is for you.

A Note on AI Skepticism¶

If you are skeptical about AI-generated code, good. Skepticism is healthy. The engineers who produce the worst AI-assisted code are the ones who trust it blindly. The engineers who produce the best AI-assisted code are the ones who set up guardrails – tests, linting, reviews, hooks – because they know AI makes mistakes.

This book does not ask you to trust Claude Code. It asks you to configure Claude Code so that mistakes are caught by automated quality gates. Test-first workflows, auto-linting, security review agents, and destructive command blockers exist precisely because the AI is not infallible. Professional usage assumes fallibility and engineers around it.

The Road Ahead¶

Part I (Chapters 1-3) gives you foundations: what Claude Code is, how to set it up, and how it works under the hood. If you are already using Claude Code and want to jump to configuration, start at Chapter 4 – but Chapter 3’s architecture deep dive will pay dividends later.

Part II (Chapters 4-8) is the professional configuration stack. This is where most readers will see the biggest immediate impact. A well-crafted CLAUDE.md, proper settings, path-scoped rules, custom skills, and automation hooks transform Claude Code from a smart assistant into an engineering system.

Part III (Chapters 9-12) covers the tool system in depth. Understanding tools is not optional – it is how you predict what Claude Code will do and configure it to do what you want.

Part IV (Chapters 13-15) tackles context engineering: how to manage the 200K token window, use memory effectively, and write prompts that produce professional-grade output.

Part V (Chapters 16-20) is building real applications. Full-stack web apps, CLI tools, APIs, test-driven development, and CI/CD integration – all through Claude Code.

Part VI (Chapters 21-24) is advanced mastery: custom agents, intent routing, multi-file refactoring at scale, and extending Claude Code with MCP servers.

Part VII (Chapters 25-27) closes with a production case study, a complete reference, and expert-level troubleshooting.

Let’s get started. Chapter 2 gets your installation right – and “right” means more than npm install.

Key Takeaways¶

  1. Claude Code is an agentic system, not a chatbot. It reads files, edits code, runs commands, and loops autonomously. Understanding this distinction changes how you use it.

  2. There are five levels of mastery. Most users stay at Level 1-2 (basic prompting and tool-aware usage). This book teaches Level 3-5 (configuration engineering, workflow automation, and architecture-level collaboration).

  3. The professional advantage is configuration, not prompting. The same six-word prompt produces radically different outcomes depending on whether you have configured rules, hooks, skills, and agents.

  4. The economics are compelling. Professional usage costs more in AI tokens but saves 5-10x in engineer time, while producing higher quality output.

  5. Claude Code’s unique strength is depth of configuration. No other AI coding tool offers skills, hooks, custom agents, and intent routing. If you want to engineer AI workflows rather than just use AI tools, this is the platform.

Chapter 2: Setup and Configuration Mastery¶ ~18 min read

A junior engineer installs Claude Code with npm install -g and starts prompting. A senior engineer installs Claude Code, configures the provider, sets environment variables, wires up shell integration, creates a project configuration, and verifies the entire stack before writing a single prompt. The junior gets an AI chatbot. The senior gets an engineering system.

This chapter gets your installation right. Not “it runs” right – professionally configured right.

Installation Methods¶

npm (Recommended)¶

Claude Code is distributed as an npm package. You need Node.js 18+ installed.

bash
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

Verify the installation:

bash
claude --version

You should see output like claude-code 1.x.x. If you get command not found, your npm global bin directory is not in your PATH. Fix it:

bash
# Find where npm puts global binaries
npm config get prefix

# Add to your shell profile (~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc)
export PATH="$(npm config get prefix)/bin:$PATH"

Version Management¶

Pin to a specific version when stability matters (CI environments, team standardization):

bash
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@1.0.32

Update to the latest:

bash
npm update -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

Check for updates without installing:

bash
npm outdated -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

For teams that need to coordinate versions, add the version to your project documentation or use a setup script:

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

readonly REQUIRED_VERSION="1.0.32"
CURRENT_VERSION=$(claude --version 2>/dev/null | grep -oE '[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+' || echo "none")

if [[ "${CURRENT_VERSION}" != "${REQUIRED_VERSION}" ]]; then
    echo "Installing Claude Code v${REQUIRED_VERSION}..."
    npm install -g "@anthropic-ai/claude-code@${REQUIRED_VERSION}"
fi

First Launch: What Happens¶

The first time you run claude, several things happen in sequence:

bash
$ claude

  1. API key prompt. If ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not set, Claude Code asks for it interactively and stores it in your system keychain (macOS) or credential store (Linux).

  2. Directory creation. ~/.claude/ is created with default configuration files.

  3. Project detection. If you are in a git repository, Claude Code looks for .claude/ directory and CLAUDE.md files.

  4. System prompt assembly. Claude Code builds the system prompt from your configuration (more on this in Chapter 3).

  5. REPL. You get an interactive prompt. Type natural language, get results.

First launch output looks like this:

$ claude

  Claude Code v1.0.32
  Model: claude-sonnet-4-20250514

  Tips:
  - /help for available commands
  - /model to switch models
  - /doctor to diagnose issues
  - @ to reference files

  >

Environment Setup¶

API Keys and Providers¶

Claude Code supports four provider backends. You only need one.

Option 1: Anthropic Direct (default)

bash
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-api03-..."

Add this to your shell profile (~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc) for persistence:

bash
echo 'export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-api03-..."' >> ~/.zshrc

Option 2: AWS Bedrock

For organizations that route through AWS. Bedrock is common in enterprises that require data to stay within their AWS account and compliance boundary:

bash
export CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1
export AWS_REGION="us-east-1"
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="AKIA..."
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="..."

Bedrock uses your standard AWS credential chain, so IAM roles, SSO, and credential files all work:

bash
# Using AWS SSO (recommended for organizations)
aws sso login --profile my-profile
export AWS_PROFILE="my-profile"
export CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1

Bedrock model IDs differ from direct API. Claude Code handles the mapping automatically – you still use /model opus and it resolves to the correct Bedrock model ARN.

Option 3: Google Vertex AI

For organizations on Google Cloud Platform:

bash
export CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1
export CLOUD_ML_REGION="us-east5"
export ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT_ID="my-gcp-project"

Vertex AI uses Application Default Credentials:

bash
gcloud auth application-default login

Like Bedrock, Vertex has its own model identifiers, but Claude Code handles the translation. You configure the provider once and use the same /model opus and /model sonnet commands regardless of backend.

Option 4: OpenAI-Compatible Endpoints

For custom proxies or alternative providers:

bash
export CLAUDE_CODE_USE_THIRD_PARTY=1
export ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL="https://my-proxy.example.com/v1"
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="my-proxy-key"

The 20 Most Important Environment Variables¶

These are the variables that control Claude Code’s behavior. Set them in your shell profile for persistence, or in .claude/settings.json under the "env" key for project-scoped configuration.

Variable Default What It Does
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (none) Your API key for Anthropic direct
CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK 0 Enable AWS Bedrock backend
CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX 0 Enable Google Vertex AI backend
ANTHROPIC_MODEL claude-sonnet-4-20250514 Default model override
CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS 16384 Max tokens per response (raise for long outputs)
CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS 200000 Context window size to use
MAX_THINKING_TOKENS 10000 Budget for extended thinking
CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TOOL_USE_CONCURRENCY 1 Parallel tool execution (raise for speed)
CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_RETRIES 3 Retry count on API failures
CLAUDE_CODE_IDLE_THRESHOLD_MINUTES 60 Session timeout for idle detection
CLAUDE_CODE_IDLE_TOKEN_THRESHOLD 100000 Token usage threshold for idle sessions
DISABLE_TELEMETRY 0 Disable anonymous usage telemetry
CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC 0 Block non-essential API calls
ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH off Enable semantic tool search from MCP servers
ENABLE_MCP_LARGE_OUTPUT_FILES 0 Allow large MCP tool outputs via temp files
CLAUDE_ENABLE_STREAM_WATCHDOG 0 Monitor for stalled streams
PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE (unset) Prevent .pyc file creation in Python
CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TURNS (none) Limit agentic loop iterations
CLAUDE_CODE_CLEANUP_PERIOD_DAYS 30 Auto-cleanup old session data
CLAUDE_CODE_GIT_COMMIT_SIGNING (unset) Enable GPG commit signing

A professional configuration for power users looks like this in settings.json:

json
{
  "env": {
    "CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS": "128000",
    "CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS": "1000000",
    "MAX_THINKING_TOKENS": "100000",
    "CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TOOL_USE_CONCURRENCY": "8",
    "CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_RETRIES": "5",
    "CLAUDE_CODE_IDLE_THRESHOLD_MINUTES": "180",
    "DISABLE_TELEMETRY": "1",
    "PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE": "1"
  }
}

Key insight: CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TOOL_USE_CONCURRENCY set to 8 allows Claude Code to execute up to eight tool calls in parallel. This dramatically speeds up operations like searching multiple files or running multiple commands. The default of 1 is conservative; if your machine can handle it, raise it.

Model Selection: Opus vs. Sonnet vs. Haiku¶

Claude Code supports three model tiers. Choosing the right one per task is a genuine engineering decision.

Model Strengths Weaknesses Cost (Input/Output per MTok)
Opus Deep reasoning, complex architecture, security analysis, multi-file refactoring Slower, expensive $15 / $75
Sonnet Balanced speed/quality, code generation, reviews, most daily tasks Less depth than Opus on complex reasoning $3 / $15
Haiku Fast responses, simple queries, formatting, boilerplate Misses nuance, weaker on complex code $0.25 / $1.25

The practical rule: Use Sonnet as your default. Switch to Opus for security reviews, complex debugging, architectural decisions, and multi-file refactoring. Use Haiku for quick questions, documentation formatting, and simple boilerplate generation.

Switch models during a session:

/model opus          # Deep reasoning for the next task
/model sonnet        # Back to balanced
/model haiku         # Quick answers

Override the default model globally:

bash
export ANTHROPIC_MODEL="claude-opus-4-20250514"

Or per-skill in SKILL.md frontmatter:

yaml
---
name: security-audit
model: opus
---

This is powerful. Your security audit skill uses Opus ($15/MTok) for depth, while your code formatting skill uses Haiku ($0.25/MTok) for speed. You pay for intelligence where it matters.

Shell Integration¶

Bash/Zsh Completion¶

Claude Code supports tab completion for commands, file paths, and arguments. Enable it:

bash
# For zsh (add to ~/.zshrc)
eval "$(claude completion zsh)"

# For bash (add to ~/.bashrc)
eval "$(claude completion bash)"

After sourcing your profile, you get:

bash
$ claude /      # Tab shows all slash commands
$ claude @src/  # Tab shows files in src/

Terminal Requirements¶

Claude Code works best in terminals that support:

  • Unicode. For rendering special characters and Mermaid diagrams.
  • 256-color or TrueColor. For syntax highlighting in diffs and code blocks.
  • Minimum 80 columns wide. For readable output. 120+ recommended.

Terminals known to work well: iTerm2 (macOS), WezTerm, Alacritty, Kitty, Windows Terminal, VS Code integrated terminal. The default macOS Terminal.app works but has limited color support.

Non-Interactive Mode¶

Claude Code runs in headless mode for scripting and CI/CD:

bash
# Single prompt, get output, exit
claude -p "What is the purpose of src/auth/middleware.py?"

# Pipe input
echo "Review this for security issues" | claude -p --stdin

# JSON output for programmatic consumption
claude -p "List all Python files" --output-format json

# Stream output as it generates
claude -p "Explain this codebase" --output-format stream-json

Non-interactive mode is essential for CI integration (Chapter 20) and building automation scripts that use Claude Code as a component.

Command-Line Flags Reference¶

Claude Code accepts several flags that control its behavior at launch:

Flag Purpose Example
--version Print version and exit claude --version
-p Non-interactive single prompt claude -p "explain main.py"
--resume Resume previous session claude --resume
--output-format Output format: text, json, stream-json claude -p "..." --output-format json
--stdin Read additional input from stdin cat error.log \| claude -p "explain" --stdin
--model Override model for this session claude --model opus
--allowedTools Restrict available tools claude --allowedTools Read,Grep,Glob
--no-cache Bypass prompt cache claude --no-cache
--verbose Extra debugging output claude --verbose

These flags compose. For example, a CI script might use:

bash
claude -p "Run the test suite and report failures" \
  --output-format json \
  --model sonnet \
  --allowedTools Bash,Read,Grep,Glob

This runs a single prompt, outputs JSON (for programmatic parsing), uses Sonnet (cheaper for CI), and restricts tools to read-only plus Bash (so it can run tests but not edit files).

Directory Structure: What Gets Created Where¶

Understanding where Claude Code stores its data prevents confusion later.

Global Configuration: ~/.claude/¶

~/.claude/
├── settings.json           # Your global user settings
├── credentials.json        # Encrypted API keys (auto-managed)
├── projects/               # Per-project auto-memory
│   └── <project-hash>/
│       └── MEMORY.md       # What Claude remembers about this project
├── sessions/               # Session history (SQLite)
└── todos/                  # Task tracking

~/.claude/settings.json contains your user-level settings. These apply to every project unless overridden. Example:

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(git *)",
      "Bash(npm *)"
    ]
  },
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  "effortLevel": "high"
}

Project Configuration: .claude/¶

your-project/
├── .claude/
│   ├── settings.json       # Shared project settings (committed to git)
│   ├── settings.local.json # Personal overrides (gitignored)
│   ├── rules/              # Path-scoped coding standards
│   ├── skills/             # Reusable workflow definitions
│   ├── agents/             # Specialized subagent definitions
│   └── hooks/              # Automation shell scripts
├── CLAUDE.md               # Project brain (committed to git)
└── ... your code ...

The settings cascade: Settings are merged in this order, with later values overriding earlier ones:

Managed (enterprise) → User (~/.claude/) → Project (.claude/) → Local (.claude/settings.local.json)

This means your team commits .claude/settings.json with safe defaults and shared hooks, while individual engineers override with personal permissions in settings.local.json (which is .gitignored).

What to Commit vs. What to Gitignore¶

File Commit? Why
CLAUDE.md Yes Team-shared project instructions
.claude/settings.json Yes Shared hooks, permissions, env vars
.claude/settings.local.json No Personal overrides (add to .gitignore)
.claude/rules/*.md Yes Team coding standards
.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md Yes Team workflow definitions
.claude/agents/*.md Yes Team subagent definitions
.claude/hooks/*.sh Yes Team automation scripts

Add to your .gitignore:

.claude/settings.local.json

IDE Integration¶

VS Code Extension¶

Claude Code has a VS Code extension that connects the terminal-based agent to your editor:

bash
# Install the extension
code --install-extension anthropic.claude-code

The extension provides:

  • Inline diff view for Claude Code’s edits (accept/reject per hunk)
  • Status bar showing current model, token usage, session status
  • Terminal integration for the Claude Code REPL
  • File decorations marking files Claude Code has modified

Using Claude Code primarily through VS Code is a valid workflow, especially if you want visual diffs. But the terminal-native workflow is faster for power users who configure skills and hooks.

JetBrains Plugin¶

A JetBrains plugin provides similar functionality for IntelliJ, PyCharm, WebStorm, and other JetBrains IDEs.

Terminal-Native Workflow (Recommended for This Book)¶

This book assumes you are working in the terminal. The terminal-native workflow is:

  1. Open a terminal in your project directory
  2. Run claude
  3. Type prompts, get results
  4. Use /commands for built-in operations
  5. Exit with /exit or Ctrl+D

Every example in this book works in the terminal. IDE-specific features are bonuses, not requirements.

Multi-Model Setup¶

A professional setup uses different models for different tasks. Here is how to configure that.

Per-Session Model Switching¶

During a session, switch models with the /model command:

> /model opus
Switched to claude-opus-4-20250514

> Analyze the security implications of our auth middleware
[deep analysis using Opus]

> /model sonnet
Switched to claude-sonnet-4-20250514

> Add a docstring to this function
[quick task using Sonnet]

Per-Skill Model Overrides¶

In your skill’s SKILL.md frontmatter, specify which model to use:

yaml
---
name: security-audit
description: Deep security analysis...
model: opus
effort: high
---
yaml
---
name: scaffold
description: Bootstrap a new project...
model: sonnet
effort: medium
---

This means /security-audit automatically uses Opus (the most capable model for security reasoning), while /scaffold uses Sonnet (fast enough for generating boilerplate). You never think about model selection during the workflow – it is baked into the configuration.

Per-Agent Model Selection¶

Agents (Chapter 6) can specify their model:

yaml
---
name: security-reviewer
model: opus
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---
yaml
---
name: code-reviewer
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob
---

When Claude Code delegates to the security-reviewer agent, it uses Opus. When it delegates to code-reviewer, it uses Sonnet. Parallel agent delegation means both can run simultaneously, each on the optimal model.

Cost Management¶

With different models at different price points, monitoring cost matters:

bash
# Check current session usage
/stats

# Output:
#   Session tokens: 45,231 input / 12,456 output
#   Estimated cost: $0.87
#   Duration: 23 minutes
#   Tool calls: 14

A rough monthly cost guide:

Usage Pattern Monthly Cost
Light (1-2 hours/day, mostly Sonnet) $50-100
Moderate (4 hours/day, Sonnet + occasional Opus) $200-400
Heavy (8+ hours/day, frequent Opus for security/architecture) $500-1000
Enterprise (team of 5, mixed models) $2000-5000

The Anthropic Max plan ($100-200/month) provides a fixed-price alternative to API usage. It includes generous limits for individual use and is worth considering if your monthly API costs consistently exceed $150.

Essential Built-In Commands¶

Claude Code ships with dozens of built-in commands. Here are the ones you should know from day one, before building any custom skills:

Session Management¶

Command What It Does
/clear Wipe conversation context and start fresh. Use between unrelated tasks
/compact [topic] Summarize conversation to free context. Optionally keep topic-specific content
/exit or Ctrl+D End the session
/resume Resume the most recent session
/context Show how much context is used (tokens, percentage)
/rewind Roll back to an earlier conversation state

Model and Reasoning Control¶

Command What It Does
/model [name] Switch model: opus, sonnet, haiku
/effort [level] Set reasoning depth: low, medium, high, max
/fast Toggle fast output mode
/thinking Show/hide Claude’s extended thinking

The effort level is a surprisingly powerful control. At low, Claude Code answers quickly with minimal reasoning – good for “what does this function do?” questions. At high (the default in a professional setup), it reasons deeply before responding – better for debugging, architecture, and security analysis. At max, it engages extended thinking with large thinking budgets – use for the hardest problems.

> /effort low
> What does the @app decorator do in Flask?
[Quick, direct answer in 2 seconds]

> /effort high
> Why is our authentication middleware leaking sessions under load?
[Deep analysis with hypothesis formation, evidence search, root cause]

Git Operations¶

Command What It Does
/diff Show uncommitted changes with syntax highlighting
/commit Create a commit with auto-generated message
/pr-comments [N] Show comments on PR #N

Exploration¶

Command What It Does
/btw [question] Side question that does not pollute the main context
/stats Show session usage: tokens, cost, duration, tool calls
/export [file] Save the entire conversation to a file
/help List all available commands

Pro tip: /btw is underused. If you are deep in a debugging session and want to ask “what does __slots__ do in Python?” without contaminating your debug context, /btw is the answer. The question and response exist in a side channel.

Effort Level Configuration¶

The effort level deserves its own section because it directly impacts quality, speed, and cost.

Set the default effort in settings.json:

json
{
  "effortLevel": "high"
}

Override per-skill:

yaml
---
name: security-audit
effort: high
---
yaml
---
name: scaffold
effort: medium
---

The thinking budget also matters. Extended thinking gives Claude more “internal scratch space” to reason through complex problems. Configure it:

json
{
  "env": {
    "MAX_THINKING_TOKENS": "100000"
  },
  "alwaysThinkingEnabled": true
}

With alwaysThinkingEnabled, you see Claude’s thinking process in real-time – useful for understanding why it made certain choices. With thinking disabled, you only see the final output – cleaner but less transparent.

Setting Use Case
effort: low, thinking off Quick questions, boilerplate, formatting
effort: high, thinking on Debugging, architecture, security analysis
effort: max, thinking on + high budget Complex multi-file refactoring, novel problem solving

First Professional Session Walkthrough¶

Let’s put it all together. You have a fresh project, and you want to set it up for professional Claude Code usage from day one.

Step 1: Clone Your Repo¶

bash
git clone https://github.com/your-org/your-project.git
cd your-project

Step 2: Create the Configuration Structure¶

bash
mkdir -p .claude/rules .claude/skills .claude/hooks .claude/agents

Step 3: Create CLAUDE.md¶

This is the single most important file. Start minimal and iterate:

bash
cat > CLAUDE.md << 'EOF'
# Project: Your Project Name

## Communication
- Be terse. Lead with the answer
- Reference code as `file:line`
- No emojis

## Coding Standards
- Python: type hints, f-strings, pathlib, PEP 8
- Tests: pytest, test-first for bug fixes
- Formatting: ruff

## Git
- Branch prefixes: feat/, fix/, docs/, refactor/
- Commits: imperative mood, explain WHY

## Verification
- Run tests before committing
- Lint with ruff check
EOF

Step 4: Create Project Settings¶

bash
cat > .claude/settings.json << 'EOF'
{
  "$schema": "https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json",
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(git *)",
      "Bash(python -m pytest *)",
      "Bash(ruff *)"
    ],
    "deny": [
      "Read(./.env)",
      "Read(./.env.*)",
      "Read(./*credentials*)",
      "Read(./*secret*)"
    ]
  },
  "env": {
    "PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE": "1"
  },
  "effortLevel": "high",
  "includeGitInstructions": true
}
EOF

Step 5: Create a Python Rule¶

bash
cat > .claude/rules/python.md << 'EOF'
---
paths:
  - "**/*.py"
  - "**/*.pyi"
---

# Python Rules

- Type hints on all function signatures
- f-strings over .format() or %
- pathlib over os.path
- Use dataclasses or Pydantic for data structures
- Prefer `from __future__ import annotations`
EOF

Step 6: Launch and Verify¶

bash
claude

Inside the session, verify your configuration is loaded:

> /doctor

Claude Code Diagnostics
=======================
Version: 1.0.32
Model: claude-sonnet-4-20250514
API: Anthropic (direct)
Project: your-project
CLAUDE.md: Found (42 lines)
Settings: .claude/settings.json (loaded)
Rules: 1 active (python.md)
Skills: 0 defined
Hooks: 0 defined
Agents: 0 defined
Status: Healthy

Step 7: Run Your First Professional Task¶

> Review src/main.py

Claude Code reads src/main.py, applies your coding standards from CLAUDE.md and .claude/rules/python.md, and produces severity-ranked findings with file:line references. No extra instructions needed – the configuration handles it.

Step 8: Verify the Configuration Stack¶

Run a quick smoke test to confirm everything works together:

> Write a simple Python function in src/utils/helpers.py
  that validates email addresses

Watch for these indicators of a working configuration: - Claude Code uses type hints (from your Python rule) - Code uses f-strings, not .format() (from your Python rule) - If you have auto-lint hooks, ruff runs after the file is written - The commit message, if you commit, uses imperative mood (from your CLAUDE.md)

If any of these are missing, your configuration has a gap. Check the file paths in your rules’ paths: frontmatter and verify CLAUDE.md is in the project root.

What Most People Get Wrong in Setup¶

Using Default Permissions Forever¶

The default permission mode asks for confirmation on nearly every tool call. After a week of clicking “Allow” on git diff, pytest, and ruff hundreds of times, most engineers either switch to AllowAll (too permissive) or just tolerate the interruptions (too slow).

The professional approach: explicitly allow the safe, frequently-used commands and explicitly deny the dangerous ones. Chapter 5 covers the permission system in depth, but even a basic allow list transforms the experience:

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(git *)",
      "Bash(python -m pytest *)",
      "Bash(ruff *)",
      "Read(*)",
      "Glob(*)",
      "Grep(*)"
    ],
    "deny": [
      "Read(./.env)",
      "Bash(rm -rf *)"
    ]
  }
}

Not Setting Up a settings.local.json¶

Your team’s settings.json should be conservative – safe defaults for everyone. Your personal settings.local.json can be more permissive because you know your own habits and risk tolerance:

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(*)",
      "Edit(*)",
      "Write(*)"
    ]
  }
}

This lets you work without permission prompts while the team config stays safe. Remember to add .claude/settings.local.json to .gitignore.

Ignoring the env Configuration¶

The "env" block in settings.json sets environment variables for every Claude Code session. Most engineers do not configure it. The professional setup uses it to tune concurrency, output limits, and tool behavior:

json
{
  "env": {
    "CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TOOL_USE_CONCURRENCY": "8",
    "CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS": "128000",
    "MAX_THINKING_TOKENS": "100000",
    "PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE": "1"
  }
}

These four settings alone make Claude Code noticeably faster (parallel tool execution), capable of longer outputs (128K vs 16K default), better at complex reasoning (100K thinking tokens), and cleaner (no .pyc files cluttering your project).

Verifying Installation: The Doctor¶

The /doctor command is your diagnostic tool. Run it whenever something feels off:

> /doctor

It checks: - Claude Code version and update availability - API connectivity (can it reach the provider?) - Model availability (is the selected model accessible?) - Configuration files (are they valid JSON/YAML?) - Hook executability (are hook scripts marked executable?) - MCP server connectivity (if configured) - Disk space for session storage

If /doctor reports issues, fix them before doing anything else. A misconfigured setup produces inconsistent results that are hard to debug.

Troubleshooting Common Setup Issues¶

“command not found: claude”¶

Your npm global bin is not in your PATH.

bash
# Find the bin directory
npm config get prefix
# Output: /usr/local/lib (or similar)

# The binary is at <prefix>/bin/claude
# Add to PATH in your shell profile
export PATH="$(npm config get prefix)/bin:$PATH"

“API key not found”¶

Claude Code looks for the key in this order: 1. ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable 2. System keychain (macOS Keychain, Linux credential store) 3. Interactive prompt

If the environment variable is set but Claude Code cannot find it, check for typos and ensure it is exported (not just assigned):

bash
# Wrong (not exported):
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

# Right (exported):
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

“Model not available”¶

If you get errors about model availability:

bash
# Check which models your API key can access
claude -p "What model are you?" --output-format json

Opus requires a separate permission tier on some API plans. If Opus is unavailable, Sonnet is the fallback. Set it explicitly:

bash
export ANTHROPIC_MODEL="claude-sonnet-4-20250514"

“Permission denied” on Hook Scripts¶

Hook scripts must be executable:

bash
chmod +x .claude/hooks/*.sh

CLAUDE.md Not Loading¶

The file must be named exactly CLAUDE.md (case-sensitive) and placed in the project root (the directory where you run claude). It can also be placed in parent directories for global rules:

~/CLAUDE.md                    # Global (applies to all projects)
~/projects/CLAUDE.md           # Workspace-level
~/projects/my-app/CLAUDE.md    # Project-level (highest priority)

Settings Not Merging Correctly¶

Settings merge in order: Managed > User > Project > Local. To debug which settings are active:

bash
# Validate JSON syntax
python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('.claude/settings.json'))"
python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('.claude/settings.local.json'))"

If a setting from your project settings.json is not taking effect, check if settings.local.json is overriding it. Local settings always win.

Slow Response Times¶

If Claude Code feels sluggish:

  1. Check your network. API calls go to api.anthropic.com (or your Bedrock/Vertex endpoint). High latency to Anthropic’s servers directly impacts response time. A quick test: curl -o /dev/null -s -w "%{time_total}\n" https://api.anthropic.com – anything under 200ms is fine, over 500ms is a problem.
  2. Reduce context. Large projects with many files in the root directory slow down initial context assembly. Use /clear between unrelated tasks.
  3. Use Sonnet for quick tasks. Opus is slower (first-token latency ~1.5s vs ~0.7s for Sonnet). Switch to Sonnet for simple edits and questions. As we’ll see in Chapter 3, model choice directly impacts response time.
  4. Increase concurrency. Set CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TOOL_USE_CONCURRENCY to 4 or 8 to parallelize tool execution. This is one of the highest-impact performance settings – when Claude Code needs to read 5 files, it reads them simultaneously instead of sequentially.
json
{
  "env": {
    "CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TOOL_USE_CONCURRENCY": "8"
  }
}

What’s Next¶

You now have a properly installed, configured, and verified Claude Code setup. The configuration stack is in place: CLAUDE.md for project instructions, settings.json for permissions and hooks, and a path-scoped Python rule.

Chapter 3 takes you under the hood. Understanding the agentic loop, tool execution pipeline, permission system, and context window is not academic – it is the foundation for every configuration decision in Part II. When you know how Claude Code assembles its system prompt, you write better CLAUDE.md files. When you know how tools execute, you write better hooks. When you know how context compaction works, you manage your sessions effectively.

The architecture is the map. Let’s read it.

What Most People Get Wrong: The Setup Checklist¶

Before moving on, verify your setup against this checklist. These are the items that most engineers skip and later regret:

  • [ ] API key set in shell profile (not just the current session)
  • [ ] CLAUDE.md exists in project root with coding standards
  • [ ] .claude/settings.json has explicit allow and deny permissions
  • [ ] .claude/settings.local.json exists and is in .gitignore
  • [ ] At least one path-scoped rule (for your primary language)
  • [ ] .claude/ directory structure created (rules, skills, hooks, agents)
  • [ ] CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TOOL_USE_CONCURRENCY set to 4 or higher
  • [ ] MAX_THINKING_TOKENS set to 10000 or higher
  • [ ] /doctor runs clean with no warnings
  • [ ] You have run one real task and verified configuration applied

If all ten items are checked, you have a professional setup. If fewer than five are checked, you are leaving significant capability on the table.

Key Takeaways¶

  1. Configure your provider early. Whether Anthropic direct, Bedrock, or Vertex, set the environment variables in your shell profile so they persist across sessions.

  2. The settings cascade matters. Managed > User > Project > Local. Commit .claude/settings.json for team standards; use settings.local.json for personal overrides. Gitignore the local file.

  3. Match models to tasks. Opus for security and architecture, Sonnet for daily work, Haiku for quick queries. Configure this per-skill so it is automatic.

  4. Set up the configuration structure immediately. CLAUDE.md, .claude/settings.json, .claude/rules/, .claude/skills/, .claude/hooks/, .claude/agents/. Even if they are mostly empty, the structure enables growth.

  5. Use /doctor liberally. It catches configuration issues before they manifest as inconsistent behavior. Run it after any setup change.

Chapter 3: Architecture Deep Dive – How Claude Code Actually Works¶ ~21 min read

You are about to write your first hook. It fires on PreToolUse and checks the command against a blocklist. But you are unsure: does the hook see the command before permission checks or after? Does it get the raw command string or a JSON object? What exit code blocks execution?

These are not trivia questions. They are the difference between a hook that works and one that silently fails. And you cannot answer them without understanding the architecture.

This chapter is the map. Every configuration decision in Part II – how you structure CLAUDE.md, what permissions to set, which lifecycle events to hook – flows from understanding what happens between your keystroke and Claude Code’s response.

The Agentic Loop¶

This is the single most important concept in Claude Code. Everything else is implementation detail.

When you type a prompt and press Enter, Claude Code enters a loop:

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     THE AGENTIC LOOP                         │
│                                                              │
│  User message                                                │
│       │                                                      │
│       ▼                                                      │
│  ┌─────────────────┐                                         │
│  │ Assemble system  │ ◄── CLAUDE.md + rules + skills +       │
│  │ prompt           │     settings + git context + memory     │
│  └────────┬────────┘                                         │
│           │                                                  │
│           ▼                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────┐                                         │
│  │ Call LLM API     │ ──► Streaming response                 │
│  └────────┬────────┘                                         │
│           │                                                  │
│           ▼                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐                    │
│  │ Parse response   │────►│ end_turn?    │──► YES ──► Done   │
│  └────────┬────────┘     └──────────────┘                    │
│           │                      │                           │
│           │                     NO (tool_use)                │
│           ▼                      │                           │
│  ┌─────────────────┐            ▼                            │
│  │ Permission check │   ┌──────────────┐                     │
│  │ (deny→ask→allow) │   │ Too many     │──► YES ──► Stop    │
│  └────────┬────────┘   │ turns?        │    (warn user)     │
│           │             └──────────────┘                     │
│           ▼                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────┐                                         │
│  │ PreToolUse hooks │ ──► Exit 2? ──► Block + feedback       │
│  └────────┬────────┘                                         │
│           │                                                  │
│           ▼                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────┐                                         │
│  │ Execute tool     │ ──► With timeout                       │
│  └────────┬────────┘                                         │
│           │                                                  │
│           ▼                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────┐                                         │
│  │ PostToolUse hooks│ ──► (auto-lint, notify, etc.)          │
│  └────────┬────────┘                                         │
│           │                                                  │
│           ▼                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────┐                                         │
│  │ Truncate result  │ ──► If too large for context           │
│  │ if needed        │                                        │
│  └────────┬────────┘                                         │
│           │                                                  │
│           ▼                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────┐                                         │
│  │ Append to        │                                        │
│  │ conversation     │ ──► Loop back to "Call LLM API"        │
│  └─────────────────┘                                         │
│                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Let’s walk through each step with concrete detail.

Step 1: System Prompt Assembly¶

Before the LLM sees your message, Claude Code builds a system prompt. This is not a simple template – it is a dynamically assembled document that can exceed 10,000 tokens. Here is what gets included, in order:

  1. Base system prompt. Claude Code’s core instructions: how to use tools, how to format output, general behavior guidelines. You do not control this.

  2. CLAUDE.md content. Hierarchically merged. If you have a ~/CLAUDE.md (global), a ~/projects/CLAUDE.md (workspace), and ~/projects/my-app/CLAUDE.md (project), all three are included with project-level taking highest priority.

  3. Matching rules. Claude Code checks which files are “in scope” (recently read, recently edited, mentioned in the conversation) and loads rules whose paths: frontmatter matches those files.

  4. Active skill instructions. If a skill is triggered (by /command or auto-detection), its SKILL.md content is injected.

  5. Git context. Current branch, recent commit messages, staged changes, dirty files. This helps Claude Code understand the project’s current state.

  6. Directory listing. A snapshot of the project’s file structure so Claude Code knows what files exist without having to call Glob first.

  7. Memory context. Auto-memory entries from ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/MEMORY.md – things Claude Code has learned about your project in previous sessions.

  8. Tool definitions. JSON schema definitions for every available tool: parameters, descriptions, constraints.

Here is an approximate token budget for a typical system prompt:

Component Typical Tokens % of Budget
Base system prompt 3,000-5,000 15-25%
CLAUDE.md + rules 500-2,000 2-10%
Active skill 200-800 1-4%
Git context 200-500 1-2%
Directory listing 200-1,000 1-5%
Memory 100-500 0.5-2%
Tool definitions 3,000-5,000 15-25%
Total system prompt 7,000-15,000 35-75%

The remaining context window is for your conversation (messages, tool results, thinking).

Why this matters for configuration: A 200-line CLAUDE.md consumes roughly 600 tokens on every single API call. Those tokens are paid for repeatedly, turn after turn. A 42-line CLAUDE.md with @imports to rules that load only when relevant is dramatically more efficient. As we’ll see in Chapter 4, brevity in CLAUDE.md is not laziness – it is engineering.

Step 2: LLM API Call¶

With the system prompt assembled, Claude Code sends the full conversation (system + message history + new user message) to the Claude API. The response streams back as Server-Sent Events (SSE).

The streaming format looks like this on the wire:

event: content_block_start
data: {"type":"content_block_start","index":0,"content_block":{"type":"thinking","thinking":""}}

event: content_block_delta
data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":0,"delta":{"type":"thinking_delta","thinking":"Let me analyze..."}}

event: content_block_stop
data: {"type":"content_block_stop","index":0}

event: content_block_start
data: {"type":"content_block_start","index":1,"content_block":{"type":"text","text":""}}

event: content_block_delta
data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":1,"delta":{"type":"text_delta","text":"I'll read the file"}}

event: content_block_start
data: {"type":"content_block_start","index":2,"content_block":{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_01...","name":"Read","input":{}}}

event: content_block_delta
data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":2,"delta":{"type":"input_json_delta","partial_json":"{\"file_path\":\"/src/main.py\"}"}}

Each response can contain multiple content blocks:

Block Type Purpose
thinking Extended thinking (reasoning, planning) – visible with /thinking or when alwaysThinkingEnabled is true
text Human-readable text response shown to the user
tool_use A tool call that Claude Code will execute

A single response might contain: one thinking block (internal reasoning), one text block (“Let me investigate…”), and three tool_use blocks (Read + Grep + Bash). Claude Code processes all of them.

Step 3: Response Parsing¶

Claude Code parses the streamed content blocks and makes a decision:

  • If stop_reason is end_turn: The response is complete. Display it and wait for the next user message.
  • If stop_reason is tool_use: There are tool calls to execute. Enter the tool execution pipeline.

This is what makes Claude Code agentic. The LLM itself decides whether to respond with text (done) or request tool execution (continue working). Claude Code does not make this decision – the model does.

Step 4: Permission Check¶

Before executing any tool call, Claude Code evaluates permissions. The cascade is:

deny rules → ask rules → allow rules → default behavior

First match wins. Here is how it evaluates a Bash(git push origin main) call:

  1. Check deny rules. Is there a deny pattern matching Bash(git push *)? If yes, block immediately. Claude Code receives an error message that the tool was denied.
  2. Check ask rules. Is there an explicit ask pattern? If yes, prompt the user for approval.
  3. Check allow rules. Is there an allow pattern matching Bash(git *)? If yes, execute without asking.
  4. Default. If no rule matches, the behavior depends on the permission mode (Default asks for most things, AllowAll permits everything).

Permission patterns use simple glob matching:

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(git *)",           // Any git command
      "Bash(python -m pytest *)", // pytest only
      "Read(*)"                // Read any file
    ],
    "deny": [
      "Read(./.env)",          // Never read .env
      "Bash(rm -rf *)"        // Never rm -rf
    ]
  }
}

The pattern format is ToolName(argument_pattern). For Bash, the argument is the command string. For Read, it is the file path. For Edit, it is the file path.

Practical implication: If you allow Bash(git *), Claude Code can run git push --force without asking. If that concerns you, be more specific:

json
"allow": [
  "Bash(git diff *)",
  "Bash(git status *)",
  "Bash(git log *)",
  "Bash(git add *)",
  "Bash(git commit *)"
]

And explicitly deny the dangerous ones:

json
"deny": [
  "Bash(git push --force *)",
  "Bash(git push * --force *)",
  "Bash(git reset --hard *)"
]

Step 5: PreToolUse Hooks¶

If the tool passes permission checks, PreToolUse hooks fire. These are shell scripts that receive the tool call details via stdin as JSON:

json
{
  "tool_name": "Bash",
  "tool_input": {
    "command": "rm -rf ./tmp/cache"
  }
}

The hook script reads this JSON, applies its logic, and exits:

Exit Code Meaning
0 Allow the tool to proceed
2 Block the tool. Stderr output becomes feedback to Claude Code
Other Non-blocking error (logged, execution continues)

Here is a real PreToolUse hook that blocks destructive commands:

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)
TOOL_NAME=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get('tool_name',''))" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

if [[ "${TOOL_NAME}" != "Bash" ]]; then
    exit 0
fi

COMMAND=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get('tool_input',{}).get('command',''))" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

# Destructive patterns
BLOCKED_PATTERNS=(
    "rm -rf /"
    "rm -rf ~"
    "rm -rf \."
    "DROP TABLE"
    "DROP DATABASE"
    "git push --force main"
    "git push --force origin main"
    "git push origin main --force"
)

for pattern in "${BLOCKED_PATTERNS[@]}"; do
    if [[ "${COMMAND}" == *"${pattern}"* ]]; then
        cat >&2 << HEREDOC
{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"BLOCKED: Command matches destructive pattern '${pattern}'. This action could cause irreversible damage."}}
HEREDOC
        exit 2
    fi
done

exit 0

Hooks are wired in settings.json:

json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Bash",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "./.claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh",
            "timeout": 5000,
            "statusMessage": "Safety check..."
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

The matcher field determines which tool triggers the hook. "Bash" means only Bash tool calls fire this hook. An empty matcher "" fires on every tool call.

Step 6: Tool Execution¶

The tool executes with the provided input and a configured timeout. Here is what happens for each major tool:

Bash: - Spawns a child process with the command - Captures stdout and stderr - Respects the timeout (default 120 seconds, configurable up to 600 seconds) - Working directory is the project root - Environment variables from settings.json "env" are applied

Read: - Opens the file, reads content - Returns content with line numbers (cat -n format) - For large files: truncates with a warning - For binary files: returns a description or renders (images)

Edit: - Performs exact string replacement in the file - The old_string must match exactly one location - Returns the result (success or failure + reason)

Write: - Overwrites the file entirely - Creates the file if it does not exist - Creates parent directories if needed

Grep: - Runs ripgrep under the hood - Returns matching lines, file paths, or counts depending on output mode - Respects .gitignore patterns

Agent: - Spawns a child Claude Code process (a subagent) - The subagent has its own conversation context - Can run in the same directory or an isolated worktree - Results are returned to the parent conversation

Step 7: PostToolUse Hooks¶

After the tool executes successfully, PostToolUse hooks fire. These cannot block (the action already happened), but they can perform follow-up actions.

The canonical example: auto-linting Python files after every edit.

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)
TOOL_NAME=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get('tool_name',''))" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

if [[ "${TOOL_NAME}" != "Edit" && "${TOOL_NAME}" != "Write" ]]; then
    exit 0
fi

FILE_PATH=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin).get('tool_input',{}); print(d.get('file_path',d.get('path','')))" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

if [[ "${FILE_PATH}" == *.py ]]; then
    ruff check --fix "${FILE_PATH}" 2>/dev/null || true
    ruff format "${FILE_PATH}" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

exit 0

Wired as:

json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Edit|Write",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "./.claude/hooks/auto-lint-python.sh",
            "timeout": 10000,
            "statusMessage": "Auto-linting..."
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

Step 8: Result Truncation¶

Tool results can be large. A Grep across a large codebase might return thousands of lines. A Read on a 10,000-line file returns all of it. Claude Code handles this:

  1. Result size check. If the result exceeds the truncation threshold (roughly 50K characters), it is truncated.
  2. Truncation message. A note is appended: “Output truncated. N lines omitted.”
  3. The truncated result is what Claude sees. The LLM works with incomplete information and may need to make additional, more targeted tool calls.

Why this matters: If you configure a Grep with no head_limit, it might return 10,000 matches. Claude Code truncates to fit context, possibly losing the relevant result. Always use targeted searches:

// Bad: returns everything
Grep(pattern="error", path="./")

// Good: targeted, limited
Grep(pattern="AuthenticationError", path="./src/auth/", head_limit=20)

Step 9: Append and Loop¶

The tool result is appended to the conversation as a tool_result message, and the loop returns to Step 2 (LLM API call). The LLM now has:

  • The original system prompt
  • The user’s message
  • Its previous response (text + tool calls)
  • The tool results

It decides what to do next: call more tools (loop continues) or respond with text (loop ends).

A typical task might loop 3-8 times:

Turn Action
1 User: “Fix the auth bug”
2 Claude: Grep for auth-related files
3 Claude: Read the main auth file
4 Claude: Read the test file
5 Claude: Edit the auth file (fix the bug)
6 Claude: Bash(pytest tests/test_auth.py)
7 Claude: Text response (“Fixed the bug, here’s what I changed…”)

Max turns: Claude Code has a configurable turn limit to prevent runaway loops. If the model keeps calling tools without converging, the loop stops and the user is warned. The default is generous (around 100 turns) but can be controlled with CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TURNS.

The Context Window¶

The context window is the total amount of text (measured in tokens) that can be in a single conversation. For Claude models, this is approximately 200K tokens.

What Fills the Context¶

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              200K Token Window                   │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ System Prompt: ~10K tokens               │    │
│  │ (CLAUDE.md + rules + tools + git + memory)│    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Conversation History: ~150K tokens       │    │
│  │ (messages + tool calls + tool results)   │    │
│  │                                          │    │
│  │ Turn 1: User message (50 tokens)         │    │
│  │ Turn 1: Claude response (200 tokens)     │    │
│  │ Turn 1: Tool result - Read (3000 tokens) │    │
│  │ Turn 2: Claude response (150 tokens)     │    │
│  │ Turn 2: Tool result - Grep (500 tokens)  │    │
│  │ ...                                      │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Reserve for Response: ~40K tokens        │    │
│  │ (Claude needs room to generate output)   │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Approximate budget:

Component Tokens Percentage
System prompt 7K-15K 4-8%
Conversation history 100K-150K 50-75%
Response reserve 30K-50K 15-25%

The conversation history is where tokens accumulate fast. Every Read of a 500-line Python file adds roughly 2,000 tokens. Every Grep result adds hundreds. Every Bash output adds the full stdout. After 20-30 tool calls, you have consumed tens of thousands of tokens in tool results alone.

Context Compaction¶

When the context approaches the window limit, Claude Code performs compaction. This is an automatic process that summarizes older parts of the conversation to free up space.

Here is what compaction does:

  1. Identifies old turns. Turns from early in the conversation that are unlikely to be relevant to the current task.
  2. Summarizes them. Replaces verbose tool results and messages with a brief summary: “Earlier, I read src/auth/login.py and found a token validation bug on line 47.”
  3. Keeps recent turns intact. The last N turns remain uncompacted to preserve current context.
  4. Preserves key information. File paths, error messages, and decisions are retained even in compacted form.

You can also trigger compaction manually:

/compact               # Compact everything
/compact security      # Compact but preserve security-related context

The /clear discipline: Between unrelated tasks, use /clear to reset the conversation entirely. This is more effective than compaction for task switching because it starts with a fresh context window:

> /clear
Context cleared. Starting fresh.

> [new task here]

If you are fixing a bug in auth, then switching to writing documentation for the API, /clear between them. The auth context is irrelevant to the docs task and wastes tokens.

The Permission System in Depth¶

Permissions have four layers, evaluated in order:

Managed → User → Project → Local

The Four Layers¶

Managed (enterprise): Set by your organization’s IT. You cannot override these. They are delivered by the enterprise deployment and typically deny dangerous operations globally.
