Key differences from the team setup:

  • No write access. CI should read and test, not modify. Edit(*) and Write(*) are denied.
  • No package installation. Dependencies should already be in the CI image.
  • No git writes. No push, no commit from the AI agent.
  • No rm. CI should not delete files.
  • Memory disabled. CI runs are ephemeral. Memory between runs would cause inconsistencies.
  • Hooks disabled. Hooks add latency and may depend on tools not available in the CI image.

Enterprise Setup¶

Enterprise environments add a managed policy layer that individual developers and projects cannot override. This is configured by organization administrators, not in the repository.

The managed policy typically enforces:

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "deny": [
      "Bash(*sudo *)",
      "Bash(curl * | bash*)",
      "Bash(wget * -O - | *)",
      "Bash(*pip install*--index-url*)",
      "Read(./**/*.pem)",
      "Read(./**/*.key)",
      "Read(./**/*.p12)",
      "WebFetch(*)",
      "WebSearch(*)"
    ]
  },
  "autoMemoryEnabled": false,
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
}

Enterprise policies often:

  • Block web access entirely to prevent data exfiltration
  • Force a specific model to control costs
  • Disable memory to prevent sensitive information from persisting
  • Block all privilege escalation and remote code execution patterns
  • Restrict package sources to approved registries only

The enterprise layer is the only one that cannot be overridden by any settings file in the repository. It is the hard ceiling.

Real-World Security Hardening¶

Let’s walk through hardening a real project: a Python web API that handles user authentication and payment processing.

Step 1: Identify Sensitive Files¶

project/
├── .env                    # DATABASE_URL, STRIPE_KEY, JWT_SECRET
├── .env.production         # Production secrets
├── certs/
│   ├── server.pem          # TLS certificate
│   └── server.key          # TLS private key
├── config/
│   ├── settings.py         # Safe — reads from env vars
│   └── secrets.json        # Stripe webhook secrets
├── src/
│   ├── auth/               # Authentication logic
│   ├── payments/           # Payment processing
│   └── users/              # User management
└── tests/

Step 2: Write Deny Rules for Every Sensitive File¶

json
{
  "deny": [
    "Read(./.env)",
    "Read(./.env.*)",
    "Read(./certs/**)",
    "Read(./config/secrets.json)",
    "Read(./**/*secret*)",
    "Read(./**/*credential*)",
    "Read(./**/*password*)"
  ]
}

Step 3: Allow Rules Scoped to Safe Directories¶

json
{
  "allow": [
    "Read(./src/**)",
    "Read(./tests/**)",
    "Read(./config/settings.py)",
    "Read(./docs/**)",
    "Read(./migrations/**)",
    "Read(*.md)",
    "Read(*.toml)",
    "Read(Dockerfile)",
    "Read(docker-compose.yml)",
    "Edit(./src/**/*.py)",
    "Edit(./tests/**/*.py)",
    "Write(./src/**/*.py)",
    "Write(./tests/**/*.py)"
  ]
}

Step 4: Deny Destructive Operations on Production Data¶

json
{
  "deny": [
    "Bash(*DROP*)",
    "Bash(*TRUNCATE*)",
    "Bash(*DELETE FROM*WHERE 1*)",
    "Bash(*--production*)",
    "Bash(*PROD*=*)",
    "Bash(stripe *)",
    "Bash(heroku run *)",
    "Bash(aws *)"
  ]
}

Step 5: Add a PreToolUse Hook for Extra Safety¶

The deny list catches static patterns. A hook catches dynamic ones:

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)
COMMAND=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(data.get('tool_input', {}).get('command', ''))
")

# Block any command that references production environment
if echo "${COMMAND}" | grep -qiE '(production|prod[^u]|--prod|PROD_)'; then
  echo '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"Blocked: command references production environment"}}' >&2
  exit 2
fi

exit 0

This hook catches commands where the static deny pattern would miss edge cases – variable names containing “prod”, flags like --prod, environment references like PROD_DB_HOST.

Step 6: Validate the Configuration¶

Run a mental simulation. For each sensitive file, ask: “If Claude Code tries to read this, what happens?”

File Deny Rule Result
.env Read(./.env) Blocked
.env.production Read(./.env.*) Blocked
certs/server.key Read(./certs/**) Blocked
config/secrets.json Read(./config/secrets.json) Blocked
config/settings.py Allowed explicitly Reads fine
src/auth/login.py Read(./src/**) Reads fine

If any sensitive file would be readable, add a deny rule. If any safe file would be blocked, add an allow rule.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

After reviewing hundreds of Claude Code configurations, these are the recurring mistakes.

Mistake 1: Allowing Everything, Denying Nothing¶

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": ["Bash(*)"]
  }
}

This works fine until Claude Code reads your .env, deletes your build directory, or force-pushes to main. “It hasn’t happened yet” is not a security strategy. You need deny rules.

Mistake 2: Deny Rules Without Wildcards¶

json
{
  "deny": [
    "Read(./.env)"
  ]
}

This blocks .env but not .env.local, .env.production, or .env.staging. Always use Read(./.env*) to catch variants. Similarly, Bash(rm -rf .) does not catch Bash(rm -rf ./) or Bash(rm -rf ./src). Use Bash(rm -rf *).

Mistake 3: Putting Secrets in the env Field¶

json
{
  "env": {
    "DATABASE_URL": "postgres://admin:password@prod-db:5432/myapp"
  }
}

This file is committed to git. Your database password is now in version history forever. Use system environment variables or a secrets manager. The env field is for non-sensitive configuration only.

Mistake 4: Disabling Hooks in Shared Settings¶

json
{
  "disableAllHooks": true
}

If this is in .claude/settings.json, the entire team loses their guardrails. One developer having a bad hook experience should not disable protections for everyone. Put disableAllHooks only in settings.local.json, and only temporarily.

Mistake 5: Overly Broad Allow Rules on File Writes¶

json
{
  "allow": [
    "Write(*)"
  ]
}

This lets Claude Code write to any file in the project – including .claude/settings.json itself. Claude Code could, theoretically, modify its own permissions. Scope writes to specific directories: Write(src/**), Write(tests/**).

Mistake 6: Not Reviewing Settings Changes in PRs¶

Treat .claude/settings.json like infrastructure code. A change to the permission deny list is as security-critical as a change to your firewall rules. Require code review. Add a CODEOWNERS entry:

# .github/CODEOWNERS
.claude/settings.json @security-team @tech-lead

Mistake 7: Forgetting the Precedence Cascade¶

A developer adds Read(./.env) to their local allow list, thinking it overrides the project deny. It does not. Deny beats allow regardless of which file the rules come from. The developer then wastes an hour trying to figure out why Claude Code “ignores” their permissions.

The rule is simple: deny always wins. If you need Claude Code to read a file that is denied at the project level, the deny rule must be removed from the project file – which means a PR, a review, and a justification. This is by design.

Key Takeaways¶

  1. Two files, clear separation. .claude/settings.json is the team contract – committed, reviewed, shared. .claude/settings.local.json is personal – gitignored, flexible, overridable. Never put team guardrails in the local file. Never put personal preferences in the shared file.

  2. Deny always wins. The evaluation order is deny, then allow, then ask. First match in each phase wins. This means you can safely combine broad allow rules with specific deny rules – the deny rules act as guardrails that cannot be bypassed by any allow rule, from any settings layer.

  3. Permissions are your security perimeter. Every tool call passes through the permission system before execution. A well-configured deny list protects against secret exposure, data destruction, repository damage, and prompt injection attacks. Treat settings.json changes like infrastructure security changes.

  4. Environment determines configuration. Solo developers optimize for speed (broad allow, targeted deny). Teams optimize for safety (specific allow, broad deny). CI/CD optimizes for restriction (read-only, no writes, no memory). Enterprise adds an unbreakable managed policy layer. Match your configuration to your threat model.

  5. The settings file is code. Review it in PRs. Add it to CODEOWNERS. Validate it before committing. Test your deny rules against realistic scenarios. A misconfigured permission is a vulnerability – and unlike a code bug, it silently waits until the worst possible moment to manifest.

Chapter 6: Rules – Path-Scoped Coding Standards¶ ~20 min read

A CLAUDE.md that tries to hold every coding standard for every language in your project will bloat to 400 lines. Those 400 lines are injected into every single API call, whether you are editing a Python file or a shell script. You pay for all of them in tokens, every turn, and Claude pays for them in attention – the more instructions it has to juggle, the more likely it is to ignore the ones that matter right now.

Rules solve this problem. They are modular, scoped, and loaded on demand. A Python rule loads when you are working on Python. A shell rule loads when you are editing a bash script. A security rule loads on every session because security is never out of scope. This is not just organization – it is engineering the context window for maximum compliance.

This chapter covers everything you need to know about rules: how they work, how to write them, how scoping works under the hood, and how to avoid the mistakes that make rules useless.

What Rules Are¶

Rules are markdown files that live in .claude/rules/. Each file contains coding standards, conventions, tool preferences, or domain knowledge that Claude Code should follow when generating or reviewing code.

There are two kinds:

Always-on rules have no paths: frontmatter. They load into Claude Code’s context on every session, every turn, regardless of what files you are working with. Use these for cross-cutting concerns that apply everywhere: security practices, testing philosophy, commit conventions.

Path-scoped rules have YAML frontmatter with paths: declarations. They load only when you are working with files that match the specified glob patterns. Use these for language-specific conventions, framework-specific patterns, or domain-specific knowledge that only matters in certain parts of the codebase.

Here is the key mental model: rules are an extension of CLAUDE.md, but with conditional loading. Claude Code merges them into the system prompt alongside CLAUDE.md content, and the model sees them as authoritative project instructions – not suggestions, not hints, but rules to follow.

How Claude Code Decides Which Rules to Load¶

Every time Claude Code assembles a system prompt (which happens before every LLM API call), it evaluates which rules are “in scope.” The process works like this:

  1. Collect in-scope files. Claude Code maintains a set of files that are currently relevant: files you have recently read, files you have recently edited, files mentioned in your conversation, files in your current working directory listing.

  2. Evaluate each rule. For every .md file in .claude/rules/:

  3. If the rule has no paths: frontmatter, it is always in scope. Load it.

  4. If the rule has paths: frontmatter, check each glob pattern against the in-scope files. If any in-scope file matches any pattern, load the rule.

  5. Inject into system prompt. Loaded rules are appended to the system prompt, after CLAUDE.md content and before tool definitions.

This means rules are not statically loaded at session start. They are dynamically evaluated on every turn. If you start a session working on Python files (Python rules load), then switch to editing shell scripts (shell rules load, Python rules may unload if no Python files remain in scope), the rules adapt in real time.

As discussed in Chapter 3, this dynamic loading is part of the system prompt assembly step in the agentic loop. The token cost of rules is paid only when they are in scope, which is why path-scoped rules are dramatically more efficient than stuffing everything into CLAUDE.md.

Rule File Anatomy¶

A rule file is a standard markdown file with optional YAML frontmatter. Here is the complete structure:

markdown
---
paths:
  - "**/*.py"
  - "**/*.pyi"
---

# Python Rules

## Style
- Use type hints on all function signatures
- Use f-strings over .format() or %
- Use pathlib over os.path for file operations
- Use dataclasses or Pydantic for data structures, not raw dicts

## Imports
- Group: stdlib, third-party, local -- separated by blank line
- Use absolute imports. Avoid wildcard imports

## Error Handling
- Catch specific exceptions, never bare `except:`
- Use `logging` module, not print(), for production code

## Tools
- Formatter: `ruff format`
- Linter: `ruff check --fix`
- Type checker: `pyright` or `mypy --strict`

Let’s break down each section.

The Frontmatter¶

The YAML frontmatter block is delimited by --- on its own line. The only recognized field is paths:, which takes a list of glob patterns.

yaml
---
paths:
  - "**/*.py"
  - "**/*.pyi"
---

If the frontmatter is omitted entirely, the rule is always-on. If the frontmatter is present but paths: is empty or missing, the behavior is undefined – do not do this. Either include paths: with at least one pattern, or omit the frontmatter block entirely.

The Content¶

After the frontmatter, the rest of the file is standard markdown. Claude Code injects this content verbatim into the system prompt. You can use any markdown formatting: headers, lists, code blocks, tables, bold, links.

The content is read by the model as authoritative instructions. Write rules the way you would write instructions for a new team member who is technically skilled but unfamiliar with your project’s conventions. Be specific. Be actionable. Do not assume context.

File Location¶

Rules must live in .claude/rules/ at the root of your project. Claude Code scans this directory (non-recursively – subdirectories are not supported as of this writing) for .md files.

The filename does not affect behavior, but use descriptive names: python.md, security.md, testing.md, react-components.md. Your future self will thank you.

Path Scoping Mechanics¶

Path scoping is the mechanism that makes rules efficient. Understanding how glob patterns work and how “in scope” is determined will save you from rules that never load or rules that load when they should not.

Glob Pattern Syntax¶

Rules use standard glob patterns with these wildcards:

Pattern Matches Example
* Any characters within a single directory level *.py matches main.py but not src/main.py
** Any number of directories (including zero) **/*.py matches main.py, src/main.py, src/lib/util.py
? Exactly one character test_?.py matches test_a.py but not test_ab.py
{a,b} Either a or b *.{ts,tsx} matches both .ts and .tsx files

The most common pattern you will use is **/*.ext, which means “any file with this extension, at any depth in the project.” This is almost always what you want for language-specific rules.

Multiple Patterns Per Rule¶

A single rule file can have multiple path patterns. The rule loads if any pattern matches any in-scope file. This is a logical OR:

yaml
---
paths:
  - "**/*.ts"
  - "**/*.tsx"
  - "**/*.js"
  - "**/*.jsx"
---

# JavaScript/TypeScript Rules

This rule loads when you are working with any JavaScript or TypeScript file. You do not need separate rule files for .ts and .tsx – combine them when the rules apply equally.

However, if the rules differ significantly between file types, use separate rule files:

yaml
# .claude/rules/react-components.md
---
paths:
  - "**/*.tsx"
  - "**/*.jsx"
---

# React Component Rules
- Use functional components, not class components
- Extract hooks into custom hooks when reused across 3+ components
yaml
# .claude/rules/node-scripts.md
---
paths:
  - "scripts/**/*.ts"
  - "tools/**/*.ts"
---

# Node Script Rules
- Use commander or yargs for CLI argument parsing
- Always set process.exitCode, never call process.exit() directly

How “In Scope” Files Are Determined¶

This is where many people get confused. A file is “in scope” if it meets any of these conditions:

  • Recently read. You or Claude Code used the Read tool to view the file in the current conversation.
  • Recently edited. You or Claude Code used the Edit or Write tool to modify the file.
  • Mentioned in conversation. You referenced the file by name in your message (e.g., “look at src/auth/login.py”).
  • In the current directory listing. Claude Code includes a snapshot of the project’s file structure in the system prompt. Files visible in this listing are loosely in scope.
  • Part of staged changes. Files that show up in git diff --staged are in scope.

The practical implication: if you open a session and immediately ask “How should I handle errors in Python?”, the Python rule may or may not load depending on whether any .py files are visible in the directory listing or recent git context. But if you say “Read src/main.py” first, the Python rule will definitely load because src/main.py matches **/*.py.

This is by design. Rules load based on what you are actually working on, not what you might hypothetically work on.

When Rules Load vs. When They Do Not¶

Here is a concrete scenario that illustrates the loading behavior:

Turn 1: "Read src/api/routes.py"
         → Python rules load (routes.py matches **/*.py)
         → Security rules load (always-on)

Turn 2: "Now read deploy/run.sh"
         → Shell rules load (run.sh matches **/*.sh)
         → Python rules still loaded (routes.py is still in scope)
         → Security rules still loaded (always-on)

Turn 3: /clear
         → Context reset. All per-turn scope is cleared.

Turn 4: "Read deploy/run.sh"
         → Shell rules load
         → Security rules load
         → Python rules do NOT load (no Python files in scope)

The /clear command resets the conversation context, which clears the in-scope file set. This is one reason Chapter 10 recommends using /clear between unrelated tasks – it keeps the rule set clean and relevant.

Built-In Rule Examples¶

Let’s examine five production-grade rules that cover the most common needs. These are not toy examples – they are rules you can use directly in your own projects.

python.md – Python Coding Standards¶

markdown
---
paths:
  - "**/*.py"
  - "**/*.pyi"
---

# Python Rules

## Style
- Use type hints on all function signatures
- Use f-strings over .format() or %
- Use pathlib over os.path for file operations
- Use dataclasses or Pydantic for data structures, not raw dicts
- Prefer `from __future__ import annotations` for forward references

## Imports
- Group: stdlib, third-party, local -- separated by blank line
- Use absolute imports. Avoid wildcard imports
- Prefer specific imports: `from pathlib import Path` over `import pathlib`

## Error Handling
- Catch specific exceptions, never bare `except:`
- Use `logging` module, not print(), for production code
- Let exceptions propagate unless you can handle them meaningfully

## Tools
- Formatter: `ruff format`
- Linter: `ruff check --fix`
- Type checker: `pyright` or `mypy --strict`
- Package manager: `uv` preferred, `pip` fallback

Notice the specificity. It does not say “write clean Python.” It says “use ruff format” – not black, not autopep8, not yapf. It says “pathlib over os.path” – not “use modern path handling.” Every rule is a concrete instruction that Claude Code can follow without judgment calls.

The “Tools” section is particularly important. Without it, Claude Code might run black for formatting or flake8 for linting, both of which are reasonable defaults but not your defaults. Specifying tool preferences eliminates the “which formatter?” question entirely.

typescript.md – TypeScript Conventions¶

markdown
---
paths:
  - "**/*.ts"
  - "**/*.tsx"
---

# TypeScript Rules

## Language
- Enable strict mode in tsconfig.json
- Prefer `const` over `let`. Never use `var`
- Use async/await over raw Promises or callbacks
- Prefer `interface` over `type` for object shapes (unless union/intersection needed)
- Use `unknown` over `any`. If `any` is truly needed, add a comment explaining why

## Functions
- Explicit return types on exported functions
- Use arrow functions for callbacks: `items.map((x) => x.id)`
- Prefer named exports over default exports

## Error Handling
- Use discriminated unions for error states, not exceptions
- Type-narrow errors in catch blocks: `if (error instanceof SpecificError)`

## React (if applicable)
- Functional components only. No class components
- Props interface named `{ComponentName}Props`
- Hooks before any conditional logic

## Tools
- Formatter: `prettier`
- Linter: `eslint` with `@typescript-eslint/parser`
- Build: `tsup` or `tsc`

security.md – Cross-Cutting Security (Always-On)¶

markdown
# Security Rules

## Code
- NEVER hardcode secrets, tokens, API keys, or passwords
- NEVER commit .env, credentials.json, token.json, *.pem files
- Use environment variables or secret managers for all credentials
- Validate and sanitize ALL external input (user input, API responses, file contents)
- Use parameterized queries -- never string concatenation for SQL/commands

## Dependencies
- Pin dependency versions. Audit with `pip audit` or `npm audit`
- Prefer well-maintained packages with recent commits
- Check for known CVEs before adding new dependencies

## Infrastructure
- Default to least privilege for all permissions
- Encrypt data in transit (HTTPS/TLS). Encrypt at rest for sensitive data
- Never log sensitive data (tokens, passwords, PII)
- Set CORS headers explicitly -- never use wildcard in production

## Review Checklist
- [ ] No secrets in code or config committed to git
- [ ] Input validation at all system boundaries
- [ ] Authentication/authorization checks on all protected endpoints
- [ ] Error messages don't leak internal details
- [ ] Dependencies audited for known vulnerabilities

No paths: frontmatter. This rule loads on every session. Security is never optional and never out of scope.

The checklist at the end is intentional. When Claude Code generates or reviews code, it will reference this checklist as acceptance criteria. Checklists are surprisingly effective in rules because they give the model a concrete verification step.

testing.md – Test Philosophy (Always-On)¶

markdown
# Testing Rules

## Approach
- Write tests for new features and bug fixes
- For bug fixes: write a failing test FIRST, then fix the bug
- Prefer running single tests over full suite for speed during development
- Run full suite before committing

## Python Testing
- Framework: `pytest` with `pytest-asyncio` for async code
- Use fixtures for setup/teardown, not setUp/tearDown methods
- Use `tmp_path` fixture for filesystem tests
- Integration tests hit real services -- never mock databases unless explicitly asked
- Name tests: `test_<what>_<condition>_<expected>`

## Test Structure
tests/
  unit/          -- Fast, isolated, no I/O
  integration/   -- External services, databases
  conftest.py    -- Shared fixtures

## Coverage
- Focus on critical paths, not coverage percentage
- Test edge cases: empty input, None, boundary values, error conditions
- Don't test framework internals or getter/setter boilerplate

This is always-on because testing philosophy applies regardless of language. The Python-specific section could be split into a separate path-scoped rule if your project is multilingual, but for Python-dominant projects, keeping it here avoids an extra file.

shell.md – Shell Script Standards¶

markdown
---
paths:
  - "**/*.sh"
  - "**/*.bash"
---

# Shell Script Rules

## Header
Every script starts with:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

## Style
- Quote all variables: `"${var}"` not `$var`
- Use `[[ ]]` not `[ ]` for conditionals
- Use `$(command)` not backticks
- Use `readonly` for constants
- Use `local` for function variables
- Prefer `printf` over `echo` for portability

## Error Handling
- Check command existence with `command -v`
- Use trap for cleanup: `trap cleanup EXIT`
- Provide meaningful exit codes (0=success, 1=general error, 2=usage error)

## Naming
- Functions: `snake_case`
- Constants: `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE`
- Local variables: `lower_snake_case`
- Files: `kebab-case.sh`

The set -euo pipefail rule is critical. Without it, Claude Code might generate shell scripts that silently swallow errors – a common source of hard-to-debug failures in CI/CD pipelines and automation.

Writing Effective Rules¶

The difference between a rule that Claude Code follows reliably and one it ignores is specificity. Here are the principles that make rules work.

Be Specific and Actionable¶

Bad rule:

markdown
- Write clean, maintainable code

Claude Code cannot act on this. “Clean” and “maintainable” are subjective – the model will fall back to its training defaults, which may not match your standards.

Good rule:

markdown
- Use type hints on all function signatures
- Maximum function length: 30 lines. Extract helper functions beyond that
- No nested callbacks deeper than 2 levels -- use async/await or extract functions

Each of these is a concrete, verifiable instruction. Claude Code can check: “Does this function have type hints? Yes/No.” There is no ambiguity.

Include Tool Preferences¶

Bad:

markdown
- Format code properly

Good:

markdown
- Formatter: `ruff format`
- Linter: `ruff check --fix`
- Run both after editing Python files

Without explicit tool preferences, Claude Code will choose based on its training data. That means it might run black (popular but not your choice), autopep8 (older), or prettier (wrong language). Specifying the tool eliminates this failure mode.

Include Anti-Patterns¶

Rules that say what to do are good. Rules that also say what not to do are better, because they address the model’s most likely mistakes.

markdown
## Do
- Use `pathlib.Path` for all file operations
- Use `tmp_path` fixture for filesystem tests

## Do Not
- Do not use `os.path.join()` -- use `Path / "subdir" / "file.txt"`
- Do not use `open()` with string paths -- use `Path.read_text()` / `Path.write_text()`
- Do not create temp files with `tempfile` in tests -- use the `tmp_path` fixture

The “Do Not” section directly addresses patterns that Claude’s training data would otherwise suggest. os.path.join() is overwhelmingly common in Python codebases, so the model gravitates toward it unless explicitly told not to.

Keep Rules Focused¶

One domain per file. Do not create a rules.md that covers Python, TypeScript, shell, security, and testing. Split them:

.claude/rules/
├── python.md       # Python language conventions
├── typescript.md   # TypeScript language conventions
├── shell.md        # Shell script conventions
├── security.md     # Security practices (always-on)
├── testing.md      # Testing philosophy (always-on)
└── react.md        # React component patterns

Focused rules have two advantages:

  1. Efficient loading. When you are working on a shell script, you get shell rules but not Python rules. This keeps the system prompt lean and focused.

  2. Easier maintenance. When your Python conventions change, you edit one file. When you add React conventions, you add one file. No risk of accidentally breaking unrelated rules.

Show Examples of Good and Bad Patterns¶

For complex rules, inline examples dramatically improve compliance:

markdown
## API Error Responses

Good:
```python
@app.exception_handler(ValidationError)
async def validation_error_handler(request: Request, exc: ValidationError) -> JSONResponse:
    return JSONResponse(
        status_code=422,
        content={"error": "validation_error", "details": exc.errors()},
    )

Bad:

python
@app.exception_handler(Exception)
async def error_handler(request, exc):
    return {"error": str(exc)}  # Leaks internal details, no status code, catches too broadly


The model learns from examples more reliably than from abstract descriptions. When you show the exact pattern you want, Claude Code reproduces it. When you show the exact anti-pattern with a comment explaining why it is wrong, Claude Code avoids it.

## Rules vs. CLAUDE.md -- When to Use Which

This is one of the most common questions, and the answer is straightforward:

| Use Case | Where | Why |
|----------|-------|-----|
| Communication style ("be terse", "no emojis") | `CLAUDE.md` | Applies to every interaction regardless of code |
| Git conventions (commit format, branch naming) | `CLAUDE.md` | Applies to every commit regardless of language |
| Verification workflow ("run tests before committing") | `CLAUDE.md` | Applies to every task |
| Language-specific conventions (type hints, formatting) | `.claude/rules/` with `paths:` | Only relevant when working in that language |
| Framework-specific patterns (React hooks, FastAPI) | `.claude/rules/` with `paths:` | Only relevant in that part of the codebase |
| Security practices | `.claude/rules/` (always-on) | Important enough to warrant a dedicated file, but applies everywhere |
| Domain knowledge (this API uses pagination like X) | `.claude/rules/` with `paths:` | Only relevant when working in that domain |
| One-off project instructions ("we are migrating to v2") | `CLAUDE.md` | Temporary, applies broadly |

The guiding principle: **if it applies to all code in all languages at all times, it goes in `CLAUDE.md`. If it applies to a subset of files, it goes in a path-scoped rule.**

There is a grey area for things like testing philosophy. If your project is exclusively Python, putting `pytest` conventions in `CLAUDE.md` is fine. If your project is multilingual, extract Python testing rules into a path-scoped `python-testing.md` and JavaScript testing rules into `js-testing.md`, and keep only the universal testing philosophy ("test-first for bugs", "run tests before committing") in `CLAUDE.md`.

### Token Budget Considerations

As discussed in Chapter 3, `CLAUDE.md` content is loaded on every single API call. Every line of `CLAUDE.md` costs you tokens on every turn. Rules with `paths:` frontmatter only cost tokens when relevant files are in scope.

For a typical session of 50 turns:

- A 50-line block in `CLAUDE.md` costs: 50 lines x ~3 tokens/line x 50 turns = **7,500 tokens**
- The same 50 lines in a path-scoped rule that is in scope for 15 turns: 50 x 3 x 15 = **2,250 tokens**

Over a day of heavy usage (500+ turns), the savings are substantial. More importantly, a leaner `CLAUDE.md` means the model has fewer instructions competing for attention on any given turn, which improves compliance with the rules that *are* loaded.

## Advanced: Combining Rules with @imports in CLAUDE.md

`CLAUDE.md` supports `@` imports that reference other files. This is how you keep `CLAUDE.md` lean while ensuring rules are discoverable:

```markdown
# CLAUDE.md

## Communication
- Be terse. Lead with the answer
- No emojis. No trailing summaries

## Coding Standards
- Run tests before committing
- Never commit secrets

## Domain Rules
@.claude/rules/python.md
@.claude/rules/security.md
@.claude/rules/testing.md
@.claude/rules/shell.md

The @ import mechanism and path-scoped rule loading are complementary but distinct features:

@ imports are static. They are resolved when CLAUDE.md is loaded and the referenced content is always included in the system prompt. Use @ imports for rules that you want loaded every session regardless of file scope – typically always-on rules like security and testing.

Path-scoped loading is dynamic. Rules with paths: frontmatter are loaded based on which files are in scope on each turn. These do not need @ imports – Claude Code discovers them automatically by scanning .claude/rules/.

So what is the right approach? Consider this pattern:

markdown
# CLAUDE.md

## Core Standards
- [your universal rules here]

## Domain Rules
@.claude/rules/security.md
@.claude/rules/testing.md

Here, security.md and testing.md are @ imported because they are always-on and you want them loaded unconditionally. Language-specific rules like python.md and shell.md are not imported – they are automatically discovered and loaded by the path-scoping mechanism when relevant files come into scope.

If you @ import a rule that also has paths: frontmatter, the content will be included via the import regardless of path matching. The paths: scoping is effectively bypassed for imported rules. This is rarely what you want, so as a general practice: @ import always-on rules, and let path-scoped rules be discovered automatically.

Complete Example: Full-Stack TypeScript + Python Project¶

Let’s walk through setting up rules for a realistic project: a FastAPI backend (Python) with a Next.js frontend (TypeScript), shared infrastructure scripts (shell), and a common security baseline.

Project Structure¶

my-app/
├── CLAUDE.md
├── .claude/
│   └── rules/
│       ├── python-backend.md
│       ├── typescript-frontend.md
│       ├── shell.md
│       ├── security.md
│       ├── testing.md
│       └── api-conventions.md
├── backend/
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── api/
│   │   ├── models/
│   │   └── services/
│   └── tests/
├── frontend/
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── components/
│   │   ├── hooks/
│   │   └── pages/
│   └── tests/
├── infra/
│   └── scripts/
└── shared/
    └── types/

CLAUDE.md (Lean, Universal)¶

markdown
# Project: MyApp

Full-stack application: FastAPI backend, Next.js frontend.

## Communication
- Be terse. Lead with the answer
- Reference code as `file_path:line_number`

## Git
- Branch: feat/, fix/, docs/ prefixes
- Commits: imperative mood, focus on WHY
- Run tests before committing

## Verification
- Always verify: run tests, check types, lint
- Prefer single tests over full suite during development

## Domain Rules
@.claude/rules/security.md
@.claude/rules/testing.md

Forty lines. Everything else is in rules.

python-backend.md¶

markdown
---
paths:
  - "backend/**/*.py"
---

# Python Backend Rules

## Style
- Type hints on all function signatures
- f-strings over .format()
- pathlib over os.path
- Pydantic models for request/response schemas

## FastAPI
- Use dependency injection for database sessions
- Use `Annotated[Type, Depends()]` syntax
- Return explicit status codes: `status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED`
- Background tasks for operations > 100ms

## Database
- SQLAlchemy 2.0 style (select(), not query())
- Alembic for migrations -- never modify tables directly
- Parameterized queries only

## Tools
- Formatter: `ruff format`
- Linter: `ruff check --fix`
- Type checker: `pyright`
- Package manager: `uv`

Note the paths: pattern: backend/**/*.py. This rule loads only when working with Python files inside the backend/ directory. If your project has Python scripts in infra/ that follow different conventions, those scripts will not trigger this rule.

typescript-frontend.md¶

markdown
---
paths:
  - "frontend/**/*.ts"
  - "frontend/**/*.tsx"
---

# TypeScript Frontend Rules

## Language
- strict mode enabled in tsconfig.json
- `const` over `let`. Never `var`
- async/await over raw Promises
- `unknown` over `any`

## React
- Functional components only
- Props interface: `{ComponentName}Props`
- Custom hooks for shared logic (prefix: `use`)
- Server components by default, `"use client"` only when needed

## State Management
- URL state for shareable state (search params)
- React context for theme/auth
- Server state via React Query (TanStack Query)

## Tools
- Formatter: `prettier`
- Linter: `eslint` with `@typescript-eslint/parser`
- Build: `next build`
- Test: `vitest`

api-conventions.md¶

markdown
---
paths:
  - "backend/src/api/**/*.py"
  - "frontend/src/**/api*.ts"
  - "shared/types/**/*"
---

# API Convention Rules

## REST
- Resources are plural nouns: /users, /orders (not /user, /getOrders)
- Use HTTP methods correctly: GET reads, POST creates, PUT replaces, PATCH updates, DELETE removes
- Return 201 for creation, 204 for deletion, 422 for validation errors

## Response Format
- Envelope: `{"data": ..., "meta": {"page": 1, "total": 100}}`
- Error envelope: `{"error": {"code": "VALIDATION_ERROR", "message": "...", "details": [...]}}`
- Timestamps in ISO 8601 UTC: `2026-04-01T12:00:00Z`

## Versioning
- URL prefix: /api/v1/
- Breaking changes get a new version. Additive changes do not

This rule triggers when working with API route files in the backend, API client files in the frontend, or shared type definitions. It ensures consistent conventions across the stack boundary.

The Result¶

When you work on backend/src/api/routes.py, Claude Code loads: - python-backend.md (matches backend/**/*.py) - api-conventions.md (matches backend/src/api/**/*.py) - security.md (always-on, via @ import) - testing.md (always-on, via @ import)

When you switch to frontend/src/components/LoginForm.tsx: - typescript-frontend.md (matches frontend/**/*.tsx) - security.md (always-on) - testing.md (always-on)

When you edit infra/scripts/deploy.sh: - shell.md (matches **/*.sh) - security.md (always-on) - testing.md (always-on)

Each context gets exactly the rules it needs. No Python rules polluting your TypeScript work. No React conventions cluttering your shell script editing. The system prompt stays focused, and Claude Code’s compliance stays high.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

After reviewing dozens of Claude Code configurations, the same mistakes appear repeatedly. Here is what to avoid.

Mistake 1: Putting Everything in CLAUDE.md¶

The most common mistake. A developer writes a 300-line CLAUDE.md with Python rules, TypeScript rules, SQL conventions, deployment procedures, and team communication standards. Every token of this is injected into every API call. The model has so many instructions that it follows none of them reliably.

Fix: Keep CLAUDE.md under 50 lines. Extract language and domain rules into .claude/rules/ with path scoping.

Mistake 2: Vague, Aspirational Rules¶

“Write performant code.” “Follow best practices.” “Use appropriate design patterns.” These are not rules – they are wishes. The model interprets them according to its training data, which may not match your expectations.

Fix: Every rule should be a concrete instruction that can be verified as followed or not followed. “Use ruff format for Python formatting” is verifiable. “Format code nicely” is not.

Mistake 3: Not Specifying Tools¶

If your rule says “lint the code” but does not say which linter, Claude Code will pick one. It might pick the wrong one. You will spend a turn correcting it, or worse, you will not notice and end up with inconsistent tooling.

Fix: Always specify the exact tool and command. ruff check --fix, not “run the linter.” prettier --write, not “format the file.”

Mistake 4: Overlapping Path Patterns¶

Two rules with overlapping patterns that give contradictory instructions:

yaml
# rule-a.md
---
paths: ["**/*.ts"]
---
# Use semicolons in TypeScript
yaml
# rule-b.md
---
paths: ["**/*.ts", "**/*.tsx"]
---
# Do not use semicolons -- Prettier removes them

Both load when working with .ts files. Claude Code sees contradictory instructions. It will follow one and ignore the other, unpredictably.

Fix: Audit your rules for overlapping patterns. If two rules apply to the same files, ensure their instructions are complementary, not contradictory.

Mistake 5: Rules That Are Too Long¶

A 200-line rule file defeats the purpose of modularity. If your Python rule file covers style, testing, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, Celery, Redis, and deployment, it is too long.

Fix: Split by subdomain. python-style.md for language conventions, fastapi.md for framework patterns, sqlalchemy.md for database patterns. Each loads only when its relevant files are in scope.

Mistake 6: Forgetting That Rules Are Dynamic¶

A developer writes a path-scoped rule, opens a new session, and asks “What rules are you following?” without first reading any matching files. Claude Code reports that the path-scoped rule is not loaded. The developer concludes rules are broken.

Fix: Rules load based on in-scope files. Read or mention a file that matches the rule’s patterns first. Or use an always-on rule (no frontmatter) if the rule should apply regardless.

Chapter Summary¶

Five takeaways from this chapter:

  1. Rules are modular, conditional extensions of CLAUDE.md. Always-on rules (no frontmatter) load every session. Path-scoped rules (with paths: globs) load only when matching files are in scope. This keeps the system prompt lean and focused.

  2. Specificity is everything. “Use ruff format” works. “Format code properly” does not. Every rule should be a concrete, verifiable instruction – include the tool name, the command, the pattern to follow, and the anti-pattern to avoid.

  3. One domain per file. Keep rules focused. Python conventions in one file, TypeScript in another, security in a third. This maximizes the benefit of path scoping and makes rules easier to maintain.

  4. Understand the loading mechanics. Rules load based on in-scope files (recently read, edited, mentioned). Use @ imports in CLAUDE.md for always-on rules. Let path-scoped rules be discovered automatically. Do not @ import rules that have paths: frontmatter.

  5. Audit for conflicts. When multiple rules apply to the same files, ensure their instructions are complementary. Contradictory rules produce unpredictable behavior – the model will follow one and ignore the other, and you will not know which until you notice the inconsistency in generated code.

Rules are the mechanism that turns Claude Code from a general-purpose coding assistant into your coding assistant – one that knows your formatter, your linter, your conventions, and your anti-patterns. In Chapter 7, we will build on this foundation with skills: reusable AI workflows that teach Claude Code complex, multi-step procedures that go far beyond coding standards.

Chapter 7: Skills – Building Reusable AI Workflows¶ ~20 min read

You have used Claude Code enough to notice a pattern. Every code review follows the same steps: read the file, check security, check logic, check performance, output findings with severity and line numbers. Every bug fix follows the same arc: read the issue, search the codebase, write a test, implement the fix, run tests, commit, create a PR. Every deployment check walks through the same checklist: git clean, no secrets, dependencies audited, tests pass, build succeeds.

You keep repeating the same instructions. You keep getting slightly different results depending on how you phrase the prompt that day. Sometimes Claude remembers to check security first. Sometimes it forgets. Sometimes it includes line numbers. Sometimes it does not.

Skills solve this permanently. A skill is a packaged set of instructions that teaches Claude Code a specific workflow, exactly once, and ensures it executes that workflow the same way every time you invoke it. You write the instructions in a markdown file. Claude Code loads them into the system prompt when triggered. The workflow becomes deterministic, repeatable, and shareable across your team.

This chapter is the complete guide to building, configuring, and deploying skills. By the end of it, you will have built three production-grade skills from scratch and understood every configuration option available.

What Skills Are¶

A skill is a markdown file named SKILL.md that lives in a specific directory structure. When activated, its contents are injected into Claude Code’s system prompt – the invisible instructions that govern how the model behaves. This means a skill does not run code. It teaches Claude Code how to approach a category of work.

There are two locations for skills:

Location Scope Use Case
.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md Project-specific Workflows tied to this codebase
~/.config/claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md Global (all projects) Personal workflows you use everywhere

Project skills travel with the repository. Global skills travel with you.

How Skills Get Activated¶

Three activation mechanisms:

  1. Slash command. You type /skill-name in the REPL. Claude Code finds the matching skill directory, loads SKILL.md into context, and follows the instructions.

  2. Auto-trigger. Claude Code reads the description field in the skill’s frontmatter and decides whether the current user prompt matches. If you type “review this code for security issues” and your security-audit skill’s description includes “security”, “vulnerability”, and “audit” as trigger phrases, Claude Code loads it automatically.

  3. Path-based auto-activation. The skill’s frontmatter specifies file glob patterns. When you work on files matching those patterns, the skill loads automatically. An api-conventions skill with paths: ["src/api/**"] activates whenever you edit API route handlers.

These mechanisms are not mutually exclusive. A single skill can be triggered by all three.

What Happens When a Skill Loads¶

User types: /fix-issue 42
                │
                ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Claude Code finds                    │
│ .claude/skills/fix-issue/SKILL.md   │
└────────────────┬────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Reads frontmatter:                   │
│   model: opus                        │
│   effort: high                       │
│   allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Bash... │
│   context: fork                      │
└────────────────┬────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Injects SKILL.md body into          │
│ system prompt                        │
│ $ARGUMENTS = "42"                    │
└────────────────┬────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Claude executes the workflow         │
│ following skill instructions         │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘

The system prompt injection is the critical detail. Skills are not plugins or scripts. They are instructions that reshape Claude Code’s behavior for the duration of that task. This is why well-written skills produce consistent results – the model receives the exact same instructions every time.

The SKILL.md Anatomy¶

Every skill is a single markdown file with two parts: YAML frontmatter that configures how the skill behaves, and a markdown body that contains the actual instructions Claude Code follows.

Here is a complete example with every available frontmatter field:

yaml
---
name: fix-issue
description: Fix a GitHub issue end-to-end. Use when user says "fix issue",
  "fix #N", "work on issue", or provides a GitHub issue number.
argument-hint: [issue-number]
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write
model: opus
effort: high
context: fork
agent: general-purpose
user-invocable: true
disable-model-invocation: false
paths:
  - "src/**"
---

# Fix Issue Workflow

## Instructions
1. Read the GitHub issue: `gh issue view $ARGUMENTS`
2. Analyze the issue description and comments
3. Search the codebase for relevant files
4. Write a failing test that demonstrates the bug
5. Implement the minimal fix
6. Run the test suite
7. Commit with message: `fix: description (fixes #$ARGUMENTS)`
8. Create a pull request linking the issue

Let us examine every frontmatter field in detail.

name¶

yaml
name: fix-issue

The name becomes the slash command. Setting name: fix-issue means the skill is invoked with /fix-issue. Names must be kebab-case (lowercase, hyphens, no spaces). The name also determines the directory name: .claude/skills/fix-issue/SKILL.md.

Choose names that are short and unambiguous. /review is better than /perform-code-quality-review. /scaffold is better than /create-new-project-from-template. Your future self will type this hundreds of times.

description¶

yaml
description: Fix a GitHub issue end-to-end. Use when user says "fix issue",
  "fix #N", "work on issue", or provides a GitHub issue number.

The description serves double duty. For humans, it appears in the / autocomplete menu so you can scan what skills are available. For Claude Code, it is the primary signal for auto-triggering.

When you type a natural language prompt without a slash command, Claude Code reads every skill’s description and decides whether to load one. If your description says “Use when user says ‘security’, ‘vulnerability’, ‘audit’, ‘OWASP’, ‘pentest’, or ‘CVE’” and the user types “check this for vulnerabilities,” the skill activates.

The most common skill problem is a vague description. If your skill does not trigger automatically when it should, the fix is almost always improving the description with more trigger phrases.

Bad:

yaml
description: Reviews code.

Good:

yaml
description: Reviews code for bugs, security issues, performance problems,
  and readability. Use when user says "review", "check my code", "code review",
  "find bugs", "audit this file", "look at this code", "is this good".

argument-hint¶

yaml
argument-hint: [issue-number]

Shown in the autocomplete tooltip when you type /fix-issue and press Tab. It is purely cosmetic – a hint to the user about what argument the skill expects. It does not validate or parse the argument.

Common patterns:

yaml
argument-hint: [issue-number]        # /fix-issue 42
argument-hint: [PR-number]           # /pr-summary 123
argument-hint: [type] [name]         # /scaffold python my-api
argument-hint: [v1..v2]              # /changelog v1.0..v2.0

The actual argument text is available inside the skill body as $ARGUMENTS. More on this in the dynamic context injection section.

allowed-tools¶

yaml
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write

This field lists tools that the skill can use without triggering permission prompts. Normally, Claude Code checks your settings.json permission rules before every tool call. If a skill declares allowed-tools: Bash, then Bash commands executed within that skill’s context bypass the permission check.

This is a convenience and a speed optimization. Without it, a complex skill like fix-issue would pause for permission approval on every git commit, every pytest run, every gh pr create. With it, the skill runs uninterrupted.

Security note: allowed-tools grants broad permission. Listing Bash means the skill can run any shell command. For skills shared across a team, be deliberate about what you allow. A code-review skill needs Read, Grep, Glob – it does not need Bash or Write.

model¶

yaml
model: opus

Overrides the default model for this skill. Available values: opus, sonnet, haiku.

Use this strategically:

Model When to Use Example Skills
opus Complex reasoning, security analysis, debugging fix-issue, security-audit, threat-model
sonnet Balanced speed and quality, most workflows code-review, refactor, pr-summary
haiku Fast, simple, high-volume tasks changelog, git-cleanup

A security audit that misses a vulnerability because you used a cheaper model is not a cost saving. A changelog generation that takes 30 seconds instead of 5 because you used Opus is not a quality improvement. Match the model to the cognitive demand of the task.

effort¶

yaml
effort: high

Controls the reasoning depth. Available values: low, medium, high, max.

  • low: Quick pattern matching, simple transformations. Good for formatting, renaming, simple lookups.
  • medium: Standard reasoning. Good for code review, documentation, straightforward implementation.
  • high: Deep analysis, multi-step reasoning, consideration of edge cases. Good for debugging, security audit, complex implementation.
  • max: Extended thinking with maximum token allocation. Good for threat modeling, architecture analysis, difficult debugging.

Combined with model, this gives you fine-grained control:

yaml
# Fast and cheap: simple categorization
model: haiku
effort: low

# Balanced: standard development work
model: sonnet
effort: medium

# Deep analysis: security and debugging
model: opus
effort: high

context: fork¶

yaml
context: fork

When set to fork, the skill runs in an isolated subagent context. This means it gets its own conversation context, separate from your main conversation. The subagent executes the skill’s workflow, returns the result, and its context is discarded.

Why this matters: in a long conversation where you have been discussing architecture, debugging, and planning, the main context is full of information. If you trigger a code-review skill, all that prior context is noise – it wastes tokens and can confuse the model. context: fork gives the skill a clean slate.

When to use context: fork: - Skills that are self-contained (fix-issue, scaffold) - Skills where prior conversation context would be distracting - Long-running skills that generate a lot of intermediate output

When NOT to use context: fork: - Skills that need to reference the current conversation (e.g., “review the code we just discussed”) - Quick skills where the forking overhead is not worth it

agent¶

yaml
agent: general-purpose

When context: fork is set, agent specifies which type of subagent to spawn. The value corresponds to agent definitions in .claude/agents/. If you have a custom security-reviewer agent defined, you could set agent: security-reviewer and the skill would run with that agent’s system prompt and model configuration layered on top.

user-invocable¶

yaml
user-invocable: true

Controls whether the skill appears in the / autocomplete menu. Default is true.

Set to false for skills that should only be triggered automatically (via description matching or path activation), not by direct user command. Example: an api-conventions skill that auto-loads when working in API files but is not useful as a standalone command.

disable-model-invocation¶

yaml
disable-model-invocation: false

When true, Claude Code will never auto-trigger this skill based on the description. Only explicit /command invocation works.

Use this for skills with side effects (deployment, destructive operations) or skills whose descriptions might overlap with common conversation topics. You do not want a /deploy skill auto-triggering because someone said “let’s deploy this approach to the codebase.”

yaml
# Safe: only manual trigger
name: deploy
description: Deploy the application to production
disable-model-invocation: true

paths¶

yaml
paths:
  - "src/api/**"
  - "src/routes/**"

Glob patterns that trigger automatic skill activation. When you open, edit, or reference a file matching any of these patterns, the skill loads into context automatically.

This is powerful for convention enforcement. An api-conventions skill with the paths above ensures that every time you touch an API route handler, the conventions are loaded – without you remembering to invoke anything.

yaml
# Auto-load Python testing conventions when editing tests
paths:
  - "tests/**/*.py"
  - "**/test_*.py"

Caveat: Path-activated skills load silently. If you have too many, they consume context window space without you realizing it. Keep path-activated skills focused and concise.

Dynamic Context Injection¶

Skills can execute shell commands before their content reaches Claude Code’s context. This is done with the !`command` syntax – backtick-wrapped commands prefixed with an exclamation mark.

The !command`` Syntax¶

Inside a skill body, any line containing !`command` is executed as a shell command during skill loading. The command’s output replaces the !`command` expression in the final prompt.

Here is a real-world example from a pr-summary skill:

markdown
---
name: pr-summary
description: Summarize a pull request with risk assessment
argument-hint: [PR-number]
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Bash
---

# PR Summary

## Context
The PR diff:
!`gh pr diff $ARGUMENTS`

Changed files:
!`gh pr diff $ARGUMENTS --name-only`

PR details:
!`gh pr view $ARGUMENTS`

## Instructions
1. Analyze the diff for: breaking changes, security implications,
   performance impact, test coverage
2. Output a summary table, risk assessment, and review checklist

When you run /pr-summary 45, here is what happens:

  1. $ARGUMENTS is substituted with 45
  2. gh pr diff 45 executes and its output is injected into the prompt
  3. gh pr diff 45 --name-only executes and its output is injected
  4. gh pr view 45 executes and its output is injected
  5. Claude Code receives the fully-populated skill with all the PR data already in context

The model never has to call tools to fetch this data. It arrives pre-loaded. This saves tool call round-trips and ensures the model has everything it needs to begin analysis immediately.

$ARGUMENTS Substitution¶

$ARGUMENTS is replaced with whatever text the user provided after the slash command:

Invocation $ARGUMENTS Value
/fix-issue 42 42
/scaffold python my-api python my-api
/changelog v1.0..v2.0 v1.0..v2.0
/code-review (empty string)

The substitution happens before command execution, so gh pr diff $ARGUMENTS becomes gh pr diff 42 before the shell runs it.

When to Use Dynamic Injection vs. Tool Calls¶

Scenario Use Dynamic Injection Use Tool Calls
Data is always needed for this skill Yes No
Data is sometimes needed, depending on context No Yes
Data is large (hundreds of lines) Carefully – it consumes context Yes (model reads on demand)
Speed is critical Yes (eliminates round-trips) No
Data requires user interaction or judgment No Yes

Dynamic injection is a pre-loading optimization. If a skill always needs the PR diff, pre-load it. If a skill sometimes needs to read additional files based on what it finds, let the model decide with tool calls.

Warning: Dynamic injection output counts against your context window. Injecting a 2,000-line diff means 2,000 lines of your context budget are consumed before the model even starts reasoning. For large diffs, consider injecting only the file list and letting the model read specific files as needed.

Building Custom Skills: Three Complete Examples¶

Theory is useful. Working code is better. Here are three production-grade skills, built step by step.

Skill 1: /commit – Consistent Git Commits¶

The problem: you want every commit to follow the same format – conventional commit messages, co-authorship attribution, pre-commit verification. Typing these instructions every time is tedious and error-prone.

Create the directory:

bash
mkdir -p .claude/skills/commit

Create the skill:

markdown
---
name: commit
description: Create a well-formatted git commit. Use when user says "commit",
  "save changes", "commit this", or "check in".
allowed-tools: Bash, Read, Grep
model: sonnet
effort: medium
disable-model-invocation: true
---

# Commit Workflow

## Pre-Commit Checks
1. Run `git status` to see all changes (staged and unstaged)
2. Run `git diff` to review what will be committed
3. Run `git log --oneline -5` to see recent commit message style

## Commit Message Format
Use conventional commits:
- `feat:` new feature
- `fix:` bug fix
- `docs:` documentation only
- `refactor:` code restructuring, no behavior change
- `test:` adding or updating tests
- `chore:` maintenance tasks

Write a concise message (under 72 characters) that explains WHY,
not WHAT. The diff shows what changed. The message explains the intent.

## Execution
1. Stage relevant files (prefer specific files over `git add -A`)
2. Do NOT stage files that appear to contain secrets (.env, *.pem,
   credentials.*, token.*)
3. Create the commit with:
   ```
   git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
   <type>: <description>

   Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
   EOF
   )"
   ```
4. Run `git status` to verify the commit succeeded
5. Show the commit hash and summary

## Rules
- NEVER amend a previous commit unless explicitly asked
- NEVER force push
- If pre-commit hooks fail, fix the issue and create a NEW commit
- If there are no changes to commit, say so and stop

Note the disable-model-invocation: true setting. You do not want Claude Code auto-triggering a commit just because you mentioned the word “commit” in conversation. Commits are explicit actions.

Skill 2: /code-review – Severity-Ranked Findings¶

The problem: code reviews are inconsistent. Sometimes you get surface-level feedback, sometimes deep analysis. You want every review to follow the same priority order and output format.

markdown
---
name: code-review
description: Reviews code for bugs, security issues, performance,
  and readability. Use when user says "review", "check my code",
  "code review", "find bugs", "audit this file", "look at this",
  "is this good", "what do you think of this code".
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob
model: sonnet
effort: high
---

# Code Review

## Review Order (this order is mandatory)
1. **Security** -- injection, auth bypass, data exposure, secrets
2. **Correctness** -- logic errors, race conditions, null handling, edge cases
3. **Performance** -- N+1 queries, unnecessary allocations, missing indexes
4. **Readability** -- naming, structure, complexity, missing types

## Output Format
For each finding, use exactly this format:

**[SEVERITY] file_path:line_number -- Description**
Fix: concrete code change

Severity levels:
- **CRITICAL** -- Will cause data loss, security breach, or crash
  in production. Must fix before merge.
- **IMPORTANT** -- Bug or significant issue. Should fix before merge.
- **SUGGESTION** -- Improvement. Nice to have, not blocking.

## Rules
- Read the ENTIRE file before making findings. Do not review
  line-by-line as you read.
- Group findings by severity, CRITICAL first.
- Skip nitpicks. If it works and is readable, do not comment.
- Include the fix, not just the problem.
- If the code is clean, say "No significant issues found" and stop.
  Do not invent findings.
- Reference specific line numbers for every finding.
- If you find zero CRITICAL or IMPORTANT issues, say so explicitly
  so the author knows it was reviewed, not skipped.

This skill uses model: sonnet rather than opus. Code review is well within Sonnet’s capability for most codebases, and the speed difference matters when you are reviewing multiple files in a session.

Skill 3: /scaffold – Project Bootstrapping¶

The problem: every new project starts with the same boilerplate – directory structure, config files, linting setup, test framework, gitignore. You want a single command that generates a production-ready project skeleton.

markdown
---
name: scaffold
description: Bootstrap a new project with proper structure and tooling.
  Use when user says "new project", "scaffold", "bootstrap", "init",
  "create project", "set up a new", "start a new".
argument-hint: [type] [name]
allowed-tools: Bash, Write, Read
model: sonnet
effort: medium
---

# Project Scaffold

## Arguments
$ARGUMENTS should be: [type] [name]
- type: python, typescript, node, rust
- name: project name (kebab-case)

Example: `/scaffold python my-api`

## Python Project Structure

$NAME/ ├── src/ │ └── $NAME/ │ ├── init.py │ └── main.py ├── tests/ │ ├── init.py │ ├── conftest.py │ └── test_main.py ├── pyproject.toml ├── .gitignore ├── .python-version └── README.md


## pyproject.toml Template
Include:
- Project metadata (name, version 0.1.0, requires-python >= 3.12)
- Dependencies section (empty)
- Dev dependencies: pytest, pytest-asyncio, ruff, pyright
- Ruff config: line-length 88, target Python 3.12
- Pyright config: strict mode

## TypeScript Project Structure

$NAME/ ├── src/ │ └── index.ts ├── tests/ │ └── index.test.ts ├── package.json ├── tsconfig.json ├── .gitignore └── README.md


## After Scaffolding
1. Initialize git: `git init`
2. Create initial commit: `git add -A && git commit -m "chore: initial scaffold"`
3. Run linter to verify clean start
4. Run test suite to verify it passes
5. Show the project tree and confirm everything is working

## Rules
- Always use the latest stable language version
- Always include a test that passes out of the box
- Always include linting and type checking config
- README should contain: project name, one-line description,
  setup instructions, and how to run tests
- .gitignore should cover: OS files, IDE files, build artifacts,
  virtual environments, dependency directories

Notice how the skill provides exact directory structures and specific configuration requirements. Vague instructions like “set up testing” produce inconsistent results. Explicit instructions like “dev dependencies: pytest, pytest-asyncio, ruff, pyright” produce the same result every time.

Auto-Activating Skills¶

Some skills should load without any explicit invocation. They enforce conventions by being present whenever you work in certain areas of the codebase.

Example: API Conventions¶

markdown
---
name: api-conventions
description: Enforces API coding conventions. Auto-activates for API files.
user-invocable: false
paths:
  - "src/api/**"
  - "**/routes/**"
  - "**/endpoints/**"
---

# API Conventions

When working in API route handlers, follow these conventions:

## Request Validation
- Validate all request parameters at the handler level
- Use Pydantic models for request/response schemas
- Return 422 for validation errors with field-level details

## Response Format
All responses follow this structure:
```json
{
  "data": {},
  "error": null,
  "meta": { "request_id": "...", "timestamp": "..." }
}

Error Handling¶

  • 400: Bad request (client error in request format)
  • 401: Unauthorized (missing or invalid authentication)
  • 403: Forbidden (authenticated but insufficient permissions)
  • 404: Not found
  • 422: Validation error (request parsed but semantically invalid)
  • 500: Internal server error (never expose internal details)

Security¶

  • Never include stack traces in production error responses
  • Log the full error server-side with the request_id
  • Rate limit all public endpoints
  • Validate Content-Type header matches expected format

This skill has `user-invocable: false` because `/api-conventions` is not a meaningful standalone command. It exists to inject conventions into context whenever you touch API files. You never think about it. It is always there when you need it.

### Combining Path Activation with Explicit Invocation

A skill can be both path-activated and manually invocable:

```yaml
name: test-conventions
description: Testing conventions and patterns
paths:
  - "tests/**"
  - "**/test_*.py"

This loads automatically when you edit test files, and you can also explicitly invoke /test-conventions when you want to review the conventions without opening a test file.

Skill Libraries: Organizing Skills for a Team¶

When skills are in .claude/skills/ and committed to the repository, they are shared with everyone who clones the repo. This is powerful – and requires discipline.

Directory Structure for Team Skills¶

.claude/
└── skills/
    ├── commit/
    │   └── SKILL.md
    ├── code-review/
    │   └── SKILL.md
    ├── security-audit/
    │   ├── SKILL.md
    │   └── references/
    │       ├── owasp-top-10.md
    │       └── cwe-checklist.md
    ├── fix-issue/
    │   └── SKILL.md
    ├── pr-summary/
    │   └── SKILL.md
    ├── deploy-check/
    │   └── SKILL.md
    └── api-conventions/
        └── SKILL.md

Note the references/ subdirectory in security-audit. Skills can include additional files alongside SKILL.md. The skill body can reference these files with instructions like “Read the OWASP checklist in this skill’s references/ directory.” This keeps specialized knowledge co-located with the skill that uses it.

Personal vs. Team Skills¶

Location Visibility Use Case
.claude/skills/ (project) Everyone on the team Team standards, project workflows
~/.config/claude/skills/ (global) Only you Personal preferences, cross-project tools

Keep team skills focused on team needs: commit conventions, review standards, deployment checklists. Keep personal skills for your own workflow: your preferred research format, your note-taking style, your debugging approach.

Version Control Considerations¶

Skills in .claude/skills/ are code. Treat them like code:

  • Review skill changes in PRs. A skill that changes the deployment checklist affects every engineer on the team.
  • Test skills after modification. Run the skill against a known input and verify the output matches expectations.
  • Document skill purpose. The description field is not just for Claude Code – it is for your teammates who see the skill in the autocomplete menu.
  • Keep skills small. A 500-line skill is hard to review, hard to maintain, and consumes context budget. If a skill is growing beyond 200 lines, consider whether it should be two skills.

Skill Naming Conventions¶

Establish a convention for your team. Consistent naming makes the skill library scannable.

Convention Example When
Action-noun /review-pr, /fix-issue, /audit-deps Most skills
Noun /changelog, /scaffold When the action is obvious
Domain-prefix /api-review, /db-migrate When you have domain-specific skills

Avoid: - Names that overlap with built-in commands (/help, /clear, /compact) - Names longer than 20 characters - Names with underscores (use hyphens)

What Most People Get Wrong¶

After building and reviewing hundreds of skill configurations, these are the recurring mistakes.

1. The Description Is Too Vague¶

The most common problem. Your skill does not auto-trigger because the description says “Reviews code” instead of listing the twelve phrases that should trigger it. Write the description as if you are teaching a junior engineer which Slack messages should route to this skill.

2. The Instructions Are Too Abstract¶

“Review the code for issues” produces wildly different results depending on the model’s mood. “Check in this order: (1) SQL injection, (2) authentication bypass, (3) data exposure, (4) hardcoded secrets. For each finding, output severity, CWE number, file path, line number, and a concrete fix” produces the same result every time.

Specificity is not rigidity. It is reliability.

3. Overusing context: fork¶

Forking creates a clean context, but it also loses all conversational history. If you have been discussing a complex architecture for twenty minutes and then invoke a skill that forks, the skill has no idea what you were talking about. Use fork for standalone tasks. Keep the main context for tasks that build on the conversation.

4. Allowing Too Many Tools¶

allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, WebFetch, WebSearch, Agent is not a skill configuration. It is an abdication of configuration. Every tool in that list is a tool the skill can use without permission checks. A code-review skill that can write files and run shell commands is a code-review skill that might accidentally modify your codebase during review. Be deliberate: grant the minimum tools necessary.

5. Building Monolithic Skills¶

A single skill that handles “review code, fix issues, write tests, and deploy” will do all four things poorly. Skills work best when they are focused on one workflow. Build four skills, not one mega-skill. Each skill is better because it is smaller, more specific, and easier to iterate on.

6. Not Testing the Skill¶

You write a SKILL.md, use it once, and ship it to the team. The first time a teammate uses it, the output is different from what you expected because the model interpreted an ambiguous instruction differently.

Test your skills. Run them against known inputs. Check that the output format matches. Check that the priority order is correct. Check that edge cases are handled (what happens when there are no findings? what happens when the argument is empty?).

7. Ignoring Context Budget¶

Every skill instruction, every dynamic injection, every reference file consumes context tokens. A skill that injects a 3,000-line diff plus a 500-line OWASP checklist plus a 200-line instruction set has consumed a significant portion of the context window before the model starts working. Monitor your context usage with /context after loading heavy skills. If you are consistently hitting context limits, your skills are too large.

8. Duplicating Logic Between Skills and CLAUDE.md¶

If your CLAUDE.md says “always use conventional commits” and your /commit skill also says “use conventional commits,” you have duplication. When you update one, you will forget the other. Put universal rules in CLAUDE.md or rules files. Put workflow-specific instructions in skills. Never put the same instruction in both places.

Building a Skill Development Workflow¶

The best approach to building skills is iterative:

  1. Manual first. Do the workflow manually three times. Note exactly what steps you follow, what tools you use, and what format you want the output in.

  2. Draft the skill. Write the SKILL.md based on your manual workflow. Be more specific than you think necessary.

  3. Test against known input. Run the skill against a file or scenario where you already know what the correct output looks like. Compare.

  4. Adjust specificity. If the output varies between runs, your instructions are too vague somewhere. Find where and add specificity.

  5. Test the auto-trigger. Say the phrases you think should trigger the skill without using the slash command. If it does not trigger, improve the description.

  6. Ship and monitor. Commit the skill. Ask a teammate to use it. Get feedback on the first run.

This workflow takes 30 minutes per skill. The return is every future invocation producing consistent, high-quality results without any prompt engineering from the user.

Chapter Summary¶

Five things to remember about skills:

  1. Skills are instructions, not code. They reshape Claude Code’s behavior by injecting instructions into the system prompt. The quality of the skill is the quality of the instructions.

  2. The description field controls auto-triggering. If your skill does not activate when it should, the description is the problem. List every phrase that should trigger it.

  3. Dynamic context injection (!command``) eliminates round-trips. Pre-load data the skill always needs. Let the model fetch data it sometimes needs via tool calls.

  4. Specificity produces consistency. “Check security, then logic, then performance” with exact output formats produces the same result every time. “Review the code” does not.

  5. Skills are team infrastructure. Project skills in .claude/skills/ are shared through version control. Treat them with the same rigor as production code – review changes in PRs, test after modification, keep them focused and small.

The next chapter covers agents – specialized subagent configurations that give you model and tool isolation for complex tasks. Skills tell Claude Code what to do. Agents tell it who to be while doing it. As discussed in Chapter 1, the configuration stack layers these primitives: CLAUDE.md sets the foundation, rules scope the standards, skills define the workflows, and agents provide the expertise. We are building up, one layer at a time.

Chapter 8: Hooks – Automation Guardrails¶ ~20 min read

A developer on your team runs rm -rf . inside a Claude Code session. Not maliciously – they told Claude to “clean up the temp files” and Claude interpreted that as a recursive delete from the current directory. Without hooks, the command executes before anyone blinks. With a PreToolUse hook, a twelve-line shell script catches the pattern, returns exit code 2, and the operation never touches the filesystem. Claude gets feedback explaining why the command was blocked and reformulates a safer approach.

That is what hooks do. They are shell scripts – or webhooks, or LLM prompts, or subagent invocations – that fire at specific points in Claude Code’s execution lifecycle. They can observe, they can transform, and most critically, they can block. Hooks are the difference between trusting an AI agent with good intentions and trusting an AI agent with enforced boundaries.

This chapter covers every hook lifecycle event, the JSON protocol hooks use to communicate, the four hook types available, and five production-ready hook implementations you can drop into your project today. By the end, you will have the knowledge to build custom hooks that enforce your team’s specific guardrails.

The Hook Lifecycle¶

Claude Code exposes seven lifecycle events where hooks can execute. Each event has different capabilities, different timing, and different access to context. Understanding when each fires – and what it can do – is the foundation for everything else in this chapter.

SessionStart¶

Fires once when a Claude Code session begins, before any user interaction occurs.

Timing: After Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md, settings, and rules, but before the first prompt is processed.

Can block: No. SessionStart hooks run for side effects only.

Use cases: Setting environment variables, initializing project state, logging session start times, checking prerequisites (is Docker running? Is the VPN connected?).

What it receives: Session metadata – session ID, project path, working directory.

Session begins
    │
    ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Load CLAUDE.md       │
│ Load settings.json   │
│ Load rules           │
├─────────────────────┤
│ SessionStart hooks   │ ◄── Fire here
└─────────┬───────────┘
          │
          ▼
  Waiting for first prompt

PreToolUse¶

Fires before any tool executes. This is the most powerful hook event because it can block execution entirely.

Timing: After Claude decides to use a tool, after the permission system evaluates (deny/ask/allow), but before the tool actually runs.

Can block: Yes. Exit code 2 blocks the tool call. The stderr output is fed back to Claude as an explanation.

Use cases: Blocking dangerous commands, scanning for secrets before commits, enforcing naming conventions, rate-limiting API calls, requiring confirmation for destructive operations.

What it receives: The tool name and its full input – for Bash tools, that means the exact command string.

This is the event you will wire most often. Every safety guardrail in this chapter uses PreToolUse.

PostToolUse¶

Fires after a tool executes successfully.

Timing: Immediately after the tool completes with a successful result, before the result is sent back to the LLM for the next iteration of the agentic loop.

Can block: No. The tool has already executed. PostToolUse hooks run for side effects.

Use cases: Auto-formatting code after edits, running linters, logging tool usage, updating dashboards, sending notifications for specific operations.

What it receives: The tool name, input, and the tool’s output/result.

Notification¶

Fires when Claude Code sends a notification to the user – typically when a long-running task completes or when Claude needs attention.

Timing: When the notification event is emitted.

Can block: No.

Use cases: Desktop notifications with sound, Slack/Teams messages, logging.

What it receives: The notification title and message.

Stop¶

Fires when Claude finishes its response and is about to return control to the user.

Timing: After the final LLM turn completes, before the response is displayed.

Can block: Yes. A Stop hook returning exit code 2 forces Claude to continue working – effectively acting as a quality gate. The stderr feedback tells Claude what is missing or wrong with its response.

Use cases: Verifying that Claude’s output meets quality criteria, checking that tests were run before claiming a fix is complete, ensuring documentation was updated alongside code changes.

What it receives: The full assistant response and conversation context.

Other Events¶

Claude Code also exposes UserPromptSubmit (validate user input before processing), SubagentStop (verify subagent output), TaskCompleted (post-completion checks), and FileChanged (react to filesystem changes). These follow the same patterns as the events above – some can block, some are observation-only. The five events covered in detail above handle 95% of real-world hook needs.
