Execution Order¶

Here is the complete lifecycle for a single tool call, showing exactly where each hook fires:

User types prompt
       │
       ▼
  ┌────────────────────────┐
  │ LLM decides: use tool  │
  │ (e.g., Bash: rm -rf .) │
  └───────────┬────────────┘
              │
              ▼
  ┌────────────────────────┐
  │ Permission evaluation   │
  │ deny → ask → allow      │
  │ (settings.json rules)   │
  └───────────┬────────────┘
              │
              ▼
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ PreToolUse hooks fire (in order)       │
  │                                        │
  │  Hook 1: block-destructive.sh          │
  │    → Checks command against blocklist  │
  │    → Exit 2? ──► BLOCKED. Tool does    │
  │                  not execute. Stderr    │
  │                  sent to Claude.        │
  │    → Exit 0? ──► Continue to next hook │
  │                                        │
  │  Hook 2: secret-scan.sh                │
  │    → Scans for secrets in staged files │
  │    → Exit 2? ──► BLOCKED.             │
  │    → Exit 0? ──► All hooks passed.    │
  └───────────┬────────────────────────────┘
              │
              ▼
  ┌────────────────────────┐
  │ Tool executes           │
  │ (actual command runs)   │
  └───────────┬────────────┘
              │
              ▼
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ PostToolUse hooks fire (in order)      │
  │                                        │
  │  Hook 1: auto-lint-python.sh           │
  │    → If .py file was edited, run ruff  │
  │    → Exit code ignored (non-blocking)  │
  └───────────┬────────────────────────────┘
              │
              ▼
  Tool result sent back to LLM
              │
              ▼
  LLM decides: another tool call? Or finish?
              │
         ┌────┴────┐
         │         │
    More tools   Done
         │         │
         ▼         ▼
  (loop back)  ┌──────────────────────┐
               │ Stop hooks fire      │
               │ Quality gate check   │
               │ Exit 2? → Continue   │
               │ Exit 0? → Respond    │
               └──────────────────────┘

Multiple PreToolUse hooks execute sequentially, in the order they appear in settings.json. If any hook returns exit code 2, subsequent hooks do not run – the tool call is blocked immediately. This is a fail-fast design: the first objection wins.

Hook Wiring in settings.json¶

Hooks live as scripts in your project (typically .claude/hooks/), but they only fire when wired into settings.json. The wiring configuration tells Claude Code which scripts to run, when to run them, and what tool calls they apply to.

Basic Structure¶

json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Bash",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "./.claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh",
            "timeout": 5000,
            "statusMessage": "Safety check..."
          }
        ]
      }
    ],
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Edit|Write",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "./.claude/hooks/auto-lint-python.sh",
            "timeout": 10000,
            "statusMessage": "Auto-formatting..."
          }
        ]
      }
    ],
    "SessionStart": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "./.claude/hooks/session-setup.sh",
            "timeout": 5000
          }
        ]
      }
    ],
    "Notification": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "./.claude/hooks/notify-macos.sh",
            "timeout": 3000
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

The Matcher Field¶

The matcher field determines which tool calls trigger the hook. It accepts a tool name or a pipe-separated list of tool names:

Matcher Matches
"Bash" Any Bash tool call
"Edit" Any Edit tool call
"Write" Any Write tool call
"Edit\|Write" Edit or Write tool calls
"Bash\|Edit\|Write" Bash, Edit, or Write tool calls
(omitted) All tool calls

When you omit the matcher entirely (as in the SessionStart and Notification examples above), the hook fires for every event of that type regardless of tool. For PreToolUse and PostToolUse, you almost always want a matcher – otherwise your secret-scanning hook would fire on every Read, Grep, and Glob call, not just Bash commands.

Configuration Fields¶

Each hook entry accepts these fields:

Field Required Description
type Yes Hook type: "command", "http", "prompt", or "agent"
command Yes (for command type) Path to the script, relative to project root
timeout No Maximum execution time in milliseconds (default: 60000)
statusMessage No Message shown in the UI while the hook runs

The timeout matters more than you think. A PreToolUse hook that hangs blocks the entire agentic loop. Set aggressive timeouts – 5000ms for safety checks, 10000ms for linting. If your hook needs more than 10 seconds, it is doing too much.

The JSON Stdin Protocol¶

Every hook receives context as a JSON object on standard input. The structure varies by event type, but the core pattern is consistent.

What Hooks Receive¶

For PreToolUse and PostToolUse on a Bash tool:

json
{
  "event": "PreToolUse",
  "tool_name": "Bash",
  "tool_input": {
    "command": "rm -rf ./build",
    "description": "Clean build directory",
    "timeout": 120000
  },
  "session_id": "abc123-def456"
}

For PreToolUse on an Edit tool:

json
{
  "event": "PreToolUse",
  "tool_name": "Edit",
  "tool_input": {
    "file_path": "/Users/dev/project/src/auth.py",
    "old_string": "password = request.body",
    "new_string": "password = sanitize(request.body)"
  },
  "session_id": "abc123-def456"
}

For Notification:

json
{
  "event": "Notification",
  "title": "Task Complete",
  "message": "Finished refactoring auth module. 3 files changed, all tests pass.",
  "session_id": "abc123-def456"
}

Reading JSON in Bash¶

The standard pattern for parsing JSON stdin in a hook script:

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Read all stdin into a variable
INPUT=$(cat)

# Extract fields using Python's json module (available everywhere)
TOOL_NAME=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(data.get('tool_name', ''))
")

COMMAND=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(data.get('tool_input', {}).get('command', ''))
")

Why python3 -c instead of jq? Because python3 is installed on every macOS and most Linux systems. jq is not. For hooks that run on every tool call, you want zero dependency assumptions.

If your team standardizes on jq (and you should if you are writing many hooks):

bash
COMMAND=$(echo "${INPUT}" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // ""')

Exit Codes¶

This is the contract between your hook and Claude Code:

Exit Code Meaning Effect
0 Allow / success Tool call proceeds (PreToolUse) or hook completed (PostToolUse)
2 Block Tool call is blocked. Stderr is sent to Claude as feedback
Any other Non-blocking error Logged, but execution continues. Hook is treated as if it did not object

Exit code 2 is special. It is the only code that blocks execution. Exit code 1 (the standard error code) does not block – it is treated as “the hook itself had a problem” and the tool call proceeds. This is a deliberate design choice: a buggy hook should not halt your workflow.

Providing Feedback to Claude¶

When a PreToolUse hook blocks with exit code 2, the message on stderr is sent directly to Claude as context. Claude uses this feedback to understand why the command was blocked and to formulate an alternative.

Write clear, actionable feedback:

bash
# Bad: Claude learns nothing
echo "Blocked." >&2
exit 2

# Good: Claude knows exactly what went wrong and what to do instead
echo "BLOCKED: 'rm -rf .' would delete the entire project directory. Use 'rm -rf ./build' to clean only the build directory, or list specific files to remove." >&2
exit 2

The structured JSON feedback format for the permission system:

bash
echo '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"Blocked: rm -rf with wildcard targets project root. Specify exact directory."}}' >&2
exit 2

Both formats work. The plain text format is simpler and sufficient for most hooks. The JSON format integrates more cleanly with Claude Code’s internal permission system.

The Four Hook Types¶

command – Shell Scripts¶

The workhorse. A command hook runs a shell script as a subprocess, passing context on stdin and reading the exit code.

json
{
  "type": "command",
  "command": "./.claude/hooks/my-hook.sh",
  "timeout": 5000
}

The command field is relative to the project root. The script must be executable (chmod +x). It runs with the same user permissions as Claude Code itself.

Use command hooks for: everything that does not require an HTTP call, LLM evaluation, or subagent orchestration. That covers about 90% of hooks.

http – Webhook URLs¶

An http hook sends the JSON context as a POST request to a URL and interprets the HTTP status code as the hook result.

json
{
  "type": "http",
  "url": "https://your-server.com/hooks/pre-tool-check",
  "timeout": 5000
}
HTTP Status Meaning
200 Allow
403 Block (response body = feedback to Claude)
Other Non-blocking error

Use http hooks for: centralized policy enforcement across a team, logging to external systems, integration with security platforms. A team lead can run a policy server that all developers’ Claude Code instances call before executing commands.

prompt – LLM Evaluation¶

A prompt hook sends a prompt to Claude (or another configured model) for evaluation. The model’s response determines whether the action proceeds.

json
{
  "type": "prompt",
  "prompt": "Review this command for safety: {{tool_input.command}}. Is it destructive? Reply YES or NO with explanation."
}

Use prompt hooks for: nuanced safety checks that regex cannot handle. A regex can catch rm -rf /, but it takes an LLM to evaluate whether find . -name '*.log' -delete is dangerous in the context of the current project.

The tradeoff is latency. A prompt hook adds an LLM API call to every matched tool invocation. Use sparingly.

agent – Subagent Invocation¶

An agent hook spawns a Claude Code subagent with its own context, tools, and instructions to evaluate the action.

json
{
  "type": "agent",
  "agent": "./.claude/agents/safety-reviewer.md",
  "timeout": 30000
}

Use agent hooks for: complex multi-step evaluations – the agent can read files, search the codebase, and make informed judgments about whether an action is safe. This is the most powerful hook type and the most expensive.

In practice, most teams use command hooks for 90% of their guardrails, http hooks for centralized team policies, and only reach for prompt or agent hooks when the safety check genuinely requires LLM reasoning.

Five Production-Ready Hook Examples¶

1. block-destructive.sh – Preventing Catastrophic Commands¶

This hook catches the commands that end careers: recursive deletes at root, dropping production databases, force-pushing to main.

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Read JSON input from stdin
INPUT=$(cat)

# Extract the command being executed
COMMAND=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(data.get('tool_input', {}).get('command', ''))
")

# Skip if no command (not a Bash tool call)
if [[ -z "${COMMAND}" ]]; then
    exit 0
fi

# Patterns that should NEVER execute
# Each pattern: regex | human-readable description
readonly BLOCKED_PATTERNS=(
    "rm -rf /|Recursive delete from root"
    "rm -rf \.|Recursive delete from current directory"
    "rm -rf ~|Recursive delete of home directory"
    "rm -rf \*|Recursive delete with wildcard"
    "DROP TABLE|SQL table deletion"
    "DROP DATABASE|SQL database deletion"
    "TRUNCATE TABLE|SQL table truncation"
    "git push.*--force.*main|Force push to main branch"
    "git push.*--force.*master|Force push to master branch"
    "git push.*-f.*main|Force push to main branch"
    "git push.*-f.*master|Force push to master branch"
    "chmod -R 777|World-writable permissions"
    "mkfs\.|Format filesystem"
    "dd if=.*of=/dev/|Direct disk write"
    "> /dev/sda|Direct disk overwrite"
    ":(){ :|:& };:|Fork bomb"
)

for pattern_entry in "${BLOCKED_PATTERNS[@]}"; do
    IFS='|' read -r pattern description <<< "${pattern_entry}"
    if echo "${COMMAND}" | grep -qE "${pattern}"; then
        cat >&2 << EOF
BLOCKED: ${description}

Command: ${COMMAND}
Pattern matched: ${pattern}

This command was blocked by the safety hook because it could cause
irreversible damage. If you need to perform this operation, be more
specific about the target (e.g., 'rm -rf ./build' instead of 'rm -rf .').
EOF
        exit 2
    fi
done

exit 0

Wire it:

json
"PreToolUse": [
  {
    "matcher": "Bash",
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "./.claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh",
        "timeout": 5000,
        "statusMessage": "Safety check..."
      }
    ]
  }
]

2. secret-scan.sh – Catching Secrets Before They Commit¶

This hook fires on git commit commands and scans staged files for patterns that look like secrets.

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)

COMMAND=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(data.get('tool_input', {}).get('command', ''))
")

# Only scan on git commit commands
if ! echo "${COMMAND}" | grep -qE "^git commit"; then
    exit 0
fi

# Get staged files
STAGED_FILES=$(git diff --cached --name-only 2>/dev/null || true)

if [[ -z "${STAGED_FILES}" ]]; then
    exit 0
fi

# Secret patterns to scan for
readonly SECRET_PATTERNS=(
    "AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}|AWS Access Key ID"
    "aws_secret_access_key\s*=|AWS Secret Key assignment"
    "ghp_[0-9a-zA-Z]{36}|GitHub Personal Access Token"
    "github_pat_[0-9a-zA-Z_]{82}|GitHub Fine-Grained PAT"
    "sk-[0-9a-zA-Z]{48}|OpenAI/Anthropic API Key"
    "xox[bporas]-[0-9a-zA-Z-]{10,}|Slack Token"
    "-----BEGIN (RSA |EC |DSA )?PRIVATE KEY|Private Key"
    "password\s*=\s*['\"][^'\"]{8,}['\"]|Hardcoded password"
    "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY\s*=\s*['\"]sk-|Anthropic API Key"
)

FOUND_SECRETS=0
FINDINGS=""

while IFS= read -r file; do
    # Skip binary files and deleted files
    if [[ ! -f "${file}" ]]; then
        continue
    fi

    for pattern_entry in "${SECRET_PATTERNS[@]}"; do
        IFS='|' read -r pattern description <<< "${pattern_entry}"
        MATCHES=$(grep -nE "${pattern}" "${file}" 2>/dev/null || true)
        if [[ -n "${MATCHES}" ]]; then
            FOUND_SECRETS=$((FOUND_SECRETS + 1))
            FINDINGS="${FINDINGS}\n  [${description}] ${file}:\n${MATCHES}\n"
        fi
    done
done <<< "${STAGED_FILES}"

if [[ ${FOUND_SECRETS} -gt 0 ]]; then
    cat >&2 << EOF
BLOCKED: Potential secrets detected in staged files.

Found ${FOUND_SECRETS} potential secret(s):
$(echo -e "${FINDINGS}")

Remove these secrets before committing. Use environment variables
or a secrets manager instead of hardcoding credentials.

To inspect staged changes: git diff --cached
To unstage a file: git reset HEAD <file>
EOF
    exit 2
fi

exit 0

3. auto-lint-python.sh – Automatic Formatting After Edits¶

This hook runs ruff after any Python file is edited or written, ensuring code is always formatted and linted.

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)

# Extract the file path from the tool input
FILE_PATH=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
tool_input = data.get('tool_input', {})
# Edit tool uses file_path, Write tool uses file_path
print(tool_input.get('file_path', ''))
")

# Only process Python files
if [[ ! "${FILE_PATH}" == *.py ]]; then
    exit 0
fi

# Verify the file exists
if [[ ! -f "${FILE_PATH}" ]]; then
    exit 0
fi

# Check if ruff is available
if ! command -v ruff >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    exit 0
fi

# Run ruff check with auto-fix (suppress output unless there are errors)
ruff check --fix --quiet "${FILE_PATH}" 2>/dev/null || true

# Run ruff format
ruff format --quiet "${FILE_PATH}" 2>/dev/null || true

exit 0

Wire it on PostToolUse with an Edit|Write matcher:

json
"PostToolUse": [
  {
    "matcher": "Edit|Write",
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "./.claude/hooks/auto-lint-python.sh",
        "timeout": 10000,
        "statusMessage": "Auto-formatting..."
      }
    ]
  }
]

The key design choice: this hook exits 0 even if ruff fails. A linting failure should never block the workflow – it is a convenience, not a guardrail. The || true after each ruff command ensures the hook never exits non-zero due to unfixable lint issues.

4. notify-macos.sh – Desktop Notifications¶

When Claude finishes a long-running task, you want to know. This hook uses macOS’s built-in osascript to pop a notification with sound.

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)

# Extract notification details
TITLE=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(data.get('title', 'Claude Code'))
")

MESSAGE=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(data.get('message', 'Task completed'))
")

# macOS notification via osascript
if command -v osascript >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    osascript -e "display notification \"${MESSAGE}\" with title \"${TITLE}\" sound name \"Glass\""
fi

exit 0

Wire it:

json
"Notification": [
  {
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "./.claude/hooks/notify-macos.sh",
        "timeout": 3000
      }
    ]
  }
]

No matcher needed – Notification events do not have tool-specific context.

5. session-setup.sh – Environment Initialization¶

This hook runs once at session start, setting environment variables and verifying prerequisites.

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Prevent Python from creating __pycache__ directories
export PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1

# Ensure local bin is in PATH
export PATH="${HOME}/.local/bin:${HOME}/bin:${PATH}"

# Verify critical tools are available
MISSING_TOOLS=()

for tool in git python3 node ruff; do
    if ! command -v "${tool}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        MISSING_TOOLS+=("${tool}")
    fi
done

if [[ ${#MISSING_TOOLS[@]} -gt 0 ]]; then
    echo "Warning: Missing tools: ${MISSING_TOOLS[*]}" >&2
    echo "Some features may not work correctly." >&2
fi

# Log session start (optional)
echo "[$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")] Session started in $(pwd)" >> "${HOME}/.claude/session.log" 2>/dev/null || true

exit 0

Wire it:

json
"SessionStart": [
  {
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "./.claude/hooks/session-setup.sh",
        "timeout": 5000
      }
    ]
  }
]

Building a Custom Hook – Step by Step¶

You need a hook that enforces a project rule: no one should add a new Python dependency without also updating the requirements.txt pinned versions. Here is how to build it from scratch.

Step 1: Write the Script¶

Create .claude/hooks/check-deps.sh:

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)

COMMAND=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(data.get('tool_input', {}).get('command', ''))
")

# Only check pip install commands
if ! echo "${COMMAND}" | grep -qE "pip install|uv add|uv pip install"; then
    exit 0
fi

# Extract the package name (simplified -- handles 'pip install requests')
PACKAGE=$(echo "${COMMAND}" | grep -oE "(pip install|uv add|uv pip install)\s+\S+" | awk '{print $NF}')

if [[ -z "${PACKAGE}" ]]; then
    exit 0
fi

# Check if requirements.txt exists and if the package is already pinned
if [[ -f "requirements.txt" ]]; then
    if ! grep -qi "^${PACKAGE}[=>~!]" requirements.txt; then
        cat >&2 << EOF
REMINDER: You are installing '${PACKAGE}' which is not yet in requirements.txt.

After installing, please add it with a pinned version:
  pip freeze | grep -i ${PACKAGE} >> requirements.txt

Or better, use: pip install ${PACKAGE} && pip freeze | grep -i ${PACKAGE} >> requirements.txt
EOF
        # Exit 0, not 2 -- this is a reminder, not a block
        exit 0
    fi
fi

exit 0

Notice this hook exits 0 even when the package is not pinned. It provides a reminder, not a block. Whether to block or remind is a judgment call for each hook. Blocking pip installs would disrupt the workflow too aggressively – a reminder is sufficient.

Step 2: Make It Executable¶

bash
chmod +x .claude/hooks/check-deps.sh

Forgetting this step is the number one reason hooks silently fail. Claude Code will try to execute the script, get a permission denied error, treat it as a non-blocking error (exit code != 2), and move on. Your hook simply never fires. No error message, no warning, no indication that anything went wrong.

Step 3: Wire It in settings.json¶

json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Bash",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "./.claude/hooks/check-deps.sh",
            "timeout": 5000,
            "statusMessage": "Checking dependencies..."
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

Step 4: Test It¶

Test your hook manually before relying on it in a session:

bash
echo '{"tool_input":{"command":"pip install requests"}}' | ./.claude/hooks/check-deps.sh
echo "Exit code: $?"
bash
echo '{"tool_input":{"command":"git status"}}' | ./.claude/hooks/check-deps.sh
echo "Exit code: $?"

The first command should print the reminder. The second should exit silently with code 0. If either behaves unexpectedly, fix the script before wiring it.

Step 5: Test It Inside Claude Code¶

Start a Claude Code session and ask it to install a package:

Install the httpx library for HTTP requests

You should see the statusMessage (“Checking dependencies…”) appear briefly, followed by the reminder in Claude’s context. Claude will likely respond by both installing the package and adding it to requirements.txt – which is exactly the behavior you wanted.

Advanced Hook Patterns¶

Chaining Multiple Hooks¶

You can wire multiple hooks to the same event. They execute sequentially:

json
"PreToolUse": [
  {
    "matcher": "Bash",
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "./.claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh",
        "timeout": 5000,
        "statusMessage": "Safety check..."
      },
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "./.claude/hooks/secret-scan.sh",
        "timeout": 5000,
        "statusMessage": "Secret scan..."
      },
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "./.claude/hooks/check-deps.sh",
        "timeout": 5000,
        "statusMessage": "Dependency check..."
      }
    ]
  }
]

Execution order is top-to-bottom. If block-destructive.sh returns exit 2, secret-scan.sh and check-deps.sh never run. Design your hook chain with the most critical checks first.

Conditional Execution with Matchers¶

Different hooks for different tools:

json
"PreToolUse": [
  {
    "matcher": "Bash",
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "./.claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh",
        "timeout": 5000
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    "matcher": "Edit|Write",
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "./.claude/hooks/check-file-size.sh",
        "timeout": 5000
      }
    ]
  }
]

This runs the destructive command blocker only on Bash calls, and a file size checker only on Edit/Write calls. No wasted cycles.

Hook Debugging¶

When a hook does not behave as expected, debug it in isolation:

bash
# Test with exact JSON Claude Code would send
echo '{"event":"PreToolUse","tool_name":"Bash","tool_input":{"command":"rm -rf ."}}' | \
  ./.claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh 2>&1
echo "Exit: $?"
bash
# Test with a command that should pass
echo '{"event":"PreToolUse","tool_name":"Bash","tool_input":{"command":"ls -la"}}' | \
  ./.claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh 2>&1
echo "Exit: $?"

Common debugging issues:

  1. Hook does not fire. Check: Is it executable? Is it wired in settings.json? Is the matcher correct? Is the JSON valid?

  2. Hook fires but does not block. Check: Are you returning exit code 2 (not 1)? Is the regex pattern correct? Is set -e causing an early exit before your blocking logic?

  3. Hook blocks everything. Check: Is there an early exit 2 before your conditional logic? Is the regex too broad?

  4. Hook produces no stderr feedback. Check: Are you writing to stderr (>&2), not stdout?

The Stop Hook as a Quality Gate¶

The Stop hook is underused but powerful. Here is an example that checks whether Claude ran tests before claiming a bug fix is complete:

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)

# Extract the assistant's response
RESPONSE=$(echo "${INPUT}" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(data.get('assistant_response', ''))
")

# If the response mentions a fix but no test execution evidence
if echo "${RESPONSE}" | grep -qi "fix\|fixed\|resolved\|patched"; then
    if ! echo "${RESPONSE}" | grep -qi "test.*pass\|tests.*pass\|pytest\|npm test\|test suite"; then
        echo "Quality gate: You mentioned fixing a bug but did not show test results. Please run the relevant tests to verify the fix." >&2
        exit 2
    fi
fi

exit 0

When this hook returns exit 2, Claude does not stop – it continues working, using the stderr feedback as guidance. It will run the tests and include the results before trying to finish again.

Environment Variable Hooks¶

Sometimes a hook’s behavior should vary by environment. Use environment variables:

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Allow overriding strict mode for CI environments
if [[ "${CLAUDE_HOOKS_STRICT:-true}" == "false" ]]; then
    exit 0
fi

# ... normal hook logic

In development: hooks run normally. In CI: export CLAUDE_HOOKS_STRICT=false to relax them.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

1. Using exit code 1 to block. Exit code 1 means “the hook crashed.” Exit code 2 means “block this action.” Every week, someone files a bug report saying “my hook doesn’t block anything” because they wrote exit 1 instead of exit 2. The contract is explicit: 2 blocks, everything else does not.

2. Forgetting chmod +x. The script exists. The wiring is correct. The matcher is right. But the hook never fires. Claude Code cannot execute a script without execute permissions. It treats the permission error as a non-blocking failure and continues silently. Add chmod +x to your hook setup checklist and to your workspace validation script.

3. Overly broad matchers. Wiring a heavy hook to fire on every tool call – Read, Grep, Glob, Edit, Write, Bash – adds latency to every single step of the agentic loop. A ten-turn task with 30 tool calls and a 500ms hook means 15 seconds of pure hook overhead. Use specific matchers.

4. Hooks that are too clever. A hook that parses SQL queries, evaluates their safety against the database schema, and checks row counts before allowing execution is impressive engineering and terrible in practice. It will be fragile, slow, and impossible for your teammates to debug. Start with simple regex patterns. Add complexity only when the simple approach demonstrably fails.

5. Not testing hooks in isolation. Hooks run inside Claude Code’s process pipeline. If your hook has a bug, the debugging cycle is: start Claude Code session, trigger the hook, observe the behavior, read the logs, edit the script, restart the session. That is a five-minute cycle. Testing with echo '...' | ./hook.sh is a five-second cycle. Always test outside Claude Code first.

6. Ignoring timeout. A hook that makes an HTTP call to a slow server with no timeout will hang the entire agentic loop for the default 60 seconds. Set explicit timeouts in both the settings.json configuration and within your script logic (using timeout command or similar).

7. Writing blocking hooks for non-critical checks. Reserve exit code 2 for genuinely dangerous operations – things that would lose data, leak secrets, or break production. Style violations, missing documentation, and naming convention issues should be reminders (exit 0 with stderr output), not blocks (exit 2). If you block too aggressively, developers will disable your hooks entirely.

Chapter Summary¶

Five things to remember from this chapter:

  1. Hooks are lifecycle interceptors. They fire at specific points in Claude Code’s execution loop – PreToolUse (before, can block), PostToolUse (after, observe only), SessionStart (once, setup), Notification (alerts), and Stop (quality gate). The execution order is: permission check, then PreToolUse hooks sequentially, then tool execution, then PostToolUse hooks.

  2. Exit code 2 is the blocking signal. Not exit 1, not exit 255 – exit code 2. Anything written to stderr when exiting with code 2 becomes feedback that Claude uses to reformulate its approach. Write clear, actionable messages.

  3. Hooks receive JSON on stdin. Every hook gets tool_name, tool_input, session_id, and event-specific fields. Parse with python3 -c for zero-dependency reliability or jq for cleanliness. Test by piping JSON manually.

  4. Four types serve different needs. command (shell scripts) handles 90% of cases. http (webhooks) enables centralized team policies. prompt (LLM evaluation) catches nuanced dangers that regex misses. agent (subagent) provides deep, multi-step evaluation.

  5. Start simple, stay simple. A twelve-line script that catches rm -rf / prevents more disasters than a thousand-line policy engine that no one understands. Build hooks that your least experienced team member can read, debug, and modify. Complexity is the enemy of guardrails that actually get used.

As discussed in Chapter 3, hooks fire after the permission system has already evaluated the tool call. They are a second layer of defense, not a replacement for proper permission configuration. In Chapter 5, we covered how settings.json permissions provide the first gate. Hooks provide the second. Together, they form a defense-in-depth strategy for autonomous AI tool use.

In Chapter 9, we will explore the complete tool system that these hooks protect – every built-in tool, how tool selection works, and strategies for controlling which tools Claude reaches for.

Part III: Tool System Mastery

Chapter 9: The Complete Tool System¶ ~19 min read

You open Claude Code, type “fix the authentication bug in src/auth/login.ts,” and watch it work. It reads the file. Searches for related test files. Edits the broken logic. Runs the tests. Commits the fix. Every one of those actions is a tool call – Claude Code’s mechanism for interacting with the world beyond generating text.

Understanding the tool system is not optional. It is the difference between a user who watches Claude Code fumble through tasks and one who configures it to operate with surgical precision. This chapter is the complete reference for every tool Claude Code has, how it selects between them, and the execution pipeline that governs every tool invocation.

How Tools Work¶

Claude Code operates in an agentic loop: the LLM generates a response, which may include one or more tool calls. The system executes those calls, returns the results, and the LLM generates another response based on the new information. This loop continues until the task is complete.

Tools are Claude Code’s hands. Without them, it can only produce text. With them, it can read your codebase, modify files, run tests, search the web, and spawn child agents to parallelize work.

Every tool follows the same contract:

  1. The LLM outputs a tool_use block specifying the tool name and parameters
  2. The permission system checks whether the call is allowed
  3. PreToolUse hooks fire (can block the call)
  4. The tool executes
  5. PostToolUse hooks fire (can modify results or trigger side effects)
  6. The result is truncated if necessary and returned to the LLM as a ToolResult
LLM returns tool_use → Permission check → PreToolUse hooks →
Execute tool → PostToolUse hooks → Truncate result → Return to LLM

This pipeline applies to every single tool call. No exceptions. Understanding it means understanding exactly where your hooks, permissions, and configurations intercept.

The Complete Tool Inventory¶

Claude Code ships with a rich set of built-in tools. Some you will use constantly. Others exist for specific scenarios. Here is every tool, what it does, and when to reach for it.

1. Read – Read Files from the Filesystem¶

Read loads file contents into Claude Code’s context. It is the most frequently called tool and the foundation of all code understanding.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
file_path string (required) Absolute path to the file
offset integer Line number to start reading from
limit integer Number of lines to read
pages string Page range for PDFs (e.g., “1-5”)

What it reads: - Source code files (any language, any extension) - Images (PNG, JPG, GIF – Claude is multimodal and sees the image visually) - PDF files (requires pages parameter for documents over 10 pages) - Jupyter notebooks (.ipynb – returns all cells with their outputs) - Configuration files, logs, data files – anything on the filesystem

Output format: Line-numbered text using cat -n format, with line numbers starting at 1. This is important because Edit operations reference these line numbers for context.

When to use: Before every Edit. Before analyzing code. Before understanding project structure. When the user references a file.

When NOT to use: To read directories (use ls via Bash). To find files by name (use Glob). To search file contents (use Grep).

Example invocation:

Read(file_path: "/Users/dev/project/src/auth/login.ts")

For a large file where you only need a specific section:

Read(file_path: "/Users/dev/project/src/auth/login.ts", offset: 150, limit: 50)

For a PDF:

Read(file_path: "/Users/dev/docs/spec.pdf", pages: "1-5")

Common mistakes: - Using relative paths. Read requires absolute paths – always. - Reading a directory path instead of a file path. Read only handles files. - Trying to read a large PDF without the pages parameter. It will fail for PDFs over 10 pages. - Forgetting to read before editing. The Edit tool will refuse to operate on a file that has not been read in the current session.

2. Edit – Exact String Replacement¶

Edit modifies existing files by replacing exact strings. It is the primary tool for code changes, and it operates on diffs rather than rewriting entire files.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
file_path string (required) Absolute path to the file
old_string string (required) The exact text to find
new_string string (required) The replacement text
replace_all boolean Replace all occurrences (default: false)

How it works: Edit finds old_string in the file and replaces it with new_string. The match must be exact – same characters, same indentation, same whitespace. If old_string appears more than once and replace_all is false, the edit fails.

When to use: Any time you need to modify an existing file. Changing function logic, updating configuration values, fixing bugs, adding imports.

When NOT to use: Creating new files (use Write). Complete file rewrites (use Write). When you have not read the file first (it will error).

Example – fixing a bug:

Edit(
  file_path: "/Users/dev/project/src/auth/login.ts",
  old_string: "if (password === storedHash) {",
  new_string: "if (await bcrypt.compare(password, storedHash)) {"
)

Example – renaming a variable across a file:

Edit(
  file_path: "/Users/dev/project/src/utils.ts",
  old_string: "userName",
  new_string: "userId",
  replace_all: true
)

Common mistakes: - Not reading the file first. Edit enforces a “read before write” invariant and will error if you skip it. - Indentation mismatch. The old_string must preserve the exact indentation from the file. When you read a file, the output shows line_number\tfile_content – the indentation is everything after the tab following the line number. - Non-unique old_string. If the string appears multiple times and you want to change just one, include more surrounding context to make it unique. - Using Edit when Write is more appropriate. If you are replacing more than 60-70% of a file, Write is cleaner.

3. Write – Create or Rewrite Files¶

Write creates new files or completely overwrites existing ones.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
file_path string (required) Absolute path to the file
content string (required) The complete file content

When to use: Creating new files. Complete rewrites where Edit would require too many individual operations. Generated files (configs, boilerplate).

When NOT to use: Modifying a few lines of an existing file (use Edit – it only transmits the diff). Creating documentation files unless explicitly asked. Overwriting a file you have not read first.

Example – creating a new test file:

Write(
  file_path: "/Users/dev/project/tests/test_login.py",
  content: "import pytest\nfrom src.auth.login import authenticate\n\n\ndef test_valid_credentials():\n    result = authenticate('user@test.com', 'correct-password')\n    assert result.success is True\n"
)

Common mistakes: - Using Write to make a small change to a large file. This sends the entire file content, wasting tokens and risking accidental changes. Use Edit instead. - Not reading an existing file before overwriting it. Write will error if the file exists and you haven’t read it.

4. Glob – Find Files by Pattern¶

Glob finds files matching a pattern. It is fast at any codebase size and returns results sorted by modification time.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
pattern string (required) Glob pattern (e.g., **/*.ts)
path string Directory to search in (defaults to cwd)

Pattern syntax: - * – matches any characters within a single directory level - ** – matches any characters across directory levels (recursive) - ? – matches a single character - {a,b} – matches either a or b

When to use: Finding files by name or extension. Discovering project structure. Locating test files, configs, components.

When NOT to use: Searching file contents (use Grep). Listing directory contents (Glob works, but ls via Bash is simpler for a single directory).

Example – find all TypeScript files:

Glob(pattern: "**/*.ts", path: "/Users/dev/project/src")

Example – find test files for a specific module:

Glob(pattern: "**/auth/**/*.test.{ts,tsx}")

Common mistakes: - Using find in Bash when Glob exists. Glob is faster and its output is optimized for Claude Code’s context. - Forgetting ** for recursive searches. *.ts only matches the current directory; **/*.ts searches all subdirectories.

5. Grep – Search File Contents¶

Grep searches file contents using regular expressions. It is built on ripgrep, which means it is fast, Unicode-aware, and respects .gitignore by default.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
pattern string (required) Regex pattern to search for
path string File or directory to search
output_mode string files_with_matches (default), content, count
glob string Filter by file pattern (e.g., *.py)
type string Filter by file type (e.g., py, ts)
-i boolean Case-insensitive search
-A / -B / -C number Lines after/before/context
multiline boolean Match patterns across line boundaries
head_limit number Max results (default: 250)
offset number Skip first N results

Three output modes:

Mode Returns Use when
files_with_matches File paths only Finding which files contain a pattern
content Matching lines with context Reading actual matches
count Match counts per file Measuring frequency

When to use: Finding function definitions, imports, usages of a variable, TODO comments, error messages, configuration values.

When NOT to use: Finding files by name (use Glob). Reading a file you already know the path to (use Read).

Example – find all usages of a function:

Grep(
  pattern: "authenticateUser\\(",
  type: "ts",
  output_mode: "content",
  -C: 2
)

Example – find files containing a specific import:

Grep(
  pattern: "from.*auth.*import",
  glob: "*.py",
  output_mode: "files_with_matches"
)

Common mistakes: - Using grep or rg in Bash. The Grep tool is optimized for Claude Code’s permission model and output formatting. - Not setting head_limit on broad searches. The default of 250 is usually fine, but a regex like .* on a large codebase will flood the context window. - Forgetting to escape special regex characters. Literal braces need escaping (interface\\{\\} to find interface{}), and parentheses need \\( in patterns.

6. Bash – Execute Shell Commands¶

Bash runs arbitrary shell commands. It is the most powerful tool and the one with the most security implications. It gets its own chapter (Chapter 10), but here is the overview.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
command string (required) Shell command to execute
description string Human-readable description of the command
timeout number Timeout in ms (max 600,000 – 10 min)
run_in_background boolean Run without blocking

When to use: Running tests, git operations, build commands, package management, any command-line operation that does not have a dedicated tool.

When NOT to use: Reading files (use Read). Searching files (use Grep/Glob). Editing files (use Edit). These dedicated tools are faster and produce better-formatted output.

Example:

Bash(command: "cd /Users/dev/project && python -m pytest tests/test_auth.py -v", description: "Run auth tests")

7. Agent – Spawn Subagents¶

Agent creates a child Claude instance for complex or parallel tasks. It protects your main context window from exploration noise and enables parallel execution.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
prompt string (required) Task description for the agent
subagent_type string Agent type: general-purpose, Explore, Plan
model string Override model (sonnet, opus, haiku)
run_in_background boolean Non-blocking execution
isolation string “worktree” for git isolation

When to use: Multi-step investigations, parallel independent tasks, work that would pollute main context. See Chapter 12 for full coverage.

When NOT to use: Simple reads, searches, or operations involving 2-3 files.

8. WebFetch – Fetch Web Content¶

WebFetch retrieves content from a URL and processes it with a small, fast model.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
url string (required) Fully-formed URL to fetch
prompt string (required) What to extract from the page

What it does: Fetches the URL, converts HTML to markdown, processes with a small model using your prompt, and returns the extracted information. Includes a 15-minute cache.

When to use: Reading documentation, extracting data from public web pages, checking API references.

When NOT to use: Authenticated pages (Google Docs, Jira, private GitHub repos). For GitHub, use gh CLI. For authenticated services, use MCP tools that provide authenticated access.

Example:

WebFetch(
  url: "https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html",
  prompt: "List all the methods on the Path class with brief descriptions"
)

Common mistakes: - Fetching authenticated URLs. WebFetch cannot handle login-protected pages. It will fail silently or return a login page. - Fetching GitHub URLs when gh CLI is available. gh api, gh pr view, gh issue view are faster and more reliable.

9. WebSearch – Search the Web¶

WebSearch performs a web search and returns results with links.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
query string (required) Search query
allowed_domains array Only include results from these domains
blocked_domains array Exclude results from these domains

When to use: Researching current topics, finding documentation, checking for recent updates.

When NOT to use: Searching your local codebase (use Grep).

Example:

WebSearch(query: "Python 3.13 new features 2025", allowed_domains: ["docs.python.org", "realpython.com"])

10. NotebookEdit – Edit Jupyter Notebooks¶

NotebookEdit modifies individual cells in .ipynb files.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
notebook_path string (required) Absolute path to the notebook
new_source string (required) New cell content
cell_id string Cell ID to target
cell_type string code or markdown
edit_mode string replace, insert, or delete

When to use: Modifying data science notebooks, adding analysis cells, fixing notebook code.

When NOT to use: Creating a notebook from scratch (use Write to create the .ipynb JSON structure).

11. AskUserQuestion – Request Clarification¶

AskUserQuestion pauses execution and asks the user for input. Claude Code uses this when it genuinely cannot proceed without information.

When it fires: When the task is ambiguous and Claude Code cannot make a reasonable assumption. For example, “fix it” with no error context and multiple recently changed files.

Design principle: A well-configured Claude Code instance should rarely need this. The auto-execute protocol prioritizes making reasonable assumptions and proceeding. AskUserQuestion is the escape valve, not the default.

12. EnterPlanMode – Switch to Planning¶

EnterPlanMode transitions Claude Code from execution mode to planning mode. In planning mode, Claude Code analyzes and strategizes without making changes.

When to use: Complex multi-file refactors that need a plan before execution. Architecture decisions requiring analysis. When the user says /plan.

When it matters: Planning mode prevents accidental modifications while Claude Code is still figuring out the right approach.

13. Task Management – TaskCreate, TaskList, TaskUpdate, TaskGet¶

These four tools manage a task system for tracking multi-step work.

Tool Purpose
TaskCreate Create a new task with description and steps
TaskList List all active tasks
TaskUpdate Update task status, add subtasks
TaskGet Get details on a specific task

When to use: Complex projects with multiple phases. When the user says /plan and the work spans many files. Tracking progress on long-running tasks.

14. EnterWorktree – Git Isolation¶

EnterWorktree creates an isolated git worktree for experimental work.

What it does: Creates a new branch and working copy inside .claude/worktrees/. The agent operates on this copy without affecting the main working directory.

When to use: Risky refactoring experiments. Testing approaches that might need to be discarded. Parallel development of independent features.

Related: ExitWorktree leaves the worktree, either keeping it (for later) or removing it (clean exit).

15. Skill – Invoke a Skill¶

Skill loads and executes a skill defined in .claude/skills/.

Key parameters:

Parameter Type Description
skill string (required) Skill name (e.g., “code-review”)
args string Optional arguments

When to use: When the user types a slash command like /code-review. When Claude Code detects an intent matching a skill’s description.

Example:

Skill(skill: "fix-issue", args: "42")

16. Cron Management – CronCreate, CronDelete, CronList¶

These tools manage scheduled recurring tasks.

Tool Purpose
CronCreate Schedule a recurring task with interval and prompt
CronDelete Remove a scheduled task
CronList List all active cron tasks

When to use: Monitoring deployments, polling for CI status, recurring checks. When the user says /loop.

Tool Selection Strategy¶

Claude Code does not randomly pick tools. It follows a deliberate selection hierarchy that prioritizes specialized tools over general-purpose ones. Understanding this hierarchy helps you predict what Claude Code will do and configure permissions accordingly.

The Hierarchy¶

Rule 1: Read before Edit. Always.

Every Edit call must be preceded by a Read of the target file in the same session. This is not a suggestion – the Edit tool enforces it and will error if you skip it. This prevents blind edits based on stale assumptions.

Rule 2: Specialized tools over Bash.

Instead of Use
cat file.py in Bash Read(file_path: "file.py")
grep -r "pattern" . in Bash Grep(pattern: "pattern")
find . -name "*.ts" in Bash Glob(pattern: "**/*.ts")
sed -i 's/old/new/' in Bash Edit(old_string: "old", new_string: "new")
echo "content" > file.py in Bash Write(file_path: "file.py", content: "content")

Why? Specialized tools produce output formatted for Claude Code’s context window. They respect permissions more granularly. They have better error handling. And they are faster because they skip shell initialization.

Rule 3: Glob for names, Grep for contents.

Looking for files named *.test.ts? Glob. Looking for files containing describe("Auth? Grep. Different tools for different searches.

Rule 4: Edit for modifications, Write for creation.

Edit sends only the diff. Write sends the entire file. For a one-line change in a 500-line file, Edit uses a fraction of the tokens.

Rule 5: Agent for complexity, direct tools for simplicity.

If the task requires reading 2 files and making an edit, do it directly. If it requires searching through 20 files to understand a pattern before making changes, spawn an Agent to do the exploration.

How Claude Code Decides¶

When Claude Code needs to perform an action, its decision tree looks roughly like this:

Need to access file content?
├── Know the path? → Read
├── Know the name pattern? → Glob → then Read
└── Know content pattern? → Grep → then Read

Need to modify a file?
├── File exists? → Read → Edit
├── New file? → Write
└── Many changes across file? → Read → Write (full rewrite)

Need to run a command?
├── Is there a dedicated tool? → Use it
└── No dedicated tool? → Bash

Need to search broadly?
├── File names? → Glob
├── File contents? → Grep
└── Multi-round search? → Agent with Explore type

Need external information?
├── Public web page? → WebFetch
├── General search? → WebSearch
├── GitHub data? → Bash(gh ...)
└── Authenticated service? → MCP tool

The Tool Execution Pipeline¶

Every tool call passes through the same pipeline. Understanding each stage lets you configure Claude Code precisely.

Stage 1: Permission Check¶

The permission system evaluates the call against your settings:

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "deny": ["Read(./.env)"],
    "allow": ["Bash(git status *)"],
    "ask": []
  }
}

Evaluation order: deny first, then allow, then ask. First match wins. If nothing matches, Claude Code prompts you for permission.

A denied tool call never reaches the hooks. It is stopped immediately, and Claude Code receives an error message explaining the denial.

Stage 2: PreToolUse Hooks¶

If the permission check passes, PreToolUse hooks fire. These hooks receive a JSON payload on stdin describing the tool call:

json
{
  "tool_name": "Bash",
  "tool_input": {
    "command": "rm -rf /tmp/old-cache"
  }
}

A hook that exits with code 2 blocks the call. The hook’s stderr becomes feedback that Claude Code sees. This is how the block-destructive.sh hook prevents dangerous commands – it pattern-matches the command and blocks known-dangerous patterns.

Stage 3: Execution¶

The tool runs. For Bash, this means spawning a shell process. For Read, it means reading the filesystem. For Agent, it means starting a new Claude session. Each tool has its own timeout – Bash defaults to 120 seconds but can be set up to 600 seconds.

Stage 4: PostToolUse Hooks¶

After successful execution, PostToolUse hooks fire. They receive the tool name, input, and output. They cannot block (the action already happened), but they can trigger side effects.

The canonical example is auto-lint-python.sh: after any Edit or Write to a .py file, it runs ruff check --fix and ruff format automatically.

Stage 5: Result Truncation and Return¶

Tool results can be large. A Grep across a big codebase might return thousands of lines. Claude Code truncates results that exceed token limits, keeping the beginning and end to preserve context.

The result goes back to the LLM as a ToolResult containing: - Text content: the tool’s output - Error flag: whether the tool errored - Truncation indicator: whether the output was truncated

ToolResult Format¶

Every tool returns a structured result. When a tool succeeds, Claude Code sees the output text. When it fails, Claude Code sees the error message and adjusts its approach.

Successful Read:
  content: "1\timport express from 'express';\n2\t..."
  is_error: false

Failed Edit (non-unique match):
  content: "Error: old_string appears 3 times in the file. Include more context to make it unique, or use replace_all."
  is_error: true

Truncated Grep:
  content: "[first 200 matches...]\n\n--- output truncated (showing 200 of 1,847 matches) ---"
  is_error: false

How Claude Code handles errors: When a tool returns is_error: true, Claude Code reads the error message, understands what went wrong, and tries a different approach. This is one of the key behaviors of the agentic loop – failure is information, not a dead end. If an Edit fails because the old_string is not unique, Claude Code will read the file again, include more surrounding context, and retry.

Parallel Tool Execution¶

Claude Code can call multiple tools in a single response. This is a critical optimization – it reduces round trips and speeds up complex operations.

When Claude Code parallelizes: - Reading multiple independent files at once - Running Grep and Glob simultaneously for different searches - Spawning multiple Agent instances for independent subtasks

When Claude Code serializes: - Read then Edit (Edit depends on Read output) - Glob then Read (Read depends on Glob finding the files) - Any chain where tool B’s input depends on tool A’s output

Example of parallel execution:

In a single response, Claude Code might output:

Tool call 1: Read(file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts")
Tool call 2: Read(file_path: "/project/src/types.ts")
Tool call 3: Grep(pattern: "AuthService", type: "ts")

All three execute simultaneously. The results return together, and Claude Code proceeds with full knowledge of all three.

Cost implication: Parallel execution does not cost extra tokens for the calls themselves. But the combined results must fit in the context window. Five parallel Reads of large files will consume context fast.

Tool Timeouts¶

Each tool has timeout behavior:

Tool Default Timeout Max Timeout Configurable?
Bash 120,000 ms (2 min) 600,000 ms (10 min) Yes, per-call
Read Fast (filesystem I/O) N/A No
Edit Fast (filesystem I/O) N/A No
Write Fast (filesystem I/O) N/A No
Glob Fast (filesystem scan) N/A No
Grep Fast (ripgrep) N/A No
WebFetch Network-dependent N/A No
Agent Session-dependent N/A No

For Bash commands that take longer than 2 minutes (builds, long test suites), set the timeout explicitly:

Bash(
  command: "python -m pytest tests/ -v --timeout=300",
  timeout: 360000,
  description: "Run full test suite (may take up to 5 minutes)"
)

For commands where you do not need the result immediately:

Bash(
  command: "npm run build",
  run_in_background: true,
  description: "Build the project in the background"
)

Background commands notify Claude Code when they complete. No polling needed.

Tool-Specific Configuration¶

Grep: head_limit and Pagination¶

Grep’s head_limit defaults to 250 entries. This prevents a broad search from flooding the context window. For most searches, 250 is plenty. But if you need more, you can paginate:

# First 250 results
Grep(pattern: "TODO", type: "py", head_limit: 250)

# Next 250 results
Grep(pattern: "TODO", type: "py", head_limit: 250, offset: 250)

Edit: Making old_string Unique¶

When your target string appears multiple times, include surrounding context:

# This might match multiple places:
Edit(old_string: "return null;", new_string: "return undefined;")

# This is unique:
Edit(
  old_string: "  if (!user) {\n    return null;\n  }",
  new_string: "  if (!user) {\n    return undefined;\n  }"
)

Bash: Description Field¶

The description field is not just documentation. It appears in the user’s permission prompt, helping them decide whether to allow the command:

Bash(
  command: "pip install requests",
  description: "Install requests library for HTTP operations"
)

A clear description makes the permission prompt informative rather than cryptic.

Common Tool Patterns¶

These are the patterns you will see Claude Code use repeatedly.

The Read-Understand-Edit Pattern¶

1. Read(file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts")
2. [LLM analyzes the code, identifies the issue]
3. Edit(file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts", old_string: "...", new_string: "...")

This is the most common three-step pattern. Read provides understanding. The LLM reasons about what to change. Edit makes the minimal modification.

The Search-Narrow-Act Pattern¶

1. Grep(pattern: "deprecated.*API", output_mode: "files_with_matches")
2. [LLM identifies the most relevant file]
3. Read(file_path: "/project/src/api/legacy.ts")
4. Edit(...)

Start broad with Grep, narrow to the right file, then act.

The Parallel Investigation Pattern¶

Tool call 1: Grep(pattern: "handleAuth", type: "ts", output_mode: "content")
Tool call 2: Glob(pattern: "**/auth/**/*.ts")
Tool call 3: Read(file_path: "/project/package.json")

Three independent lookups executing simultaneously. The LLM gets all the context it needs in one round trip.

The Delegated Exploration Pattern¶

Agent(
  prompt: "Search the codebase for all places where user sessions are created or destroyed. List each location with file:line and a one-sentence description.",
  subagent_type: "Explore"
)

Offload complex exploration to an agent. The main context stays clean.

The Verify-After-Change Pattern¶

1. Edit(file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts", old_string: "...", new_string: "...")
2. Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/test_auth.py -v", description: "Verify fix passes tests")

Edit, then immediately verify. Claude Code pairs modifications with verification to catch regressions.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

Mistake 1: Using Bash for everything. New users type “find all Python files” and Claude Code runs find . -name "*.py". The output is unformatted, permissions are checked as a generic Bash command, and PostToolUse hooks for file operations do not fire. Glob does all of this better.

Mistake 2: Editing without reading. Trying to be “efficient” by skipping the Read step. Edit will error. More importantly, editing without reading means editing based on assumptions about the file’s current state. Those assumptions are often wrong.

Mistake 3: Ignoring head_limit on Grep. Running Grep(pattern: "import") on a large codebase without thinking about result volume. The default 250 limit helps, but the real fix is narrowing your search with glob, type, or a more specific pattern.

Mistake 4: Using Write when Edit is appropriate. Write replaces the entire file. If you use it to change one line in a 500-line file, you are transmitting 499 unchanged lines through the context window. Edit sends only the changed portion.

Mistake 5: Not setting Bash timeouts for long operations. A full test suite or a Docker build can take minutes. The default 2-minute timeout will kill it. Set explicit timeouts for known long-running commands.

Summary¶

The tool system is Claude Code’s interface with reality. Every action – reading code, modifying files, running tests, searching the web – flows through the same pipeline: permission check, hooks, execution, hooks, result. The hierarchy is deliberate: specialized tools over Bash, Read before Edit, Glob over find, Grep over grep. Master this system and you control exactly what Claude Code can and cannot do.

Key takeaways: 1. Every tool call passes through the permission-hook-execute-hook pipeline – no exceptions 2. Read before Edit is an enforced invariant, not a guideline 3. Specialized tools (Read, Edit, Glob, Grep) produce better output than their Bash equivalents 4. Parallel tool execution reduces round trips – Claude Code uses it when calls are independent 5. Tool errors are information, not failures – Claude Code reads them and adjusts

Chapter 10: Bash Tool and Shell Operations¶ ~17 min read

The Bash tool is a loaded weapon. It can run any command your user account can run – install packages, delete files, push code to production, spin up servers, destroy databases. No other tool in Claude Code’s arsenal has this breadth of power or this potential for damage. That combination makes it both the most useful tool for experienced practitioners and the most dangerous for everyone else.

This chapter covers how the Bash tool actually works, how to secure it, and the patterns that separate productive shell usage from chaos.

What the Bash Tool Can Do¶

The Bash tool executes shell commands on your machine, under your user account, with your permissions. There is no sandbox. There is no container. When Claude Code runs pip install requests, it installs requests into your real environment. When it runs git push origin main, it pushes to your real repository.

Capabilities: - Run any shell command (single or chained) - Access the full filesystem - Access the network - Install and remove packages - Start and stop processes - Execute scripts in any language (Python, Node, Ruby, etc.) - Interact with Docker, Kubernetes, cloud CLIs - Run build systems, test suites, linters

Parameters:

Parameter Type Description
command string (required) The shell command to execute
description string Human-readable description (shown in permission prompt)
timeout number Max execution time in ms (default: 120,000; max: 600,000)
run_in_background boolean Run without blocking (default: false)

The Execution Model¶

Understanding how Bash commands execute is critical because it differs from your normal terminal experience in important ways.

Working Directory Persists, Shell State Does Not¶

The working directory carries over between Bash calls. If one call runs cd /tmp/project, the next call starts in /tmp/project. However, shell state – variables, aliases, functions – does not persist. Each Bash invocation is a fresh shell process.

bash
# Call 1: This works
Bash(command: "cd /Users/dev/project && ls")

# Call 2: Still in /Users/dev/project (cwd persisted)
Bash(command: "ls src/")

# But this FAILS:
# Call 1:
Bash(command: "export MY_VAR=hello")
# Call 2: MY_VAR does not exist here
Bash(command: "echo $MY_VAR")  # Empty output

Why this matters: You cannot set a shell variable in one call and use it in the next. If you need state across commands, chain them in a single call:

bash
Bash(command: "export DB_URL='postgres://localhost/mydb' && python migrate.py")

Environment Initialization¶

Each Bash call inherits your shell profile (.bashrc, .zshrc, etc.). This means: - Your PATH is available - Your shell aliases are loaded (though relying on them is fragile) - Any environment variables set in your profile exist - Tool versions match what you see in your terminal

The shell type is detected from your system – bash or zsh. If your profile sets up pyenv, nvm, or other version managers, those are available.

Command Chaining¶

You have the standard shell operators:

Operator Behavior Use when
&& Run next only if previous succeeds Commands depend on each other
; Run next regardless Independent commands, don’t care about failures
\| Pipe stdout Processing output
bash
# Dependent chain: stop if any step fails
Bash(command: "cd /project && npm install && npm run build && npm test")

# Independent: run both even if first fails
Bash(command: "ruff check src/; mypy src/")

# Pipe: process output
Bash(command: "git log --oneline -20 | head -5")

Important: Do not use newlines to separate commands in a single Bash call. Use &&, ;, or | instead. Newlines inside quoted strings (like heredocs) are fine.

Security Implications¶

The Bash tool runs commands as your user. This means:

Filesystem access: It can read, write, and delete any file your user can. There is no chroot, no filesystem restriction beyond Unix permissions.

Network access: It can make HTTP requests, SSH to remote servers, push to Git remotes, deploy to cloud services.

Package installation: pip install, npm install, cargo add – all modify your real environment. A malicious or buggy package gets installed for real.

Process control: It can start servers, kill processes, modify system services (within your user’s permissions).

The permission system is your primary guardrail. Configure it deliberately.

Permission Patterns¶

The Bash tool’s permission rules use prefix matching with wildcard support. A rule like Bash(git status *) means “allow any Bash command that starts with git status.”

Safe Commands to Allow¶

These commands are read-only or low-risk. Allow them to avoid constant permission prompts:

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(git status *)",
      "Bash(git diff *)",
      "Bash(git log *)",
      "Bash(git branch *)",
      "Bash(git stash list *)",
      "Bash(python -m pytest *)",
      "Bash(npm test *)",
      "Bash(cargo test *)",
      "Bash(ruff *)",
      "Bash(prettier *)",
      "Bash(eslint *)",
      "Bash(mypy *)",
      "Bash(pyright *)",
      "Bash(gh *)",
      "Bash(ls *)",
      "Bash(wc *)",
      "Bash(which *)",
      "Bash(cat *)"
    ]
  }
}

Commands to Deny¶

Block patterns that could cause irreversible damage:

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "deny": [
      "Bash(rm -rf /)",
      "Bash(rm -rf ~)",
      "Bash(rm -rf .)",
      "Bash(*DROP TABLE*)",
      "Bash(*DROP DATABASE*)",
      "Bash(*--force*main*)",
      "Bash(*--force*master*)",
      "Bash(:(){ :|:& };:)",
      "Bash(chmod -R 777 *)",
      "Bash(*> /dev/sda*)",
      "Bash(mkfs*)"
    ]
  }
}

The Permissive Local Override¶

For experienced users who trust Claude Code and want zero friction:

json
// .claude/settings.local.json (gitignored, personal only)
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": ["Bash(*)"]
  }
}

This allows all Bash commands without prompting. Use this only if you have PreToolUse hooks that catch dangerous patterns. The block-destructive.sh hook (covered in Chapter 8) is your safety net here.

Permission Evaluation with Hooks¶

Even with Bash(*) allowed, your PreToolUse hooks still fire. This is the layered security model:

Bash command → Permission check (allow) → PreToolUse hook → Execute
                                           ↑
                                    block-destructive.sh
                                    can still block rm -rf

This is why the recommended setup for power users is Bash(*) in local settings plus block-destructive.sh as a PreToolUse hook. You get zero friction for legitimate commands and a safety net for dangerous ones.

Git Operations via Bash¶

Git is probably the most common category of Bash commands Claude Code runs. Understanding the risk levels helps you configure permissions appropriately.

Safe (Read-Only)¶

These commands never modify your repository:

bash
git status
git diff
git diff --staged
git log --oneline -20
git branch -a
git stash list
git show HEAD
git blame src/auth.ts
git remote -v

Allow all of these freely.

Needs Care (Modifies Local State)¶

These commands change your local repository but are reversible:

bash
git add src/auth.ts          # Stages files
git commit -m "fix: auth"    # Creates a commit (local only)
git checkout -b feat/new     # Creates a branch
git stash                    # Stashes changes
git stash pop                # Restores stashed changes
git merge feature-branch     # Merges locally

Most users allow these. They modify local state, but nothing leaves your machine until you push.

Dangerous (Modifies Remote or Destroys History)¶

These commands can cause permanent damage:

bash
git push origin main         # Pushes to remote (usually fine, but irreversible)
git push --force             # Rewrites remote history
git reset --hard HEAD~3      # Destroys local commits
git clean -fd                # Deletes untracked files
git checkout -- .            # Discards all unstaged changes
git branch -D feature        # Force-deletes a branch

Claude Code’s git safety protocol: - Never uses --force on push without explicit user request - Prefers new commits over amending existing ones - Never skips hooks (--no-verify) - Warns before pushing to main/master - After a failed pre-commit hook, creates a NEW commit (not --amend, which would modify the previous commit)

This protocol is baked into Claude Code’s system instructions. It applies even if you have Bash(*) allowed.

Example: The Safe Git Workflow¶

bash
# Claude Code's typical git workflow:
Bash(command: "git status", description: "Check working tree status")
Bash(command: "git diff", description: "Review changes")
Bash(command: "git add src/auth.ts tests/test_auth.py", description: "Stage specific files")
Bash(command: "git commit -m 'fix: validate token expiry before auth check'", description: "Commit fix")
Bash(command: "git push -u origin fix/token-expiry", description: "Push to feature branch")

Notice: specific files added (not git add .), descriptive commit message, push to a feature branch (not main).

Package Management¶

Claude Code uses Bash for package operations. Each has permission implications.

Python (pip / uv)¶

bash
# Install a package
Bash(command: "pip install requests", description: "Install requests library")

# Install from requirements
Bash(command: "pip install -r requirements.txt", description: "Install project dependencies")

# Using uv (faster)
Bash(command: "uv pip install fastapi", description: "Install FastAPI with uv")

Risk: Packages execute code during installation. A typosquatted package name could run malicious code. Claude Code cannot verify package authenticity – that responsibility is on you.

Node.js (npm / yarn / pnpm)¶

bash
# Install dependencies
Bash(command: "npm install", description: "Install project dependencies")

# Add a specific package
Bash(command: "npm install --save zod", description: "Add zod for schema validation")

# Audit for vulnerabilities
Bash(command: "npm audit", description: "Check for known vulnerabilities")

Rust (cargo)¶

bash
Bash(command: "cargo add serde --features derive", description: "Add serde with derive feature")

Process Management¶

Background Processes¶

The run_in_background parameter lets Claude Code start long-running processes without blocking:

bash
# Start a dev server in the background
Bash(
  command: "npm run dev",
  run_in_background: true,
  description: "Start development server"
)

# Start a database
Bash(
  command: "docker compose up -d postgres",
  run_in_background: true,
  description: "Start PostgreSQL container"
)

When a background process completes (or fails), Claude Code receives a notification. No polling or sleep loops needed.

Reading Background Output¶

After a background process finishes, Claude Code can read its output. For processes that are still running, it can check on them:

bash
# Check if the server is up
Bash(command: "curl -s http://localhost:3000/health", description: "Health check dev server")

Killing Processes¶

bash
# Stop a specific process
Bash(command: "kill $(lsof -t -i:3000)", description: "Kill process on port 3000")

# Stop Docker containers
Bash(command: "docker compose down", description: "Stop all containers")

Testing via Bash¶

Running tests is one of the most frequent Bash operations. Claude Code uses it to verify its changes work.

Running Specific Tests (Fast Feedback)¶

bash
# Python: single test file
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/test_auth.py -v", description: "Run auth tests")

# Python: single test function
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/test_auth.py::test_login_invalid_password -v", description: "Run specific test")

# JavaScript: single test file
Bash(command: "npx jest tests/auth.test.ts", description: "Run auth tests")

# Rust: single test
Bash(command: "cargo test test_auth_flow -- --nocapture", description: "Run auth flow test")

Running Full Test Suites (Before Commits)¶

Full suites take longer. Set appropriate timeouts:

bash
# Python full suite
Bash(
  command: "python -m pytest tests/ -v --tb=short",
  timeout: 300000,
  description: "Run full test suite (up to 5 min)"
)

# Node full suite
Bash(
  command: "npm test",
  timeout: 300000,
  description: "Run full test suite"
)

Capturing Test Output¶

Claude Code reads the full test output. When tests fail, it sees the assertion errors, stack traces, and failure messages. This is how it identifies what to fix:

FAILED tests/test_auth.py::test_login_expired_token
  AssertionError: Expected 401, got 200

  # Claude Code sees this and knows:
  # 1. The test expects a 401 for expired tokens
  # 2. The code is returning 200 instead
  # 3. The token expiry check is likely missing or broken

Build Systems¶

bash
# Node/TypeScript build
Bash(command: "npm run build", timeout: 180000, description: "Build project")

# Rust build
Bash(command: "cargo build --release", timeout: 300000, description: "Release build")

# Python package build
Bash(command: "python -m build", description: "Build Python package")

# Make
Bash(command: "make all", timeout: 300000, description: "Build everything")

# Docker build
Bash(
  command: "docker build -t myapp:latest .",
  timeout: 600000,
  description: "Build Docker image"
)

Build commands are the primary use case for extended timeouts. A Docker build or a Rust release compilation can take several minutes.

Output Handling¶

Understanding how Claude Code processes Bash output is critical for writing effective commands.

stdout and stderr¶

Bash captures both stdout and stderr. Both are returned to Claude Code as the tool result. This means Claude Code sees error messages, warnings, and diagnostic output – not just the “happy path.”

bash
# Claude Code sees both the lint warnings (stderr) and results (stdout)
Bash(command: "ruff check src/ 2>&1")

In most cases, you do not need to redirect stderr. Let it flow naturally – Claude Code uses error messages to diagnose and correct issues.

Output Truncation¶

When a command produces output that exceeds Claude Code’s result size limit, the output is truncated. Truncation keeps the beginning and end, dropping the middle. This preserves: - The first few hundred lines (usually the start of output or header) - The last few hundred lines (usually summary, totals, final errors)

For commands that produce large output, filter before it reaches Claude Code:

bash
# BAD: Returns ALL test output, likely truncated
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/ -v")

# BETTER: Returns only failures
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/ -v --tb=short -q")

# BEST for large suites: Return summary only, with failure details
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/ --tb=line -q")

Exit Codes¶

Claude Code checks the exit code of every Bash command. Non-zero exit codes mark the result as an error. This triggers Claude Code’s error-handling behavior: it reads the error, forms a hypothesis about what went wrong, and adjusts its approach.

bash
# Exit code 0 → success → Claude Code proceeds
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/test_auth.py -v")

# Exit code 1 → test failure → Claude Code reads the failure output and investigates
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/test_auth.py -v")
# Output: "FAILED test_login_expired_token - AssertionError..."
# Claude Code: "The test expects 401 for expired tokens but gets 200. Let me check the expiry logic..."

Commands that “fail successfully” (non-zero exit but expected behavior) can confuse Claude Code. Use || true to suppress expected failures:

bash
# grep returns exit code 1 when no matches found -- not really an error
Bash(command: "grep -r 'DEPRECATED' src/ || true")

Binary and Large File Output¶

Some commands produce binary output (images, compiled files). Claude Code cannot meaningfully process binary data. If you need to verify a binary was created, check existence and size:

bash
# Check that the build produced the expected binary
Bash(command: "ls -lh dist/app && file dist/app")

Sandboxing and Safety Modes¶

Claude Code can operate with different safety levels depending on the environment.

The Seatbelt Model¶

In standard operation, the Bash tool has no sandbox. But Claude Code’s safety comes from layers:

Layer 1: Claude Code's built-in safety (refuses destructive commands by default)
Layer 2: Permission system (deny/allow rules in settings.json)
Layer 3: PreToolUse hooks (block-destructive.sh pattern matching)
Layer 4: OS-level permissions (your user account's Unix permissions)

Each layer catches what the previous layer missed. A well-configured setup has all four layers active.

CI/CD Environments¶

When Claude Code runs in CI (covered in detail in Chapter 20), the Bash tool operates differently: - No interactive permission prompts (everything must be pre-allowed or pre-denied) - Typically runs in a container with limited filesystem and network access - Environment variables are set by the CI system - Timeouts may need adjustment for container startup overhead

Audit Trail¶

Every Bash command Claude Code runs is logged in the session. You can review what commands were executed using /export to save the full session transcript. This is valuable for: - Post-incident review (“What did Claude Code run before the failure?”) - Compliance documentation - Debugging unexpected behavior - Learning what commands Claude Code chose for different tasks

Linting and Code Quality via Bash¶

Linting is a frequent Bash use case because lint tools are command-line programs with no dedicated Claude Code tool.

Python¶

bash
# Check for issues
Bash(command: "ruff check src/auth/", description: "Lint Python code")

# Auto-fix issues
Bash(command: "ruff check --fix src/auth/", description: "Auto-fix lint issues")

# Format code
Bash(command: "ruff format src/auth/", description: "Format Python code")

# Type checking
Bash(command: "mypy src/auth/ --strict", description: "Type check auth module")
Bash(command: "pyright src/auth/", description: "Type check with pyright")

JavaScript/TypeScript¶

bash
# ESLint
Bash(command: "npx eslint src/ --fix", description: "Lint and auto-fix JS/TS")

# Prettier
Bash(command: "npx prettier --write 'src/**/*.{ts,tsx}'", description: "Format TypeScript files")

# TypeScript compiler check
Bash(command: "npx tsc --noEmit", description: "Type check TypeScript")

Rust¶

bash
# Clippy (Rust linter)
Bash(command: "cargo clippy -- -D warnings", description: "Lint Rust code")

# Format
Bash(command: "cargo fmt --check", description: "Check Rust formatting")

Combined Quality Check¶

A pattern Claude Code uses before committing:

bash
# Run all quality checks in sequence
Bash(
  command: "ruff check src/ && ruff format --check src/ && mypy src/ --strict && python -m pytest tests/ -q",
  timeout: 180000,
  description: "Full quality check: lint, format, types, tests"
)

If any step fails, && stops the chain and Claude Code sees exactly which step failed.

Advanced Patterns¶

Heredocs for Multi-line Input¶

When Claude Code needs to pass multi-line content to a command, it uses heredocs:

bash
Bash(command: "cat > /tmp/test-config.json << 'EOF'\n{\n  \"database\": \"postgres://localhost/test\",\n  \"port\": 8080,\n  \"debug\": true\n}\nEOF")

This is commonly used for: - Creating temporary configuration files - Writing inline scripts - Passing multi-line SQL queries - Git commit messages with body text

Environment Variables Within a Single Call¶

Since environment variables do not persist across calls, set them inline:

bash
# Set and use in same call
Bash(command: "DATABASE_URL='postgres://localhost/mydb' python -m pytest tests/integration/")

# Multiple variables
Bash(command: "NODE_ENV=production API_KEY=test123 npm run build")

Working with Docker¶

bash
# Run a command in a container
Bash(command: "docker exec -it myapp-db psql -U postgres -c 'SELECT count(*) FROM users;'")

# Compose operations
Bash(command: "docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up -d")

# Build and run
