Bash(command: "docker build -t myapp . && docker run --rm -p 8080:8080 myapp")

Chaining with Error Handling¶

bash
# Try to run tests, show coverage regardless
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/ -v; python -m pytest --co -q | wc -l")

# Build, and if it fails, show the error log
Bash(command: "npm run build 2>&1 || cat build.log")

Using jq for JSON Processing¶

bash
# Parse API response
Bash(command: "curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/owner/repo | jq '.stargazers_count'")

# Extract from package.json
Bash(command: "jq '.dependencies | keys' package.json")

Network Operations and API Calls¶

Claude Code uses Bash for HTTP requests when WebFetch is not suitable (authenticated APIs, POST requests, custom headers):

bash
# POST request with JSON body
Bash(command: "curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/users -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{\"name\": \"Alice\", \"email\": \"alice@test.com\"}'")

# Authenticated request
Bash(command: "curl -s -H 'Authorization: Bearer ${API_TOKEN}' https://api.example.com/data")

# Download a file
Bash(command: "curl -sL -o /tmp/schema.json https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema")

# Check SSL certificate expiry
Bash(command: "echo | openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates")

Cloud CLI Operations¶

Claude Code frequently interacts with cloud services:

bash
# AWS
Bash(command: "aws s3 ls s3://my-bucket/data/ --region us-east-1")
Bash(command: "aws ecs describe-services --cluster prod --services api-service | jq '.services[0].runningCount'")

# Azure
Bash(command: "az webapp list --resource-group myapp-rg --output table")
Bash(command: "az keyvault secret show --vault-name myapp-vault --name db-password --query value -o tsv")

# GCP
Bash(command: "gcloud run services list --project my-project --format='table(name,status,url)'")

# GitHub CLI (the most common cloud CLI in Claude Code sessions)
Bash(command: "gh pr list --state open --label 'needs-review'")
Bash(command: "gh issue view 42")
Bash(command: "gh api repos/owner/repo/pulls/123/reviews")

The gh CLI deserves special mention. Claude Code uses it extensively for GitHub operations because it is authenticated (via gh auth login), handles pagination, and provides structured output. As discussed in Chapter 9, use gh instead of WebFetch for any GitHub URL.

Database Operations¶

bash
# PostgreSQL
Bash(command: "psql -h localhost -U postgres -d myapp -c 'SELECT count(*) FROM users WHERE created_at > now() - interval \\'7 days\\';'")

# SQLite
Bash(command: "sqlite3 /project/data/app.db '.schema users'")
Bash(command: "sqlite3 /project/data/app.db 'SELECT * FROM sessions ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 5;'")

# Redis
Bash(command: "redis-cli KEYS 'session:*' | wc -l")

What NOT to Do with Bash¶

The Bash tool should be your last resort for tasks that have dedicated tools. Here is the rule: if a specialized tool exists, use it.

Do NOT do this Do this instead Why
Bash(command: "cat src/auth.ts") Read(file_path: "src/auth.ts") Read provides line numbers, handles binary/image files, respects read permissions
Bash(command: "grep -r 'TODO' .") Grep(pattern: "TODO") Grep tool uses ripgrep, respects gitignore, has structured output modes
Bash(command: "find . -name '*.ts'") Glob(pattern: "**/*.ts") Glob is faster, sorted by mtime, optimized output
Bash(command: "sed -i 's/old/new/' file.py") Edit(old_string: "old", new_string: "new") Edit is atomic, has read-first safety, triggers PostToolUse hooks
Bash(command: "echo 'content' > new.py") Write(file_path: "new.py", content: "content") Write handles encoding, triggers hooks, tracked in tool history

Why this matters beyond aesthetics:

  1. Permission granularity. You can allow Read(*) and deny Read(./.env). You cannot do that with Bash(cat *) vs Bash(cat .env) because both match Bash(cat *).

  2. Hook integration. PostToolUse hooks fire based on tool name. auto-lint-python.sh triggers on Edit/Write of .py files. It cannot trigger on Bash(sed ...).

  3. Output formatting. Dedicated tools produce output optimized for Claude Code’s context window. Bash output is raw text that may need parsing.

  4. Error handling. Edit’s error “old_string not found” is unambiguous. sed silently does nothing if the pattern does not match.

10 Practical Bash Patterns¶

These are the patterns that come up in real Claude Code sessions.

1. Check Before Acting¶

bash
Bash(command: "test -f package-lock.json && echo 'npm' || (test -f yarn.lock && echo 'yarn' || echo 'unknown')")

Detect the package manager before running install commands.

2. Single Test with Verbose Output¶

bash
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/test_auth.py::test_login_expired_token -v --tb=long")

Run one test with full traceback. The fastest feedback loop for bug fixes.

3. Search and Count¶

bash
Bash(command: "git log --oneline --since='1 week ago' | wc -l")

Count commits in the last week. Useful for changelog generation.

4. Safe Branch Cleanup¶

bash
Bash(command: "git branch --merged main | grep -v main | grep -v '\\*'")

List branches that have been merged into main. Never auto-delete – just list.

5. Port Check¶

bash
Bash(command: "lsof -i :3000 -t 2>/dev/null || echo 'Port 3000 is free'")

Check if a port is in use before starting a server.

6. Quick HTTP Check¶

bash
Bash(command: "curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:8080/health")

Get just the HTTP status code. Clean for health checks.

7. Disk Usage for Project¶

bash
Bash(command: "du -sh node_modules/ .git/ dist/ 2>/dev/null")

See where disk space is going.

8. Find Recently Modified Files¶

bash
Bash(command: "find . -name '*.py' -mmin -60 -not -path './venv/*' | head -20")

Files modified in the last hour. Useful for understanding what was just changed. (Note: for finding files by name pattern in general, use Glob.)

9. Inline Python for Data Processing¶

bash
Bash(command: "python3 -c \"import json; data = json.load(open('config.json')); print(json.dumps(data, indent=2))\"")

Quick Python one-liners for data processing tasks that would be awkward in pure shell.

10. Git Commit with Heredoc Body¶

bash
Bash(command: "git commit -m \"$(cat <<'EOF'\nfix: validate token expiry before auth check\n\nThe authentication middleware was accepting expired tokens because\nthe expiry check happened after the signature verification.\nThis fix moves the expiry check to run first.\n\nCo-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>\nEOF\n)\"")

Multi-line commit messages with the heredoc pattern. Claude Code uses this for commits with body text.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

Mistake 1: Sleep loops for polling.

bash
# BAD: Wastes time and tokens
Bash(command: "sleep 10 && curl http://localhost:3000/health")
Bash(command: "sleep 10 && curl http://localhost:3000/health")
Bash(command: "sleep 10 && curl http://localhost:3000/health")

Use run_in_background for the server start, then check when notified. Or use a single check command rather than sleeping first.

Mistake 2: Using interactive flags.

bash
# FAILS: No interactive input available
Bash(command: "git rebase -i HEAD~3")
Bash(command: "git add -i")
Bash(command: "python3")  # Opens interactive REPL

Claude Code cannot interact with prompts. Use non-interactive equivalents or pass input via pipes/heredocs.

Mistake 3: Ignoring timeouts on long builds.

bash
# FAILS after 2 minutes
Bash(command: "cargo build --release")

# WORKS
Bash(command: "cargo build --release", timeout: 600000)

Know your build times and set timeouts accordingly.

Mistake 4: cd without chaining.

bash
# Does nothing useful by itself
Bash(command: "cd /Users/dev/other-project")

# Better: cd and run in one call
Bash(command: "cd /Users/dev/other-project && cargo test")

# Best: use absolute paths
Bash(command: "cargo test --manifest-path /Users/dev/other-project/Cargo.toml")

While cd does persist the working directory, it is cleaner to use absolute paths or chain commands.

Mistake 5: Redirecting stderr to /dev/null unconditionally.

bash
# BAD: Hides errors that Claude Code should see
Bash(command: "npm run build 2>/dev/null")

# GOOD: Let errors flow through
Bash(command: "npm run build")

Claude Code uses error output to diagnose and fix issues. Silencing stderr removes that signal.

Summary¶

The Bash tool is unrestricted power over your machine. It runs as your user, accesses your filesystem, and talks to your network. The permission system and hooks are your guardrails. Configure allow lists for safe commands, deny patterns for destructive ones, and use block-destructive.sh as the catch-all safety net. Prefer dedicated tools (Read, Edit, Grep, Glob) for tasks they handle better. Reserve Bash for git operations, test execution, builds, package management, and the command-line operations that no other tool covers.

Key takeaways: 1. Working directory persists between calls, but shell state (variables, functions) does not 2. Bash runs as your user with full system access – permissions and hooks are your only guardrails 3. Use dedicated tools instead of Bash for file reading, searching, and editing 4. Set explicit timeouts for builds and test suites that exceed 2 minutes 5. Use run_in_background for long-running processes instead of sleep loops

Chapter 11: File Operations at Scale¶ ~17 min read

You inherit a codebase with 3,000 files. A library you depend on renamed its main export. You need to find every import, update each one, verify nothing broke, and commit the result. This is not a hypothetical – it is Tuesday.

Claude Code’s file operation tools – Read, Edit, Write, Glob, and Grep – are purpose-built for this kind of work. Each tool does one thing well, and together they handle operations ranging from reading a single file to refactoring across an entire monorepo. This chapter covers each tool in depth, the patterns that make them powerful at scale, and the recipes you will reach for repeatedly.

The File Operation Toolkit¶

Five tools, five jobs:

Tool Job Analogy
Read Load file contents into context Opening a file in your editor
Edit Change specific text in a file Find-and-replace
Write Create a file or rewrite one completely Save As / overwrite
Glob Find files by name pattern File explorer search
Grep Find files by content IDE “Find in Files”

These tools share a design philosophy: be specialized, be fast, produce output optimized for Claude Code’s context window. They are not wrappers around shell commands. They are first-class tools with their own permission handling, hook integration, and error reporting.

Read: Loading Files Into Context¶

Read is the most-called tool in any Claude Code session. It loads file contents, line-numbered, into the conversation context where Claude Code can analyze them.

What Read Handles¶

Read is not limited to source code:

File Type Behavior
Source code (any language) Returns line-numbered text
Plain text, config files Returns line-numbered text
Images (PNG, JPG, GIF, etc.) Renders visually (Claude is multimodal)
PDFs Extracts text, requires pages param for >10 pages
Jupyter notebooks (.ipynb) Returns all cells with outputs
Binary files Returns error or raw representation

Output Format¶

Read returns content in cat -n format:

1   import express from 'express';
2   import { authenticate } from './auth';
3   
4   const app = express();
5   
6   app.post('/login', async (req, res) => {
7     const { email, password } = req.body;
8     const result = await authenticate(email, password);
9     res.json(result);
10  });

The format is: line_number + tab character + file content. This is important for Edit operations – the indentation you see after the tab is the exact indentation in the file.

Partial File Reading¶

For large files, use offset and limit to read specific sections:

Read(file_path: "/project/src/database.ts", offset: 200, limit: 50)

This reads lines 200-249. Use this when: - A file exceeds 2,000 lines (Read’s default limit) - You only need a specific function or section - You want to conserve context window tokens

Default behavior: Without offset and limit, Read returns up to 2,000 lines from the beginning. Files shorter than 2,000 lines are read completely.

Reading Images¶

Claude Code is multimodal. When you Read an image file, Claude sees the actual image:

Read(file_path: "/project/docs/architecture-diagram.png")

Claude Code can then describe the diagram, identify components, spot inconsistencies with the code, or suggest updates. This works with screenshots, mockups, diagrams, and any standard image format.

Reading PDFs¶

PDFs require the pages parameter for documents over 10 pages:

# Short PDF (10 pages or fewer) -- no pages param needed
Read(file_path: "/project/docs/api-spec.pdf")

# Long PDF -- specify page range (max 20 pages per call)
Read(file_path: "/project/docs/full-spec.pdf", pages: "1-10")
Read(file_path: "/project/docs/full-spec.pdf", pages: "11-20")

Reading Jupyter Notebooks¶

Notebooks return all cells with their types, source code, and outputs:

Read(file_path: "/project/analysis/data-exploration.ipynb")

The output includes: - Cell type (code or markdown) - Cell source code - Cell outputs (text, tables, error traces) - Cell execution order

The Read-Before-Edit Invariant¶

This is the most important rule about Read: you must Read a file before you can Edit it. The Edit tool enforces this and will return an error if you try to edit a file that has not been read in the current session.

Why? Because editing without reading means editing based on assumptions. Maybe the file changed since you last looked at it. Maybe it has a different structure than you expected. Maybe the line you want to change was already changed by someone else. Reading first eliminates these failure modes.

Pattern you will see constantly:

1. Read(file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts")       # Load the file
2. [Claude Code analyzes the content]              # Understand it
3. Edit(file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts", ...)   # Modify it

This three-step sequence – read, understand, edit – is the fundamental building block of all file modification workflows.

Edit: Precision Modifications¶

Edit replaces exact strings in files. It is the workhorse of code modification because it transmits only the change, not the entire file.

How Matching Works¶

Edit performs an exact string match on old_string. “Exact” means character-for-character identical, including: - Whitespace (spaces, tabs) - Indentation - Line endings - Blank lines

If the exact string appears once in the file, the replacement succeeds. If it appears zero times, Edit errors with “old_string not found.” If it appears more than once and replace_all is false, Edit errors with a message about multiple matches.

Making old_string Unique¶

The most common Edit failure is a non-unique old_string. The fix is always the same: include more surrounding context.

Non-unique (likely fails):

Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/utils.ts",
  old_string: "return null;",
  new_string: "return undefined;"
)

The string return null; probably appears in multiple functions.

Unique (succeeds):

Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/utils.ts",
  old_string: "function findUser(id: string): User | null {\n  const user = cache.get(id);\n  if (!user) {\n    return null;\n  }",
  new_string: "function findUser(id: string): User | undefined {\n  const user = cache.get(id);\n  if (!user) {\n    return undefined;\n  }"
)

By including the function signature and surrounding lines, the match becomes unique.

Preserving Indentation¶

The most subtle Edit mistake is wrong indentation. When Read shows you:

15      if (!user) {
16        return null;
17      }

The actual file content starts after the tab following the line number. Line 16’s content is return null; (six spaces of indentation). Your old_string must include those six spaces exactly.

Wrong (will fail):

Edit(old_string: "return null;", new_string: "return undefined;")

Right:

Edit(old_string: "      return null;", new_string: "      return undefined;")

Or, better, include surrounding lines for uniqueness and let the indentation match naturally:

Edit(
  old_string: "    if (!user) {\n      return null;\n    }",
  new_string: "    if (!user) {\n      return undefined;\n    }"
)

replace_all for Bulk Changes¶

When you intentionally want to change every occurrence, use replace_all: true:

Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts",
  old_string: "UserName",
  new_string: "UserId",
  replace_all: true
)

This renames every UserName to UserId in the file. Use this for variable renames, import path updates, and consistent terminology changes.

Caution: replace_all is literal. If you rename User to Account, it will also change UserService to AccountService and getUser to getAccount. Be specific enough to avoid unintended replacements.

Multi-Part Edits¶

To make multiple distinct changes to a file, use separate Edit calls:

# Edit 1: Fix the import
Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts",
  old_string: "import { hash } from 'crypto';",
  new_string: "import { hash, compare } from 'crypto';"
)

# Edit 2: Fix the function (later in the same file)
Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts",
  old_string: "if (password === storedHash) {",
  new_string: "if (await compare(password, storedHash)) {"
)

These execute sequentially because the second edit depends on the file state after the first. Claude Code handles this correctly – each Edit updates the file, and subsequent Edits operate on the updated content.

Edit vs. Write: When to Choose Which¶

Scenario Tool Reason
Change 1-5 specific sections Edit Sends only the diffs, saves tokens
Change > 60% of the file Write Full rewrite is simpler than many Edits
Create a new file Write Edit cannot create files
Rename a variable across one file Edit with replace_all Surgical, handles multiple occurrences
Generate a new config file Write No existing content to edit
Fix a single bug Edit Minimal change, easy to review

Write: Creating and Rewriting Files¶

Write creates new files or overwrites existing ones completely.

Creating New Files¶

Write(
  file_path: "/project/tests/test_auth.py",
  content: "import pytest\nfrom src.auth import authenticate, AuthError\n\n\nclass TestAuthenticate:\n    def test_valid_credentials(self, mock_db):\n        result = authenticate('user@test.com', 'correct-pass')\n        assert result.success is True\n        assert result.user_id is not None\n\n    def test_invalid_password(self, mock_db):\n        with pytest.raises(AuthError, match='invalid credentials'):\n            authenticate('user@test.com', 'wrong-pass')\n\n    def test_nonexistent_user(self, mock_db):\n        with pytest.raises(AuthError, match='user not found'):\n            authenticate('nobody@test.com', 'any-pass')\n"
)

Rewriting Existing Files¶

When a file needs fundamental restructuring – not just line-level changes but a complete rewrite of its structure – Write is cleaner than multiple Edits:

# Read the file first (required for existing files)
Read(file_path: "/project/config/database.yml")

# Rewrite with new structure
Write(
  file_path: "/project/config/database.yml",
  content: "# Database Configuration\n\ndevelopment:\n  adapter: postgresql\n  host: localhost\n  port: 5432\n  database: myapp_dev\n  pool: 5\n\ntest:\n  adapter: postgresql\n  host: localhost\n  port: 5432\n  database: myapp_test\n  pool: 2\n\nproduction:\n  adapter: postgresql\n  host: ${DATABASE_HOST}\n  port: ${DATABASE_PORT}\n  database: ${DATABASE_NAME}\n  pool: ${DATABASE_POOL}\n  ssl: true\n"
)

When Write Is the Wrong Choice¶

  • Small changes to large files. Rewriting a 500-line file to change 3 lines wastes tokens and makes the change harder to review. Use Edit.
  • Files you have not read. Write errors if the file exists and you have not Read it. This prevents accidental overwrites.
  • Documentation files. Unless the user explicitly asks, Claude Code avoids creating .md or README files.

Glob: Finding Files by Pattern¶

Glob searches for files by name pattern. It is fast at any scale and returns results sorted by modification time (most recent first).

Pattern Syntax¶

Pattern Matches Example
* Any characters in one directory level *.ts matches app.ts but not src/app.ts
** Any characters across all directory levels **/*.ts matches src/app.ts, src/lib/utils.ts
? One character test?.py matches test1.py, testA.py
{a,b} Either a or b *.{ts,tsx} matches .ts and .tsx files
[abc] One of the listed characters test[123].py matches test1.py

Common Glob Patterns¶

# All TypeScript files in a project
Glob(pattern: "**/*.ts", path: "/project")

# Test files only
Glob(pattern: "**/*.test.{ts,tsx,js,jsx}", path: "/project")

# Config files at project root
Glob(pattern: "*.{json,yml,yaml,toml}", path: "/project")

# React components in a specific directory
Glob(pattern: "src/components/**/*.tsx", path: "/project")

# Python files excluding virtual environment
Glob(pattern: "**/*.py", path: "/project/src")

# All files in a specific directory (not recursive)
Glob(pattern: "src/auth/*", path: "/project")

# Migration files (numbered)
Glob(pattern: "**/migrations/*.sql", path: "/project")

Glob’s Sort Order¶

Results come back sorted by modification time, most recent first. This is useful when you want to find the most recently changed files:

# What was edited recently?
Glob(pattern: "src/**/*.py", path: "/project")
# First result = most recently modified Python file

Glob vs. ls vs. find¶

Task Best tool Why
Find files by name pattern Glob Optimized output, sorted by mtime
List one directory’s contents Bash(ls …) Simpler for a flat listing
Find files by complex criteria (size, permissions) Bash(find …) Glob cannot filter by metadata
Find files by content Grep Glob only matches file names

Grep: Searching File Contents¶

Grep is built on ripgrep, the fastest file content searcher available. It searches your codebase, respects .gitignore, handles Unicode, and produces structured output.

Three Output Modes¶

files_with_matches (default)¶

Returns just the file paths that contain the pattern:

Grep(pattern: "authenticateUser", type: "ts")

Output:

/project/src/auth/login.ts
/project/src/middleware/auth.ts
/project/tests/test_auth.ts

Use this when you want to know which files to investigate.

content¶

Returns matching lines with optional context:

Grep(
  pattern: "authenticateUser",
  type: "ts",
  output_mode: "content",
  -C: 3
)

Output:

/project/src/auth/login.ts
10-
11-export async function authenticateUser(
12:  email: string,
13:  password: string
14-): Promise<AuthResult> {
15-  const user = await findByEmail(email);

Use this when you need to see the actual code around matches.

count¶

Returns match counts per file:

Grep(pattern: "TODO", output_mode: "count")

Output:

/project/src/auth/login.ts:3
/project/src/api/routes.ts:7
/project/tests/fixtures.ts:1

Use this for measuring technical debt, usage frequency, or deciding which files to prioritize.

Filtering Results¶

Grep offers two filtering mechanisms:

By file pattern (glob):

Grep(pattern: "import.*React", glob: "*.tsx")

By file type:

Grep(pattern: "import.*React", type: "tsx")

The type parameter uses ripgrep’s built-in type definitions, which handle associated extensions automatically. type: "py" matches .py and .pyi files. type: "ts" matches .ts and .tsx.

Regex Patterns¶

Grep supports full regex syntax. Some common patterns:

# Function definitions
Grep(pattern: "^(export )?(async )?function \\w+", type: "ts", output_mode: "content")

# Class definitions
Grep(pattern: "^class \\w+", type: "py", output_mode: "content")

# Import statements for a specific module
Grep(pattern: "from ['\"]express['\"]", type: "ts")

# TODO with assignee
Grep(pattern: "TODO\\(\\w+\\):", output_mode: "content")

# Environment variable usage
Grep(pattern: "process\\.env\\.", type: "ts", output_mode: "content")

# SQL injection risk (string concatenation in queries)
Grep(pattern: "query.*\\+.*\\$|query.*\\+.*req\\.", type: "ts", output_mode: "content")

Case-Insensitive Search¶

Grep(pattern: "authentication", -i: true, type: "ts")

Matches Authentication, AUTHENTICATION, authentication, and any other casing.

Multiline Patterns¶

By default, patterns match within single lines. For patterns that span lines, enable multiline mode:

Grep(
  pattern: "interface User \\{[\\s\\S]*?email:",
  type: "ts",
  multiline: true,
  output_mode: "content"
)

This finds interface User { blocks that contain an email: field, even if they span many lines.

Controlling Result Volume¶

head_limit (default: 250) caps the number of results:

# Only show first 10 matches
Grep(pattern: "console\\.log", type: "ts", head_limit: 10, output_mode: "content")

offset for pagination:

# Skip first 250, show next 250
Grep(pattern: "import", type: "py", offset: 250, head_limit: 250)

Why head_limit matters: A broad pattern on a large codebase can return thousands of matches. Without head_limit, all those matches go into the context window, consuming tokens and potentially pushing out important earlier context.

Common Grep Mistakes¶

Forgetting to escape regex metacharacters:

# WRONG: Trying to find "Array<string>"
Grep(pattern: "Array<string>")

# RIGHT: Escape the angle brackets
Grep(pattern: "Array<string>")
# Actually, < and > are not regex metacharacters in ripgrep, so this works.
# But for braces:
# WRONG:
Grep(pattern: "interface{}")
# RIGHT:
Grep(pattern: "interface\\{\\}")

Using Grep when Glob is appropriate:

# WRONG: Using Grep to find files named "config"
Grep(pattern: "config", output_mode: "files_with_matches")
# This finds files CONTAINING "config", not files NAMED "config"

# RIGHT:
Glob(pattern: "**/config.*")

Not using type/glob filters:

# SLOW: Searches everything including node_modules, .git, binaries
Grep(pattern: "handleAuth")

# FAST: Searches only TypeScript files, skips gitignored directories
Grep(pattern: "handleAuth", type: "ts")

NotebookEdit: Modifying Jupyter Notebooks¶

Jupyter notebooks are structured JSON files containing an array of cells. While you can Read a notebook to see all cells, modifying individual cells requires the NotebookEdit tool.

Cell Operations¶

NotebookEdit supports three operations:

Operation edit_mode What It Does
Replace replace Replace a cell’s content
Insert insert Add a new cell after a specified cell
Delete delete Remove a cell

Replacing a Cell¶

# First, Read the notebook to see cell IDs and content
Read(file_path: "/project/analysis/exploration.ipynb")

# Replace a code cell's content
NotebookEdit(
  notebook_path: "/project/analysis/exploration.ipynb",
  cell_id: "abc123",
  new_source: "import pandas as pd\nimport matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n\ndf = pd.read_csv('data/sales.csv')\ndf.describe()"
)

Inserting a New Cell¶

# Insert a markdown cell after an existing cell
NotebookEdit(
  notebook_path: "/project/analysis/exploration.ipynb",
  cell_id: "abc123",
  edit_mode: "insert",
  cell_type: "markdown",
  new_source: "## Analysis Results\n\nThe following chart shows the distribution of sales by region."
)

Deleting a Cell¶

NotebookEdit(
  notebook_path: "/project/analysis/exploration.ipynb",
  cell_id: "def456",
  edit_mode: "delete",
  new_source: ""
)

When to Use NotebookEdit vs. Write¶

Use NotebookEdit when modifying individual cells in an existing notebook. Use Write when creating a notebook from scratch (you would write the entire .ipynb JSON structure). The .ipynb format is JSON, so a Write-based creation looks like:

Write(
  file_path: "/project/analysis/new_notebook.ipynb",
  content: "{\"cells\": [{\"cell_type\": \"code\", \"source\": [\"import pandas as pd\"], \"metadata\": {}, \"outputs\": []}], \"metadata\": {\"kernelspec\": {\"display_name\": \"Python 3\", \"language\": \"python\", \"name\": \"python3\"}}, \"nbformat\": 4, \"nbformat_minor\": 5}"
)

Performance Considerations¶

File operations have performance characteristics worth understanding, especially in large projects.

Read Performance¶

Read is filesystem I/O. For local files, it is effectively instant. The bottleneck is not the read itself but the context window cost of the content. A 2,000-line file might be 50-80K tokens – a significant chunk of the 200K context window.

Performance tips: - Use offset/limit for targeted reads of large files - Read small files completely, large files selectively - After /compact, previously read files are no longer in context and must be re-read

Edit Performance¶

Edit is a find-and-replace operation on disk. It is fast regardless of file size. The performance consideration is token cost: old_string and new_string both consume tokens in the tool call.

Performance tips: - For small changes, Edit is always more efficient than Write - For replace_all operations across a very large file, the operation itself is fast but the file re-read needed to verify the change costs tokens

Glob Performance¶

Glob uses optimized filesystem scanning. It is fast even in repositories with millions of files because it uses the same efficient traversal as ripgrep, skipping .gitignore entries and common non-source directories.

Grep Performance¶

Grep inherits ripgrep’s performance. Ripgrep is often faster than IDE search because it: - Respects .gitignore (skips node_modules, dist, .git) - Uses memory mapping for large files - Parallelizes across CPU cores - Uses SIMD instructions for pattern matching

The type parameter makes searches faster because ripgrep can skip files with non-matching extensions before reading them.

The Tool Interaction Matrix¶

Understanding which tools work together and in what order:

Glob ─── finds files ──→ Read ─── loads content ──→ Edit ─── modifies content
  │                        │                           │
  │                        └── provides understanding──┘
  │                                                    
Grep ─── finds content ─→ Read ─── loads full file ──→ Edit
  │                        │
  │                        └── context for unique old_string
  │
  └── head_limit ─→ prevents context exhaustion

The most important flow: Search (Glob/Grep) then Read then Edit. Each step narrows the scope for the next.

Large Codebase Patterns¶

When working with codebases containing thousands of files, the order and combination of tool calls matters significantly.

The Funnel Pattern¶

Start broad, narrow progressively:

Step 1: Glob to understand structure
  Glob(pattern: "src/**/*.ts", path: "/project")
  → 847 files

Step 2: Grep to find relevant files
  Grep(pattern: "PaymentService", type: "ts", output_mode: "files_with_matches")
  → 12 files

Step 3: Grep with content to understand usage
  Grep(pattern: "PaymentService", type: "ts", output_mode: "content", -C: 5, head_limit: 50)
  → See how it's used in context

Step 4: Read the key files
  Read(file_path: "/project/src/services/payment.ts")
  Read(file_path: "/project/src/api/checkout.ts")

Step 5: Edit what needs changing
  Edit(file_path: "/project/src/services/payment.ts", ...)

Each step reduces the scope. You never read 847 files – you narrow to the 2-3 that matter.

The Parallel Read Pattern¶

When you know which files to read, read them all at once:

# These execute in parallel (independent reads)
Read(file_path: "/project/src/auth/login.ts")
Read(file_path: "/project/src/auth/register.ts")
Read(file_path: "/project/src/auth/types.ts")
Read(file_path: "/project/src/auth/middleware.ts")

Four files loaded in one round trip. Claude Code can then analyze them together and make coordinated edits.

The Agent Delegation Pattern¶

When exploration scope is too broad for direct tool calls:

Agent(
  prompt: "Search the entire codebase for all places where database connections are opened but might not be properly closed. Check for: missing finally blocks, missing context managers, connections opened in try without finally. Report each location as file:line with a one-sentence description.",
  subagent_type: "Explore"
)

The agent does the multi-round search, reads files, follows leads, and reports back. Your main context stays clean.

Avoiding Context Window Exhaustion¶

Every file you Read, every Grep result, every Bash output consumes context window tokens. On a large codebase, careless reads can exhaust your context fast.

Strategies:

  1. Read sections, not files. For a 2,000-line file where you only care about one function, use offset and limit: Read(file_path: "/project/src/massive-file.ts", offset: 340, limit: 60)

  2. Use files_with_matches before content. First find which files match, then read only the relevant ones: Grep(pattern: "deprecated", type: "ts") # Just file paths # Then Read only the files that matter

  3. Set head_limit on Grep. If you only need a few examples: Grep(pattern: "TODO", type: "py", output_mode: "content", head_limit: 10)

  4. Delegate broad searches to Agent. An Agent has its own context window. Its exploration noise stays in its context, and only the summary comes back to you.

Multi-File Editing Patterns¶

Sequential Edits (Dependent Changes)¶

When editing file B depends on what you changed in file A:

# Step 1: Read both files
Read(file_path: "/project/src/types.ts")
Read(file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts")

# Step 2: Edit types first (auth.ts depends on these types)
Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/types.ts",
  old_string: "interface User {\n  name: string;\n}",
  new_string: "interface User {\n  name: string;\n  email: string;\n}"
)

# Step 3: Now edit auth to use the new field
Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/auth.ts",
  old_string: "const greeting = `Hello, ${user.name}`;",
  new_string: "const greeting = `Hello, ${user.name} (${user.email})`;"
)

Parallel Edits (Independent Changes)¶

When changes in different files are independent:

# Both edits in one response (execute in parallel)
Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/config/dev.ts",
  old_string: "port: 3000",
  new_string: "port: 8080"
)

Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/config/test.ts",
  old_string: "port: 3000",
  new_string: "port: 8081"
)

The Rename Across a Project¶

Renaming a function, class, or variable across multiple files:

# Step 1: Find all files using the old name
Grep(pattern: "fetchUserData", type: "ts", output_mode: "files_with_matches")

# Step 2: Read each file, then edit
# File 1
Read(file_path: "/project/src/api/users.ts")
Edit(file_path: "/project/src/api/users.ts", old_string: "fetchUserData", new_string: "getUserById", replace_all: true)

# File 2
Read(file_path: "/project/src/components/UserProfile.tsx")
Edit(file_path: "/project/src/components/UserProfile.tsx", old_string: "fetchUserData", new_string: "getUserById", replace_all: true)

# File 3
Read(file_path: "/project/tests/api.test.ts")
Edit(file_path: "/project/tests/api.test.ts", old_string: "fetchUserData", new_string: "getUserById", replace_all: true)

For a rename that touches many files (10+), consider using the Agent tool or the /batch command to parallelize.

10 File Operation Recipes¶

1. Find and Read All Test Files for a Module¶

Glob(pattern: "**/auth/**/*.test.{ts,tsx}")
# Then Read each result

2. Count Lines of Code by Language¶

Bash(command: "find /project/src -name '*.ts' | xargs wc -l | tail -1")
Bash(command: "find /project/src -name '*.py' | xargs wc -l | tail -1")

(This is a valid Bash use case – there is no dedicated tool for line counting.)

3. Find All Exported Functions in a Directory¶

Grep(
  pattern: "^export (async )?function \\w+",
  path: "/project/src/api",
  type: "ts",
  output_mode: "content"
)

4. Find Dead Code (Exported but Never Imported)¶

# Step 1: Find all exports
Grep(pattern: "^export ", path: "/project/src", type: "ts", output_mode: "content", head_limit: 100)

# Step 2: For each export, search for imports
Grep(pattern: "import.*specificFunction", type: "ts", output_mode: "count")
# If count is 0, it might be dead code

5. Update a Version Number Everywhere¶

Grep(pattern: "1\\.2\\.3", output_mode: "files_with_matches")
# For each file:
Read(file_path: "...")
Edit(file_path: "...", old_string: "1.2.3", new_string: "1.3.0", replace_all: true)

6. Add a License Header to All Source Files¶

Glob(pattern: "src/**/*.ts")
# For each file:
Read(file_path: "...")
Edit(
  file_path: "...",
  old_string: "[first line of file]",
  new_string: "// Copyright 2026 MyCompany. All rights reserved.\n// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT\n\n[first line of file]"
)

7. Find All TODO Comments with Context¶

Grep(
  pattern: "TODO|FIXME|HACK|XXX",
  output_mode: "content",
  -C: 1,
  head_limit: 50
)

8. Find Configuration Inconsistencies¶

# Compare dev and production configs
Read(file_path: "/project/config/development.json")
Read(file_path: "/project/config/production.json")
# Claude Code analyzes differences and flags inconsistencies

9. Extract All API Endpoints¶

Grep(
  pattern: "(app|router)\\.(get|post|put|patch|delete)\\(",
  type: "ts",
  output_mode: "content",
  -B: 1,
  -A: 3
)

10. Find Potential Security Issues¶

# Hardcoded secrets
Grep(pattern: "(password|secret|key|token)\\s*=\\s*['\"][^'\"]{8,}", -i: true, output_mode: "content")

# SQL injection risks
Grep(pattern: "query.*\\$\\{|query.*\\+\\s*req", type: "ts", output_mode: "content")

# Eval usage
Grep(pattern: "\\beval\\(", output_mode: "content")

What Most People Get Wrong¶

Mistake 1: Reading entire large files when you need one section.

A 3,000-line file read without offset/limit consumes significant context tokens. If you know the function starts around line 450, read just that section:

Read(file_path: "/project/src/massive.ts", offset: 440, limit: 40)

Mistake 2: Grep without type or glob filters.

On a large codebase, an unfiltered Grep searches everything – including node_modules, .git, build output, and binary files. Always filter:

# SLOW and noisy
Grep(pattern: "handleAuth")

# FAST and precise
Grep(pattern: "handleAuth", type: "ts")

Mistake 3: Using Write for small changes.

Every time you Write an existing file, you send the entire file content through the context window. For a 500-line file where you change one line, that is 499 wasted lines. Edit sends only the change.

Mistake 4: Not using replace_all when renaming.

Editing the same string three times in three separate Edit calls when one replace_all: true would handle all of them:

# THREE calls for one rename (wasteful)
Edit(old_string: "oldName", new_string: "newName")  # First occurrence
Edit(old_string: "oldName", new_string: "newName")  # Second occurrence
Edit(old_string: "oldName", new_string: "newName")  # Third occurrence

# ONE call (correct)
Edit(old_string: "oldName", new_string: "newName", replace_all: true)

Mistake 5: Forgetting Glob sorts by modification time.

When Glob returns 200 files, the first ones are the most recently modified. If you are looking for files that were just changed (maybe by a teammate, or by Claude Code in a previous step), they are at the top.

Summary¶

The five file operation tools – Read, Edit, Write, Glob, Grep – are designed to work together. Glob finds files by name. Grep finds files by content. Read loads them into context. Edit modifies them precisely. Write creates new ones. The patterns scale from single-file edits to project-wide refactoring when you combine them correctly: funnel from broad to narrow, parallelize independent operations, delegate exploration to agents, and always read before you edit.

Key takeaways: 1. Read before Edit is enforced, not optional – the Edit tool errors if you skip it 2. Edit sends only the diff; Write sends the entire file – prefer Edit for modifications 3. Glob for names, Grep for contents – never confuse the two 4. Filter Grep with type or glob to avoid searching irrelevant files 5. For large codebases, use the funnel pattern: Glob/Grep to narrow, Read the relevant files, Edit the specific lines

Chapter 12: Agent Tool and Multi-Agent Patterns¶ ~18 min read

You need to refactor an authentication system that spans 47 files across three services. Reading all 47 files in your main session would consume half your context window before you write a single line of code. Or you need a security review and a performance audit at the same time, and each requires deep investigation. These are the problems the Agent tool solves.

The Agent tool spawns a child Claude instance – a subagent – that operates in its own context window with its own tool access. It explores, analyzes, or implements independently, then reports back. The main session stays clean. And when you launch multiple agents in parallel, you get concurrent workstreams executing simultaneously.

This chapter covers how the Agent tool works, when to use it, how to write effective agent prompts, and the multi-agent patterns that handle complex engineering tasks.

What the Agent Tool Does¶

When Claude Code calls the Agent tool, it creates a new Claude session – a separate instance with its own context window, its own tool calls, and its own reasoning. The parent session provides a prompt describing the task. The child session executes it and returns a result.

The flow:

Main session                    Subagent session
     │                               │
     ├── Agent(prompt: "...")  ──────>│
     │                               ├── Grep(...)
     │   [main waits or               ├── Read(...)
     │    continues other work]        ├── Read(...)
     │                               ├── Grep(...)
     │                               ├── [analyzes findings]
     │<──────── result ──────────────┤
     │                               └── [session ends]
     ├── [uses result to proceed]
     │

The subagent has full tool access (or restricted access, depending on its type). It can Read files, run Grep searches, execute Bash commands, even spawn its own subagents. When it finishes, its final response becomes the Agent tool’s result in the parent session.

Why This Matters¶

Context protection. Every file Read, every Grep result, every Bash output consumes tokens in your context window. Exploration often requires reading 10-20 files to find the 2-3 that matter. An agent does that exploration in its own context. Only the summary comes back to the main session.

Parallel execution. You can launch multiple agents simultaneously for independent tasks. A security review agent and a performance review agent running in parallel complete in the time of the slower one, not the sum of both.

Specialized configuration. Different agents can use different models. Use Opus for security analysis, Sonnet for code quality checks, Haiku for simple file searches. Each task gets the right model at the right cost.

Agent Types¶

The subagent_type parameter configures the agent’s capabilities and restrictions.

general-purpose¶

The default type. Full tool access. Use for any task that requires reading, writing, running commands, or making changes.

Agent(
  prompt: "Refactor the PaymentService class in src/services/payment.ts to use the Strategy pattern. Create separate strategy files for each payment provider. Write tests for the new structure.",
  subagent_type: "general-purpose"
)

Tools available: Read, Edit, Write, Glob, Grep, Bash, WebFetch, WebSearch, NotebookEdit, Agent (can spawn sub-subagents)

Explore¶

A read-only agent optimized for codebase investigation. No write tools – it cannot modify files or run destructive commands. This makes it safe to let loose on a codebase.

Agent(
  prompt: "Map the entire authentication flow from the login endpoint to the session creation. Trace every function call, identify all database queries, and note any external service calls. Report as: file:line → function → description.",
  subagent_type: "Explore"
)

Tools available: Read, Glob, Grep, Bash (read-only commands)

When to use Explore: When you need to understand code without changing it. Tracing call chains, mapping dependencies, finding patterns, answering questions about the codebase.

Plan¶

An architecture and planning agent. Like Explore, it has no write tools, but it is oriented toward producing plans, architectures, and strategies rather than raw code findings.

Agent(
  prompt: "Design the database migration strategy for splitting the monolithic users table into separate users, profiles, and preferences tables. Consider: data migration approach, backward compatibility, rollback plan, and timeline.",
  subagent_type: "Plan"
)

Tools available: Read, Glob, Grep

claude-code-guide¶

A specialized agent that answers questions about Claude Code itself. It has access to internal documentation and can explain features, configuration, and behavior.

Agent(
  prompt: "How do PreToolUse hooks work? What JSON format do they receive on stdin? What exit codes control behavior?",
  subagent_type: "claude-code-guide"
)

statusline-setup¶

A utility agent for configuring Claude Code’s status line display. Rarely used directly.

When to Use Agent¶

Use Agent When:¶

The task requires extensive exploration. If you need to search through many files, follow call chains, or investigate a pattern across the codebase, an agent keeps that exploration noise out of your main context.

Agent(
  prompt: "Find all places in the codebase where we create database connections. For each: (1) Is the connection properly closed? (2) Is there a timeout set? (3) Is there error handling? Report as a table: file:line | connection type | properly closed | has timeout | has error handling.",
  subagent_type: "Explore"
)

Multiple independent tasks can run in parallel. If tasks A and B do not depend on each other, launch both as agents and get results simultaneously.

You need a different model for a subtask. Security reviews benefit from Opus’s deeper reasoning. Simple file searches work fine with Haiku. Agents let you match the model to the task.

Investigation would pollute main context. Reading 15 files to understand one bug fills your context with code that is irrelevant after the investigation. An agent absorbs that cost.

Do NOT Use Agent When:¶

The task involves 1-3 files. Reading two files and making an edit is faster done directly. The overhead of spawning an agent (new session, context initialization) is not worth it for simple operations.

The result is a simple lookup. “What line is the authenticate function defined on?” – just use Grep directly. Do not spawn an agent for a single search.

You need tight back-and-forth control. Agents execute autonomously. You give them a prompt, they execute, they return. If the task requires iterative human feedback at each step, do it in the main session.

The task requires sequential human decisions. “Read this file, show me the options, I’ll pick one, then proceed” – this is a main-session workflow, not an agent workflow.

Agent Parameters¶

Parameter Type Description Default
prompt string (required) Complete task description –
subagent_type string Agent type “general-purpose”
model string Override model Parent’s model
run_in_background boolean Non-blocking execution false
isolation string “worktree” for git isolation none

The prompt Parameter¶

This is the most important parameter. The agent sees your prompt as its primary instruction. Everything it needs to know must be in this prompt or discoverable through its tools.

The model Parameter¶

Override the model for cost or capability optimization:

# Use Opus for security-critical analysis
Agent(
  prompt: "Audit src/auth/ for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities...",
  model: "opus"
)

# Use Haiku for simple file-finding tasks
Agent(
  prompt: "List all Python files that import the requests library",
  model: "haiku"
)

The run_in_background Parameter¶

When true, the agent runs without blocking the main session. The main session can continue working while the agent executes. When the agent finishes, the result arrives as a notification.

# Start a background investigation
Agent(
  prompt: "Map all external API calls in the codebase...",
  subagent_type: "Explore",
  run_in_background: true
)

# Main session continues immediately -- can do other work
# Agent result arrives when it finishes

Use background execution when: - The agent task is independent of your current work - The task is long-running and you want to continue working - You are launching multiple agents and do not need all results at once

Use foreground execution (default) when: - You need the result before proceeding - The agent’s findings determine your next action

The isolation Parameter¶

Setting isolation: "worktree" creates a temporary git worktree for the agent. The agent gets its own copy of the repository and can make changes without affecting your working directory.

Agent(
  prompt: "Refactor the database layer to use the repository pattern. Make all necessary changes, including tests.",
  isolation: "worktree"
)

The worktree is created inside .claude/worktrees/ with a new branch based on the current HEAD. After the agent finishes: - You can review the changes in the worktree - Merge the branch if the changes look good - Discard the worktree if the approach was wrong

Use worktree isolation for: - Experimental refactoring you might discard - Risky changes that could break the codebase - Parallel development of independent features - “What if” explorations

Writing Effective Agent Prompts¶

The quality of an agent’s output depends entirely on the quality of its prompt. An agent cannot ask you clarifying questions mid-execution. It cannot read your mind about context you have but have not shared. Everything must be in the prompt.

Include WHAT and WHY¶

# BAD: No context, no goal
Agent(prompt: "Look at the auth module")

# GOOD: Clear task with purpose
Agent(
  prompt: "Review src/auth/ for security vulnerabilities. We recently had a report of session fixation attacks. Check: (1) How are session IDs generated? (2) Are sessions invalidated on login? (3) Are session IDs rotated after privilege changes? Report findings with CWE references and severity ratings."
)

Include Known Context¶

If you already know relevant facts, include them. Do not make the agent rediscover what you already know.

# BAD: Agent must rediscover everything
Agent(prompt: "Fix the bug where users can't log in")

# GOOD: Agent starts with your knowledge
Agent(
  prompt: "Fix the login failure bug. Known facts: (1) The error is in src/auth/login.ts around line 45. (2) The bcrypt.compare call is receiving the hash and password in the wrong order. (3) The test in tests/test_auth.py::test_valid_login is failing with 'invalid credentials'. Write the fix and verify the test passes."
)

Specify the Desired Output Format¶

Agent(
  prompt: "Audit the dependency list in package.json. For each dependency, check: (1) Latest version available. (2) Any known CVEs. (3) Whether our version is pinned. Output a markdown table: | Package | Current | Latest | Pinned? | CVEs | Action |"
)

Do Not Delegate Understanding¶

# BAD: "Based on your findings" assumes shared context
Agent(prompt: "Investigate the auth module. Based on your findings, fix whatever is wrong.")

# GOOD: Separate investigation from action, or be specific
Agent(
  prompt: "Investigate all authentication paths in src/auth/. Report: (1) List every authentication method (password, OAuth, API key, etc.). (2) For each, trace the flow from request to session creation. (3) Identify any path where authentication can be bypassed. DO NOT make any changes -- just report findings.",
  subagent_type: "Explore"
)
# Then in main session, use findings to make targeted fixes

Set Boundaries¶

Agent(
  prompt: "Search for all uses of the deprecated `fetchData` function. Only search in src/ -- ignore node_modules, dist, and test directories. Report as a list of file:line entries. Do not modify any files.",
  subagent_type: "Explore"
)

Parallel Agent Patterns¶

Pattern 1: Parallel Code Review¶

Launch multiple reviewers simultaneously, each focused on a different concern:

# Security review (Opus for depth)
Agent(
  prompt: "Security review of src/api/. Check for: injection, auth bypass, data exposure, SSRF. Output: severity | CWE | file:line | description | fix.",
  model: "opus",
  run_in_background: true
)

# Performance review (Sonnet for speed/cost balance)
Agent(
  prompt: "Performance review of src/api/. Check for: N+1 queries, missing indexes, unbounded result sets, blocking I/O in async code. Output: impact | file:line | description | fix.",
  model: "sonnet",
  run_in_background: true
)

# Code quality review (Sonnet)
Agent(
  prompt: "Code quality review of src/api/. Check for: dead code, duplicated logic, unclear naming, missing error handling, type safety gaps. Output: priority | file:line | description | suggestion.",
  model: "sonnet",
  run_in_background: true
)

Three reviews running simultaneously. Each agent has its own context window, its own model, and its own focus area. Results arrive as each finishes.

Pattern 2: Investigation Then Fix¶

Use an Explore agent to investigate, then fix in the main session:

# Step 1: Investigation (agent)
Agent(
  prompt: "The /api/users endpoint is returning 500 errors intermittently. Investigate: (1) Read the route handler in src/api/users.ts. (2) Check the database query in src/db/users.ts. (3) Look at error logs if available. (4) Trace the full request path. Report: root cause, affected file:line, and recommended fix.",
  subagent_type: "Explore"
)

# Step 2: Fix (main session, using agent's findings)
Read(file_path: "/project/src/db/users.ts")
Edit(file_path: "/project/src/db/users.ts", old_string: "...", new_string: "...")
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/test_users.py -v")

The investigation agent reads 10+ files and traces the call chain. Only its summary enters the main context. The fix is then precise and targeted.

Pattern 3: Multi-File Refactor with Worktrees¶

For a large refactoring that touches multiple subsystems:

# Agent 1: Refactor auth subsystem
Agent(
  prompt: "Refactor src/auth/ to use the new TokenService interface. Update all functions to use dependency injection. Write tests.",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true
)

# Agent 2: Refactor payments subsystem
Agent(
  prompt: "Refactor src/payments/ to use the new TokenService interface. Update all functions to use dependency injection. Write tests.",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true
)

# Agent 3: Update API layer
Agent(
  prompt: "Update src/api/ to pass the TokenService to auth and payment handlers. Wire up dependency injection at the route level. Write integration tests.",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true
)

Each agent works in its own git worktree. No conflicts. When all three finish, you review each branch and merge them in order.

Pattern 4: Research Then Implement¶

# Step 1: Research (background agent)
Agent(
  prompt: "Research the best rate-limiting library for Express.js in 2026. Compare: express-rate-limit, rate-limiter-flexible, and @nestjs/throttler (if applicable to Express). Check: maintenance status, features, performance benchmarks. Recommend one with rationale.",
  subagent_type: "Explore",
  run_in_background: true
)

# While research runs, prepare the codebase
Read(file_path: "/project/src/api/middleware/index.ts")
# ... main session continues working

# When research result arrives, implement the recommendation

Communicating with Running Agents¶

Claude Code supports sending messages to previously spawned agents using the SendMessage capability. This allows you to provide additional context or redirect an agent that is already running.

The key requirement: the agent must still be active (not yet returned its final response). Once an agent has completed, you would need to spawn a new one.

This is useful when: - You realize the agent needs additional context you forgot to include - The agent’s intermediate output (visible in streaming) suggests it is going in the wrong direction - You want to narrow the scope after seeing initial findings

Custom Agents from .claude/agents/¶

As discussed in Chapter 6 and Chapter 8, Claude Code supports custom agent definitions in .claude/agents/. These are markdown files with YAML frontmatter that define specialized agents. The key distinction from ad-hoc Agent tool calls: custom agents have a persistent identity, a model preference, tool restrictions, and a system prompt.

When Custom Agents Beat Ad-Hoc Agents¶

Scenario Use Why
Recurring task (weekly security review) Custom agent Same prompt, same model, same tools every time
One-off investigation Ad-hoc Agent call Not worth creating a persistent definition
Team-shared expertise (company-specific patterns) Custom agent Checked into git, shared via settings
Specialized model requirement (always use Opus) Custom agent Model is defined in frontmatter, not forgotten

Example: A Database Migration Agent¶

markdown
---
name: migration-expert
description: Expert in database migrations. Use when creating or reviewing
  schema changes, migration scripts, or data transformations.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: sonnet
effort: high
---

You are a database migration expert. When invoked:

1. Read the current schema (check src/db/schema/ or migrations/)
2. Understand the requested change
3. Generate a migration script that:
   - Is reversible (include both up and down)
   - Handles large tables with zero-downtime patterns
   - Includes data backfill if needed
   - Has appropriate indexes
4. Generate a test for the migration
5. Verify the migration runs against the test database

Always check for:
- Foreign key constraints that might block the migration
- Large table concerns (> 1M rows: use batched operations)
- Nullable vs NOT NULL with defaults
- Index creation concurrency (CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY in PostgreSQL)

Claude Code delegates to this agent whenever it encounters migration-related tasks, using the right model, the right tools, and the right approach every time.

Agent Composition: Agents Calling Agents¶

A general-purpose agent can spawn its own subagents. This creates a hierarchy:

Main session
├── Agent: "Review this PR for security and quality"
│   ├── Sub-agent (Opus): Security review
│   └── Sub-agent (Sonnet): Code quality review
└── [Main session continues after results arrive]

This is powerful but expensive. Each layer multiplies token costs. Use agent composition for genuinely complex tasks where each level adds meaningful specialization.

Worktree Workflows in Depth¶

The worktree isolation feature deserves deeper exploration because it enables workflows that are impossible (or risky) without it.

What Happens When You Use isolation: “worktree”¶

  1. Claude Code creates a new directory under .claude/worktrees/ with a unique name
  2. A new git branch is created based on the current HEAD
  3. A git worktree is checked out at that branch
  4. The agent’s working directory is set to the worktree
  5. All file operations (Read, Edit, Write, Bash) operate on the worktree copy
  6. The original working directory is untouched

The Experiment-and-Merge Pattern¶

# Step 1: Agent experiments in a worktree
Agent(
  prompt: "Attempt to migrate the Express.js server to Hono. Update route handlers, middleware, error handling, and tests. This is experimental -- try your best but it may not be feasible.",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true
)

# Step 2: Review the result
# If the agent succeeded, a new branch exists with the changes
Bash(command: "git diff main..worktree-branch-name --stat")

# Step 3: Merge if it looks good
Bash(command: "git merge worktree-branch-name")

# Or discard if the experiment failed
Bash(command: "git worktree remove .claude/worktrees/the-worktree")
Bash(command: "git branch -D worktree-branch-name")

Parallel Feature Development¶

Multiple agents, each in their own worktree, developing different features simultaneously:

Agent(
  prompt: "Implement the user notification system in src/notifications/. Create: service, routes, tests, database migration.",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true
)

Agent(
  prompt: "Implement the audit logging system in src/audit/. Create: logger, middleware, storage adapter, tests.",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true
)

Both agents work independently. Neither’s changes affect the other or your working directory. When both finish, review and merge each branch.

Worktree Limitations¶

  • Cost: Creating a worktree is a git checkout operation plus whatever file operations the agent performs. It is fast but not free.
  • Merge conflicts: If two worktree agents modify the same files, you will have merge conflicts when combining their branches.
  • Disk space: Each worktree is a near-full copy of the working tree (though git shares the object database). Large repos consume significant space.
  • Agent context: The agent in a worktree does not have your main session’s context. It starts fresh with only its prompt.

MCP: Extending Claude Code with External Tools¶

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is the standard protocol for connecting Claude Code to external tools and data sources. While the built-in tools cover filesystem, shell, and web operations, MCP lets you add tools for anything else – databases, APIs, custom services, specialized workflows.

What MCP Is¶

MCP is a JSON-RPC protocol over stdio. An MCP server is a program that: 1. Accepts JSON-RPC messages on stdin 2. Exposes tools (functions Claude Code can call) 3. Exposes resources (data Claude Code can read) 4. Returns results on stdout

When you register an MCP server, its tools appear alongside Claude Code’s built-in tools. Claude Code can call them the same way it calls Read or Bash.

Registering MCP Servers¶

In your settings, configure MCP servers:

json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "my-database": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["/path/to/db-mcp-server/index.js"],
      "env": {
        "DATABASE_URL": "postgres://localhost/mydb"
      }
    },
    "my-api": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["/path/to/api-mcp-server/main.py"],
      "env": {
        "API_KEY": "${API_KEY}"
      }
    }
  }
}

Each server runs as a child process. Claude Code communicates with it via stdin/stdout using the MCP protocol.

What MCP Tools Look Like¶

Once registered, MCP tools appear to Claude Code like built-in tools. If your database MCP server exposes a query tool, Claude Code can call it:

mcp__my-database__query(sql: "SELECT count(*) FROM users WHERE created_at > '2026-01-01'")

The naming convention is mcp__<server-name>__<tool-name>.

Common MCP Servers¶

Server Purpose
Database MCP Run SQL queries against your database
Memory MCP Persistent memory across sessions
GitHub MCP Enhanced GitHub operations (beyond gh CLI)
Slack MCP Read/post messages to Slack channels
Browser MCP Authenticated browser operations
Custom API MCP Interface with internal company APIs

Building a Simple MCP Server¶

A minimal MCP server in Python:

python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Minimal MCP server that provides a 'greet' tool."""
import json
import sys


def handle_request(request: dict) -> dict:
    method = request.get("method", "")

    if method == "initialize":
        return {
            "protocolVersion": "2024-11-05",
            "capabilities": {"tools": {}},
            "serverInfo": {"name": "example-server", "version": "1.0.0"},
        }

    if method == "tools/list":
        return {
            "tools": [
                {
                    "name": "greet",
                    "description": "Generate a greeting for a person",
                    "inputSchema": {
                        "type": "object",
                        "properties": {
                            "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Person's name"}
                        },
                        "required": ["name"],
                    },
                }
            ]
        }

    if method == "tools/call":
        tool_name = request["params"]["name"]
        args = request["params"].get("arguments", {})
        if tool_name == "greet":
            name = args.get("name", "World")
            return {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": f"Hello, {name}!"}]}

    return {"error": {"code": -32601, "message": f"Unknown method: {method}"}}


def main() -> None:
    for line in sys.stdin:
        line = line.strip()
        if not line:
            continue
        request = json.loads(line)
        response = handle_request(request)
        response["jsonrpc"] = "2.0"
        response["id"] = request.get("id")
        print(json.dumps(response), flush=True)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Register it:

json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "example": {
      "command": "python3",
      "args": ["/path/to/example_server.py"]
    }
  }
}

Claude Code can now call mcp__example__greet(name: "Alice"). Building production MCP servers is covered in detail in Chapter 24.

MCP vs. Bash¶

When should you use an MCP server instead of just running commands via Bash?

Scenario Use Why
One-off database query Bash(psql -c "...") Simple, no setup needed
Frequent database queries Database MCP Persistent connection, schema awareness
Read a web page WebFetch Built-in, no setup
Authenticated API with complex logic Custom MCP Handles auth, retries, pagination
Call an internal tool MCP Typed parameters, proper error handling

MCP servers make sense when you repeatedly need to interact with an external system in a structured way. For one-off operations, Bash is simpler.

Cost Implications¶

Multi-agent workflows multiply costs. Each agent is a separate API session with its own token consumption.

Cost Factors¶

Factor Impact
Number of agents Multiplied by number of parallel agents
Model per agent Opus costs more than Sonnet costs more than Haiku
Agent exploration depth More tool calls = more tokens
Context size per agent Larger contexts = more input tokens

Cost Optimization Strategies¶

Match model to task complexity:

# Expensive but necessary: security audit needs deep reasoning
Agent(prompt: "Security audit...", model: "opus")

# Cost-effective: simple file search
Agent(prompt: "Find all files importing lodash...", model: "haiku")

# Balanced: code quality review
Agent(prompt: "Code quality review...", model: "sonnet")

Scope agent prompts tightly: An agent that knows exactly where to look spends fewer tokens exploring.

# EXPENSIVE: Broad scope, agent must search the entire codebase
Agent(prompt: "Find the bug")

# CHEAP: Narrow scope, agent reads 2-3 files
Agent(prompt: "The bug is in src/auth/login.ts around line 45. The bcrypt.compare arguments are reversed. Verify and fix.")

Use Explore agents for investigation, not general-purpose: Explore agents have no write tools, so they consume fewer tokens on tool calls and make fewer mistakes that require correction.

Monitor costs: Use the /cost command to see your token spend. After a multi-agent workflow, check how much each agent consumed to calibrate future decisions.

Cost Comparison Example¶

A typical code review workflow:

Approach Estimated Cost
Single-agent review (main session, Opus) $0.50-1.50
Three parallel agents (Sonnet for each) $0.30-0.90
Three parallel agents (Opus for security, Sonnet for quality and perf) $0.40-1.20

Parallel Sonnet agents can be cheaper AND faster than a single Opus agent doing everything sequentially.

Real-World Workflow: End-to-End Bug Investigation¶

Here is a complete multi-agent workflow for a production bug:

# Step 1: User reports "API returns 500 on /api/orders for some users"

# Step 2: Launch investigation agent
Agent(
  prompt: "Investigate 500 errors on /api/orders. (1) Read the route handler. (2) Trace the database query. (3) Check for any user-specific logic that could fail. (4) Look at error handling. Report: suspected root cause, affected file:line, and what data conditions trigger the failure.",
  subagent_type: "Explore"
)

# Step 3: Agent reports back:
# "Root cause: src/db/orders.ts:78 - the query joins on user.org_id,
#  but some users have null org_id. The JOIN fails silently, and the
#  null result causes a TypeError at src/api/orders.ts:23 when
#  accessing result.rows.length."

# Step 4: Main session writes failing test
Write(
  file_path: "/project/tests/test_orders.py",
  content: "..."  # Test with a user that has null org_id
)

# Step 5: Verify test fails
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/test_orders.py::test_orders_null_org -v")

# Step 6: Fix the bug
Read(file_path: "/project/src/db/orders.ts")
Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/db/orders.ts",
  old_string: "JOIN organizations ON users.org_id = organizations.id",
  new_string: "LEFT JOIN organizations ON users.org_id = organizations.id"
)

# Step 7: Add null check
Read(file_path: "/project/src/api/orders.ts")
Edit(
  file_path: "/project/src/api/orders.ts",
  old_string: "const count = result.rows.length;",
  new_string: "const count = result?.rows?.length ?? 0;"
)

# Step 8: Verify test passes
Bash(command: "python -m pytest tests/test_orders.py -v")

The agent did the expensive exploration (reading route handlers, tracing database queries, understanding the data model). The main session stayed focused: write test, fix bug, verify.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

Mistake 1: Using agents for simple tasks.

Spawning an agent to read one file and report back is like calling a meeting to ask one question. Just Read the file directly.

Mistake 2: Vague agent prompts.

“Look at the code and tell me what you think” produces vague results. Be specific about what to check, where to look, and how to format the output.

Mistake 3: Not including known context.

If you already know the bug is in src/auth/login.ts near line 45, tell the agent. Do not make it rediscover what you already know. Agent tokens are real money.

Mistake 4: Foreground agents for independent tasks.

If you launch three foreground agents sequentially, you wait for each one to finish before starting the next. Use run_in_background: true for independent tasks and let them run in parallel.

Mistake 5: Not using worktree isolation for risky changes.

An agent that modifies files in your working directory can break things you are working on. For experimental refactoring, always use isolation: "worktree".

Summary¶

The Agent tool is Claude Code’s mechanism for scaling beyond a single context window. Subagents explore codebases without polluting your context, execute in parallel for independent tasks, and use appropriate models for each job. The key is knowing when to delegate (complex exploration, parallel work, risky experiments) and when not to (simple reads, lookups, 2-3 file operations). Write agent prompts like briefings to a colleague: include what you know, be specific about what you need, and define the output format. MCP extends the tool system beyond built-in capabilities, letting you connect Claude Code to databases, APIs, and custom tools through a standard protocol.

Key takeaways: 1. Agents protect your main context from exploration noise – use them for investigation, reserve the main session for targeted action 2. Parallel agents reduce wall-clock time for independent tasks – launch them simultaneously with run_in_background: true 3. Match models to tasks: Opus for security/complexity, Sonnet for general work, Haiku for simple searches 4. Write agent prompts with full context, specific scope, and defined output format 5. MCP servers extend Claude Code’s tool system with custom tools – use them for repeated interactions with external systems

Part IV: Context Engineering

Chapter 13: Token Economics and Context Windows¶ ~17 min read

You open Claude Code on a Monday morning, feed it a 1,500-line Python module, ask it to refactor the authentication layer, and thirty minutes later it responds with something nonsensical about a database migration you discussed on Friday. What happened? You ran out of context. The conversation history from your previous task bled into the new one, Claude’s understanding of your codebase degraded, and you burned $12 in API costs getting confused output.

This chapter is about understanding the single most important constraint in working with Claude Code: the context window. Every professional who uses this tool daily needs to internalize token economics the way a systems engineer internalizes memory allocation. Context is a finite resource. Managing it well is the difference between a $5 productive session and a $50 frustrating one.

What the Context Window Actually Is¶

The context window is the total amount of text Claude can “see” at one time. Think of it as working memory. Everything Claude needs to understand your request – your system prompt, the conversation history, files it has read, tool results, its own previous responses – must fit inside this window. Once the window fills up, older content gets compressed or dropped.

Every model in the Claude family has a defined context window size:

Model Context Window Approximate Words Approximate Pages
Claude Opus 4 200K tokens ~150,000 words ~375 pages
Claude Sonnet 4 200K tokens ~150,000 words ~375 pages
Claude Haiku 3.5 200K tokens ~150,000 words ~375 pages

That sounds enormous – 375 pages of text. But in practice, you never get all 200K tokens for your actual work. The system prompt, tool definitions, and Claude’s own response all compete for the same space. Understanding where those tokens go is the key to working effectively.

What a Token Is¶

A token is the fundamental unit Claude uses to process text. It is not a word, not a character, and not a line of code. It is a chunk of text determined by the model’s tokenizer. Here are the practical conversion factors:

  • 1 token is roughly 4 characters of English text
  • 1 token is roughly 0.75 words
  • 1 word is roughly 1.3 tokens
  • 100 tokens is roughly 75 words

For code, tokens are less predictable. Variable names, punctuation, indentation, and language syntax all affect token count differently. Some practical benchmarks:

Content Approximate Tokens
A typical source file (500 lines) 2,000 - 4,000 tokens
A large source file (2,000 lines) 8,000 - 16,000 tokens
The Claude Code system prompt 10,000 - 30,000 tokens
A 200-word user message ~270 tokens
A detailed tool result (file listing, grep output) 500 - 5,000 tokens
A full test suite output (100 tests) 3,000 - 8,000 tokens

Here is what 200K tokens means in practical terms:

200,000 tokens = ~150,000 words = ~375 pages of text
                = ~50 typical source files read end-to-end
                = ~25 large source files
                = ~4 hours of dense conversation

This seems like a lot, but it gets consumed faster than you expect. A single Read of a large file followed by a Bash command that dumps 500 lines of test output can consume 20,000 tokens in two tool calls. That is 10% of your total window, gone in seconds.

Token Budgeting: Where the 200K Actually Goes¶

Here is the critical insight most people miss: you do not get 200K tokens for your messages. The context window is shared across everything Claude needs to function. Here is how Claude Code allocates the window:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              200K Token Context Window            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  System Prompt        ~25%    (~50K tokens)      │
│  ├── CLAUDE.md + @imported rules                 │
│  ├── Git context (recent commits, branch info)   │
│  ├── Active skill instructions                   │
│  └── Memory files (MEMORY.md + loaded memories)  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Tool Definitions     ~15%    (~30K tokens)      │
│  ├── JSON schema for every available tool        │
│  ├── MCP server tool definitions                 │
│  └── Skill-provided tool instructions            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Conversation History ~50%    (~100K tokens)     │
│  ├── Your messages                               │
│  ├── Claude's responses                          │
│  ├── Tool calls and results                      │
│  └── File contents that were read                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Response Reserve     ~10%    (~20K tokens)      │
│  └── Space for Claude's next response            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

That means your actual working space for conversation and tool results is roughly 100K tokens – half the advertised window. And that 100K fills up fast.

The System Prompt Tax¶

Every Claude Code session starts by loading the system prompt. This includes:

  1. CLAUDE.md and all @imported rule files
  2. Tool definitions – every tool available to Claude gets a JSON schema describing its parameters, constraints, and usage
  3. Git context – current branch, recent commits, repository state
  4. Memory files – auto-memory index and any relevant memory files
  5. Active skill instructions – if a skill is loaded, its SKILL.md adds to the prompt

A lean CLAUDE.md with five rule files might cost 5,000 tokens. But add ten MCP servers with 50 custom tools, and your tool definitions alone could consume 30,000 tokens. Add a detailed SKILL.md for the currently active skill, and your system prompt can easily hit 50,000 tokens before you type a single message.

This is why the guidance from Chapter 4 about keeping CLAUDE.md concise matters for performance, not just readability.

What Consumes the Most Tokens¶

In order from most to least expensive:

1. Large file reads. A single Read of a 2,000-line file consumes 8,000-16,000 tokens. Read five files to understand a feature, and you have spent 40,000-80,000 tokens – nearly half your conversation budget.

# This single command can consume 15K tokens:
Read /src/services/authentication.py    # 2,100 lines → ~10K tokens

# Better: read only what you need
Read /src/services/authentication.py offset=450 limit=50  # ~200 tokens

2. Bash command output. Test suites, build logs, and directory listings produce enormous output. A failing test suite with verbose output can generate 10,000+ tokens.

bash
# This can produce 5,000+ tokens of output:
python -m pytest tests/ -v

# Better: run the specific test you care about:
python -m pytest tests/test_auth.py::test_login_invalid_password -v

3. Accumulated tool results. Every tool call adds its result to the conversation history. In a 30-turn session, even modest tool results accumulate to tens of thousands of tokens.

4. System prompt bloat. Too many MCP servers, oversized CLAUDE.md, or verbose skill files inflate the baseline cost of every single turn.

5. Previous messages in long sessions. Your messages and Claude’s responses accumulate. A detailed explanation Claude gave on turn 5 still occupies tokens on turn 50.

How Tokens Work in Code vs. Prose¶

The 4-characters-per-token rule of thumb holds for English prose but breaks down in code. Understanding why helps you estimate context consumption more accurately.

Whitespace and indentation are tokenized. A deeply nested Python function with four levels of indentation burns tokens on spaces that carry no semantic meaning. Here is a concrete example:

python
# This function uses ~45 tokens:
def greet(name: str) -> str:
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

# This equivalent function uses ~120 tokens (indentation + boilerplate):
class GreetingService:
    def __init__(self, prefix: str = "Hello") -> None:
        self.prefix = prefix

    def greet(self, name: str) -> str:
        if name is None:
            raise ValueError("Name cannot be None")
        return f"{self.prefix}, {name}!"

Language affects token density. Python is relatively token-efficient because of its minimal syntax. Languages with verbose syntax like Java or XML consume more tokens per line of functionality:

Language Tokens per 100 Lines (Typical)
Python 400 - 600
TypeScript 500 - 800
Java 600 - 1,000
Go 500 - 700
HTML/XML 300 - 500
JSON (data) 200 - 400
YAML 250 - 450
