Comments are expensive. A 20-line docstring explaining a function costs the same tokens as 20 lines of code. When reading files into context, heavily-commented code consumes significantly more tokens than sparse code. This is another reason to use offset and limit when reading – skip the module-level docstring and jump straight to the function you need.

Import blocks are cheap but repetitive. A standard Python import section of 15 lines costs only ~60-100 tokens, but if you read 10 files that all have similar imports, that is 600-1,000 tokens of near-identical content adding no new information.

Estimating Token Cost Before Acting¶

Before asking Claude to read a large file or run a verbose command, develop the habit of estimating the token cost:

Lines of code / 4 ≈ tokens (rough estimate for Python/TypeScript)
Lines of output / 3 ≈ tokens (rough estimate for command output)

If a file is 2,000 lines: ~500 tokens minimum, likely 2,000-4,000 in practice. If a test suite produces 800 lines of output: ~250-2,500 tokens depending on verbosity. These rough estimates help you decide whether to read the whole file or just a section.

Token Pricing¶

Every token you send to or receive from Claude costs money. The pricing differs significantly across models:

Model Input ($/MTok) Output ($/MTok) Cache Read ($/MTok)
Claude Opus 4 $15.00 $75.00 $1.50
Claude Sonnet 4 $3.00 $15.00 $0.30
Claude Haiku 3.5 $0.80 $4.00 $0.08

MTok = million tokens

A few things jump out immediately:

  • Output tokens cost 5x input tokens. Claude’s responses are five times more expensive than what you send. Long, verbose responses are the biggest cost driver.
  • Opus costs 5x Sonnet, which costs 3.75x Haiku. Model selection has a massive impact on your bill.
  • Cache reads are 10x cheaper than fresh input. This is where prompt caching (next section) becomes critical.

Let us put real numbers on common operations:

Reading a 10K-token file on Opus:
  Input cost: 10,000 / 1,000,000 * $15 = $0.15

Claude writing a 2K-token response on Opus:
  Output cost: 2,000 / 1,000,000 * $75 = $0.15

A single turn (10K input + 2K output) on Opus: $0.30

Same turn on Sonnet: $0.06
Same turn on Haiku: $0.016

Over a 50-turn session, these costs compound. Model selection is the single biggest lever you have for cost control.

Prompt Caching¶

Prompt caching is Claude Code’s most important cost optimization feature, and it works automatically. Here is how it works:

The system prompt – CLAUDE.md, rules, tool definitions, memory – is the same on every turn of a conversation. Without caching, Claude would re-process all 50,000 tokens of system prompt on every single turn. With caching, Claude processes it once and reads it from cache on subsequent turns.

Turn 1 (cold cache):
  System prompt (50K tokens): 50,000 / 1,000,000 * $15 = $0.75 (Opus)

Turn 2+ (cached):
  System prompt (50K tokens): 50,000 / 1,000,000 * $1.50 = $0.075 (Opus)

Savings: 90% on system prompt costs after first turn

The cache has a TTL (time-to-live) of 5 minutes. If you pause for more than 5 minutes between turns, the cache expires and the next turn pays full price again. This means:

  • Rapid back-and-forth is cheaper per turn than slow, deliberate prompting
  • Stepping away for lunch resets the cache – your next turn is expensive again
  • The first turn of every session always pays full price

In practice, prompt caching reduces the average cost of a multi-turn session by 40-60% compared to what it would cost without caching.

Extended Thinking Tokens¶

When Claude reasons about a complex problem, it uses extended thinking – an internal chain-of-thought that happens before the visible response. These thinking tokens are not shown to you by default, but they still cost money.

Extended thinking is controlled by the /effort command:

Effort Level Thinking Tokens When to Use
/effort low ~1,000 Simple edits, formatting, quick lookups
/effort medium ~5,000 Standard coding tasks, reviews
/effort high ~10,000+ Complex debugging, architecture, security audits
ultrathink Maximum One-off deep reasoning for a single prompt

The ultrathink keyword is special: add it to any prompt to get maximum reasoning depth for that one turn only, without changing the persistent effort setting.

# Standard prompt
Fix the race condition in worker.py

# Maximum reasoning for this one turn
ultrathink Fix the race condition in worker.py

Extended thinking tokens are billed at input rates, not output rates. On Opus, 10K thinking tokens cost $0.15, which is modest. But on long sessions with high effort, thinking tokens can add up to 20-30% of your total cost.

Cost optimization tip: Use /effort low for simple tasks like formatting, renaming, or small edits. Reserve /effort high for debugging, security analysis, and architectural decisions. The quality difference is real – low effort produces shallower reasoning – so match effort to task complexity.

Context Window Management Strategies¶

These are the habits that separate professionals from amateurs. Each one directly affects both the quality of Claude’s output and your costs.

1. /clear Between Unrelated Tasks¶

This is the single most impactful habit you can develop. When you finish one task and start another, run /clear. It resets the conversation history, freeing up the entire 100K conversation budget.

# BAD: Everything in one session
You: Fix the login bug in auth.py
Claude: [fixes bug, reads 5 files, runs tests]
You: Now write me a blog post about our new feature
Claude: [confused, tries to relate blog post to auth bug]

# GOOD: Clean context between tasks
You: Fix the login bug in auth.py
Claude: [fixes bug, reads 5 files, runs tests]
You: /clear
You: Write me a blog post about our new feature
Claude: [focused, clean context, better output]

Without /clear, the context from your auth bug investigation – the five files Claude read, the test output, the discussion about error handling – is still taking up space and potentially confusing Claude’s response to your completely unrelated blog post request.

2. /compact for Long Sessions¶

When you are deep in a single task and running low on context, /compact summarizes older messages while preserving the focus area:

/compact authentication    # Summarize everything, keep auth-related details

After compaction, old messages are replaced with a summary. You lose granular detail from earlier in the session, but you free up tokens for continued work. Think of it as moving data from RAM to disk – it is still there conceptually, but access is lossy.

3. Use the Agent Tool for Investigation¶

When Claude needs to investigate something – searching through many files, reading documentation, exploring architecture – delegate to a subagent. The subagent runs in its own context, does its investigation, and returns only a summary to your main session.

# BAD: Investigation fills your context
You: Figure out how the payment system works
Claude: [reads 20 files, context now 80% full]
You: Now refactor the payment module
Claude: [barely enough context left for the actual work]

# GOOD: Delegate investigation
You: Use a subagent to investigate how the payment system works,
     then summarize the architecture for me
Claude: [spawns agent, agent reads 20 files, returns 500-token summary]
You: Now refactor the payment module
Claude: [has the summary AND plenty of context for the refactoring]

As discussed in Chapter 12, the Agent tool is the primary mechanism for keeping your main context clean during research-heavy tasks.

4. Read Specific Line Ranges¶

Never read an entire file when you only need a function:

# Costly: 2000 lines, ~8K tokens
Read /src/services/payment.py

# Efficient: 30 lines, ~150 tokens
Read /src/services/payment.py offset=142 limit=30

Use Grep first to find the relevant lines, then Read with an offset:

# Find the function
Grep: pattern="def process_payment" path="/src/services/payment.py"
# Result: line 142

# Read just that function
Read /src/services/payment.py offset=140 limit=40

5. Limit Search Results¶

The Grep tool has a head_limit parameter that defaults to 250 results. For exploratory searches, you rarely need more than 10-20 results:

# Default: up to 250 results, could be thousands of lines
Grep: pattern="TODO" path="/src"

# Better: just show me the first 10
Grep: pattern="TODO" path="/src" head_limit=10

6. Suppress Unnecessary Bash Output¶

Build logs, dependency installation output, and verbose test runs generate enormous amounts of tokens that rarely contain useful information:

bash
# Bad: dumps entire npm install output into context
npm install

# Better: suppress output, only show errors
npm install 2>&1 | tail -5

# Bad: full verbose test output
python -m pytest tests/ -v --tb=long

# Better: summary only
python -m pytest tests/ -q

What Happens When You Hit the Limit¶

When the conversation approaches the 200K token limit, Claude Code takes action automatically:

  1. Auto-compaction triggers. Older messages are summarized to free space. This happens transparently – you will not see a notification, but you might notice Claude losing awareness of earlier discussion details.

  2. Response quality degrades. As context fills, Claude’s attention to older content becomes less reliable. Instructions from early in the session may be followed less accurately.

  3. Eventually, the session stalls. If compaction cannot free enough space, Claude may be unable to generate a useful response. At this point, you need to /clear or start a fresh session.

The practical takeaway: Sessions are not meant to last forever. For most work, a focused 20-40 turn session is optimal. If you find yourself at 100+ turns, you are almost certainly carrying dead context from earlier tasks.

Monitoring Your Context Usage¶

Claude Code provides three commands for monitoring:

/context¶

Shows a visual bar of how full your context window is:

Context: ████████████░░░░░░░░ 62% (124K / 200K tokens)
  System prompt:  48K (24%)
  Conversation:   71K (35.5%)
  Tool defs:      28K (14%)
  Reserve:        20K (10%)
  Available:      33K (16.5%)

Run this periodically during long sessions. When you see conversation above 70%, consider /compact or /clear.

/cost¶

Shows the API cost for the current session:

Session cost: $8.42
  Input tokens:  892,000 ($13.38 gross, $4.12 after cache)
  Output tokens: 56,000 ($4.20)
  Thinking:      12,000 ($0.10)
  Turns: 34
  Avg cost/turn: $0.25

/tokens¶

Shows a detailed token breakdown per message, useful for identifying which tool calls consumed the most context.

Cost Optimization Patterns¶

Here are six patterns ordered from highest to lowest impact:

1. Start with Sonnet, Escalate to Opus When Needed¶

Sonnet is 5x cheaper than Opus and handles most coding tasks well. Use Opus for tasks that require deep reasoning: complex debugging, security audits, architectural decisions, nuanced code review.

# Daily workflow
/model sonnet          # Default for most work

# Escalate for hard problems
/model opus
ultrathink Debug why the distributed lock fails under contention

# Drop back after
/model sonnet

2. Match Effort to Task Complexity¶

/effort low            # Formatting, renaming, simple edits
/effort medium         # Standard feature work, test writing
/effort high           # Debugging, security, architecture

The difference between low and high effort can be 5-10x in thinking tokens per turn. Over a 50-turn session, that adds up.

3. /clear Aggressively¶

Every /clear saves you money on subsequent turns. The cached system prompt reprices at cache-read rates (10x cheaper), and you stop paying to re-read irrelevant conversation history.

4. Delegate to Agents with Appropriate Models¶

Use the Agent tool with model-appropriate delegation:

# Expensive: Opus agent for simple research
Agent: [model=opus] "What version of React is in package.json?"

# Cheap: Haiku or Sonnet agent for simple lookups
Agent: [model=haiku] "What version of React is in package.json?"

5. Read Files Strategically¶

Before reading a file, ask: do I need the whole thing, or just a section? Use Grep to find relevant lines, then Read with offset and limit.

6. Suppress Verbose Output¶

Pipe build and test output through tail, head, or grep to keep only the relevant lines. A full build log can consume 10,000+ tokens that add zero value to the conversation.

Real-World Cost Scenarios¶

Here is what different work patterns actually cost with Opus 4 as the primary model:

Scenario Turns Duration Estimated Cost
Quick bug fix (read file, fix, test) 5-10 5-10 min $0.50 - $2.00
Feature implementation 20-40 20-40 min $5 - $15
Large refactoring (multi-file) 40-80 1-2 hours $15 - $40
Multi-agent investigation + fix 30-60 30-60 min $20 - $50
Full workday of active use 200-400 6-8 hours $50 - $150
Book chapter drafting 10-20 30-60 min $3 - $10

These numbers assume a mix of file reads, code generation, test execution, and conversation. Heavy file-reading sessions (architecture analysis, code review across many files) skew toward the higher end.

A Worked Example: Bug Fix Session¶

Let us trace the token flow through a real bug fix to make these costs concrete. You are using Opus 4.

Turn 1: You type "Fix the race condition in session.py:89"
  System prompt (cached): 45K tokens × $1.50/MTok = $0.067
  Your message: 20 tokens × $15/MTok = $0.0003
  Claude reads session.py (800 lines): ~3,200 tokens input
  Claude's response (analysis + plan): ~500 tokens output × $75/MTok = $0.037
  Turn cost: ~$0.15

Turn 2: Claude reads two related files
  History replay (cached portions): ~5K tokens
  File reads: ~6,000 tokens input
  Response (identifies root cause): ~800 tokens output = $0.06
  Turn cost: ~$0.20

Turn 3: Claude writes a failing test
  History replay: ~12K tokens
  Writes test file: ~400 tokens output = $0.03
  Turn cost: ~$0.22

Turn 4: Claude implements the fix
  History replay: ~15K tokens
  Edits session.py: ~300 tokens output = $0.022
  Turn cost: ~$0.25

Turn 5: Claude runs pytest
  History replay: ~17K tokens
  Bash output (test results): ~500 tokens
  Response (confirms fix): ~200 tokens output = $0.015
  Turn cost: ~$0.28

Total session: ~$1.10 across 5 turns

The key observation: input costs dominate because the conversation history replays on every turn. By turn 5, Claude is re-reading 17K tokens of history. If this were an unfocused session with 50K tokens of irrelevant prior context, that same 5-turn bug fix would cost $3-4 instead of $1.10.

Cost reduction with Sonnet: Divide the Opus estimates by roughly 4-5x. A full workday on Sonnet typically costs $10-$35.

Cost reduction with effort management: Using /effort low for simple tasks and /effort high only when needed typically saves 20-30% compared to running high effort all day.

The Context Engineering Mindset¶

The term context engineering describes the discipline of deliberately managing what information Claude has access to at any given moment. It is not just about saving money – it is about getting better output.

Consider an analogy: if you walked into a meeting and dumped 375 pages of documents on the table, then asked a question, you would get a worse answer than if you brought just the three relevant pages. Claude works the same way. More context is not always better context. Relevant context is better context.

The context engineering mindset has three principles:

1. Load what you need, not what you have. Before reading a file, ask: does Claude need this to do the task? If the task is “fix the login bug,” Claude probably needs auth.py and test_auth.py, not the entire src/ directory.

2. Clear what you do not need. After finishing a task, /clear. After investigation, /compact. Do not let dead context accumulate and dilute the signal.

3. Budget before you build. For complex tasks, estimate the context cost before starting. If you need to read 30 files to understand a system, delegate that to a subagent. If you need to run a full test suite, capture only the failures.

This mindset connects directly to the CLAUDE.md design principles from Chapter 4 (keep it lean), the skill design from Chapter 7 (focused instructions), and the agent patterns from Chapter 12 (delegation for context isolation). Context engineering is not a single technique – it is the thread that ties professional Claude Code usage together.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

“I’ll just read everything so Claude has full context.” Reading 20 files to give Claude “the whole picture” fills 60% of your context window with content Claude may never reference. It also costs $3-5 in input tokens on Opus. Read surgically.

“I’ve been in this session for 3 hours, it should remember everything.” After auto-compaction kicks in, Claude’s recall of early-session content is lossy at best. If you need details from earlier, re-read the relevant file or re-state the key facts.

“Opus is better, so I should always use Opus.” Opus is better at reasoning, not at everything. For straightforward code generation, test writing, formatting, and simple edits, Sonnet produces equivalent output at 20% of the cost.

“I don’t need to worry about tokens, my company pays for it.” Even if cost is not a concern, context window saturation is. A full context window produces worse output regardless of who is paying.

Chapter Summary¶

  1. The 200K context window is shared across system prompt (~25%), tool definitions (~15%), conversation history (~50%), and response reserve (~10%). Your working budget is roughly 100K tokens.

  2. Token costs are asymmetric. Output costs 5x input. Opus costs 5x Sonnet. Cache reads cost 10x less than fresh input. These ratios define your optimization strategy.

  3. The biggest token consumers are large file reads, verbose bash output, and accumulated tool results. Manage these three and you control your costs.

  4. /clear is your most important habit. Use it between every unrelated task. It frees context, reduces costs, and improves output quality.

  5. Context engineering is the meta-skill of professional Claude Code usage. Load what you need, clear what you do not, and budget before you build.

Chapter 14: Memory, Sessions, and Persistence¶ ~18 min read

You spend forty-five minutes teaching Claude Code about your project’s authentication architecture – the custom JWT rotation scheme, the Redis session store, the edge cases around token refresh during deployments. The session produces excellent results. You close the terminal. The next morning, you open Claude Code and ask a follow-up question about the same authentication system. Claude has no idea what you are talking about.

This is the persistence problem. By default, Claude Code sessions are ephemeral. When the session ends, the conversation is gone. But Anthropic built three mechanisms to bridge this gap: auto-memory, session storage, and plan files. Understanding how each works – and when to use which – is the difference between starting fresh every morning and having Claude Code that actually learns from working with you.

The Three Persistence Mechanisms¶

Before diving into details, here is the landscape:

Mechanism What Persists Where Stored Lifespan Loaded Automatically
Auto-memory Facts, preferences, corrections ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/memory/ Permanent (until deleted) Yes, every session
Sessions Full conversation history SQLite database Until explicitly deleted No, must /resume
Plan files Structured implementation plans .claude/plans/ Until deleted No, loaded on demand

Each serves a different purpose. Auto-memory captures what Claude should always know. Sessions capture what happened in a conversation. Plans capture what we decided to do and why.

Auto-Memory: Claude’s Long-Term Knowledge¶

The auto-memory system is Claude Code’s mechanism for retaining important information across sessions. When you correct Claude, state a preference, or share project context that should persist, Claude writes it to a memory file that gets loaded at the start of every future session.

Where Memory Lives¶

Memory files are stored at:

~/.claude/projects/<project-hash>/memory/
├── MEMORY.md                          # Index file (loaded every session)
├── user_preferences.md                # Your personal preferences
├── project_architecture.md            # Project-specific context
├── feedback_no_force_push.md          # A correction you gave
└── reference_api_conventions.md       # Domain knowledge

The <project-hash> is derived from your project’s absolute path, so memories are scoped per project. Claude working on /projects/webapp has different memories than Claude working on /projects/cli-tool.

MEMORY.md – The Index¶

MEMORY.md is the master index file. It is loaded into the system prompt at the start of every session. It contains one-line summaries of each memory file with links:

markdown
# Memory Index

- [user_preferences.md](user_preferences.md) -- Prefers terse output, uses ruff, type hints required
- [project_architecture.md](project_architecture.md) -- Django 5.1, PostgreSQL, Redis cache, Celery workers
- [feedback_no_force_push.md](feedback_no_force_push.md) -- Never force-push to main; all changes via PR
- [reference_api_conventions.md](reference_api_conventions.md) -- REST API follows JSON:API spec, snake_case fields

Because MEMORY.md is loaded into the system prompt, it counts toward your system prompt token budget (as discussed in Chapter 13). A bloated MEMORY.md with 50 entries adds significant overhead. Keep it under 200 lines – more on hygiene later.

Memory File Anatomy¶

Each memory file follows a consistent structure with YAML frontmatter:

markdown
---
name: No force push to main
description: Team rule -- all changes go through PRs, never push directly to main
type: feedback
---

Never force-push to main or push directly to main branch.

**Why:** Production deploys from main automatically. A bad push = instant outage.
**How to apply:** Always create a branch, always create a PR, always wait for CI.

The frontmatter fields:

Field Purpose
name Short identifier for the memory
description One-line summary (appears in MEMORY.md index)
type Category: user, feedback, project, reference

Memory Types¶

user type – Information about you as an engineer:

markdown
---
name: User profile
description: Senior engineer, security focus, prefers concise output
type: user
---

- Senior security engineer at a mid-stage startup
- Expert in Python, TypeScript, and infrastructure
- Prefers concise output -- lead with the answer
- Uses vim keybindings
- Timezone: US Pacific

feedback type – Corrections and behavioral adjustments:

markdown
---
name: Always run tests before committing
description: Correction -- never commit without running the test suite first
type: feedback
---

Always run `python -m pytest tests/` before creating a git commit.
Previous behavior: committed code without running tests.
Corrected behavior: run tests, fix failures, then commit.

project type – Project-specific technical context:

markdown
---
name: Authentication architecture
description: JWT + Redis session store with 15-minute rotation
type: project
---

## Authentication System
- JWT tokens with 15-minute expiry
- Refresh tokens stored in Redis (7-day TTL)
- Token rotation: new access token issued on each refresh
- Edge case: during deployments, accept tokens up to 30 min old (grace period)
- Auth middleware: src/middleware/auth.py
- Token service: src/services/token_service.py

reference type – Domain knowledge and conventions:

markdown
---
name: API conventions
description: REST API follows JSON:API spec with snake_case fields
type: reference
---

## API Conventions
- All endpoints return JSON:API format
- Field names: snake_case (not camelCase)
- Pagination: cursor-based, not offset
- Error format: { "errors": [{ "code": "...", "detail": "..." }] }
- Versioning: URL-based (/api/v2/)

When Claude Auto-Saves Memories¶

Claude creates memories automatically in these situations:

  1. You correct Claude’s behavior. “Don’t use print statements, use logging.” Claude saves this as a feedback memory so it applies the correction in all future sessions.

  2. You state a preference. “I prefer Pydantic over dataclasses for API models.” Claude saves this as a user preference.

  3. You share project context that would be lost. “The payment service uses Stripe webhooks on /api/webhooks/stripe.” Claude saves this as a project memory.

  4. Claude discovers something important during work. After investigating your codebase, Claude might save an architecture note about how your modules connect.

Auto-save is not always perfect. Sometimes Claude saves too aggressively (trivial details) or not aggressively enough (missing important context). You can manage this explicitly.

Manual Memory Management¶

Asking Claude to remember something:

Remember: our staging environment uses a separate Stripe test key,
stored in AWS Secrets Manager under /staging/stripe/api-key

Claude creates a memory file and adds it to MEMORY.md.

Asking Claude to forget something:

Forget the memory about our old deployment process -- we switched to Kubernetes last month

Claude removes the memory file and its MEMORY.md entry.

Reviewing what Claude remembers:

What do you remember about this project?

Claude lists its loaded memories. You can also read MEMORY.md directly:

bash
cat ~/.claude/projects/*/memory/MEMORY.md

Memory Hygiene¶

Over time, memories accumulate. Some become outdated. Others are too granular to be worth the token cost. Here are the hygiene practices:

Keep MEMORY.md under 200 lines. Each memory entry in MEMORY.md adds to your system prompt cost. At 200 lines, MEMORY.md alone consumes roughly 800-1,200 tokens – manageable. At 500 lines, you are spending 2,000-3,000 tokens on memory before you type a word.

Consolidate related memories. Five separate memories about API conventions should be one comprehensive reference_api_conventions.md.

Delete outdated memories. If you migrated from Django to FastAPI, the Django-specific memories are not just useless – they are actively harmful, causing Claude to suggest Django patterns in a FastAPI codebase.

Audit quarterly. Once per quarter, read through MEMORY.md and ask: is each of these still true? Is each worth the token cost?

Review all memories for this project. Flag any that are outdated
or could be consolidated.

Memory Conflict Resolution¶

What happens when a memory contradicts your current instructions? Consider this scenario:

markdown
# Memory (saved 3 months ago):
---
name: Database ORM
description: Project uses SQLAlchemy for all database operations
type: project
---
Always use SQLAlchemy for database queries.

But you just migrated to Tortoise ORM. If you ask Claude to write a database query, the memory says SQLAlchemy while the actual codebase uses Tortoise. Claude handles this with a simple priority rule: current context beats stored memory. If Claude reads your models and sees Tortoise ORM, it will use Tortoise despite the memory. But this creates confusion and wasted reasoning – Claude spends thinking tokens reconciling the conflict.

The fix is proactive: update or delete outdated memories when your stack changes.

Forget the memory about SQLAlchemy. We migrated to Tortoise ORM.
Remember: this project uses Tortoise ORM with asyncpg for PostgreSQL.

Cross-Project vs. Project-Scoped Memory¶

Memory files are scoped to the project directory path. This means:

  • Memories saved while working in /projects/webapp are only loaded in /projects/webapp
  • Working in /projects/cli-tool has its own separate memory store
  • Personal preferences (like “I prefer terse output”) need to be saved in each project separately, OR stored in the global user memory

The global memory at ~/.claude/memory/ applies across all projects. Use it for universal preferences:

# Global preference (applies everywhere)
Remember globally: I prefer concise responses. Lead with the answer,
skip the preamble.

# Project preference (applies only here)
Remember: this project uses Celery for background jobs with Redis
as the broker.

The distinction matters. Your coding style preferences belong in global memory. Your project’s database choice belongs in project memory. Putting project details in global memory pollutes every session for every project.

Session Management¶

While auto-memory captures key facts, sessions capture the full conversation history. Every Claude Code session is automatically saved to a SQLite database on your machine.

Session Storage¶

Sessions are stored locally. Each session includes:

  • Complete message history (your messages and Claude’s responses)
  • All tool calls and their results
  • Files that were read
  • Metadata: start time, duration, model used, token count

Listing and Resuming Sessions¶

/sessions                    # List recent sessions with timestamps and summaries

Output looks like:

Recent sessions:
  #1  2h ago   "Fixed auth bug in login.py" (34 turns, opus)
  #2  5h ago   "Refactored payment module" (28 turns, sonnet)
  #3  1d ago   "Wrote chapter 12 of the handbook" (15 turns, opus)
  #4  2d ago   "Set up CI/CD pipeline" (42 turns, sonnet)

To continue a previous session:

/resume 1                    # Resume the auth bug session

Resuming a session loads the full conversation history back into context. This means:

  • Claude remembers everything from that session
  • The context window fills up with the old conversation
  • You pick up exactly where you left off

When to resume vs. start fresh: Resume when you are continuing the exact same task (debugging a tricky issue across multiple sittings, for example). Start fresh when the context from the old session would be noise. In practice, starting fresh with auto-memory providing background knowledge is the better choice 80% of the time.

Exporting Sessions¶

/export session-auth-debug.md    # Save conversation as readable markdown

Exported sessions are useful for:

  • Creating documentation from a debugging session
  • Sharing a Claude Code workflow with teammates
  • Archiving a particularly productive session as a reference
  • Post-mortem analysis of what went wrong

Session Search¶

When you have dozens of past sessions, finding the right one to resume matters:

/sessions --search "payment refactor"    # Search session summaries

Claude Code indexes session summaries so you can search by topic, file names mentioned, or keywords from the conversation. This is particularly useful when you are picking up work from days or weeks ago and cannot remember which session contained the relevant context.

Session Size and Resumption Costs¶

A critical detail most people overlook: resuming a session replays its entire history into your context window. A 50-turn session with heavy file reads might contain 80,000 tokens of history. Resuming it immediately consumes 80,000 tokens – 40% of your total window – before you type a single new message.

This has cost implications too. On Opus, resuming an 80K-token session costs:

80,000 tokens * $15 / 1,000,000 = $1.20 (first turn after resume)
80,000 tokens * $1.50 / 1,000,000 = $0.12 (subsequent turns, cached)

For long sessions, it is often cheaper to start fresh and re-read the specific files you need than to resume and pay for 80K tokens of mostly-irrelevant history.

Rule of thumb: Resume sessions under 20 turns. For longer sessions, start fresh and let auto-memory bridge the gap.

Session Branching¶

Claude Code supports session branching – creating a fork of the current session to try an experimental approach without contaminating your main line of work:

/branch experiment-new-auth     # Fork the session here

If the experiment works, you continue on the branch. If it fails, the original session is uncontaminated. This is conceptually similar to git branching but for conversations.

Practical use cases for session branching:

  • Risky refactoring: Branch before attempting a large change. If it breaks everything, discard the branch.
  • Alternative approaches: Branch to try approach A, then go back and try approach B, and compare results.
  • Exploratory investigation: Branch to investigate a hypothesis without cluttering the main debugging session.

Context Compaction¶

As discussed in Chapter 13, the context window is finite. Context compaction is the mechanism for freeing space in a long-running session by summarizing older messages.

Manual Compaction¶

/compact                        # Summarize everything older, keep recent turns
/compact authentication         # Summarize everything except auth-related content
/compact --keep-last 10         # Keep the last 10 turns detailed, summarize the rest

The /compact command with a focus topic is particularly powerful. When you run /compact authentication, Claude:

  1. Reads through the entire conversation history
  2. Identifies messages related to authentication
  3. Preserves those messages in full detail
  4. Summarizes everything else into a brief recap

The result is a smaller context window that retains the information you actually need.

Automatic Compaction¶

When the context window approaches its limit, Claude Code triggers automatic compaction. You will not see a notification – it happens silently between turns. The symptoms:

  • Claude seems to “forget” details from early in the session
  • Responses that should reference earlier work miss the context
  • The /context command shows a drop in usage (older messages were summarized)

Automatic compaction is a last resort. It means you have been in the session too long without managing your context. The professional approach is to compact manually or /clear before you hit the automatic threshold.

Quality Implications¶

Compaction is lossy. When older messages are summarized:

  • Specific code details are lost. Claude will not remember the exact line numbers it suggested earlier.
  • Nuanced reasoning is flattened. A 500-word explanation of why a particular approach was chosen becomes a one-line summary.
  • Tool results disappear. The contents of files Claude read earlier are no longer in context.

This is why /clear between tasks is almost always better than running one marathon session. A fresh session with relevant files re-read is higher quality than a compacted session with summarized ghosts of old context.

Strategic Compaction¶

The best time to compact is before starting a complex sub-task within a longer session:

# You've been debugging for 20 minutes, found the root cause.
# Before starting the fix:
/compact root-cause-analysis

# Now Claude has a clean context with the root cause preserved,
# and plenty of room for the implementation work.

Plan Mode¶

For complex tasks that affect multiple files or involve architectural decisions, Claude Code offers plan mode – a structured approach where Claude investigates and plans before writing any code.

Entering Plan Mode¶

/plan                           # Enter plan mode

Or use the EnterPlanMode command:

Plan the migration from SQLAlchemy to async SQLAlchemy across the project

What Happens in Plan Mode¶

In plan mode, Claude operates in read-only. It can:

  • Read files
  • Search the codebase
  • Analyze architecture
  • Ask clarifying questions

It cannot:

  • Edit files
  • Write new files
  • Run commands that modify state
  • Create commits

The output is a structured plan document:

markdown
# Migration Plan: SQLAlchemy to Async SQLAlchemy

## Scope
- 14 files affected across src/models/, src/services/, src/api/
- 3 migration scripts needed
- Test suite changes in 8 files

## Step 1: Update Dependencies
- Replace sqlalchemy with sqlalchemy[asyncio] in pyproject.toml
- Add asyncpg (async PostgreSQL driver)
- Remove psycopg2-binary

## Step 2: Convert Session Management
- File: src/db/session.py
- Change: Replace Session with AsyncSession
- Change: Replace create_engine with create_async_engine
...

## Step 3: Convert Models
[detailed per-file changes]

## Step 4: Convert Service Layer
[detailed per-file changes]

## Step 5: Update Tests
[detailed per-file changes]

## Risks
- Async context propagation in middleware (medium risk)
- Background tasks currently use sync sessions (needs Celery async support)

## Estimated effort: 3-4 hours with Claude Code assistance

Approving and Executing Plans¶

After Claude presents the plan, you review it:

Looks good, but skip step 5 for now -- I'll update tests separately.
Execute the plan.

Claude exits plan mode and begins implementing the approved steps. The plan remains in context as a reference, keeping Claude aligned with the agreed-upon approach.

When to Use Plan Mode¶

  • Multi-file changes affecting 5+ files
  • Architectural decisions where the approach matters more than the code
  • Risky operations where you want to review before any writes happen
  • Complex refactoring where the order of changes matters
  • Onboarding to a new codebase – “Plan how you would add a notification system”

Plan Storage¶

Plans are saved to .claude/plans/:

.claude/plans/
├── 2026-03-15-async-migration.md
├── 2026-03-20-auth-refactor.md
└── 2026-04-01-api-v2-design.md

These plans serve as lightweight Architecture Decision Records. They capture not just what was done, but why it was done that way.

The /clear Discipline¶

If you take one thing from this chapter, take this: /clear between unrelated tasks is the most important productivity habit in Claude Code.

Why /clear Matters¶

When you work on Task A and then switch to Task B without clearing:

  1. Context bleed. Claude’s responses to Task B are influenced by Task A’s context. It might suggest patterns from Task A, reference files from Task A, or make assumptions based on Task A’s discussion.

  2. Token waste. Every turn of Task B pays to re-read Task A’s conversation history. If Task A consumed 40K tokens of context, you are paying input costs on those 40K tokens for every single turn of Task B.

  3. Reduced working space. Those 40K tokens of Task A context reduce your available window for Task B. Complex work in Task B might trigger premature auto-compaction.

The Anti-Pattern¶

9:00 AM  Fix login bug in auth.py
9:30 AM  Write unit tests for the payment module
10:00 AM Review PR #142
10:30 AM Debug the CI pipeline failure
11:00 AM Write API documentation

Five unrelated tasks, one continuous session. By 11 AM, the context window is saturated with fragments of all five tasks. Claude is confused, expensive, and producing mediocre output.

The Pattern¶

9:00 AM  Fix login bug in auth.py
9:25 AM  /clear
9:25 AM  Write unit tests for the payment module
9:55 AM  /clear
9:55 AM  Review PR #142
10:15 AM /clear
10:15 AM Debug the CI pipeline failure
10:45 AM /clear
10:45 AM Write API documentation

Five focused sessions. Each task gets Claude’s full attention and full context window. Auto-memory carries forward anything important. Total cost is lower because each session starts with a clean, cached system prompt.

When NOT to /clear¶

Do not clear when you are still working on the same task. If you are debugging an issue, you want Claude to remember the hypotheses it explored, the files it read, and the approaches it tried. Clearing mid-task forces you to re-establish all that context.

The rule is simple: same task, keep the context. New task, clear the context.

The /btw Command¶

Sometimes you need a quick answer to a side question without derailing your current task. The /btw command handles this:

# You're deep in refactoring auth.py
/btw What does the @lru_cache decorator do with a maxsize of None?

Claude answers the side question without incorporating it into the main conversation context. The answer appears, but the follow-up turns continue as if the side question never happened.

Use /btw for:

  • Quick syntax lookups mid-task
  • “What does this error code mean?” questions
  • Checking a fact without changing Claude’s focus
  • “How do I do X in bash again?” utility questions

The /rewind Command¶

When Claude goes down a wrong path – suggests a bad approach, makes an incorrect fix, or misunderstands your intent – /rewind undoes the last exchange:

You: Refactor auth.py to use a class-based approach
Claude: [rewrites entire file as a class hierarchy]
You: /rewind
# Claude's class rewrite is removed from context
You: Refactor auth.py to extract the validation logic into a separate function
Claude: [makes the targeted change you actually wanted]

Multiple /rewind commands go further back:

/rewind          # Undo last exchange
/rewind          # Undo the one before that
/rewind          # Go back three exchanges

This is cheaper and faster than trying to correct Claude’s course with additional prompts. Each correction prompt adds to context and cost. /rewind removes the mistake entirely.

Practical Persistence Workflow¶

Here is how all these mechanisms work together in a typical day:

Morning Start¶

# Open Claude Code in your project
# Auto-memory loads automatically:
#   - Your preferences (terse output, type hints, ruff)
#   - Project architecture (Django, PostgreSQL, Redis)
#   - Past corrections (no force-push, always test first)
#   - Domain knowledge (API conventions, deployment process)

Claude already knows who you are and how your project works. No re-explaining.

Complex Task¶

You: /plan
You: Plan how to add WebSocket support for real-time notifications

Claude: [investigates codebase in read-only mode]
Claude: [presents structured plan with 6 steps, file list, risks]

You: Approved with one change: use Redis pub/sub instead of
     in-memory channels
Claude: [exits plan mode, begins implementation]

Mid-Session Maintenance¶

# 45 minutes in, context is getting heavy
/context
# Shows: 78% full

/compact websocket-implementation
# Context drops to 45%, WebSocket work preserved in detail

Task Switch¶

# WebSocket implementation is done
/clear

# New task with fresh context
You: Review PR #287

End of Day¶

# Claude auto-saved several memories during the day:
# - WebSocket architecture decisions
# - A correction about Redis channel naming
# - Your preference for async test fixtures

# These will be available tomorrow morning automatically

Important Insight Preservation¶

If something important came up that Claude might not auto-save:

You: Remember: the WebSocket gateway runs on port 8765 in development
     and is behind the /ws path in production (nginx proxies it)

This creates an explicit memory file that loads in every future session for this project.

The Interplay Between Persistence Mechanisms¶

The three mechanisms are not isolated – they work together. Understanding their interactions makes the system more powerful.

Memory Informs Session Quality¶

When you start a fresh session, auto-memory provides the baseline knowledge that would otherwise require 10-15 turns of explanation. Without memory:

Turn 1: "We use Django with PostgreSQL"
Turn 2: "Our API follows JSON:API spec"
Turn 3: "Never force-push to main"
Turn 4: "I prefer type hints and ruff"
Turn 5: NOW you can start actual work

With memory, all of that context loads automatically. Turn 1 is your actual task. Over a year of daily use, this saves hundreds of turns and significant cost.

Plans Bridge Sessions¶

A plan created in one session persists on disk. When you start a new session the next day, you can reference it:

Continue implementing the async migration plan from yesterday.
We finished steps 1-3, pick up at step 4.

Claude reads the plan file from .claude/plans/, understands the full scope, and knows exactly where you left off – even without resuming the original session. This is more efficient than resuming because:

  1. The plan file is typically 500-2,000 tokens (vs. 50K+ for a full session)
  2. Claude has a clear roadmap instead of having to re-derive intent from old messages
  3. The context window is clean for the actual implementation work

When Each Mechanism Fails¶

Auto-memory fails when the information changes frequently. Do not save volatile data (current sprint goals, this week’s deadline, today’s branch name) as memories – they become stale immediately.

Sessions fail for long-running tasks. If a task spans three days, you do not want to resume a 3-day-old session. Use plan files to capture the state, auto-memory to capture the learnings, and start fresh each day.

Plans fail for tasks that evolve significantly. If the plan said “migrate to async SQLAlchemy” but you discovered halfway through that Tortoise ORM is a better fit, the plan is now misleading. Update or replace it.

The professional approach: use all three in combination. Plans for structure, memory for knowledge, and fresh sessions for execution. Do not rely on any single mechanism.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

“I never need /clear because my sessions are short.” Even short sessions accumulate context from tool results. If your “short” session reads 10 files and runs 5 bash commands, it might consume 50K tokens. The next task starts with half its budget gone.

“I’ll just tell Claude what to remember.” While explicit memory saves work, they are not a substitute for Claude’s auto-memory. Let Claude save what it thinks is important, and supplement with explicit saves for information Claude might not recognize as worth keeping.

“Plan mode is too slow – I’ll just start coding.” For small changes, yes, skip plan mode. But for anything touching 5+ files, the ten minutes spent planning saves an hour of rework when Claude’s approach diverges from what you wanted at step 3 of 8.

“I resumed my session from last week, so Claude should know everything.” Resuming loads the conversation history, but the context window still has a limit. A 200-turn session from last week will auto-compact heavily when resumed, losing most of the detail. It is often better to start fresh and let auto-memory provide the persistent knowledge.

“More memories are better.” Each memory file adds tokens to every session’s system prompt. Twenty memory files might add 5,000 tokens to your baseline cost. Be selective. Save what is truly persistent and useful, not every detail from every debugging session.

Chapter Summary¶

  1. Three persistence mechanisms serve different purposes: auto-memory for permanent knowledge (loaded every session), sessions for conversation history (resumed on demand), and plans for structured implementation decisions (loaded on demand).

  2. Auto-memory is your most valuable persistence tool. It bridges sessions automatically, teaching Claude about your preferences, your project, and your corrections. Keep MEMORY.md under 200 lines and audit it quarterly.

  3. Session management is about discipline. /clear between tasks, /compact when context gets heavy, /rewind when Claude goes wrong, /btw for side questions. These four commands are your context control panel.

  4. Plan mode prevents expensive mistakes. For complex multi-file changes, the upfront cost of planning is always less than the cost of correcting a wrong approach mid-implementation.

  5. The persistence workflow is: auto-memory for knowledge, /clear for hygiene, /compact for maintenance, plan mode for complex work. Master this cycle and Claude Code becomes a tool that genuinely learns from working with you.

Chapter 15: Prompt Engineering for Code Generation¶ ~18 min read

A junior engineer types “fix the bug” into Claude Code and spends thirty minutes clarifying what bug, which file, what the expected behavior should be. A senior engineer types “fix the null pointer in src/auth/validator.py:142 – the user object can be None when the session expires mid-request” and gets a working fix in one turn. Same tool. Same model. Wildly different outcomes.

The difference is not “prompting” – it is prompt engineering: the systematic practice of crafting inputs that consistently produce correct, complete, first-try results from an AI coding agent. In a chat interface, a mediocre prompt wastes a few seconds. In Claude Code, a mediocre prompt wastes minutes of tool execution, dollars of API costs, and thousands of tokens of context window. Your prompt determines not just what Claude writes, but which tools it selects, which files it reads, which approaches it considers, and when it stops.

This chapter covers the five-layer prompt stack, twenty battle-tested patterns, the anti-patterns that burn money, and the meta-skill of knowing when to stop prompting and start configuring.

Prompting vs. Prompt Engineering¶

Prompting is typing what you want and hoping for the best:

fix the login bug

Prompt engineering is systematic input design that controls the output:

Fix the race condition in src/auth/session.py:89 where concurrent
requests can both pass the token-expiry check before either invalidates
the token. Write a failing test first, then fix with a Redis-based
distributed lock. Run the test to confirm.

The second prompt is not just “more detailed.” It does five things the first prompt cannot:

  1. Locates the target (file and line number)
  2. Describes the root cause (race condition, concurrent requests)
  3. Specifies the approach (Redis distributed lock)
  4. Defines the workflow (test first, then fix)
  5. Sets the stopping condition (run the test to confirm)

Claude Code amplifies the difference between good and bad prompts because it makes autonomous decisions. With a vague prompt, Claude decides which files to read, which approach to take, and when to stop. With a precise prompt, you make those decisions and Claude executes them.

The Prompt Engineering Stack¶

When you type a message into Claude Code, your text is just one of five layers that influence Claude’s behavior. Understanding all five layers is essential because they interact – and sometimes conflict.

┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 5: Memory Layer (persistent)       │
│  Auto-memory, preferences, corrections    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 4: Message Layer (ephemeral)       │
│  Your actual typed prompt                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 3: Context Layer (accumulated)     │
│  Files read, search results, tool output  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 2: Skill Layer (per-workflow)      │
│  Active skill's SKILL.md instructions     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 1: System Layer (persistent)       │
│  CLAUDE.md, rules, settings, tool defs    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘

Layer 1: System Layer¶

This is CLAUDE.md, your rule files, settings.json, and tool definitions. It loads every session and defines Claude’s baseline behavior: coding standards, communication style, permission boundaries. As discussed in Chapter 4, this is the most cost-effective place to put instructions that apply universally.

Layer 2: Skill Layer¶

When a skill activates (either via /slash-command or auto-detection), its SKILL.md loads into context. This provides workflow-specific instructions: “When doing a code review, check security first, then logic, then performance.” Skills are the mid-tier of the stack – more specific than system instructions, more persistent than a single message.

Layer 3: Context Layer¶

Everything Claude has read or received during the session: file contents, grep results, bash output, previous tool calls. This layer is accumulated – it grows with every turn. It is also the most expensive layer in terms of tokens, as discussed in Chapter 13.

Layer 4: Message Layer¶

Your actual typed prompt. This is the most visible layer but often the least impactful, because layers 1-3 already define most of Claude’s behavior. A well-configured system means your messages can be short and still produce excellent results.

Layer 5: Memory Layer¶

Auto-memory facts and preferences from previous sessions. These load into the system prompt and influence Claude’s defaults: “User prefers Pydantic over dataclasses,” “Never force-push to main,” “Project uses Redis for caching.”

Why the Stack Matters¶

When your prompt says one thing but your CLAUDE.md says another, Claude follows CLAUDE.md. When a memory says “use dataclasses” but you type “use Pydantic for this model,” your message wins for this turn but the memory persists for future sessions.

Understanding the stack helps you place instructions at the right level:

Instruction Type Best Layer
“Always use type hints in Python” System (CLAUDE.md or rule)
“When reviewing code, check security first” Skill (SKILL.md)
“Read auth.py before fixing this bug” Message (your prompt)
“I prefer terse output” Memory (auto-saved or explicit)

The highest-leverage prompt engineering happens at layers 1 and 2, not layer 4. But since most engineers interact primarily at layer 4, the rest of this chapter focuses on message-layer patterns.

The 20 Prompt Patterns¶

These patterns are drawn from thousands of hours of professional Claude Code usage. Each one addresses a specific failure mode of unstructured prompting.

Pattern 1: The Constraint Pattern¶

When to use: You want Claude to work within specific boundaries – no external dependencies, specific language version, particular library choices.

Example:

Implement a rate limiter for our API endpoints. Constraints:
- Use only Python stdlib (no redis, no external packages)
- Must be thread-safe (we run with gunicorn workers)
- Token bucket algorithm
- Configuration via environment variables

Why it works: Without constraints, Claude makes its own technology choices. It might pull in Redis, add a new pip dependency, or choose an algorithm that does not fit your architecture. Constraints eliminate entire branches of Claude’s decision tree.

Pro tip: Negative constraints (“do NOT use X”) are even more powerful than positive ones. Claude tends to reach for popular solutions; telling it what to avoid forces creative alternatives.

Pattern 2: The Architecture-First Pattern¶

When to use: Before implementing anything complex, you want Claude to show you the design.

Example:

Before writing any code, show me the architecture for a webhook
processing system:
- Component diagram (Mermaid)
- Data flow for incoming webhooks
- How retries and dead-letter queue work
- Which files you'll create and why

Why it works: Claude’s first instinct is to start coding. This pattern forces it to think before writing, catching architectural mistakes before they become 500-line implementations you have to throw away.

Pro tip: Combine with plan mode (/plan) for even more structured output.

Pattern 3: The Test-First Pattern¶

When to use: Any bug fix or feature implementation where correctness matters more than speed.

Example:

Write failing tests for these requirements first:
1. Users with expired subscriptions cannot access premium endpoints
2. Grace period of 3 days after expiry still allows access
3. Admin users bypass subscription checks entirely

Then implement the middleware to make all tests pass.

Why it works: Tests constrain Claude’s implementation. Without tests, Claude might implement something that “looks right” but misses edge cases. With tests defined first, the implementation must satisfy concrete, verifiable requirements.

Pro tip: Include edge cases in the test specification. Claude tends to implement the happy path well but miss boundary conditions unless you explicitly name them.

Pattern 4: The Example-Driven Pattern¶

When to use: When the output format or style matters and is hard to describe in words.

Example:

Here's an existing API endpoint that follows our conventions:

```python
@router.post("/users", response_model=UserResponse, status_code=201)
async def create_user(
    payload: CreateUserRequest,
    db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db),
    current_user: User = Depends(require_admin),
) -> UserResponse:
    """Create a new user account."""
    user = await user_service.create(db, payload)
    return UserResponse.from_orm(user)

Now create endpoints for: - POST /teams (create team) - GET /teams/{team_id} (get team by ID) - PUT /teams/{team_id} (update team)

Follow exactly the same patterns: dependency injection, response models, service layer delegation.


**Why it works:** An example communicates more than a page of specifications. Claude pattern-matches against examples extremely well. The code it produces will mirror the structure, naming, and patterns of your example.

**Pro tip:** Provide examples of what you DO want, not just what you do not want. Positive examples are more effective than negative instructions.

### Pattern 5: The Progressive Refinement Pattern

**When to use:** When you are exploring a design space and do not know the final shape upfront.

**Example:**

Start with the simplest possible implementation of a task queue. Just an in-memory list with add/get/complete operations. No persistence, no retries, no priority – bare minimum.


Then iterate:

Now add Redis persistence. Tasks survive process restarts.


Then:

Now add retry logic. Failed tasks retry 3 times with exponential backoff.


**Why it works:** Each increment is small enough for Claude to get right on the first try. Building up gradually also lets you steer the design at each stage rather than discovering problems after a large implementation.

### Pattern 6: The Chain-of-Files Pattern

**When to use:** When Claude needs context from multiple files before making a change.

**Example:**

Read these files first, then fix the bug: 1. src/models/user.py (the User model) 2. src/services/auth.py (the authentication service) 3. src/middleware/session.py (the session middleware) 4. tests/test_auth.py (existing auth tests)

The bug: session middleware doesn’t check if the user is soft-deleted before allowing access.


**Why it works:** Claude reads files in the order you specify, building up context before attempting the fix. Without this pattern, Claude might read one file, attempt a fix, realize it needs another file, read it, revise the fix, and repeat -- wasting turns and tokens.

**Pro tip:** Order the files from most fundamental (models, schemas) to most applied (middleware, routes). This mirrors how a human engineer would study the code.

### Pattern 7: The Negative Constraint Pattern

**When to use:** When Claude's default behavior adds things you do not want.

**Example:**

Fix the null pointer exception in parser.py:67. Do NOT: - Add logging - Add type hints to existing code - Refactor surrounding functions - Add error handling beyond the specific fix Only change what’s necessary to prevent the null pointer.


**Why it works:** Claude has a tendency toward "while I'm here" improvements -- adding type hints, restructuring code, adding logging. This is usually helpful but sometimes destructive (especially in large codebases where unrelated changes create merge conflicts or break other team members' work). Negative constraints keep Claude focused.

### Pattern 8: The Role Pattern

**When to use:** When you want Claude to adopt a specific perspective for analysis or review.

**Example:**

Review src/api/payment.py as a senior security engineer who has seen PCI compliance audits fail. Focus exclusively on: - Data exposure (are card numbers ever logged?) - Input validation (can malformed requests cause unexpected behavior?) - Authentication gaps (are all endpoints properly protected?)


**Why it works:** The role frames Claude's evaluation criteria. A "security engineer" review produces different findings than a "performance engineer" review, even on the same code. The role also suppresses irrelevant findings -- a security reviewer does not comment on variable naming.

### Pattern 9: The Output Format Pattern

**When to use:** When you need structured output for further processing or consistent presentation.

**Example:**

Audit all TODO comments in src/. Output as a markdown table:

| File | Line | TODO Text | Priority (P1/P2/P3) | Suggested Action |


**Why it works:** Without format specification, Claude outputs free-form text that you then have to restructure. Specifying the format upfront gets usable output in one turn.

**Pro tip:** For data you need to process programmatically, ask for JSON output explicitly.

### Pattern 10: The Diff Pattern

**When to use:** Before allowing Claude to modify files, you want to preview the changes.

**Example:**

Show me what you would change in auth.py to fix the session timeout bug. Don’t make any edits yet – just show the diff as a code block.


**Why it works:** This gives you a review gate before Claude touches files. Particularly useful for large files where a wrong edit is expensive to undo, or when you want to understand Claude's approach before committing to it.

### Pattern 11: The Scope Limiter

**When to use:** When you want Claude to modify specific files or functions and nothing else.

**Example:**

Only modify the validate_token() function in src/auth/tokens.py. Do not change any other function in that file. Do not modify any other file.


**Why it works:** Without scope limits, Claude may "helpfully" update callers of the function, adjust related tests, or refactor neighboring code. In a team environment, touching unexpected files creates surprise diffs in pull requests.

### Pattern 12: The Reference Pattern

**When to use:** When your codebase has an established pattern and you want new code to match it.

**Example:**

Create a new service for notifications following the exact same pattern as src/services/email_service.py: - Same class structure - Same error handling approach - Same logging pattern - Same test structure (see tests/test_email_service.py)


**Why it works:** Consistency across a codebase is more valuable than any individual file being "optimal." The reference pattern ensures Claude produces code that looks like it was written by the same team, not by an AI with different style preferences.

### Pattern 13: The Explain-First Pattern

**When to use:** When you need to understand code before changing it.

**Example:**

Before suggesting any changes, explain how the request pipeline works in src/middleware/. Walk me through a request from entry to response, noting each middleware function and what it does.


**Why it works:** Claude's explanation reveals its understanding of the code. If the explanation is wrong, you can correct it before Claude makes changes based on a flawed mental model. This is especially valuable in unfamiliar codebases.

### Pattern 14: The Batch Pattern

**When to use:** When the same change needs to be applied across many files.

**Example:**

In every file matching src/api/*/.py, replace: from src.db import get_session with: from src.db import get_async_session

Then update every function that calls get_session() to use get_async_session() with await.


**Why it works:** Claude handles repetitive transformations across files well, but only if you define the transformation precisely. Vague batch instructions ("update all the database calls") lead to inconsistent changes across files.

**Pro tip:** For large batches (20+ files), consider using the `/batch` command, which runs parallel worktrees for each change.

### Pattern 15: The Checkpoint Pattern

**When to use:** Multi-step implementations where you want to verify correctness at each stage.

**Example:**

Implement the caching layer in three steps. After each step, run the relevant tests and stop if any fail:

Step 1: Add Redis cache client in src/cache/client.py Run: pytest tests/test_cache_client.py

Step 2: Add cache decorator in src/cache/decorators.py Run: pytest tests/test_cache_decorators.py

Step 3: Apply decorator to the 5 most expensive queries Run: pytest tests/ -k “cache”


**Why it works:** Without checkpoints, Claude plows through all three steps. If step 1 has a bug, steps 2 and 3 are built on a broken foundation. Checkpoints catch errors early, before they compound.

### Pattern 16: The Multi-Agent Pattern

**When to use:** When a task has independent sub-problems that benefit from parallel investigation.

**Example:**

Use subagents to investigate these three things in parallel: 1. How does our current auth middleware handle token refresh? 2. What rate limiting exists on our API endpoints? 3. What logging/monitoring covers our auth flow?

Then synthesize the findings into a security assessment.


**Why it works:** Each subagent gets its own context window, reads its own set of files, and returns a focused summary. Your main context stays clean for the synthesis step. As discussed in Chapter 12, this is the primary pattern for context-efficient investigation.

### Pattern 17: The Plan-Then-Execute Pattern

**When to use:** Any change affecting more than three files.

**Example:**

Plan first, don’t code yet. I need to add audit logging to every service that modifies user data. Show me: 1. Which services are affected 2. What the audit log schema should be 3. Where the logging calls go in each service 4. How to test it

Wait for my approval before implementing.


**Why it works:** This pattern prevents the most expensive mistake in Claude Code: implementing the wrong approach across multiple files and then having to undo it all. Ten minutes of planning saves an hour of rework.

### Pattern 18: The Minimal Change Pattern

**When to use:** Bug fixes, hotfixes, or any change where minimizing diff size matters.

**Example:**

Fix the off-by-one error in pagination. Make the smallest possible change. Do not refactor, do not add comments, do not improve surrounding code. I need a clean, reviewable one-line diff.


**Why it works:** In production environments, small diffs are easier to review, easier to revert, and less likely to introduce new bugs. Claude's tendency to improve "while it's in there" works against you in hotfix scenarios.

### Pattern 19: The Context-Loading Pattern

**When to use:** Complex tasks where Claude needs broad context before starting.

**Example:**

Before doing anything, read: 1. README.md (project overview) 2. docs/architecture.md (system design) 3. The last 10 git commits (recent changes) 4. .env.example (configuration shape)

Then: add a new background job that processes webhook events.


**Why it works:** Front-loading context prevents Claude from making assumptions. Without reading the architecture doc, Claude might design the background job in a way that conflicts with your existing job processing system.

**Pro tip:** Be selective. Loading too much context wastes tokens (Chapter 13). Load only what is directly relevant to the task.

### Pattern 20: The Escape Hatch Pattern

**When to use:** When there is a decision point where Claude's choice could go wrong.

**Example:**

Implement the file upload endpoint. If you’re unsure about: - Maximum file size (ask me) - Supported file types (ask me) - Storage backend (ask me) Do not guess – ask before proceeding. For everything else, use your best judgment.


**Why it works:** Claude's default mode is to make all decisions autonomously. For most decisions (variable naming, error handling style), this is fine. For domain-specific decisions (business rules, infrastructure choices), Claude's guesses are often wrong. The escape hatch tells Claude exactly where its judgment is not trusted.

## Anti-Patterns: What NOT to Do

### Anti-Pattern 1: The Vague Prompt

Bad¶

make it better

Claude changes random things: renames variables, adds comments,¶

restructures code – none of which you wanted¶


**Fix:** Be specific about what "better" means. Faster? More readable? More secure? More testable?

### Anti-Pattern 2: The Novel

Bad¶

I need you to fix the authentication system. The problem is that when users log in, sometimes their session expires too quickly. I think it might be related to the token refresh logic, but I’m not sure. Also, we’ve been having issues with the Redis connection pool, and I wonder if that’s related. Last week, Sarah mentioned that the staging environment had a similar issue but with different symptoms. The error we’re seeing in production is a 401 on the /api/dashboard endpoint, but only for users who logged in more than 2 hours ago…


**Fix:** Lead with the actionable information:

Good¶

Fix: 401 errors on /api/dashboard for sessions older than 2 hours. Likely cause: token refresh in src/auth/tokens.py. Check Redis connection pool timeouts as a contributing factor.


### Anti-Pattern 3: The Missing Context

Bad¶

fix the bug in the handler

Claude: “Which handler? Which bug? Let me search the entire codebase…”¶

5 tool calls later, it finds the wrong handler¶


**Fix:** Always include the file path, and ideally the line number or function name.

### Anti-Pattern 4: The Overload

Bad¶

Fix the login bug, then add rate limiting to all endpoints, then write tests for the payment module, then review the PR from yesterday, and also update the README with the new API endpoints.


**Fix:** One task per prompt. Use `/clear` between tasks. Claude prioritizes the first request and may lose quality on later ones, or attempt all five simultaneously and do none well.

### Anti-Pattern 5: The Context Bleed

Bad: continuing a long session without clearing¶

Turn 1-20: Debugging auth system (reads 15 files) Turn 21: “Now write a haiku about programming”

Claude’s haiku somehow references JWT tokens¶


**Fix:** `/clear` between unrelated tasks. Always.

## Advanced: Intent Routing via Prompt Structure

As introduced in Chapter 4, Claude Code's auto-execute protocol routes short prompts to specific workflows based on structure:

| Prompt Shape | Detected Intent | Workflow |
|-------------|----------------|---------|
| `filename.py` | Code review | Read file, severity-ranked findings |
| `#42` | Fix GitHub issue | Read issue, investigate, fix, test, PR |
| `PR 123` | PR summary | Load diff, risk assessment, checklist |
| `[stack trace]` | Debug | Hypothesize, investigate, root-cause, fix |
| `"this is slow"` | Performance | Profile, find bottlenecks, optimize |

This works because CLAUDE.md contains an intent routing table (detailed in `tools/prompts/intent-map.md`) that maps prompt patterns to execution workflows. Claude reads this routing table as part of its system prompt and applies it to every message.

### How It Works Under the Hood

When you type `login.py`, Claude's reasoning chain is:

1. "The user typed just a filename. Per my routing table, a bare filename maps to code review."
2. "The code review workflow says: read the file, check security, then logic, then performance, then readability."
3. "Output format: severity-ranked findings with file:line references."

The prompt you typed was two words. The actual instructions Claude followed came from layers 1 and 2 of the prompt stack (CLAUDE.md and the code-review skill). This is prompt engineering at the system level -- you invested time once in configuring the routing, and now every short prompt gets expanded automatically.

### Building Your Own Intent Routes

You can extend the routing table for your domain. Add entries to your CLAUDE.md or intent-map.md:

```markdown
## custom-routes
| User says | Route to |
|---|---|
| mentions "migration" | Database migration workflow |
| mentions "onboard" or "new hire" | Onboarding documentation |
| mentions "incident" or "outage" | Incident response runbook |

Then define the execution steps for each route, either in the intent map or as a dedicated skill.

The Meta-Skill: When to Prompt vs. When to Configure¶

The most important prompt engineering insight is knowing when to stop prompting and start configuring. Here is the rule:

If you prompt the same instruction three times, it belongs in configuration.

You keep typing… Move it to…
“Always use type hints” A rule file (.claude/rules/python.md)
“Check security first when reviewing code” A skill (.claude/skills/code-review/SKILL.md)
“Don’t allow rm -rf commands” A hook (.claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh)
“I prefer Pydantic over dataclasses” Auto-memory (ask Claude to remember)
“Start by reading the architecture doc” CLAUDE.md

Configuration is compiled prompts. You write the instruction once, and it applies to every future session automatically. The ROI compounds: a rule you write today saves you from typing the same instruction hundreds of times over the next year.

The Configuration Hierarchy¶

As you identify repeated prompts, place them at the right level:

Most persistent, broadest scope
─────────────────────────────
  CLAUDE.md              ← Universal instructions, every session
  Rules (.claude/rules/) ← Language/domain-specific, path-scoped
  Skills (.claude/skills/) ← Workflow-specific, invoked per task
  Auto-memory            ← Personal preferences, project facts
  Your message           ← This task, this moment
─────────────────────────────
Most ephemeral, narrowest scope

Move instructions up this hierarchy as they prove their value. A prompt that works well in a message becomes a memory. A memory that applies broadly becomes a rule. A rule that defines a workflow becomes a skill.

Measuring Prompt Quality¶

How do you know if your prompts are good? Four metrics:

1. First-Try Success Rate¶

Did Claude do what you wanted on the first attempt? If you frequently need to /rewind or send correction messages, your prompts are not specific enough. Track this informally: aim for 80%+ first-try success on routine tasks.

2. Tool Selection Accuracy¶

Did Claude choose the right tools? If you asked for a code review and Claude started editing files instead of analyzing them, the prompt did not convey the intent clearly. Watch for unnecessary tool calls – they waste tokens and indicate prompt ambiguity.

3. Appropriate Stopping¶

Did Claude stop at the right point? Two failure modes:

  • Under-doing: Claude fixes the bug but does not run the tests you expected
  • Over-doing: Claude fixes the bug, then refactors the surrounding code, then updates the README, then modifies the CI config

If Claude consistently over-does, add scope limits. If it under-does, add explicit completion criteria.

4. Correction Frequency¶

How often do you need to send follow-up messages correcting Claude’s approach? Each correction costs tokens and indicates a gap between your intent and Claude’s interpretation. High correction frequency on a specific type of task means you should create a skill or rule for it.

The Feedback Loop¶

Write prompt → Observe result → Identify gap → Improve prompt
     ↑                                              │
     └──────── If repeated 3x, move to config ──────┘

This cycle is the core practice of prompt engineering. Every interaction with Claude Code is an opportunity to refine your prompting and configuration. Over weeks and months, your system becomes increasingly tuned to your workflow, your codebase, and your preferences. The tool gets better not because the model improves, but because your prompt engineering stack improves.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

“Longer prompts are better prompts.” Length is not quality. A 10-word prompt with a file path and a clear action verb outperforms a 200-word prompt with background context Claude does not need. Lead with the action, add context only as needed.

“I should explain how the code works.” Claude can read the code. Do not explain what the code does – explain what you want done to it. Your prompt should be intent, not information.

“I need to be polite to get better results.” “Please kindly review this code if you wouldn’t mind” does not produce different results than “Review auth.py.” Skip the social niceties. Every pleasantry is a token that could be a constraint or specification.

“I’ll put everything in my message.” If an instruction applies to more than this one task, it does not belong in your message. Put it in CLAUDE.md, a rule, a skill, or auto-memory. Your message should contain only what is unique to this specific request.

“The prompt is the most important part.” Your message (layer 4) is actually the least important layer of the prompt stack. A well-configured system (layers 1-3 and 5) means your messages can be minimal. Invest your engineering effort in configuration, not in crafting the perfect single prompt.

Chapter Summary¶

  1. Prompt engineering is systematic input design, not just “writing better prompts.” It controls Claude’s tool selection, file choices, approach, and stopping behavior.

  2. The prompt stack has five layers: system, skill, context, message, and memory. Your typed message is just layer 4 – and often the least impactful. Invest in configuring layers 1-3 for the highest leverage.

  3. Twenty patterns cover 90% of professional use cases. The most impactful are: Constraint (bound the solution space), Test-First (verify before implementing), Chain-of-Files (load context deliberately), Negative Constraint (prevent unwanted changes), and Minimal Change (keep diffs reviewable).

  4. The meta-skill is knowing when to stop prompting and start configuring. If you type the same instruction three times, move it to a rule, skill, hook, or memory. Configuration is compiled prompts – write once, apply forever.

  5. Measure prompt quality by first-try success rate, tool selection accuracy, appropriate stopping behavior, and correction frequency. Use these metrics to continuously improve both your prompts and your configuration stack.

Part V: Building Real Applications

Chapter 16: Project Architecture Patterns¶ ~15 min read

You can hand Claude Code a messy codebase and it will do its best. But “its best” in a well-structured project is categorically different from “its best” in a pile of spaghetti. This chapter is about engineering your project so that Claude Code operates at maximum effectiveness — before you write a single line of application code.

The difference is not subtle. A project with a clear CLAUDE.md, consistent directory structure, and architecture documentation lets Claude Code make accurate changes across dozens of files with minimal correction. A project without these guardrails produces code that works but drifts — inconsistent patterns, misplaced files, missed conventions. Over weeks, the drift compounds into technical debt that takes longer to fix than it would have taken to set up properly.

The CLAUDE.md-First Approach¶

Most engineers start a project by writing code. With Claude Code, you start by writing CLAUDE.md.

This is not documentation for humans. It is configuration for your AI collaborator. Think of it as the project brain — the document that tells Claude Code how to think about your codebase, what conventions to follow, and what mistakes to avoid.

The CLAUDE.md-first approach inverts the traditional workflow:

  1. Define communication rules (how Claude should talk to you)
  2. Define coding standards (language conventions, formatting, patterns)
  3. Define architecture (where things go, how components connect)
  4. Define verification (how to run tests, lint, type-check)
  5. Then start building

Here is a minimal but effective CLAUDE.md for a new TypeScript API project:

markdown
# Project: TaskFlow API

REST API for task management. TypeScript, Express, PostgreSQL, Prisma ORM.

## Communication
- Be terse. Lead with the answer
- Reference code as `file_path:line_number`
- No emojis

## Coding Standards
- TypeScript strict mode everywhere
- Async/await, never raw Promises or callbacks
- Zod for all request validation
- Prisma for all database access (never raw SQL)
- Error responses follow RFC 7807 Problem Details format

## Architecture
- src/routes/ — Express route handlers (thin: validate, call service, respond)
- src/services/ — Business logic (all complexity lives here)
- src/middleware/ — Auth, error handling, request logging
- src/prisma/ — Schema, migrations, seed data
- src/types/ — Shared TypeScript types and Zod schemas

## Verification
- Run tests: `npm test`
- Type check: `npx tsc --noEmit`
- Lint: `npx eslint src/`
