- Run single test: `npx vitest run src/routes/__tests__/boards.test.ts`

## Rules
@.claude/rules/typescript.md
@.claude/rules/api-conventions.md

## Architecture
@docs/architecture.md

Notice what is not here: no lengthy explanations, no API documentation, no code patterns that Claude can infer from reading the code. The CLAUDE.md is a compass, not an encyclopedia. As discussed in Chapter 4, keeping it under 200 lines ensures the instructions get followed reliably.

When to Write CLAUDE.md¶

Write it before git init. Seriously. The first file in your repository should be CLAUDE.md, not README.md. README is for humans who visit your GitHub page. CLAUDE.md is for the AI that will write most of your code.

If you are retrofitting Claude Code onto an existing project, write the CLAUDE.md by reading the codebase first:

Read the codebase structure and conventions, then draft a CLAUDE.md
that captures the coding standards, architecture, and verification commands.

Claude Code will analyze your project and produce a first draft. Edit it for accuracy, remove anything redundant, and commit.

Directory Conventions That Help Claude Navigate¶

Claude Code reads your directory structure to understand where things belong. Consistent, predictable directory layouts let Claude place new files correctly without you specifying the path every time.

Flat vs. Deep Hierarchies¶

Flat structures (fewer levels of nesting) are easier for Claude Code to navigate. When Claude uses the Glob tool to find files, a flat structure means fewer directories to search and clearer file names.

Deep structures (many levels of nesting) provide more organization but require Claude to know the full path or search recursively. This works fine for large codebases but adds friction for small ones.

Structure Best For Claude Code Impact
src/routes/boards.ts Small to medium projects Claude finds files instantly
src/modules/boards/routes/index.ts Large monoliths with 50+ modules Claude needs architecture docs or more context
packages/api/src/routes/boards.ts Monorepos Claude needs per-package CLAUDE.md

The rule of thumb: use the shallowest structure that keeps related files together. Three levels deep is the sweet spot for most projects.

The Standard Layout¶

Regardless of language or framework, these directory names are nearly universal and Claude Code recognizes them immediately:

project/
├── CLAUDE.md              # AI project brain
├── .claude/               # Claude Code configuration
│   ├── settings.json
│   └── rules/
├── src/                   # Source code
├── tests/                 # Test files
├── docs/                  # Documentation
├── scripts/               # Build/deploy/utility scripts
├── config/                # Configuration files
└── .github/               # CI/CD workflows

Claude Code has been trained on millions of repositories using this layout. When you follow it, Claude places new files correctly by default. When you deviate, you need to document the deviation in CLAUDE.md.

Framework-Specific Conventions¶

Each framework has its own directory conventions. Claude Code knows them, but documenting them in your CLAUDE.md removes ambiguity.

Next.js (App Router):

src/
├── app/                   # Route segments
│   ├── layout.tsx         # Root layout
│   ├── page.tsx           # Home page
│   ├── boards/
│   │   ├── page.tsx       # /boards
│   │   └── [id]/
│   │       └── page.tsx   # /boards/:id
│   └── api/
│       └── boards/
│           └── route.ts   # API route handler
├── components/            # Shared React components
├── lib/                   # Utility functions, API clients
└── types/                 # TypeScript type definitions

Django:

project/
├── manage.py
├── config/                # Project settings
│   ├── settings/
│   │   ├── base.py
│   │   ├── dev.py
│   │   └── prod.py
│   ├── urls.py
│   └── wsgi.py
├── apps/
│   ├── boards/
│   │   ├── models.py
│   │   ├── views.py
│   │   ├── serializers.py
│   │   ├── urls.py
│   │   └── tests/
│   └── users/
│       ├── models.py
│       └── ...
└── templates/

Rails:

app/
├── controllers/
├── models/
├── views/
├── services/              # Business logic
├── serializers/
└── jobs/

When using frameworks, add a one-liner to CLAUDE.md:

markdown
## Framework
Next.js 15 with App Router. Follow standard App Router conventions for file-based routing.

That single line prevents Claude from generating Pages Router code, creating getServerSideProps, or making other version-specific mistakes.

Project Types and Their Ideal Claude Code Setups¶

Different project types need different configurations. Here are five common patterns with complete setup details.

1. Web Application (React/Next.js + API)¶

This is the most common project type. The CLAUDE.md focuses on component patterns and API conventions.

markdown
# Project: TaskFlow

Full-stack task management app. Next.js 15 (App Router), TypeScript, Prisma, PostgreSQL.

## Coding Standards
- TypeScript strict mode
- Server Components by default, 'use client' only when needed
- Zod for all form/API validation
- Prisma for database access
- Tailwind CSS for styling (no CSS modules)

## Architecture
- src/app/ — Pages and API routes (file-based routing)
- src/components/ — Reusable UI components (one component per file)
- src/lib/ — Server utilities, database client, auth helpers
- src/actions/ — Server actions for mutations
- src/types/ — Shared types

## Rules
@.claude/rules/typescript.md
@.claude/rules/react.md

Rules setup:

markdown
<!-- .claude/rules/typescript.md -->
---
paths:
  - "**/*.ts"
  - "**/*.tsx"
---

# TypeScript Rules
- Strict mode. No `any` types.
- Use `interface` for object shapes, `type` for unions/intersections
- Prefer `const` assertions for literal types
- Use `satisfies` operator for type-safe object literals
- Named exports only (no default exports except pages)
markdown
<!-- .claude/rules/react.md -->
---
paths:
  - "**/*.tsx"
---

# React Rules
- Functional components only
- Hooks at the top of the component
- Props interface named `{ComponentName}Props`
- Co-locate component tests: `ComponentName.test.tsx` next to `ComponentName.tsx`
- Server Components by default — add 'use client' only for interactivity

Custom skills:

A /component skill that scaffolds a new React component:

markdown
<!-- .claude/skills/component/SKILL.md -->
---
name: component
description: Scaffold a new React component with types, tests, and story.
argument-hint: ComponentName
---

# Create Component

Given a component name:
1. Create `src/components/{Name}/{Name}.tsx` with props interface and component
2. Create `src/components/{Name}/{Name}.test.tsx` with basic render test
3. Export from `src/components/{Name}/index.ts`
4. Use Server Component by default unless user specifies client

2. CLI Tool (Python)¶

CLI tools are text-in, text-out. Claude Code excels here.

markdown
# Project: git-analytics

CLI tool for analyzing git repository statistics. Python 3.12, Click, Rich.

## Coding Standards
- Type hints on all functions
- Click for argument parsing (not argparse)
- Rich for terminal output (tables, progress bars, colors)
- Pathlib for all file operations

## Architecture
- src/git_analytics/cli.py — Click command group (entry point)
- src/git_analytics/commands/ — One file per command
- src/git_analytics/core/ — Git analysis logic (no Click dependency)
- src/git_analytics/formatters/ — Output formatting (Rich tables, JSON, CSV)

## Verification
- Tests: `pytest tests/ -v`
- Lint: `ruff check src/`
- Format: `ruff format src/`
- Type check: `pyright src/`
- Build: `python -m build`

## Rules
@.claude/rules/python.md
@.claude/rules/cli.md

The CLI-specific rule:

markdown
<!-- .claude/rules/cli.md -->
---
paths:
  - "src/**/commands/**"
  - "src/**/cli.py"
---

# CLI Rules
- Every command has: --help text, --json flag for machine output, --verbose flag
- Exit codes: 0=success, 1=error, 2=usage error
- Errors go to stderr, data goes to stdout
- Support piping: detect if stdout is a TTY, skip Rich formatting if not

3. Library/Package¶

Libraries have stricter requirements around public API stability and documentation.

markdown
# Project: fastcache

High-performance caching library for Python. Pure Python, zero dependencies.

## Coding Standards
- Public API in src/fastcache/__init__.py — never change signatures without discussion
- All public functions have docstrings with Args/Returns/Raises
- Type hints required on all public and private functions
- Use __all__ to control exports

## Rules
@.claude/rules/python.md
@.claude/rules/api-stability.md

The API stability rule is critical for libraries:

markdown
<!-- .claude/rules/api-stability.md -->

# API Stability Rules
- NEVER remove or rename a public function/class/method
- NEVER change the type signature of a public function
- To deprecate: add @deprecated decorator with version and alternative
- New public API requires: docstring, type hints, at least 2 tests, changelog entry
- Breaking changes require a major version bump and migration guide

4. Monorepo¶

Monorepos need layered CLAUDE.md files: one at the root for cross-package rules, one per package for specific contexts.

monorepo/
├── CLAUDE.md                    # Cross-package rules
├── packages/
│   ├── api/
│   │   ├── CLAUDE.md            # API-specific rules
│   │   └── src/
│   ├── web/
│   │   ├── CLAUDE.md            # Frontend-specific rules
│   │   └── src/
│   └── shared/
│       ├── CLAUDE.md            # Shared library rules
│       └── src/
└── .claude/
    ├── settings.json
    └── rules/

Root CLAUDE.md:

markdown
# Project: TaskFlow Monorepo

Monorepo with pnpm workspaces. Three packages: api (Express), web (Next.js), shared (types + utils).

## Cross-Package Rules
- Shared types live in packages/shared/src/types/
- Never duplicate types between packages
- Import shared code via @taskflow/shared (not relative paths)
- All packages use TypeScript strict mode
- Run from root: `pnpm test` (all), `pnpm --filter api test` (one package)

## Package Contexts
@packages/api/CLAUDE.md
@packages/web/CLAUDE.md
@packages/shared/CLAUDE.md

Each package has its own focused CLAUDE.md. The packages/api/CLAUDE.md:

markdown
# Package: @taskflow/api

Express.js REST API. TypeScript, Prisma, PostgreSQL.

## Architecture
- src/routes/ — Route handlers (thin)
- src/services/ — Business logic
- src/middleware/ — Auth, validation, error handling

## Verification
- Tests: `pnpm --filter api test`
- Lint: `pnpm --filter api lint`

5. Microservices¶

Microservices benefit from a template approach: every service follows the same structure.

markdown
# Project: TaskFlow Platform

Microservices architecture. Each service follows the standard template.

## Service Template
Every service in services/ follows this structure:
- src/server.ts — HTTP server setup
- src/routes/ — Route handlers
- src/services/ — Business logic
- src/events/ — Event producers/consumers (Kafka)
- src/config.ts — Environment configuration
- Dockerfile — Multi-stage build
- docker-compose.yml — Local development

## Cross-Service Rules
- Services communicate via Kafka events and HTTP (never direct DB access)
- Every service has its own database (no shared databases)
- All services use the same error response format (RFC 7807)
- Health check endpoint: GET /health
- OpenAPI spec: src/openapi.yaml

## Services
@services/users/CLAUDE.md
@services/boards/CLAUDE.md
@services/notifications/CLAUDE.md

Architecture Documentation for Claude¶

Claude Code does not just follow instructions — it uses architecture documentation to make decisions. When you document your architecture, Claude can:

  • Determine which files a change affects (impact analysis)
  • Understand data flow to avoid breaking dependencies
  • Follow established patterns when adding new features
  • Catch architectural violations before they happen

The Architecture Doc¶

Create docs/architecture.md and import it from CLAUDE.md:

markdown
<!-- docs/architecture.md -->

# Architecture Overview

## System Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    Client[React SPA] --> API[Express API]
    API --> Auth[Auth Middleware]
    Auth --> JWT[JWT Verification]
    API --> DB[(PostgreSQL)]
    API --> Cache[(Redis)]
    API --> WS[WebSocket Server]
    WS --> Client
    API --> Queue[Bull Queue]
    Queue --> Email[Email Worker]
    Queue --> Notify[Notification Worker]

Component Responsibilities¶

Component Directory Responsibility
API Server src/routes/, src/services/ HTTP request handling, business logic
Auth src/middleware/auth.ts JWT verification, role-based access
Database src/prisma/ PostgreSQL via Prisma ORM
Cache src/lib/cache.ts Redis for session and query caching
WebSocket src/ws/ Real-time board updates
Workers src/workers/ Background jobs (email, notifications)

Data Flow¶

  1. Client sends HTTP request with JWT in Authorization header
  2. Auth middleware verifies JWT, attaches user to request
  3. Route handler validates request body with Zod
  4. Service layer executes business logic
  5. Prisma handles database operations
  6. Response sent to client
  7. If mutation: WebSocket broadcast to connected clients

API Contracts¶

  • All responses: { data: T } for success, { error: ProblemDetails } for failure
  • Pagination: ?page=1&limit=20 → { data: T[], meta: { total, page, limit } }
  • Auth: Bearer token in Authorization header
  • WebSocket: JSON messages with { type, payload } structure

Claude Code references this documentation when making decisions. If you ask "Add a notification when a card is moved," Claude reads the architecture doc, sees the Queue/Worker pattern, and implements it correctly --- producing a Bull queue job rather than a synchronous email send.

### Architecture Decision Records

**Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)** capture the *why* behind architectural choices. Claude Code reads these to avoid undoing past decisions.

```markdown
<!-- docs/decisions/0003-use-prisma-over-raw-sql.md -->

# ADR-0003: Use Prisma ORM Over Raw SQL

## Status
Accepted

## Context
We need a database access layer. Options: raw SQL (pg library), query builder (Knex), or ORM (Prisma, TypeORM).

## Decision
Use Prisma.

## Rationale
- Type-safe queries generated from schema
- Migration system built in
- Schema-as-code (prisma.schema) is the single source of truth
- Team familiar with Prisma from previous projects

## Consequences
- Cannot use advanced PostgreSQL features not supported by Prisma (rare)
- Must use Prisma Client for all DB access (no raw SQL escape hatches)
- Schema changes require `prisma migrate dev`

When Claude encounters a situation where raw SQL would be tempting, it reads this ADR and sticks with Prisma. This prevents architectural drift.

The “Claude-Ready” Project Checklist¶

Before you start building features, verify your project meets these requirements. Each item directly impacts Claude Code’s effectiveness.

# Item Why It Matters
1 CLAUDE.md exists with communication + coding standards Claude follows your conventions from day one
2 .claude/rules/ has language-specific rules Claude applies the right rules to the right files
3 .claude/settings.json has permissions + hooks Safety guardrails are active, auto-formatting works
4 docs/architecture.md exists Claude understands the system before modifying it
5 Tests exist and pass Claude verifies changes work, catches regressions
6 .gitignore is complete Claude does not commit artifacts, secrets, or build output
7 README.md has setup instructions Claude can set up the dev environment if needed
8 Linter is configured PostToolUse hooks can auto-format code
9 CI pipeline exists Changes are validated automatically on push
10 ADRs document key decisions Claude respects past architectural choices

Run through this checklist for every new project. It takes 15 minutes and saves hours of correction later.

Scaffolding a New Project with Claude Code¶

You do not need to set all of this up manually. Claude Code can scaffold a complete, Claude-ready project structure.

Full Walkthrough¶

/scaffold python git-analytics

Or as a natural language prompt:

Create a new Python CLI project called git-analytics. 
It should use Click for commands, Rich for output, and pytest for testing.
Include a complete CLAUDE.md, rules, and project configuration.

Claude Code generates:

git-analytics/
├── CLAUDE.md
├── .claude/
│   ├── settings.json
│   └── rules/
│       ├── python.md
│       └── cli.md
├── src/
│   └── git_analytics/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── cli.py              # Click command group
│       └── commands/
│           └── __init__.py
├── tests/
│   ├── conftest.py             # Shared fixtures
│   └── test_cli.py             # Smoke test
├── docs/
│   └── architecture.md
├── pyproject.toml              # Build config, dependencies, tool config
├── .gitignore
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       └── ci.yml              # GitHub Actions: test + lint
└── README.md

The generated pyproject.toml includes properly pinned dependencies, ruff configuration, pytest settings, and pyright configuration. The CI workflow runs tests, lint, and type checking on every push.

Customizing Scaffolding¶

You can create a custom /scaffold skill that matches your team’s standards. Create .claude/skills/scaffold/SKILL.md with your specific project template:

markdown
---
name: scaffold
description: Bootstrap a new project with our team's standard structure.
argument-hint: [type] [name]
allowed-tools: Read, Write, Bash
---

# Scaffold New Project

## For Python projects:
1. Use the directory structure from our standard template
2. Include: CLAUDE.md, .claude/settings.json, .claude/rules/python.md
3. pyproject.toml with: ruff, pytest, pyright configured
4. GitHub Actions CI with: test, lint, type-check jobs
5. Pre-populated .gitignore for Python
6. First test that imports the package and verifies it loads
7. docs/architecture.md with placeholder sections

## For TypeScript projects:
1. Use our standard Next.js or Express template
2. Include: CLAUDE.md, .claude/settings.json, .claude/rules/typescript.md
3. tsconfig.json with strict mode
4. ESLint + Prettier configured
5. Vitest for testing
6. GitHub Actions CI

This skill encodes your team’s conventions so every new project starts from the same foundation.

Evolving Architecture with Claude Code¶

Projects change over time. Features get added, patterns evolve, and sometimes the original architecture no longer fits. Claude Code helps you evolve your architecture safely.

ADRs as Living Documentation¶

When you make an architectural change, create an ADR before implementing:

We need to switch from REST to GraphQL for the board queries because
of the N+1 query problem with nested resources. Create an ADR documenting
this decision, then implement the change.

Claude Code creates the ADR first, then implements the change in accordance with the decision. Future sessions read the ADR and understand why GraphQL is used for boards but REST is used for auth endpoints.

Refactoring with Plan Mode¶

For large architectural changes, use /plan mode to review the approach before executing:

/plan
Refactor the API from flat route handlers to a service layer pattern.
Currently all business logic is in src/routes/. We need to extract it
to src/services/ and keep routes as thin request/response handlers.

Claude Code produces a detailed plan: which files change, what moves where, what the new structure looks like, and what risks exist. You review the plan, adjust it, then execute.

Migration Strategies Using Worktrees¶

For high-risk architectural changes, use worktrees to isolate the work:

Create a worktree for the service-layer migration. Move boards logic first
as a proof of concept.

Claude Code creates an isolated git worktree, implements the change for one module, and you can verify it works before applying the pattern to the rest of the codebase. If the approach fails, you discard the worktree with zero impact on your main branch.

When Architecture Outgrows CLAUDE.md¶

As your project grows, the CLAUDE.md and architecture docs need to grow with it. Signs that your architecture documentation needs updating:

  • Claude keeps putting files in the wrong directories
  • Claude generates patterns inconsistent with recent changes
  • New team members (human or AI) make the same mistakes
  • You find yourself correcting Claude on the same conventions repeatedly

When this happens, update the documentation immediately. The cost of a 5-minute CLAUDE.md update is trivial compared to repeatedly correcting architectural drift across dozens of files.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

Over-documenting in CLAUDE.md. The instinct is to write everything Claude needs to know. But Claude reads your code. It sees your imports, your patterns, your test structure. CLAUDE.md should contain what Claude cannot infer from the code: conventions, preferences, and decisions. If Claude can see that you use Prisma by reading package.json, you do not need to say “we use Prisma” in CLAUDE.md. But you do need to say “never use raw SQL” because that is a constraint Claude cannot infer.

Ignoring framework conventions. Claude Code knows Next.js App Router conventions. If you follow them, Claude generates correct code automatically. If you deviate without documenting the deviation, Claude generates code for the standard convention and you spend time correcting it.

Skipping architecture docs for small projects. Even a 3-file project benefits from a one-paragraph architecture note in CLAUDE.md. “Routes handle HTTP, services handle logic, models handle data.” That one sentence prevents Claude from putting business logic in route handlers.

No tests in the scaffold. A project without tests is a project where Claude cannot verify its changes. Always include at least one test in your scaffold, even if it is just an import smoke test. Claude uses test results as a feedback signal.

Chapter Summary¶

  1. Start with CLAUDE.md, not code. Engineer your project brain before writing a single line of application code. This upfront investment pays dividends throughout development.

  2. Directory structure matters. Use consistent, shallow directory layouts that follow framework conventions. Claude Code navigates predictable structures faster and more accurately.

  3. Match your project type to the right setup. Web apps, CLI tools, libraries, monorepos, and microservices each need different CLAUDE.md structures, rules, and skills.

  4. Architecture documentation is not optional. Component diagrams, data flow docs, and ADRs give Claude Code the context it needs for accurate impact analysis and consistent pattern application.

  5. Use the Claude-ready checklist. Ten items, fifteen minutes. Every project. The discipline of a proper setup eliminates hours of correction downstream.

Chapter 17: Building a Full-Stack Web Application¶ ~18 min read

This chapter is a complete walkthrough. We are building a task management application — boards, lists, and cards, similar to Trello — from an empty directory to a deployed product. Every line of code is generated by Claude Code. Every prompt shown here is real. The goal is not to teach you React or Express; it is to show you how a senior engineer uses Claude Code to build a production application efficiently.

By the end of this chapter, you will have seen every phase of full-stack development through Claude Code: project scaffolding, database design, API development, frontend components, real-time features, testing, and deployment. More importantly, you will understand when to use /plan mode, when to prompt directly, and when to step back and let Claude Code run.

The Application: TaskFlow¶

TaskFlow is a task management SaaS with these features:

  • User authentication (JWT)
  • Boards with customizable lists
  • Draggable cards with descriptions, labels, and assignees
  • Real-time updates via WebSocket (other users see changes instantly)
  • Team collaboration with role-based access

The tech stack:

Layer Technology
Frontend React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS
Backend Express.js, TypeScript
Database PostgreSQL, Prisma ORM
Auth JWT with refresh tokens
Real-time WebSocket (ws library)
Testing Vitest (frontend), Vitest (backend), Supertest
Deployment Docker, GitHub Actions

Phase 1: Project Setup¶

The Initial Scaffold¶

We start with one prompt:

Create a new full-stack TypeScript project called TaskFlow with:
- React frontend (Vite + TypeScript + Tailwind)
- Express API backend (TypeScript)
- PostgreSQL with Prisma ORM
- JWT authentication
- Monorepo structure with pnpm workspaces
Include a complete CLAUDE.md, rules, tests, and docker-compose for local dev.

Claude Code generates the entire project structure:

taskflow/
├── CLAUDE.md
├── .claude/
│   ├── settings.json
│   └── rules/
│       ├── typescript.md
│       └── react.md
├── packages/
│   ├── api/
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── server.ts
│   │   │   ├── routes/
│   │   │   │   ├── auth.ts
│   │   │   │   └── health.ts
│   │   │   ├── middleware/
│   │   │   │   ├── auth.ts
│   │   │   │   └── errorHandler.ts
│   │   │   ├── services/
│   │   │   ├── prisma/
│   │   │   │   └── schema.prisma
│   │   │   └── types/
│   │   ├── tsconfig.json
│   │   ├── vitest.config.ts
│   │   └── package.json
│   ├── web/
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── App.tsx
│   │   │   ├── main.tsx
│   │   │   ├── components/
│   │   │   ├── hooks/
│   │   │   ├── lib/
│   │   │   │   └── api.ts
│   │   │   └── types/
│   │   ├── index.html
│   │   ├── tailwind.config.ts
│   │   ├── tsconfig.json
│   │   ├── vite.config.ts
│   │   └── package.json
│   └── shared/
│       ├── src/
│       │   └── types.ts
│       ├── tsconfig.json
│       └── package.json
├── docker-compose.yml
├── pnpm-workspace.yaml
├── package.json
└── .gitignore

The Generated CLAUDE.md¶

Claude Code produces a CLAUDE.md tailored to this project:

markdown
# Project: TaskFlow

Full-stack task management app. Monorepo with pnpm workspaces.

## Communication
- Be terse. Reference code as `file_path:line_number`
- No emojis

## Packages
- packages/api — Express.js REST API (TypeScript, Prisma, PostgreSQL)
- packages/web — React SPA (TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS)
- packages/shared — Shared types and utilities

## Coding Standards
- TypeScript strict mode in all packages
- Async/await everywhere (no callbacks, no raw .then())
- Zod for API request validation
- Prisma Client for all database access
- React: functional components, hooks, Server Components not applicable (SPA)

## Architecture
- API routes are thin: validate → call service → respond
- Business logic lives in packages/api/src/services/
- Shared types in packages/shared/src/types.ts (import via @taskflow/shared)
- Frontend state: React Query for server state, Zustand for UI state

## Verification
- All tests: `pnpm test`
- API tests: `pnpm --filter api test`
- Web tests: `pnpm --filter web test`
- Type check: `pnpm --filter api exec tsc --noEmit`
- Lint: `pnpm lint`
- Database: `pnpm --filter api exec prisma migrate dev`
- Dev server: `docker-compose up -d db && pnpm dev`

## Rules
@.claude/rules/typescript.md
@.claude/rules/react.md

Reviewing the Scaffold¶

The scaffold is a starting point, not gospel. Review it and adjust:

The scaffold looks good. A few changes:
1. Add a packages/api/src/lib/logger.ts using pino instead of console.log
2. Change the error handler to use RFC 7807 Problem Details format
3. Add rate limiting middleware using express-rate-limit

Claude makes the three changes. This pattern — scaffold, review, adjust — is faster than specifying everything upfront. Let Claude generate a reasonable default, then correct the parts that do not match your preferences.

Local Development Setup¶

Claude generated a docker-compose.yml for local development:

yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: taskflow
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: taskflow_dev
      POSTGRES_DB: taskflow
    ports:
      - '5432:5432'
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - '6379:6379'

volumes:
  pgdata:

Start the database and run migrations:

Start the local database and run the initial Prisma migration.

Claude executes:

bash
docker-compose up -d db
pnpm --filter api exec prisma migrate dev --name init

Phase 2: Database and API¶

Designing the Schema¶

Design the PostgreSQL schema for TaskFlow. We need:
- Users (email, password hash, name, avatar)
- Boards (title, owner, members with roles)
- Lists within boards (title, position for ordering)
- Cards within lists (title, description, position, assignee, labels, due date)
- Activity log for audit trail

Claude generates the complete Prisma schema:

prisma
// packages/api/src/prisma/schema.prisma

generator client {
  provider = "prisma-client-js"
}

datasource db {
  provider = "postgresql"
  url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
}

model User {
  id           String   @id @default(cuid())
  email        String   @unique
  passwordHash String   @map("password_hash")
  name         String
  avatarUrl    String?  @map("avatar_url")
  createdAt    DateTime @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  updatedAt    DateTime @updatedAt @map("updated_at")

  ownedBoards   Board[]        @relation("BoardOwner")
  memberships   BoardMember[]
  assignedCards Card[]         @relation("CardAssignee")
  activities    Activity[]

  @@map("users")
}

model Board {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  title     String
  ownerId   String   @map("owner_id")
  createdAt DateTime @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt @map("updated_at")

  owner      User          @relation("BoardOwner", fields: [ownerId], references: [id])
  members    BoardMember[]
  lists      List[]
  activities Activity[]

  @@map("boards")
}

enum BoardRole {
  ADMIN
  MEMBER
  VIEWER
}

model BoardMember {
  id      String    @id @default(cuid())
  boardId String    @map("board_id")
  userId  String    @map("user_id")
  role    BoardRole @default(MEMBER)

  board Board @relation(fields: [boardId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  user  User  @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([boardId, userId])
  @@map("board_members")
}

model List {
  id       String @id @default(cuid())
  title    String
  position Int
  boardId  String @map("board_id")

  board Board  @relation(fields: [boardId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  cards Card[]

  @@map("lists")
}

model Card {
  id          String    @id @default(cuid())
  title       String
  description String?
  position    Int
  dueDate     DateTime? @map("due_date")
  labels      String[]  @default([])
  listId      String    @map("list_id")
  assigneeId  String?   @map("assignee_id")
  createdAt   DateTime  @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  updatedAt   DateTime  @updatedAt @map("updated_at")

  list     List @relation(fields: [listId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  assignee User? @relation("CardAssignee", fields: [assigneeId], references: [id])

  @@map("cards")
}

model Activity {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  action    String
  details   Json?
  userId    String   @map("user_id")
  boardId   String   @map("board_id")
  createdAt DateTime @default(now()) @map("created_at")

  user  User  @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
  board Board @relation(fields: [boardId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@index([boardId, createdAt])
  @@map("activities")
}

Notice Claude’s decisions: cuid() for IDs (URL-safe, sortable), @@map for snake_case table names (PostgreSQL convention while keeping camelCase in TypeScript), position fields for ordering, cascade deletes for owned entities, and a JSON field for flexible activity details.

Building the API¶

Now we build the API endpoints. Rather than one massive prompt, we work incrementally:

Build the board CRUD endpoints:
- POST /api/boards — create a board (creator becomes ADMIN)
- GET /api/boards — list boards the user is a member of
- GET /api/boards/:id — get board with lists and cards
- PATCH /api/boards/:id — update board title
- DELETE /api/boards/:id — delete board (ADMIN only)
All endpoints require authentication.
Include Zod validation for request bodies.

Claude generates the complete route handler, service, and validation:

typescript
// packages/api/src/routes/boards.ts

import { Router } from 'express';
import { z } from 'zod';
import { authenticate } from '../middleware/auth.js';
import { validate } from '../middleware/validate.js';
import * as boardService from '../services/board.service.js';

const router = Router();

const createBoardSchema = z.object({
  body: z.object({
    title: z.string().min(1).max(100),
  }),
});

const updateBoardSchema = z.object({
  body: z.object({
    title: z.string().min(1).max(100).optional(),
  }),
  params: z.object({
    id: z.string().cuid(),
  }),
});

router.use(authenticate);

router.post('/', validate(createBoardSchema), async (req, res) => {
  const board = await boardService.createBoard({
    title: req.body.title,
    ownerId: req.user!.id,
  });
  res.status(201).json({ data: board });
});

router.get('/', async (req, res) => {
  const boards = await boardService.getBoardsForUser(req.user!.id);
  res.json({ data: boards });
});

router.get('/:id', async (req, res) => {
  const board = await boardService.getBoardWithContents(
    req.params.id,
    req.user!.id
  );
  res.json({ data: board });
});

router.patch('/:id', validate(updateBoardSchema), async (req, res) => {
  const board = await boardService.updateBoard(
    req.params.id,
    req.user!.id,
    req.body
  );
  res.json({ data: board });
});

router.delete('/:id', async (req, res) => {
  await boardService.deleteBoard(req.params.id, req.user!.id);
  res.status(204).send();
});

export default router;
typescript
// packages/api/src/services/board.service.ts

import { prisma } from '../lib/prisma.js';
import { ForbiddenError, NotFoundError } from '../lib/errors.js';

export async function createBoard(data: { title: string; ownerId: string }) {
  return prisma.board.create({
    data: {
      title: data.title,
      ownerId: data.ownerId,
      members: {
        create: {
          userId: data.ownerId,
          role: 'ADMIN',
        },
      },
    },
    include: {
      members: { include: { user: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } } } },
    },
  });
}

export async function getBoardsForUser(userId: string) {
  return prisma.board.findMany({
    where: {
      members: { some: { userId } },
    },
    include: {
      _count: { select: { lists: true } },
      owner: { select: { id: true, name: true } },
    },
    orderBy: { updatedAt: 'desc' },
  });
}

export async function getBoardWithContents(boardId: string, userId: string) {
  const board = await prisma.board.findUnique({
    where: { id: boardId },
    include: {
      members: {
        include: {
          user: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true, avatarUrl: true } },
        },
      },
      lists: {
        orderBy: { position: 'asc' },
        include: {
          cards: {
            orderBy: { position: 'asc' },
            include: {
              assignee: { select: { id: true, name: true, avatarUrl: true } },
            },
          },
        },
      },
    },
  });

  if (!board) throw new NotFoundError('Board not found');

  const isMember = board.members.some((m) => m.userId === userId);
  if (!isMember) throw new ForbiddenError('Not a member of this board');

  return board;
}

export async function updateBoard(
  boardId: string,
  userId: string,
  data: { title?: string }
) {
  await assertBoardMember(boardId, userId, ['ADMIN', 'MEMBER']);
  return prisma.board.update({
    where: { id: boardId },
    data,
  });
}

export async function deleteBoard(boardId: string, userId: string) {
  await assertBoardMember(boardId, userId, ['ADMIN']);
  await prisma.board.delete({ where: { id: boardId } });
}

async function assertBoardMember(
  boardId: string,
  userId: string,
  roles: string[]
) {
  const membership = await prisma.boardMember.findUnique({
    where: { boardId_userId: { boardId, userId } },
  });

  if (!membership) throw new ForbiddenError('Not a member of this board');
  if (!roles.includes(membership.role)) {
    throw new ForbiddenError(`Requires one of: ${roles.join(', ')}`);
  }
}

The pattern here is important: routes are thin, services have the logic. Claude follows this pattern because we specified it in CLAUDE.md. Without that specification, Claude might put the Prisma queries directly in the route handlers — functional but harder to test.

We continue building the API incrementally:

Now build the list and card endpoints following the same pattern.
Lists: POST, PATCH, DELETE, and PATCH for reordering.
Cards: full CRUD plus PATCH for moving between lists.

Add the auth endpoints:
- POST /api/auth/register — email, password, name
- POST /api/auth/login — returns access + refresh tokens
- POST /api/auth/refresh — exchange refresh token for new access token
- POST /api/auth/logout — invalidate refresh token
Use bcrypt for passwords, JWT for tokens (15min access, 7day refresh).

Each prompt produces complete, tested code. Claude follows the patterns established in the first set of endpoints.

Phase 3: Frontend¶

Component Architecture¶

Create the React component structure for the board view. We need:
- BoardPage: fetches board data, handles WebSocket connection
- BoardHeader: title, members, settings
- ListView: column with cards, add card button, drag zone
- CardItem: card preview (title, labels, assignee avatar)
- CardModal: full card editor (opens on click)
- AddCard: inline form for creating new cards
Use React Query for data fetching and Zustand for drag state.

Claude generates the component tree. Here is the core board view:

tsx
// packages/web/src/components/Board/BoardPage.tsx

import { useParams } from 'react-router-dom';
import { useQuery } from '@tanstack/react-query';
import { api } from '../../lib/api';
import { BoardHeader } from './BoardHeader';
import { ListView } from './ListView';
import { AddList } from './AddList';
import { useWebSocket } from '../../hooks/useWebSocket';
import type { Board } from '@taskflow/shared';

export function BoardPage() {
  const { boardId } = useParams<{ boardId: string }>();

  const { data: board, isLoading, error } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ['board', boardId],
    queryFn: () => api.get<Board>(`/boards/${boardId}`),
    enabled: !!boardId,
  });

  useWebSocket(boardId);

  if (isLoading) return <BoardSkeleton />;
  if (error) return <ErrorState error={error} />;
  if (!board) return null;

  return (
    <div className="flex h-screen flex-col bg-blue-50">
      <BoardHeader board={board} />
      <div className="flex flex-1 gap-4 overflow-x-auto p-4">
        {board.lists.map((list) => (
          <ListView key={list.id} list={list} boardId={board.id} />
        ))}
        <AddList boardId={board.id} position={board.lists.length} />
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

function BoardSkeleton() {
  return (
    <div className="flex h-screen flex-col bg-blue-50">
      <div className="h-14 animate-pulse bg-blue-100" />
      <div className="flex flex-1 gap-4 p-4">
        {[1, 2, 3].map((i) => (
          <div
            key={i}
            className="h-96 w-72 animate-pulse rounded-lg bg-gray-200"
          />
        ))}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

function ErrorState({ error }: { error: Error }) {
  return (
    <div className="flex h-screen items-center justify-center">
      <div className="text-center">
        <h2 className="text-xl font-bold text-gray-900">
          Failed to load board
        </h2>
        <p className="mt-2 text-gray-600">{error.message}</p>
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}
tsx
// packages/web/src/components/Board/ListView.tsx

import { useMutation, useQueryClient } from '@tanstack/react-query';
import { api } from '../../lib/api';
import { CardItem } from './CardItem';
import { AddCard } from './AddCard';
import type { List } from '@taskflow/shared';

interface ListViewProps {
  list: List;
  boardId: string;
}

export function ListView({ list, boardId }: ListViewProps) {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();

  const deleteList = useMutation({
    mutationFn: () => api.delete(`/boards/${boardId}/lists/${list.id}`),
    onSuccess: () => queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['board', boardId] }),
  });

  return (
    <div className="flex w-72 flex-shrink-0 flex-col rounded-lg bg-gray-100">
      <div className="flex items-center justify-between p-3">
        <h3 className="font-semibold text-gray-800">{list.title}</h3>
        <button
          onClick={() => deleteList.mutate()}
          className="text-gray-400 hover:text-red-500"
          aria-label={`Delete ${list.title}`}
        >
          &times;
        </button>
      </div>

      <div className="flex-1 space-y-2 overflow-y-auto px-3">
        {list.cards.map((card) => (
          <CardItem key={card.id} card={card} />
        ))}
      </div>

      <div className="p-3">
        <AddCard listId={list.id} boardId={boardId} position={list.cards.length} />
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

State Management¶

For this application, we use two state management approaches:

Set up state management:
1. React Query for all server state (boards, lists, cards)
2. Zustand for UI state (which card modal is open, drag state, sidebar open/closed)
Create the stores and the React Query configuration.

Claude generates the Zustand store:

typescript
// packages/web/src/stores/uiStore.ts

import { create } from 'zustand';

interface UIState {
  activeCardId: string | null;
  isSidebarOpen: boolean;
  dragState: {
    cardId: string | null;
    sourceListId: string | null;
    targetListId: string | null;
  };
  openCard: (cardId: string) => void;
  closeCard: () => void;
  toggleSidebar: () => void;
  startDrag: (cardId: string, sourceListId: string) => void;
  updateDragTarget: (targetListId: string) => void;
  endDrag: () => void;
}

export const useUIStore = create<UIState>((set) => ({
  activeCardId: null,
  isSidebarOpen: true,
  dragState: { cardId: null, sourceListId: null, targetListId: null },

  openCard: (cardId) => set({ activeCardId: cardId }),
  closeCard: () => set({ activeCardId: null }),
  toggleSidebar: () => set((s) => ({ isSidebarOpen: !s.isSidebarOpen })),
  startDrag: (cardId, sourceListId) =>
    set({ dragState: { cardId, sourceListId, targetListId: null } }),
  updateDragTarget: (targetListId) =>
    set((s) => ({ dragState: { ...s.dragState, targetListId } })),
  endDrag: () =>
    set({ dragState: { cardId: null, sourceListId: null, targetListId: null } }),
}));

The separation is clean: React Query handles data from the server (fetching, caching, mutations), Zustand handles ephemeral UI state (modal open, drag in progress). Claude makes this architectural decision because we specified it in CLAUDE.md.

Phase 4: Real-Time Features¶

This is where /plan becomes valuable. Real-time updates touch the entire stack.

/plan
Add real-time updates to TaskFlow. When one user creates, moves, or deletes
a card, all other users viewing the same board should see the change instantly.
Use WebSocket with the ws library on the backend.

Claude produces a plan covering: 1. WebSocket server setup alongside Express 2. Room-based subscriptions (one room per board) 3. Event types and payload schemas 4. Frontend WebSocket hook with reconnection 5. React Query cache invalidation on WebSocket events

After reviewing the plan, we execute:

Execute the real-time plan. Start with the backend WebSocket server,
then the frontend hook.

Backend WebSocket setup:

typescript
// packages/api/src/ws/index.ts

import { WebSocketServer, WebSocket } from 'ws';
import { Server } from 'http';
import { verifyToken } from '../lib/jwt.js';
import { logger } from '../lib/logger.js';

interface Client {
  ws: WebSocket;
  userId: string;
  boardId: string | null;
}

const clients = new Map<WebSocket, Client>();

export function setupWebSocket(server: Server) {
  const wss = new WebSocketServer({ server, path: '/ws' });

  wss.on('connection', async (ws, req) => {
    const url = new URL(req.url!, `http://${req.headers.host}`);
    const token = url.searchParams.get('token');

    if (!token) {
      ws.close(4001, 'Missing token');
      return;
    }

    try {
      const payload = verifyToken(token);
      clients.set(ws, { ws, userId: payload.userId, boardId: null });

      ws.on('message', (data) => handleMessage(ws, data.toString()));
      ws.on('close', () => clients.delete(ws));

      ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type: 'connected' }));
    } catch {
      ws.close(4001, 'Invalid token');
    }
  });

  return { broadcast };
}

function handleMessage(ws: WebSocket, raw: string) {
  try {
    const msg = JSON.parse(raw);

    if (msg.type === 'join_board') {
      const client = clients.get(ws);
      if (client) {
        client.boardId = msg.boardId;
        logger.info({ userId: client.userId, boardId: msg.boardId }, 'Client joined board');
      }
    }

    if (msg.type === 'leave_board') {
      const client = clients.get(ws);
      if (client) {
        client.boardId = null;
      }
    }
  } catch (err) {
    logger.warn({ err }, 'Invalid WebSocket message');
  }
}

export function broadcast(boardId: string, event: object, excludeUserId?: string) {
  const message = JSON.stringify(event);
  for (const [, client] of clients) {
    if (
      client.boardId === boardId &&
      client.userId !== excludeUserId &&
      client.ws.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN
    ) {
      client.ws.send(message);
    }
  }
}

Frontend WebSocket hook with automatic reconnection:

typescript
// packages/web/src/hooks/useWebSocket.ts

import { useEffect, useRef, useCallback } from 'react';
import { useQueryClient } from '@tanstack/react-query';
import { useAuth } from './useAuth';

const WS_URL = import.meta.env.VITE_WS_URL || 'ws://localhost:3001/ws';
const RECONNECT_DELAY = 3000;
const MAX_RECONNECT_ATTEMPTS = 5;

export function useWebSocket(boardId: string | undefined) {
  const wsRef = useRef<WebSocket | null>(null);
  const reconnectAttempts = useRef(0);
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();
  const { token } = useAuth();

  const connect = useCallback(() => {
    if (!boardId || !token) return;

    const ws = new WebSocket(`${WS_URL}?token=${token}`);
    wsRef.current = ws;

    ws.onopen = () => {
      reconnectAttempts.current = 0;
      ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type: 'join_board', boardId }));
    };

    ws.onmessage = (event) => {
      const data = JSON.parse(event.data);

      switch (data.type) {
        case 'board_updated':
        case 'list_created':
        case 'list_updated':
        case 'list_deleted':
        case 'card_created':
        case 'card_updated':
        case 'card_moved':
        case 'card_deleted':
          queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['board', boardId] });
          break;
      }
    };

    ws.onclose = () => {
      if (reconnectAttempts.current < MAX_RECONNECT_ATTEMPTS) {
        reconnectAttempts.current++;
        setTimeout(connect, RECONNECT_DELAY);
      }
    };

    ws.onerror = () => ws.close();
  }, [boardId, token, queryClient]);

  useEffect(() => {
    connect();
    return () => {
      wsRef.current?.close();
    };
  }, [connect]);
}

Optimistic Updates¶

For a responsive feel, card movements update the UI immediately:

Add optimistic updates for card drag-and-drop. When the user drops a card:
1. Immediately update the UI
2. Send the API request
3. If the request fails, roll back the UI and show an error toast

Claude integrates React Query’s optimistic update pattern:

typescript
// packages/web/src/hooks/useMoveCard.ts

import { useMutation, useQueryClient } from '@tanstack/react-query';
import { api } from '../lib/api';
import { toast } from '../lib/toast';
import type { Board } from '@taskflow/shared';

interface MoveCardInput {
  cardId: string;
  sourceListId: string;
  targetListId: string;
  position: number;
}

export function useMoveCard(boardId: string) {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();

  return useMutation({
    mutationFn: (input: MoveCardInput) =>
      api.patch(`/boards/${boardId}/cards/${input.cardId}/move`, {
        targetListId: input.targetListId,
        position: input.position,
      }),

    onMutate: async (input) => {
      await queryClient.cancelQueries({ queryKey: ['board', boardId] });

      const previousBoard = queryClient.getQueryData<Board>(['board', boardId]);

      queryClient.setQueryData<Board>(['board', boardId], (old) => {
        if (!old) return old;

        const newLists = old.lists.map((list) => ({
          ...list,
          cards: [...list.cards],
        }));

        const sourceList = newLists.find((l) => l.id === input.sourceListId);
        const targetList = newLists.find((l) => l.id === input.targetListId);
        if (!sourceList || !targetList) return old;

        const cardIndex = sourceList.cards.findIndex((c) => c.id === input.cardId);
        if (cardIndex === -1) return old;

        const [card] = sourceList.cards.splice(cardIndex, 1);
        targetList.cards.splice(input.position, 0, card);

        return { ...old, lists: newLists };
      });

      return { previousBoard };
    },

    onError: (_err, _input, context) => {
      if (context?.previousBoard) {
        queryClient.setQueryData(['board', boardId], context.previousBoard);
      }
      toast.error('Failed to move card. Change has been reverted.');
    },

    onSettled: () => {
      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['board', boardId] });
    },
  });
}

Phase 5: Testing¶

API Integration Tests¶

Write integration tests for the board API endpoints.
Test with a real database using Prisma and a test helper that
creates/destroys test data. Use Supertest for HTTP requests.

Claude generates comprehensive tests:

typescript
// packages/api/src/__tests__/boards.test.ts

import { describe, it, expect, beforeEach } from 'vitest';
import request from 'supertest';
import { app } from '../server.js';
import { prisma } from '../lib/prisma.js';
import { createTestUser, getAuthToken, cleanDatabase } from './helpers.js';

describe('Board API', () => {
  let authToken: string;
  let userId: string;

  beforeEach(async () => {
    await cleanDatabase();
    const user = await createTestUser();
    userId = user.id;
    authToken = getAuthToken(user);
  });

  describe('POST /api/boards', () => {
    it('creates a board and adds creator as ADMIN', async () => {
      const res = await request(app)
        .post('/api/boards')
        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${authToken}`)
        .send({ title: 'My Board' });

      expect(res.status).toBe(201);
      expect(res.body.data.title).toBe('My Board');
      expect(res.body.data.members).toHaveLength(1);
      expect(res.body.data.members[0].role).toBe('ADMIN');
      expect(res.body.data.members[0].user.id).toBe(userId);
    });

    it('rejects empty title', async () => {
      const res = await request(app)
        .post('/api/boards')
        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${authToken}`)
        .send({ title: '' });

      expect(res.status).toBe(400);
    });

    it('rejects unauthenticated requests', async () => {
      const res = await request(app)
        .post('/api/boards')
        .send({ title: 'My Board' });

      expect(res.status).toBe(401);
    });
  });

  describe('GET /api/boards', () => {
    it('returns only boards the user is a member of', async () => {
      await request(app)
        .post('/api/boards')
        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${authToken}`)
        .send({ title: 'My Board' });

      const otherUser = await createTestUser({ email: 'other@test.com' });
      const otherToken = getAuthToken(otherUser);
      await request(app)
        .post('/api/boards')
        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${otherToken}`)
        .send({ title: 'Other Board' });

      const res = await request(app)
        .get('/api/boards')
        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${authToken}`);

      expect(res.status).toBe(200);
      expect(res.body.data).toHaveLength(1);
      expect(res.body.data[0].title).toBe('My Board');
    });
  });

  describe('DELETE /api/boards/:id', () => {
    it('allows ADMIN to delete', async () => {
      const createRes = await request(app)
        .post('/api/boards')
        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${authToken}`)
        .send({ title: 'To Delete' });

      const boardId = createRes.body.data.id;

      const res = await request(app)
        .delete(`/api/boards/${boardId}`)
        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${authToken}`);

      expect(res.status).toBe(204);

      const board = await prisma.board.findUnique({ where: { id: boardId } });
      expect(board).toBeNull();
    });

    it('rejects non-ADMIN member', async () => {
      const createRes = await request(app)
        .post('/api/boards')
        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${authToken}`)
        .send({ title: 'Protected' });

      const boardId = createRes.body.data.id;
      const viewer = await createTestUser({ email: 'viewer@test.com' });
      await prisma.boardMember.create({
        data: { boardId, userId: viewer.id, role: 'VIEWER' },
      });

      const viewerToken = getAuthToken(viewer);
      const res = await request(app)
        .delete(`/api/boards/${boardId}`)
        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${viewerToken}`);

      expect(res.status).toBe(403);
    });
  });
});

Frontend Component Tests¶

Write component tests for BoardPage and ListView using React Testing Library.
Mock the API with MSW (Mock Service Worker).

Claude generates tests using MSW for realistic API mocking:

typescript
// packages/web/src/components/Board/__tests__/BoardPage.test.tsx

import { describe, it, expect, beforeEach } from 'vitest';
import { render, screen, waitFor } from '@testing-library/react';
import { http, HttpResponse } from 'msw';
import { setupServer } from 'msw/node';
import { BoardPage } from '../BoardPage';
import { TestProviders } from '../../../test/providers';
import { mockBoard } from '../../../test/fixtures';

const server = setupServer(
  http.get('/api/boards/:id', () => {
    return HttpResponse.json({ data: mockBoard });
  })
);

beforeEach(() => server.resetHandlers());

describe('BoardPage', () => {
  it('renders board with lists and cards', async () => {
    render(
      <TestProviders route="/boards/board-1">
        <BoardPage />
      </TestProviders>
    );

    await waitFor(() => {
      expect(screen.getByText('Project Tasks')).toBeInTheDocument();
    });

    expect(screen.getByText('To Do')).toBeInTheDocument();
    expect(screen.getByText('In Progress')).toBeInTheDocument();
    expect(screen.getByText('Fix login bug')).toBeInTheDocument();
  });

  it('shows loading skeleton while fetching', () => {
    render(
      <TestProviders route="/boards/board-1">
        <BoardPage />
      </TestProviders>
    );

    expect(screen.getByTestId('board-skeleton')).toBeInTheDocument();
  });

  it('shows error state on API failure', async () => {
    server.use(
      http.get('/api/boards/:id', () => {
        return HttpResponse.json(
          { error: { title: 'Not Found' } },
          { status: 404 }
        );
      })
    );

    render(
      <TestProviders route="/boards/board-1">
        <BoardPage />
      </TestProviders>
    );

    await waitFor(() => {
      expect(screen.getByText('Failed to load board')).toBeInTheDocument();
    });
  });
});

Phase 6: Deployment¶

Docker Configuration¶

Create production Docker setup:
1. Multi-stage Dockerfile for the API (build + runtime stages)
2. Dockerfile for the web frontend (build + nginx)
3. docker-compose.prod.yml with all services
4. GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline

Claude generates a production-ready multi-stage Dockerfile:

dockerfile
# packages/api/Dockerfile

# Build stage
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app

COPY pnpm-workspace.yaml pnpm-lock.yaml package.json ./
COPY packages/shared/package.json packages/shared/
COPY packages/api/package.json packages/api/

RUN corepack enable pnpm && pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

COPY packages/shared/ packages/shared/
COPY packages/api/ packages/api/

RUN pnpm --filter @taskflow/shared build
RUN pnpm --filter @taskflow/api build
RUN pnpm --filter @taskflow/api exec prisma generate

# Runtime stage
FROM node:20-alpine AS runtime
WORKDIR /app

RUN addgroup -g 1001 -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -u 1001 -G appgroup

COPY --from=builder /app/packages/api/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/packages/api/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder /app/packages/api/package.json ./
COPY --from=builder /app/packages/api/src/prisma ./prisma

USER appuser

EXPOSE 3001
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]

CI/CD Pipeline¶

yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml

name: CI/CD

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: test
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
          POSTGRES_DB: taskflow_test
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: pnpm

      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - run: pnpm --filter api exec prisma migrate deploy
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://test:test@localhost:5432/taskflow_test
      - run: pnpm test
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://test:test@localhost:5432/taskflow_test
          JWT_SECRET: test-secret-not-for-production

  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: pnpm

      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - run: pnpm lint
      - run: pnpm --filter api exec tsc --noEmit
      - run: pnpm --filter web exec tsc --noEmit

  deploy:
    needs: [test, lint]
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Build and push Docker images
        run: |
          docker build -f packages/api/Dockerfile -t taskflow-api .
          docker build -f packages/web/Dockerfile -t taskflow-web .
      # Add your registry push and deployment steps here

Pre-Deploy Verification¶

Before shipping, run /deploy-check:

/deploy-check

Claude runs through the full checklist:

Check Status Details
Git clean PASS No uncommitted changes
No secrets PASS No API keys, tokens, or passwords in code
Dependencies PASS No known CVEs, all pinned
Tests PASS 47 tests passing
Type check PASS No TypeScript errors
Build PASS Both packages build successfully
Migrations PASS All migrations applied

DEPLOY READY: YES

Lessons Learned¶

After building TaskFlow entirely with Claude Code, here are the key takeaways:

Use /plan for multi-step features, direct prompts for single-step tasks. The WebSocket feature needed a plan because it touched backend, frontend, and state management. Adding a new field to a card did not.

Work incrementally. Build the board CRUD first, then lists, then cards, then real-time, then tests. Each phase builds on the previous one and can be verified independently.

The CLAUDE.md pays for itself immediately. Without the “routes are thin, services have logic” directive, Claude would have produced working but inconsistent code. That one line saved dozens of corrections across 30+ files.

Let Claude write the tests. Integration tests are tedious. Claude writes them thoroughly and correctly. The key insight: specify what to test (happy paths, auth failures, validation errors), let Claude handle how.

Use /rewind when Claude goes off track. Twice during development, Claude made an architectural decision that diverged from the plan. Rather than trying to course-correct, /rewind to the last good state and re-prompt with more specific instructions.

Chapter Summary¶

  1. Start with a scaffold, then refine. One prompt generates the project structure. A few correction prompts shape it to your exact needs. This is faster than specifying everything upfront.

  2. Work incrementally, verify at each phase. Database, API, frontend, real-time, tests, deployment. Each phase is independently verifiable. Do not try to build everything in one prompt.

  3. The CLAUDE.md prevents architectural drift. Conventions documented in CLAUDE.md (thin routes, service layer, state management strategy) are followed consistently across dozens of files.

  4. Real-time features need /plan mode. Any feature that touches the entire stack benefits from a plan. Review the plan, then execute.

  5. Testing is where Claude Code saves the most time. Claude writes comprehensive tests — happy paths, error conditions, edge cases — faster than you can type them. Invest your time in specifying what to test.

Chapter 18: Building CLI Tools and APIs¶ ~16 min read

CLI tools and APIs are Claude Code’s sweet spot. No UI rendering, no browser quirks, no CSS — just structured input, logic, and structured output. This is the domain where Claude Code produces the most reliable, production-ready code with the least correction.

This chapter walks through building two real projects: a Python CLI tool for git repository analytics, and a FastAPI notification service. We finish with a brief look at GraphQL APIs. Every code example is complete, not a stub.

Part 1: Building a Python CLI Tool¶

The Project: git-analytics¶

A CLI tool that answers questions about git repositories:

  • stats: Commit frequency by day, week, or month
  • hotspots: Most frequently changed files (bug magnets)
  • contributors: Activity breakdown by author
  • churn: Files with the most additions + deletions (complexity indicators)

Scaffolding¶

/scaffold python git-analytics

Or with explicit instructions:

Create a Python CLI project called git-analytics.
Use Click for commands, Rich for terminal output, pytest for testing.
Target Python 3.11+. Use pyproject.toml for all config.

Claude generates:

git-analytics/
├── CLAUDE.md
├── .claude/
│   ├── settings.json
│   └── rules/
│       ├── python.md
│       └── cli.md
├── src/
│   └── git_analytics/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── cli.py
│       ├── commands/
│       │   └── __init__.py
│       ├── core/
│       │   ├── __init__.py
│       │   └── git.py
│       └── formatters/
│           ├── __init__.py
│           └── table.py
├── tests/
│   ├── conftest.py
│   └── test_cli.py
├── pyproject.toml
├── .gitignore
└── README.md

The pyproject.toml Claude generates is production-ready:

toml
[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"

[project]
name = "git-analytics"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Analyze git repository statistics"
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "click>=8.1",
    "rich>=13.0",
]

[project.scripts]
git-analytics = "git_analytics.cli:cli"

[tool.ruff]
target-version = "py311"
line-length = 100

[tool.ruff.lint]
select = ["E", "F", "I", "N", "W", "UP", "B", "A", "SIM", "TCH"]

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]
pythonpath = ["src"]

[tool.pyright]
pythonVersion = "3.11"
typeCheckingMode = "strict"

Building the CLI Entry Point¶

Create the CLI entry point with a Click command group.
Include global options: --repo (path to git repo, defaults to current directory),
--json (output as JSON instead of rich tables), --verbose.
python
# src/git_analytics/cli.py

from __future__ import annotations

import sys
from pathlib import Path

import click

from git_analytics.core.git import GitRepo, GitError


class Context:
    """Shared context passed to all commands."""

    def __init__(self, repo_path: Path, json_output: bool, verbose: bool) -> None:
        self.repo_path = repo_path
        self.json_output = json_output
        self.verbose = verbose
        self._repo: GitRepo | None = None

    @property
    def repo(self) -> GitRepo:
        if self._repo is None:
            self._repo = GitRepo(self.repo_path)
        return self._repo


pass_context = click.make_pass_decorator(Context)


@click.group()
@click.option(
    "--repo",
    type=click.Path(exists=True, file_okay=False, path_type=Path),
    default=Path("."),
    help="Path to git repository.",
)
@click.option("--json", "json_output", is_flag=True, help="Output as JSON.")
@click.option("--verbose", "-v", is_flag=True, help="Verbose output.")
@click.version_option()
@click.pass_context
def cli(ctx: click.Context, repo: Path, json_output: bool, verbose: bool) -> None:
    """Analyze git repository statistics."""
    ctx.ensure_object(dict)
    try:
        ctx.obj = Context(repo_path=repo, json_output=json_output, verbose=verbose)
    except GitError as e:
        click.echo(f"Error: {e}", err=True)
        sys.exit(1)


# Import commands to register them
from git_analytics.commands import stats, hotspots, contributors, churn  # noqa: E402, F401

Building the Core Git Module¶

The core module handles all git interactions. No Click dependency here — this is pure logic that can be tested independently.

Build the core git module that reads repository data.
Use subprocess to call git (not a Python git library).
Parse git log output into structured data.
python
# src/git_analytics/core/git.py

from __future__ import annotations

import subprocess
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from pathlib import Path


class GitError(Exception):
    """Raised when git operations fail."""


@dataclass
class Commit:
    hash: str
    author: str
    email: str
    date: datetime
    message: str
    files_changed: int
    insertions: int
    deletions: int


@dataclass
class FileChange:
    path: str
    insertions: int
    deletions: int


class GitRepo:
    """Interface to a git repository via subprocess calls."""

    def __init__(self, path: Path) -> None:
        self.path = path.resolve()
        if not (self.path / ".git").is_dir():
            raise GitError(f"Not a git repository: {self.path}")

    def _run(self, *args: str) -> str:
        """Execute a git command and return stdout."""
        try:
            result = subprocess.run(
                ["git", *args],
                cwd=self.path,
                capture_output=True,
                text=True,
                check=True,
                timeout=60,
            )
            return result.stdout.strip()
        except subprocess.CalledProcessError as e:
            raise GitError(f"git {' '.join(args)} failed: {e.stderr.strip()}") from e
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired as e:
            raise GitError(f"git command timed out: {' '.join(args)}") from e

    def get_commits(self, since: str | None = None, limit: int = 1000) -> list[Commit]:
        """Get commit history with stats."""
        cmd = [
            "log",
            f"--max-count={limit}",
            "--format=%H|%an|%ae|%aI|%s",
            "--shortstat",
        ]
        if since:
            cmd.append(f"--since={since}")

        raw = self._run(*cmd)
        return self._parse_log(raw)

    def get_file_changes(
        self, since: str | None = None, limit: int = 1000
    ) -> dict[str, list[FileChange]]:
        """Get per-file change statistics grouped by commit."""
        cmd = [
            "log",
            f"--max-count={limit}",
            "--format=%H",
            "--numstat",
        ]
        if since:
            cmd.append(f"--since={since}")

        raw = self._run(*cmd)
        return self._parse_numstat(raw)

    def get_hotspots(self, limit: int = 20, since: str | None = None) -> list[tuple[str, int]]:
        """Find most frequently changed files."""
        changes = self.get_file_changes(since=since)
        counter: Counter[str] = Counter()
        for file_changes in changes.values():
            for fc in file_changes:
                if not fc.path.startswith("vendor/") and fc.path != "":
                    counter[fc.path] += 1
        return counter.most_common(limit)

    def get_churn(self, limit: int = 20, since: str | None = None) -> list[tuple[str, int, int]]:
        """Find files with most code churn (insertions + deletions)."""
        changes = self.get_file_changes(since=since)
        churn: defaultdict[str, tuple[int, int]] = defaultdict(lambda: (0, 0))

        for file_changes in changes.values():
            for fc in file_changes:
                if fc.path:
                    ins, dels = churn[fc.path]
                    churn[fc.path] = (ins + fc.insertions, dels + fc.deletions)

        sorted_churn = sorted(
            churn.items(),
            key=lambda x: x[1][0] + x[1][1],
            reverse=True,
        )
        return [(path, ins, dels) for path, (ins, dels) in sorted_churn[:limit]]

    def get_contributors(self, since: str | None = None) -> list[dict[str, int | str]]:
        """Get contributor statistics."""
        commits = self.get_commits(since=since)
        stats: defaultdict[str, dict[str, int]] = defaultdict(
            lambda: {"commits": 0, "insertions": 0, "deletions": 0}
        )

        for commit in commits:
            s = stats[commit.author]
            s["commits"] += 1
            s["insertions"] += commit.insertions
            s["deletions"] += commit.deletions

        result = []
        for author, s in sorted(stats.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]["commits"], reverse=True):
            result.append({"author": author, **s})
        return result

    def _parse_log(self, raw: str) -> list[Commit]:
        """Parse git log --format=%H|%an|%ae|%aI|%s --shortstat output."""
        commits: list[Commit] = []
        lines = raw.split("\n")
        i = 0

        while i < len(lines):
            line = lines[i].strip()
            if not line or "|" not in line:
                i += 1
                continue

            parts = line.split("|", maxsplit=4)
            if len(parts) < 5:
                i += 1
                continue

            hash_, author, email, date_str, message = parts
            files_changed = insertions = deletions = 0

            # Look for shortstat line
            if i + 1 < len(lines):
                stat_line = lines[i + 1].strip()
                if "file" in stat_line:
                    files_changed, insertions, deletions = self._parse_shortstat(stat_line)
                    i += 1

            commits.append(
                Commit(
                    hash=hash_,
                    author=author,
                    email=email,
                    date=datetime.fromisoformat(date_str),
                    message=message,
                    files_changed=files_changed,
                    insertions=insertions,
                    deletions=deletions,
                )
            )
            i += 1

        return commits

    @staticmethod
    def _parse_shortstat(line: str) -> tuple[int, int, int]:
        """Parse a git shortstat line like '3 files changed, 10 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)'."""
        files = insertions = deletions = 0
        for part in line.split(","):
            part = part.strip()
            if "file" in part:
                files = int(part.split()[0])
            elif "insertion" in part:
                insertions = int(part.split()[0])
            elif "deletion" in part:
                deletions = int(part.split()[0])
        return files, insertions, deletions

    def _parse_numstat(self, raw: str) -> dict[str, list[FileChange]]:
        """Parse git log --numstat output into per-commit file changes."""
        result: dict[str, list[FileChange]] = {}
        current_hash = ""
        lines = raw.split("\n")

        for line in lines:
            line = line.strip()
            if not line:
                continue
            if len(line) == 40 and all(c in "0123456789abcdef" for c in line):
                current_hash = line
                result[current_hash] = []
            elif "\t" in line and current_hash:
                parts = line.split("\t")
                if len(parts) == 3:
                    ins_str, del_str, path = parts
                    ins = int(ins_str) if ins_str != "-" else 0
                    dels = int(del_str) if del_str != "-" else 0
                    result[current_hash].append(FileChange(path=path, insertions=ins, deletions=dels))

        return result

Building Commands¶

Now we build each command. Here is the prompt and output for the stats command:

Add a 'stats' command that shows commit frequency grouped by day, week, or month.
Include a --since option (e.g., "3 months ago") and a --group-by option (day/week/month).
Output as a Rich table or JSON based on the global --json flag.
python
# src/git_analytics/commands/stats.py

from __future__ import annotations

import json
from collections import Counter
from datetime import datetime

import click
from rich.console import Console
from rich.table import Table

from git_analytics.cli import cli, pass_context, Context


@cli.command()
@click.option(
    "--since",
    default="6 months ago",
    help="Show stats since this date (e.g., '3 months ago', '2024-01-01').",
)
@click.option(
    "--group-by",
    type=click.Choice(["day", "week", "month"]),
    default="week",
    help="Group commits by time period.",
)
@pass_context
def stats(ctx: Context, since: str, group_by: str) -> None:
    """Show commit frequency over time."""
    commits = ctx.repo.get_commits(since=since)

    if not commits:
        click.echo("No commits found in the specified range.", err=True)
        return

    groups = _group_commits(commits, group_by)
    total_commits = len(commits)
    total_insertions = sum(c.insertions for c in commits)
    total_deletions = sum(c.deletions for c in commits)

    if ctx.json_output:
        data = {
            "summary": {
                "total_commits": total_commits,
                "total_insertions": total_insertions,
                "total_deletions": total_deletions,
                "period": since,
                "group_by": group_by,
            },
            "groups": [
                {"period": period, "commits": count}
                for period, count in sorted(groups.items())
            ],
        }
        click.echo(json.dumps(data, indent=2))
        return

    console = Console()
    console.print(f"\n[bold]Commit Statistics[/bold] (since {since})\n")
    console.print(
        f"Total: [green]{total_commits}[/green] commits, "
        f"[green]+{total_insertions}[/green] / [red]-{total_deletions}[/red] lines\n"
    )

    table = Table(show_header=True, header_style="bold cyan")
    table.add_column("Period", style="dim")
    table.add_column("Commits", justify="right")
    table.add_column("Graph", min_width=30)

    max_count = max(groups.values()) if groups else 1
    for period, count in sorted(groups.items()):
        bar_length = int((count / max_count) * 30)
        bar = "█" * bar_length
        table.add_row(period, str(count), f"[green]{bar}[/green]")

    console.print(table)


def _group_commits(commits: list, group_by: str) -> Counter[str]:
    """Group commits by time period and count them."""
    counter: Counter[str] = Counter()
    for commit in commits:
        dt: datetime = commit.date
        if group_by == "day":
            key = dt.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        elif group_by == "week":
            key = f"{dt.isocalendar()[0]}-W{dt.isocalendar()[1]:02d}"
        elif group_by == "month":
            key = dt.strftime("%Y-%m")
        else:
            key = dt.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        counter[key] += 1
    return counter

The hotspots command:

Add a 'hotspots' command that finds the most frequently changed files.
Include --limit (default 20) and --since options.
python
# src/git_analytics/commands/hotspots.py

from __future__ import annotations

import json

import click
from rich.console import Console
from rich.table import Table

from git_analytics.cli import cli, pass_context, Context


@cli.command()
@click.option("--limit", "-n", default=20, help="Number of files to show.")
@click.option("--since", default=None, help="Only consider commits since this date.")
@pass_context
def hotspots(ctx: Context, limit: int, since: str | None) -> None:
    """Find the most frequently changed files (bug magnets)."""
    results = ctx.repo.get_hotspots(limit=limit, since=since)

    if not results:
        click.echo("No file changes found.", err=True)
        return

    if ctx.json_output:
        data = [{"file": path, "changes": count} for path, count in results]
        click.echo(json.dumps(data, indent=2))
        return

    console = Console()
    console.print("\n[bold]File Hotspots[/bold] (most frequently changed files)\n")

    table = Table(show_header=True, header_style="bold cyan")
    table.add_column("#", justify="right", style="dim", width=4)
    table.add_column("File")
    table.add_column("Changes", justify="right")
    table.add_column("Frequency", min_width=20)

    max_count = results[0][1] if results else 1
    for i, (path, count) in enumerate(results, 1):
        bar_length = int((count / max_count) * 20)
        bar = "█" * bar_length
        heat = "red" if i <= 3 else "yellow" if i <= 10 else "green"
        table.add_row(str(i), path, str(count), f"[{heat}]{bar}[/{heat}]")

    console.print(table)

Testing CLI Commands¶

Testing CLIs is straightforward with Click’s CliRunner. We test against real git repositories:

python
# tests/conftest.py

from __future__ import annotations

import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

import pytest


@pytest.fixture
def temp_git_repo(tmp_path: Path) -> Path:
    """Create a temporary git repository with sample commits."""
    repo = tmp_path / "test-repo"
    repo.mkdir()

    subprocess.run(["git", "init"], cwd=repo, capture_output=True, check=True)
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "config", "user.email", "test@example.com"],
        cwd=repo, capture_output=True, check=True,
    )
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "config", "user.name", "Test User"],
        cwd=repo, capture_output=True, check=True,
    )

    # Create initial commit
    (repo / "README.md").write_text("# Test Project")
    subprocess.run(["git", "add", "."], cwd=repo, capture_output=True, check=True)
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "commit", "-m", "Initial commit"],
        cwd=repo, capture_output=True, check=True,
    )

    # Add some files with multiple commits
    for i in range(5):
        (repo / "main.py").write_text(f"# version {i}\nprint('hello')\n")
        (repo / "utils.py").write_text(f"# utils v{i}\ndef helper(): pass\n")
        subprocess.run(["git", "add", "."], cwd=repo, capture_output=True, check=True)
        subprocess.run(
            ["git", "commit", "-m", f"Update {i}"],
            cwd=repo, capture_output=True, check=True,
        )

    # main.py has more changes
    for i in range(3):
        (repo / "main.py").write_text(f"# hotfix {i}\nprint('fixed')\n")
        subprocess.run(["git", "add", "."], cwd=repo, capture_output=True, check=True)
        subprocess.run(
            ["git", "commit", "-m", f"Hotfix {i}"],
            cwd=repo, capture_output=True, check=True,
        )

    return repo
python
# tests/test_cli.py

from __future__ import annotations

from pathlib import Path

from click.testing import CliRunner
