  • Code review uses Sonnet (~$0.15 per PR)
  • Security review only runs for high-risk PRs using Sonnet with high effort (~$0.30 per PR)

Total cost for a low-risk PR: ~$0.16. For a high-risk PR: ~$0.46. At 50 PRs per week, that is $8-23 per week for automated, consistent reviews.

Running Skills in CI: /security-audit and /code-review¶

If you have Claude Code skills configured in your repository (see Chapter 21 on custom agents and intent routing), you can invoke them directly in CI. This gives you the same structured analysis in CI that you get locally:

yaml
name: Skill-Based Review

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

jobs:
  security-audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Install Claude Code
        run: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

      - name: Run security audit skill
        run: |
          CHANGED_FILES=$(git diff --name-only origin/${{ github.base_ref }}...HEAD | head -20)
          claude --print "Run /security-audit on the following changed files: ${CHANGED_FILES}" \
            --model claude-sonnet-4-6 \
            --max-turns 10 \
            --output-format text > /tmp/security-audit.txt
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}

      - name: Post audit results
        if: always()
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const audit = fs.readFileSync('/tmp/security-audit.txt', 'utf8');

            await github.rest.issues.createComment({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              body: `## 🔒 Security Audit Results\n\n${audit}\n\n---\n*Automated by Claude Code /security-audit*`,
            });

The advantage of skill-based CI is consistency. The same /security-audit skill that runs locally, with the same checklist and output format, runs in CI. Developers see the same type of findings in PR comments that they see in their terminal.

Breaking Change Detection¶

yaml
# .github/workflows/breaking-change.yml

name: Breaking Change Check

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/api/**'
      - 'packages/shared/**'
      - 'prisma/schema.prisma'

jobs:
  check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
          prompt: |
            Analyze this PR for breaking changes. A breaking change is anything
            that would cause existing clients to fail:

            - Removed or renamed API endpoints
            - Changed request/response schemas
            - Removed or renamed exported functions/types
            - Database schema changes that break existing queries
            - Changed environment variable names

            Output:
            ## Breaking Changes
            - [ ] List each breaking change with file:line

            ## Migration Required
            - [ ] Steps clients need to take

            If no breaking changes, output: "No breaking changes detected."

Automated Fixes¶

Lint Fix and Commit¶

When linting fails in CI, Claude Code can fix the issues and push the correction:

yaml
name: Auto-Fix Lint

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  lint-fix:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.head_ref }}
          token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install tools
        run: pip install ruff

      - name: Check for lint issues
        id: lint
        run: |
          if ! ruff check src/ --output-format json > lint-results.json 2>&1; then
            echo "has_issues=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          else
            echo "has_issues=false" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi

      - name: Auto-fix
        if: steps.lint.outputs.has_issues == 'true'
        run: |
          ruff check src/ --fix
          ruff format src/

      - name: Commit fixes
        if: steps.lint.outputs.has_issues == 'true'
        run: |
          git config user.name "Claude Code Bot"
          git config user.email "claude-code-bot@noreply.github.com"
          git add -A
          git diff --cached --quiet || git commit -m "style: auto-fix lint issues

          Co-Authored-By: Claude Code <noreply@anthropic.com>"
          git push

Dependency Update Analysis¶

When Dependabot or Renovate creates a dependency update PR, Claude Code can analyze the impact:

yaml
name: Analyze Dependency Update

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'package.json'
      - 'pnpm-lock.yaml'
      - 'requirements.txt'
      - 'pyproject.toml'

jobs:
  analyze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'dependencies')
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
          prompt: |
            This is a dependency update PR. Analyze it:

            1. What packages are being updated?
            2. Are there any breaking changes in the new versions?
            3. Check the changelog of each updated package for security fixes
            4. Are there any known CVEs fixed by this update?
            5. Risk assessment: safe to merge automatically, or needs manual review?

            Output:
            | Package | Old Version | New Version | Breaking? | Security Fix? | Risk |

            Recommendation: AUTO-MERGE / MANUAL REVIEW NEEDED

Deployment Pipelines¶

Staging to Production with Claude Code Verification¶

The most valuable CI/CD integration is not just reviewing code — it is verifying deployments. Claude Code can act as a deployment gatekeeper, checking that what you are about to ship matches what was approved in the PR.

Here is a full staging-to-production pipeline with Claude Code verification at each transition:

yaml
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml

name: Deploy Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: write
  deployments: write
  issues: write

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }}

jobs:
  # ── Build ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image_tag: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
      image_digest: ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Docker meta
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        with:
          images: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}
          tags: |
            type=sha,prefix=

      - name: Build and push
        id: build
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: .
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}

  # ── Deploy to Staging ──────────────────────────────────
  deploy-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: build
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/app \
            app=${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }} \
            --namespace staging
          kubectl rollout status deployment/app --namespace staging --timeout=300s

      - name: Run smoke tests
        run: |
          # Wait for deployment to stabilize
          sleep 10
          # Hit health endpoint
          HTTP_STATUS=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://staging.example.com/health)
          if [[ "${HTTP_STATUS}" != "200" ]]; then
            echo "Health check failed with status ${HTTP_STATUS}"
            exit 1
          fi

  # ── Claude Code Staging Verification ───────────────────
  verify-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: deploy-staging
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
          prompt: |
            Pre-production deployment check for commit ${{ github.sha }}.

            1. Read the git log for this commit and associated PRs
            2. Identify what changed (features, fixes, migrations)
            3. Assess deployment risk:
               - Does this include database migrations?
               - Does this change API contracts?
               - Does this modify authentication/authorization?
               - Does this update infrastructure config?
            4. Generate a deployment summary

            Output:
            ## Deployment Summary
            - **Commit:** ${{ github.sha }}
            - **Risk Level:** LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH
            - **Changes:** [bullet list]
            - **Requires Rollback Plan:** YES / NO
            - **Recommended:** PROCEED / HOLD FOR MANUAL REVIEW

  # ── Deploy to Production (Blue-Green) ──────────────────
  deploy-production:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: verify-staging
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Deploy green instance
        id: green
        run: |
          # Deploy new version as "green"
          kubectl set image deployment/app-green \
            app=${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }} \
            --namespace production
          kubectl rollout status deployment/app-green --namespace production --timeout=300s

      - name: Smoke test green
        run: |
          HTTP_STATUS=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://green.example.com/health)
          if [[ "${HTTP_STATUS}" != "200" ]]; then
            echo "Green health check failed"
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Switch traffic to green
        run: |
          # Update service selector to point to green
          kubectl patch service app-service \
            -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"version":"green"}}}' \
            --namespace production

      - name: Verify production
        run: |
          sleep 5
          HTTP_STATUS=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://app.example.com/health)
          if [[ "${HTTP_STATUS}" != "200" ]]; then
            echo "Production health check failed --- initiating rollback"
            kubectl patch service app-service \
              -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"version":"blue"}}}' \
              --namespace production
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Promote green to blue
        run: |
          # Tag current green as the new blue for next deployment
          kubectl set image deployment/app-blue \
            app=${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }} \
            --namespace production

  # ── Post-Deploy: Release Notes ─────────────────────────
  release-notes:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: deploy-production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
          prompt: |
            Generate release notes for the deployment of ${{ github.sha }}.

            Read the git log since the last deployment tag.
            Write user-facing release notes:

            ## What's New
            - Feature descriptions

            ## Bug Fixes
            - Fix descriptions

            ## Security
            - Security-related changes

            Keep it concise. Write for users, not developers.

Rollback Strategies¶

The blue-green pattern above includes automatic rollback if the health check fails. But what about semantic rollbacks — when the deployment succeeds but the feature is wrong?

Claude Code can assist with rollback decisions:

yaml
name: Rollback Decision

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      reason:
        description: 'Why are you considering a rollback?'
        required: true

jobs:
  analyze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 20

      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
          prompt: |
            A rollback is being considered. Reason: "${{ inputs.reason }}"

            Analyze the last 5 commits to production:
            1. Which commits are candidates for rollback?
            2. What would be the impact of rolling each back?
            3. Are there database migrations that make rollback dangerous?
            4. Is a feature flag a better option than a full rollback?

            Output:
            ## Rollback Analysis
            | Commit | Description | Rollback Safe? | Risk |

            ## Recommendation
            ROLLBACK / FEATURE FLAG / HOTFIX

Azure DevOps Integration¶

The patterns are the same for Azure DevOps, with different YAML syntax. Azure DevOps uses stages, jobs, and tasks instead of GitHub’s jobs and steps, but the concepts are identical.

Basic Pipeline¶

yaml
# azure-pipelines.yml

trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main

pr:
  branches:
    include:
      - main

pool:
  vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'

variables:
  - group: claude-code-secrets    # Variable group containing ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

stages:
  - stage: Quality
    displayName: 'Quality Gates'
    jobs:
      - job: LintAndTest
        displayName: 'Lint, Test, Type Check'
        steps:
          - task: UsePythonVersion@0
            inputs:
              versionSpec: '3.12'
            displayName: 'Set Python 3.12'

          - script: |
              pip install -e ".[dev]" ruff pyright
            displayName: 'Install dependencies'

          - script: ruff check src/ --output-format github
            displayName: 'Lint'

          - script: pyright src/
            displayName: 'Type check'

          - script: |
              python -m pytest tests/ \
                --cov=src \
                --cov-report=xml \
                --junitxml=test-results.xml \
                -v
            displayName: 'Test'

          - task: PublishTestResults@2
            inputs:
              testResultsFormat: 'JUnit'
              testResultsFiles: 'test-results.xml'
            condition: always()

          - task: PublishCodeCoverageResults@2
            inputs:
              summaryFileLocation: 'coverage.xml'

  - stage: Review
    displayName: 'Claude Code Review'
    condition: eq(variables['Build.Reason'], 'PullRequest')
    dependsOn: Quality
    jobs:
      - job: ClaudeReview
        displayName: 'AI Code Review'
        steps:
          - checkout: self
            fetchDepth: 0

          - script: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
            displayName: 'Install Claude Code'

          - script: |
              DIFF=$(git diff origin/main...HEAD)
              echo "${DIFF}" | claude --print \
                "Review this diff for bugs, security issues, and missing error handling. Format as severity-ranked findings with file:line references." \
                --model claude-sonnet-4-6 \
                --max-turns 5 \
                --output-format text > $(Build.ArtifactStagingDirectory)/review.md
            displayName: 'Run Claude Code Review'
            env:
              ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: $(ANTHROPIC_API_KEY)

          - task: PublishBuildArtifacts@1
            inputs:
              pathToPublish: '$(Build.ArtifactStagingDirectory)/review.md'
              artifactName: 'claude-review'
            displayName: 'Publish review results'

  - stage: Build
    displayName: 'Build'
    dependsOn: Quality
    condition: and(succeeded(), eq(variables['Build.SourceBranch'], 'refs/heads/main'))
    jobs:
      - job: DockerBuild
        displayName: 'Build Docker Image'
        steps:
          - task: Docker@2
            inputs:
              containerRegistry: 'acr-connection'
              repository: 'myapp'
              command: 'buildAndPush'
              Dockerfile: 'Dockerfile'
              tags: |
                $(Build.BuildId)
                latest

  - stage: DeployStaging
    displayName: 'Deploy to Staging'
    dependsOn: Build
    condition: succeeded()
    jobs:
      - deployment: DeployStaging
        displayName: 'Deploy to Staging'
        environment: 'staging'
        strategy:
          runOnce:
            deploy:
              steps:
                - script: |
                    az webapp config container set \
                      --name myapp-staging \
                      --resource-group myapp-rg \
                      --container-image-name myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:$(Build.BuildId)
                  displayName: 'Deploy to Azure Web App'

  - stage: DeployProduction
    displayName: 'Deploy to Production'
    dependsOn: DeployStaging
    condition: succeeded()
    jobs:
      - deployment: DeployProduction
        displayName: 'Deploy to Production'
        environment: 'production'
        strategy:
          runOnce:
            deploy:
              steps:
                - script: |
                    az webapp config container set \
                      --name myapp-production \
                      --resource-group myapp-rg \
                      --container-image-name myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:$(Build.BuildId)
                  displayName: 'Deploy to Azure Web App'

                - script: |
                    # Swap staging slot to production for zero-downtime
                    az webapp deployment slot swap \
                      --name myapp-production \
                      --resource-group myapp-rg \
                      --slot staging \
                      --target-slot production
                  displayName: 'Swap to production slot'

Posting Claude Code Results to Azure DevOps PR Comments¶

Azure DevOps does not have a direct equivalent to GitHub’s actions/github-script. You use the Azure DevOps REST API:

yaml
- script: |
    REVIEW=$(cat $(Build.ArtifactStagingDirectory)/review.md)

    # Escape for JSON
    ESCAPED=$(echo "${REVIEW}" | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.dumps(sys.stdin.read()))")

    # Post comment to PR using Azure DevOps REST API
    curl -s -X POST \
      "$(System.CollectionUri)$(System.TeamProject)/_apis/git/repositories/$(Build.Repository.ID)/pullRequests/$(System.PullRequest.PullRequestId)/threads?api-version=7.1" \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer $(System.AccessToken)" \
      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
      -d "{
        \"comments\": [{
          \"parentCommentId\": 0,
          \"content\": ${ESCAPED},
          \"commentType\": 1
        }],
        \"status\": 1
      }"
  displayName: 'Post review to PR'
  env:
    SYSTEM_ACCESSTOKEN: $(System.AccessToken)

For Azure DevOps, store the API key as a pipeline variable:

  1. Go to Pipelines > Library > Variable Groups
  2. Create a variable group named claude-code-secrets with ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
  3. Mark it as secret
  4. Link the variable group to your pipeline using the variables section

Cost Management in CI¶

Model Selection Strategy¶

This is the single most impactful decision for CI costs. The wrong model on the wrong task wastes money. The right model on the right task delivers value at minimal cost.

Task Model Cost per Run Max Turns Rationale
PR classification Haiku ~$0.01 1 Simple categorization, no reasoning needed
Label assignment Haiku ~$0.01 1 Pattern matching, not analysis
Commit message generation Haiku ~$0.02 1 Mechanical text transformation
Lint fix suggestions Haiku ~$0.02 2 Follows well-known rules
Code quality review Sonnet ~$0.15 5 Needs reasoning about logic and edge cases
Security review Sonnet (high effort) ~$0.30 8 Needs deep analysis, OWASP knowledge
Release notes Sonnet ~$0.10 3 Needs writing quality, commit interpretation
Breaking change detection Sonnet ~$0.12 5 Needs understanding of API contracts
Dependency impact analysis Sonnet ~$0.15 5 Needs cross-reference capability
Architecture review Opus ~$1.00 10 Needs expert-level reasoning
Threat model in CI Opus ~$2.00 15 Needs deep security expertise

Effort Level Optimization¶

Set effort levels based on the task complexity:

yaml
# Simple tasks: low effort
- uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
  with:
    effort: "low"
    model: "claude-haiku-3-5"
    prompt: "Classify this PR as bug-fix, feature, or chore"

# Standard reviews: medium effort
- uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
  with:
    effort: "medium"
    model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    prompt: "Review this PR for bugs and security issues"

# Critical security audits: high effort
- uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
  with:
    effort: "high"
    model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    prompt: "Perform a thorough OWASP security audit on this PR"

Caching Strategies¶

Claude Code in CI does not have built-in result caching, but you can implement it at the pipeline level. The idea: if the diff has not changed since the last review, skip the review.

yaml
name: Cached Review

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Compute diff hash
        id: diff_hash
        run: |
          HASH=$(git diff origin/${{ github.base_ref }}...HEAD | sha256sum | cut -d' ' -f1)
          echo "hash=${HASH}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Check cache
        id: cache
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: /tmp/claude-review-cache
          key: claude-review-${{ steps.diff_hash.outputs.hash }}

      - name: Run review (only if cache miss)
        if: steps.cache.outputs.cache-hit != 'true'
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
          prompt: "Review this PR for bugs and security issues"

      - name: Save to cache
        if: steps.cache.outputs.cache-hit != 'true'
        run: |
          echo "${{ steps.review.outputs.response }}" > /tmp/claude-review-cache/result.txt

This avoids re-reviewing force-pushed branches where the only change is a rebase (same diff, different commits).

Budget Controls¶

Track spending with a monthly budget:

yaml
name: Cost Guard

on:
  workflow_run:
    workflows: ['Claude Code Review', 'Security Review']
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  check-budget:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check monthly spend
        run: |
          # Query your Anthropic usage API or internal tracking
          # If spend exceeds budget, disable non-critical reviews
          MONTHLY_SPEND=$(curl -s "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/usage" \
            -H "x-api-key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}" | jq '.monthly_cost')

          if (( $(echo "$MONTHLY_SPEND > 500" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "::warning::Monthly Claude Code CI spend exceeds $500"
            # Optionally: disable non-critical workflows
          fi

Conditional Execution¶

Skip expensive reviews for trivial changes:

yaml
jobs:
  should-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      skip: ${{ steps.check.outputs.skip }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - id: check
        run: |
          # Skip review for docs-only, test-only, or config-only changes
          CHANGED=$(git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD)
          if echo "$CHANGED" | grep -qvE '\.(md|txt|yml|yaml|json)$'; then
            echo "skip=false" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          else
            echo "skip=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi

  review:
    needs: should-review
    if: needs.should-review.outputs.skip == 'false'
    # ... review job

When NOT to Use Claude Code in CI¶

Not every CI task benefits from an LLM. Claude Code adds latency (30-120 seconds per invocation) and cost. Use traditional tools when they are sufficient:

Task Use Claude Code? Better Alternative
Formatting checks No ruff format --check, prettier --check
Static type checking No pyright, mypy, tsc --noEmit
Dependency vulnerability scan No pip-audit, npm audit, safety check
Test execution No pytest, jest, go test
Docker image build No docker build
Secret scanning (regex) No gitleaks, trufflehog
License compliance No license-checker, pip-licenses
Code coverage threshold No coverage, istanbul
PR classification Yes No good alternative for nuanced classification
Code review for logic bugs Yes Static analysis catches only a subset
Security audit beyond regex Yes LLM reasoning finds logic-level vulnerabilities
Breaking change analysis Yes Requires understanding of API semantics
Release note generation Yes Git log alone produces developer-facing notes, not user-facing

The rule of thumb: if a deterministic tool can do the job, use the deterministic tool. Claude Code is for tasks that require judgment, reasoning, or natural language understanding.

The Complete Production Pipeline¶

Here is a production-grade CI/CD pipeline that integrates Claude Code at every stage. This is the full workflow a professional team would use:

yaml
# .github/workflows/pipeline.yml

name: CI/CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: write
  pull-requests: write
  issues: write

env:
  NODE_VERSION: '20'
  PYTHON_VERSION: '3.12'

jobs:
  # ── Stage 1: Build and Test ─────────────────────────────
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: test
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
          POSTGRES_DB: test
        ports: ['5432:5432']
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ env.NODE_VERSION }}
          cache: pnpm

      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - run: pnpm exec prisma migrate deploy
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://test:test@localhost:5432/test
      - run: pnpm test -- --coverage
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://test:test@localhost:5432/test
          JWT_SECRET: ci-test-secret

      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: coverage
          path: coverage/

  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ env.NODE_VERSION }}
          cache: pnpm

      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - run: pnpm lint
      - run: pnpm exec tsc --noEmit

  # ── Stage 2: Claude Code Review (PRs only) ─────────────
  claude-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    needs: [test, lint]
    timeout-minutes: 10
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
          effort: "medium"
          timeout: 300
          prompt: |
            Review this PR comprehensively:

            ## Code Quality
            - Logic bugs
            - Error handling gaps
            - Missing edge cases
            - Performance concerns

            ## Security
            - OWASP Top 10 violations
            - Auth/authz issues
            - Input validation
            - Secrets in code

            ## Testing
            - Are new code paths tested?
            - Are edge cases covered?

            ## Breaking Changes
            - API contract changes
            - Database schema changes

            Format each finding as:
            **[CRITICAL/IMPORTANT/SUGGESTION]** `file:line` --- description

  # ── Stage 3: Build (main branch only) ──────────────────
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    needs: [test, lint]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build Docker images
        run: |
          docker build -f packages/api/Dockerfile -t app-api:${{ github.sha }} .
          docker build -f packages/web/Dockerfile -t app-web:${{ github.sha }} .

      - name: Push to registry
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.REGISTRY_PASSWORD }}" | \
            docker login ghcr.io -u ${{ github.actor }} --password-stdin
          docker tag app-api:${{ github.sha }} ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}-api:${{ github.sha }}
          docker push ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}-api:${{ github.sha }}

  # ── Stage 4: Deploy ────────────────────────────────────
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    needs: [build]
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Deploy
        run: |
          # Your deployment commands here
          echo "Deploying ${{ github.sha }}"

  # ── Stage 5: Post-Deploy Verification ──────────────────
  verify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    needs: [deploy]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          model: "claude-haiku-3-5"
          prompt: |
            Generate release notes for commit ${{ github.sha }}.
            Read the git log for this commit and any PR associated with it.
            Write user-facing release notes categorized as:
            Added, Changed, Fixed, Security.

Pipeline Flow¶

The pipeline follows this sequence:

PR opened
  |---> Tests run (parallel with lint)
  |---> Lint runs (parallel with tests)
  |---> Claude reviews PR (after tests + lint pass)
  |---> Human reviews (informed by Claude's analysis)
  |---> Merge to main
          |---> Tests run again
          |---> Build Docker images
          |---> Deploy to production
          |---> Claude generates release notes

Claude Code participates at two points: reviewing the PR before merge, and generating release notes after deploy. The human reviewer sees Claude’s analysis alongside the diff, making their review faster and more thorough.

Environment Setup for CI¶

Required Secrets¶

Every CI integration needs the Anthropic API key:

yaml
# In your repository settings -> Secrets and variables -> Actions
# Add: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

For the official GitHub Action, this is the only required secret. For more complex integrations that install Claude Code CLI:

yaml
env:
  ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
  # Optional: override model
  CLAUDE_MODEL: "claude-sonnet-4-6"

API Key Security¶

Your Anthropic API key in CI is a production secret. Treat it accordingly:

  1. Never print it in logs. GitHub Actions masks secrets automatically, but verify by checking workflow logs after the first run.
  2. Use environment-scoped secrets. GitHub allows you to restrict secrets to specific environments (staging, production). Create separate API keys per environment.
  3. Rotate regularly. Create a new API key quarterly and update the secret.
  4. Set spending limits. On the Anthropic console, set a monthly spending cap for the API key used in CI. This is your financial kill switch.
  5. Use a dedicated API key for CI. Do not share your personal development API key with CI. When the CI key hits its budget, your local development is unaffected.

Timeout Configuration¶

CI jobs should have timeouts to prevent runaway costs:

yaml
jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 10    # Kill the entire job after 10 minutes
    steps:
      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          timeout: 300     # 5 minute timeout for Claude specifically

Always set both: timeout-minutes on the job (GitHub-level kill switch) and timeout on the action (Claude Code-level limit). Belt and suspenders.

Monitoring and Observability¶

Tracking Claude Code CI Performance¶

Once Claude Code is integrated into your CI pipeline, you need visibility into how it is performing. Not just cost, but quality and latency.

Create a lightweight tracking workflow that logs every Claude Code CI invocation:

yaml
name: Claude CI Metrics

on:
  workflow_run:
    workflows: ['PR Review Pipeline', 'Security Review', 'Claude Code Review']
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  log-metrics:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Record metrics
        run: |
          # Log to your observability platform (Datadog, Grafana, etc.)
          curl -s -X POST "${{ secrets.METRICS_ENDPOINT }}" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d "{
              \"workflow\": \"${{ github.event.workflow_run.name }}\",
              \"status\": \"${{ github.event.workflow_run.conclusion }}\",
              \"duration_ms\": $(( $(date -d "${{ github.event.workflow_run.updated_at }}" +%s) - $(date -d "${{ github.event.workflow_run.created_at }}" +%s) )),
              \"pr_number\": \"${{ github.event.workflow_run.pull_requests[0].number }}\",
              \"repository\": \"${{ github.repository }}\",
              \"timestamp\": \"$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)\"
            }"

Track these metrics over time:

Metric What It Tells You Target
Review latency (p50, p95) How long developers wait p50 < 60s, p95 < 180s
Findings per PR Review thoroughness 1-5 for active projects
False positive rate Review quality < 20% (measure via developer feedback)
Cost per PR (by model) Budget adherence < $0.50 for standard PRs
Timeout rate Prompt quality < 5% of invocations

Developer Feedback Loop¶

The most important metric is whether developers find Claude Code CI reviews useful. Add a simple thumbs-up/thumbs-down reaction mechanism:

yaml
- name: Post review with feedback prompt
  uses: actions/github-script@v7
  with:
    script: |
      const response = '${{ steps.review.outputs.response }}';
      const comment = await github.rest.issues.createComment({
        owner: context.repo.owner,
        repo: context.repo.repo,
        issue_number: context.issue.number,
        body: `## Claude Code Review\n\n${response}\n\n---\n*React with 👍 if this review was helpful, 👎 if it was not.*`,
      });

Periodically query the reaction data to measure signal-to-noise ratio. If more than 30% of reviews get thumbs-down, your prompts need tuning. If more than 80% get thumbs-up, consider expanding Claude Code’s CI role.

Troubleshooting CI Integration¶

Common Failure Modes¶

Claude Code returns empty or truncated output. This usually means the timeout was too short or max_tokens was too low. Increase timeout to 600 seconds and max_tokens to 8192 for complex reviews. Check the workflow logs for timeout errors.

The GitHub Action fails with authentication errors. Verify the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY secret is set correctly. Go to Settings > Secrets > Actions, edit the secret, and paste the key again. Common mistake: trailing whitespace in the secret value.

Claude Code reviews are not appearing as PR comments. Check the workflow’s permissions block. You need pull-requests: write for the action to post comments. Also verify that the workflow runs on pull_request events, not push events — push events do not have an associated PR to comment on.

Reviews are inconsistent between runs. LLMs are non-deterministic. The same diff can produce different findings on different runs. This is expected behavior, not a bug. If you need consistency, pin the model version and use effort: "high" which tends to produce more stable output due to deeper reasoning.

CI costs spiking unexpectedly. Check for: (1) missing timeout-minutes on jobs, (2) missing conditional execution for docs-only PRs, (3) a workflow running on push AND pull_request (double review), (4) Dependabot/Renovate creating many dependency PRs that all trigger reviews.

Azure DevOps pipeline cannot find Claude Code. The CLI is installed per-step, not globally. Each step in Azure DevOps runs in a fresh shell context. Either install Claude Code in a setup step that other steps depend on, or install it in each step that needs it.

What Most People Get Wrong¶

Running Opus in CI for every PR. Opus is powerful but expensive. A code review with Opus costs roughly 5-10x what Sonnet costs. Reserve Opus for security audits on high-risk PRs. Use Sonnet for standard reviews and Haiku for classification and simple tasks. A team that switches from Opus-everywhere to tiered model selection typically reduces CI LLM costs by 70-80%.

No timeout on Claude Code CI steps. Without a timeout, a runaway prompt can consume your entire API budget. Always set timeout on the action and timeout-minutes on the job. A single stuck review with Opus can cost $10+ if it loops for 15 minutes.

Reviewing every file on every PR. If only documentation files changed, you do not need a security review. Use path filters and conditional execution to skip expensive reviews for trivial changes. The should-review pattern shown earlier saves hundreds of dollars per month on active repositories.

Ignoring CI costs until the bill arrives. Track your Claude Code CI spend weekly. A team of 10 engineers doing 5 PRs per day with Sonnet reviews costs roughly $150/week. That is reasonable. But add Opus security reviews on every PR and it jumps to $1,000/week. Choose your battles.

Using Claude Code for tasks deterministic tools handle better. Formatting? Use ruff or prettier. Type checking? Use pyright or tsc. Dependency vulnerabilities? Use pip-audit or npm audit. These tools are faster, cheaper, and more reliable for their specific tasks. Claude Code is for the tasks that require judgment — logic bug detection, security reasoning, breaking change analysis, and natural language generation.

Not setting max-turns. In interactive mode, you watch Claude work and can interrupt it. In CI, nobody is watching. A 50-turn agentic loop that reads every file in your repository, runs tests, reads results, and iterates is technically working as designed — but it is also costing $5-15 per PR. Set --max-turns to the minimum needed for each task.

Treating Claude Code CI output as authoritative. Claude Code finds real bugs. It also produces false positives. Never gate deployments solely on Claude Code output. Use it as an additional signal alongside tests, linting, and type checking. A human reviewer should have final say on critical findings. The best practice: Claude Code posts findings as a PR comment, the human reviewer triages them alongside the rest of the review.

Running the same prompt for every language and framework. A Python project needs different review instructions than a TypeScript project. A database migration needs different scrutiny than a UI component. Customize your prompts per file path and change type. The path-filtered workflow pattern (triggering different reviews for src/auth/** vs src/ui/** vs prisma/**) costs the same to maintain and produces far better results.

Chapter Summary¶

  1. Claude Code runs natively in CI/CD pipelines. The official GitHub Action (anthropics/claude-code-action) handles PR reviews, classification, release notes, and arbitrary prompts. For Azure DevOps and other CI systems, install the CLI and use --print mode with --output-format json. Always set --max-turns and timeouts.

  2. Match the model to the task, ruthlessly. Haiku for classification (~$0.01/run), Sonnet for code reviews (~$0.15/run), Opus only for critical security audits (~$1.00/run). A tiered pipeline that classifies first, reviews second, and security-audits only high-risk PRs keeps costs at $8-25/week for active teams.

  3. Layer your pipeline: deterministic tools first, Claude Code second. Run linting, type checking, tests, and static security scanning before Claude Code touches the PR. This means Claude never reviews code that fails basic checks, saving money and producing better results because it focuses on the issues that deterministic tools cannot catch.

  4. Pre-commit hooks and git hooks are your local defense. Secret scanning, commit message validation, and quick Claude Code security scans catch issues before they reach CI. Wire them in .claude/settings.json for Claude Code hooks or .githooks/ for standard git hooks.

  5. Cost management is not optional — it is operational. Set budget alerts, use conditional execution to skip reviews for trivial changes, cache results for unchanged diffs, use dedicated CI API keys with spending limits, and review your weekly spend. The difference between a well-managed Claude Code CI setup ($100/month) and an unmanaged one ($2,000/month) is entirely in the configuration, not the technology.

Part VI: Advanced Mastery

Chapter 21: Custom Agents and Intent Routing¶ ~19 min read

You open Claude Code and type three words: “audit the auth.” Within seconds, an Opus-powered security reviewer agent is dissecting your authentication module, checking for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities, tracing injection paths, and generating a severity-ranked findings table with CWE references. You didn’t invoke a slash command. You didn’t specify a model. You didn’t even say “security.” Claude read your intent, matched it to the right specialist, and delegated.

This is what custom agents and intent routing unlock: a Claude Code session that behaves like a team of specialists, each with the right model, the right tools, and the right expertise for the task at hand. In this chapter, you’ll build that team from scratch.

What Agents Are¶

An agent in Claude Code is a specialized sub-instance of Claude with its own model, tool access, system prompt, and behavioral constraints. Think of agents as roles on a development team. Your main Claude Code session is the tech lead — it understands the full picture and delegates to specialists when the task demands focused expertise.

Agents differ from the main session in three critical ways:

  1. Isolated context — each agent runs in a forked context, so its work doesn’t pollute your main conversation
  2. Constrained tools — you control exactly which tools the agent can use
  3. Model override — a security audit can use Opus for deep reasoning while a documentation task uses Sonnet for speed

Agents live as markdown files in .claude/agents/. When Claude encounters a task matching an agent’s description, it can spawn that agent automatically.

The Agent Definition Format¶

Every agent is a single markdown file in .claude/agents/ with YAML frontmatter followed by a system prompt. Here is the anatomy:

markdown
---
name: security-reviewer
description: Reviews code for security vulnerabilities. Use when user mentions "security", "vulnerability", "audit", "OWASP", "CVE", "injection".
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: opus
effort: high
memory: project
---

You are a senior security engineer specializing in application security.

When reviewing code:
1. Check OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities
2. Look for injection points (SQL, command, XSS)
3. Verify authentication and authorization logic
4. Check for secrets, hardcoded credentials
5. Assess data exposure risks

Output format:
| Severity | CWE | Finding | Location | Fix |
|----------|-----|---------|----------|-----|

The frontmatter is structured configuration. The body after the --- delimiter is the system prompt — free-form instructions that shape how the agent thinks and responds.

Frontmatter Fields — Complete Reference¶

Every field controls a specific aspect of agent behavior:

name¶

The identifier Claude uses to reference this agent. Keep it lowercase with hyphens. This is how you explicitly invoke it (“use the security-reviewer agent”) and how Claude references it in delegation decisions.

yaml
name: security-reviewer

description¶

This is the single most important field. Claude reads every agent’s description to decide whether to delegate a task. If your description doesn’t contain the right trigger words, the agent never gets used.

yaml
# Good: specific trigger words that Claude can match
description: Reviews code for security vulnerabilities. Use when user mentions "security", "vulnerability", "audit", "OWASP", "CVE", "injection", "XSS", "auth bypass", "secrets in code".

# Bad: vague, no trigger words
description: Helps with code issues.

Write descriptions as if you’re telling a dispatcher: “Send this specialist when someone says X, Y, or Z.”

tools¶

Restricts which tools the agent can access. This is a security boundary — a documentation agent has no business running Bash commands.

yaml
# Full access for investigation
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash

# Read-only for analysis
tools: Read, Grep, Glob

# With web access for research
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch

Available tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch, NotebookEdit, Agent. Omit tools the agent shouldn’t have.

model¶

Override the model for this specific agent. This is where cost optimization meets quality requirements.

yaml
model: opus    # Deep analysis, complex reasoning, security audits
model: sonnet  # Balanced — code review, documentation, research
model: haiku   # Fast, cheap — simple lookups, formatting, quick checks

Use Opus for tasks where mistakes are costly (security, debugging production issues). Use Sonnet for everyday work. Use Haiku for mechanical tasks like formatting or simple lookups.

effort¶

Controls reasoning depth. Higher effort means more internal deliberation before responding.

yaml
effort: low    # Quick, surface-level
effort: medium # Standard analysis
effort: high   # Deep reasoning, considers edge cases
effort: max    # Maximum deliberation (most tokens consumed)

maxTurns¶

Limits how many agentic loop iterations the agent can take. Without this, an agent investigating a complex bug might run for dozens of turns, consuming significant tokens.

yaml
maxTurns: 10   # Stop after 10 tool-use cycles
maxTurns: 5    # Quick investigation, report back
maxTurns: 25   # Extended deep dive

memory¶

Controls where the agent persists insights across sessions.

yaml
memory: user     # Global memory — shared across all projects
memory: project  # Per-project memory — scoped to current project
memory: local    # Gitignored memory — private to this machine

A security reviewer with memory: project remembers past audit findings for this project. A personal productivity agent with memory: local keeps notes that never leave your machine.

isolation¶

Determines whether the agent operates in the same git state as your main session or in an isolated worktree.

yaml
isolation: worktree  # Agent gets its own git worktree (separate branch)

Worktree isolation is essential for agents that modify code — their changes don’t affect your working directory until explicitly merged. This is how /batch achieves parallel file modifications safely.

Five Complete Agent Definitions¶

Here are five production-ready agents that form a professional development team.

1. Security Reviewer¶

markdown
---
name: security-reviewer
description: Reviews code for security vulnerabilities, injection flaws, authentication bugs, and secret exposure. Use when user mentions "security", "vulnerability", "audit", "OWASP", "CVE", "CWE", "injection", "XSS", "CSRF", "auth bypass", "secrets", "pentest", or "hardening".
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: opus
effort: high
memory: project
---

You are a senior application security engineer with deep expertise in OWASP Top 10, CWE classifications, and secure coding practices.

## Review Protocol

For every file or module you review:

1. **Input Validation** — Trace all external input (HTTP params, headers, body, file uploads, environment variables). Verify sanitization at every trust boundary.
2. **Injection Analysis** — Check for SQL injection, command injection, XSS (reflected, stored, DOM-based), template injection, LDAP injection, path traversal.
3. **Authentication & Authorization** — Verify session management, token handling, password storage (bcrypt/argon2, not MD5/SHA), privilege escalation paths, IDOR vulnerabilities.
4. **Secrets & Configuration** — Scan for hardcoded credentials, API keys, connection strings, private keys. Check that secrets come from environment variables or secret managers.
5. **Data Exposure** — Identify PII leakage in logs, error messages, API responses. Check that sensitive fields are excluded from serialization.
6. **Cryptography** — Verify use of strong algorithms (AES-256, RSA-2048+), proper IV/nonce handling, no ECB mode, no custom crypto.
7. **Dependencies** — Flag known vulnerable packages if visible in imports or config files.

## Output Format

Present findings as a severity-ranked table:

| Severity | CWE | Finding | Location | Fix |
|----------|-----|---------|----------|-----|

Severity levels: CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, INFO.

For CRITICAL and HIGH findings, provide complete fix code — not just a description. For MEDIUM and below, a one-line fix description suffices.

End with a summary: total findings by severity, overall risk assessment, and the top 3 actions to take immediately.

2. Code Reviewer¶

markdown
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews code for bugs, performance issues, readability problems, and best practice violations. Use when user says "review", "check this code", "code review", "find bugs", "look at this", or "is this good".
tools: Read, Grep, Glob
model: sonnet
effort: high
memory: project
---

You are a senior software engineer conducting thorough code reviews. Your priorities, in order:

1. **Correctness** — Logic errors, off-by-one, null/undefined handling, race conditions, resource leaks
2. **Security** — Flag obvious issues but defer deep security analysis to the security-reviewer agent
3. **Performance** — N+1 queries, unnecessary allocations, missing indexes, inefficient algorithms
4. **Readability** — Naming, function length, nesting depth, dead code, inconsistent patterns
5. **Maintainability** — Test coverage gaps, missing error handling, tight coupling

## Review Protocol

1. Read the entire file or changeset before commenting
2. Understand the purpose and context of the code
3. Check the surrounding code for patterns to follow
4. Identify issues in priority order (correctness first, style last)

## Output Format

**[CRITICAL]** `file.py:42` — Description of the bug and how to fix it

**[IMPORTANT]** `file.py:87` — Description of the performance issue

**[SUGGESTION]** `file.py:103` — Description of the readability improvement

Skip nitpicks. Skip formatting issues that a linter handles. Focus on what breaks in production or causes maintenance pain.

End with: "N issues found: X critical, Y important, Z suggestions."

3. Debugger¶

markdown
---
name: debugger
description: Investigates bugs, errors, and unexpected behavior through systematic root cause analysis. Use when user mentions "bug", "error", "broken", "not working", "fails", "crash", "traceback", "stack trace", "exception", or "unexpected behavior".
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: opus
effort: high
maxTurns: 20
memory: project
---

You are a senior debugging specialist. Your method is systematic root cause analysis — not guessing.

## Investigation Protocol

1. **Reproduce** — Understand the exact symptoms. What was expected vs. what happened?
2. **Hypothesize** — Form 2-3 hypotheses about root cause, ranked by likelihood.
3. **Investigate** — For each hypothesis, search the codebase for evidence. Read relevant files. Check git blame for recent changes. Run targeted tests if available.
4. **Narrow** — Eliminate hypotheses with evidence until one remains.
5. **Root Cause** — Identify the exact file, line, and condition that causes the bug.
6. **Fix** — Write a minimal fix that addresses the root cause without side effects.
7. **Verify** — Run tests to confirm the fix works and nothing else broke.

## Output Format

### Root Cause
`file.py:42` — Exact description of what goes wrong and why.

### Evidence
- [What you found that confirms this]
- [What you ruled out and why]

### Fix
```diff
- old code
+ new code

Prevention¶

How to prevent this class of bug in the future (linting rule, type annotation, test pattern).

Never guess. If you can’t determine root cause with available evidence, say what you’ve ruled out and what additional information you need.


### 4. Researcher

```markdown
---
name: researcher
description: Performs deep technical research using web search and documentation. Use when user says "research", "what is", "latest", "compare", "should I use", "investigate", "look into", "find out about", or "what are the options for".
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch
model: sonnet
effort: high
maxTurns: 15
memory: user
---

You are a senior technical researcher. Your job is to find accurate, current information and synthesize it into actionable intelligence.

## Research Protocol

1. **Define the question** — What specifically does the user need to know?
2. **Search broadly** — Use WebSearch for current information. Use WebFetch for specific documentation pages. Cross-reference multiple sources.
3. **Verify accuracy** — Check dates, version numbers, and claims against official documentation. Never present a single source's claim as fact.
4. **Synthesize** — Combine findings into a clear, actionable answer.

## Output Format

### Summary
2-3 sentences answering the core question.

### Key Findings
- Specific, sourced findings as bullet points
- Include version numbers, dates, metrics where relevant

### Comparison (if comparing options)
| Criteria | Option A | Option B | Option C |
|----------|----------|----------|----------|

### Recommendation
What to use and why, with trade-offs noted.

### Sources
- [Source Title](URL) — what this source contributed

Never show failed searches, 404 errors, or "I couldn't find" messages. Only present the information you did find. If a search yields nothing useful, try different search terms before reporting back.

5. Doc Writer¶

markdown
---
name: doc-writer
description: Writes technical documentation including READMEs, API docs, ADRs, runbooks, and inline documentation. Use when user says "document", "write docs", "README", "ADR", "runbook", "API docs", "update documentation", or "document this".
tools: Read, Grep, Glob
model: sonnet
effort: medium
memory: project
---

You are a senior technical writer who understands that good documentation is code — it needs the same care, consistency, and maintenance.

## Documentation Protocol

1. **Read the code** — Understand what you're documenting by reading the actual implementation, not just function names.
2. **Read existing docs** — Match the existing style, format, and conventions. If the project uses JSDoc, write JSDoc. If ADRs use a specific template, follow it.
3. **Write for the reader** — The reader is a senior engineer who is new to this codebase. They understand programming concepts but not your project's specific decisions.
4. **Be accurate** — Every code reference, file path, and command must be verified against the actual codebase.

## Document Types

### README
- What it does (one paragraph)
- Quick start (< 5 steps to running)
- Architecture overview (if complex)
- Configuration reference
- Development setup

### ADR (Architecture Decision Record)
- Title: ADR-NNNN: Decision Title
- Status: Proposed / Accepted / Deprecated
- Context: What problem are we solving?
- Decision: What we chose and why
- Consequences: Trade-offs accepted

### Runbook
- When to use this runbook
- Prerequisites
- Step-by-step procedure (numbered, exact commands)
- Verification (how to confirm each step worked)
- Rollback procedure
- Escalation path

### API Documentation
- Endpoint: method + path
- Purpose: one sentence
- Request: headers, params, body (with example)
- Response: status codes, body (with example)
- Error responses: all possible errors

Never generate placeholder text. Every section either has real content or doesn't exist.

How Agents Get Delegated To¶

There are three paths from your prompt to agent execution.

Automatic Delegation¶

This is the default and most common path. Claude reads every agent’s description field, compares it against your prompt, and delegates when the match is strong.

You: "Check if the payment module has any SQL injection vulnerabilities"

Claude's internal reasoning:
- Keywords: "SQL injection", "vulnerabilities"
- Matches: security-reviewer description
- Action: delegate to security-reviewer agent

→ Security reviewer agent spawns with Opus model
→ Reads payment module files
→ Returns severity-ranked findings
→ Main session presents results to you

The quality of automatic delegation depends entirely on the description field. If you find an agent isn’t being used when it should be, add more trigger words to its description. If it’s being used when it shouldn’t be, make the description more specific.

Explicit Delegation¶

You can directly invoke an agent by name:

You: "Use the debugger agent to investigate why the user sync job fails silently"

Claude spawns the debugger agent regardless of keyword matching. This is useful when you know which specialist you want and don’t want to rely on automatic matching.

Delegation via Skills¶

Skills can specify an agent to handle their execution:

yaml
---
name: security-audit
description: Comprehensive security audit of the codebase
context: fork
agent: security-reviewer
---

When you run /security-audit, Claude forks context and delegates to the security-reviewer agent. This combines the invocability of skills (slash commands) with the specialization of agents (model override, tool restrictions, custom prompt).

Agent vs. Skill — Decision Matrix¶

Agents and skills serve different purposes. Here’s when to use which:

Criteria Agent Skill
Fixed, repeatable steps No — agents exercise judgment Yes — skills follow a script
Deep reasoning required Yes — use Opus agents Less common
Specific model needed Yes — each agent has its own model Possible via frontmatter
User-invocable with / Not directly Yes — /skill-name
Long investigation Yes — agents explore freely No — skills execute steps
Mechanical consistency No — agents may vary Yes — same steps every time
Context isolation Built-in (forked context) Optional (context: fork)
Cost control maxTurns limits Predictable step count

Rule of thumb: If the task has clear steps that don’t change (generate changelog, run deploy check, scaffold project), use a skill. If the task requires exploration, judgment, and domain expertise (security audit, debugging, code review), use an agent.

Intent Routing — Auto-Detecting What You Want¶

Intent routing is the system that turns “audit the auth” into a security review without you specifying the workflow. It’s the mechanism behind Claude Code’s auto-execute behavior.

The Routing Table¶

Intent routing works through a mapping of trigger keywords to workflows. You define this mapping in your CLAUDE.md or in a dedicated file that CLAUDE.md imports:

markdown
## Auto-Execute Protocol

| User says (examples) | Route to |
|---|---|
| mentions bug, error, stack trace | **Debug** → hypothesize, investigate, fix |
| mentions review, check, "look at" | **Code review** → severity-ranked findings |
| mentions security, vulnerability, OWASP | **Security audit** → CWE table, fix code |
| mentions research, "what is", compare | **Research** → multi-source synthesis |
| mentions refactor, clean up, simplify | **Refactor** → preserve behavior, small steps |
| mentions deploy, ship, release | **Deploy check** → full readiness validation |
| mentions performance, slow, optimize | **Perf profile** → bottleneck analysis |

Building Your Own Routing Table¶

Start with the workflows your team uses daily, then map natural language to each one:

markdown
| Trigger Words | Route To | Agent | Model |
|---------------|----------|-------|-------|
| bug, error, broken, crash, traceback | Debug workflow | debugger | opus |
| review, check, "how's this" | Code review | code-reviewer | sonnet |
| security, vuln, OWASP, inject, XSS | Security audit | security-reviewer | opus |
| research, "what is", compare, latest | Research | researcher | sonnet |
| docs, README, document, ADR | Documentation | doc-writer | sonnet |
| slow, perf, optimize, bottleneck | Performance | — (inline) | — |
| deploy, ship, release, "ready?" | Deploy check | — (inline) | — |
| refactor, clean, simplify | Refactoring | — (inline) | — |

Not every workflow needs an agent. Simple workflows like deploy checks can run inline in the main session. Reserve agents for tasks that benefit from model overrides, tool restrictions, or domain-specific prompts.

The Auto-Execute Protocol¶

The routing table is just data. The auto-execute protocol is the instruction that tells Claude to use it. Place this at the top of your CLAUDE.md:

markdown
## Auto-Execute Protocol

**Step 1 — Decode Intent** (silent)
Parse the user's prompt. Identify: WHAT they want, WHAT target, HOW deep.

**Step 2 — Route to Workflow** (silent)
Match intent to the routing table. Select the matching workflow.

**Step 3 — Expand & Execute** (visible to user)
Execute the full workflow. Deliver the final result. Never ask
"would you like me to..." — just do it.

Three rules make this work:

  1. Silent routing — The user never sees the decode/route steps. They see only the result.
  2. Full execution — Once routed, Claude executes the complete workflow, not just the first step.
  3. One question maximum — If Claude truly can’t proceed (ambiguous target), it asks one focused question, then executes on the answer.

This protocol eliminates the most common complaint about AI assistants: the endless back-and-forth of “would you like me to do X?” Just do X.

Building Domain-Specific Agents¶

The five standard agents cover common development tasks. But the real power is building agents specific to your domain.

Database Agent¶

markdown
---
name: database-expert
description: Expert in our PostgreSQL database schema, query optimization, and migrations. Use when user mentions "database", "query", "migration", "schema", "SQL", "index", "slow query", or "table".
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: sonnet
effort: high
memory: project
---

You are the database expert for this project. You know:

- Our schema is defined in `db/migrations/` (Alembic migrations)
- The current schema is documented in `db/schema.sql`
- We use PostgreSQL 16 with TimescaleDB extension
- Connection pooling via PgBouncer (max 100 connections)
- All queries should use parameterized statements (never string concat)

When writing queries:
- Always check for missing indexes on WHERE/JOIN columns
- Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify query plans
- Prefer CTEs over subqueries for readability
- All migrations must be reversible (include downgrade)

When reviewing queries:
- Flag N+1 patterns
- Check for missing WHERE clauses on UPDATE/DELETE
- Verify that transactions are scoped minimally
- Check for lock contention risks on high-traffic tables

Testing Agent¶

markdown
---
name: test-specialist
description: Expert in testing strategy, pytest, test fixtures, and test-driven development. Use when user mentions "test", "testing", "TDD", "coverage", "fixture", "mock", "pytest", or "test failure".
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: sonnet
effort: medium
maxTurns: 15
memory: project
---

You are a testing specialist for this project.

Our testing stack:
- Framework: pytest with pytest-asyncio
- Fixtures: defined in conftest.py files at each directory level
- Coverage: pytest-cov, minimum 80% on critical paths
- Integration tests: hit real services, tagged with @pytest.mark.integration
- Test database: SQLite for unit tests, PostgreSQL for integration

Test naming convention: test_<what>_<condition>_<expected>
Example: test_login_invalid_password_returns_401

When writing tests:
1. Start with the test name (it documents the behavior)
2. Arrange: set up minimal state using fixtures
3. Act: call the function/endpoint under test
4. Assert: verify exactly one behavior per test

When investigating test failures:
1. Read the test to understand expected behavior
2. Read the implementation to find the mismatch
3. Determine if the test or the implementation is wrong
4. Fix the correct one

Deploy Agent¶

markdown
---
name: deploy-checker
description: Validates deployment readiness by checking git state, secrets, dependencies, tests, and build. Use when user mentions "deploy", "ship", "release", "production", "staging", or "ready to deploy".
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: haiku
effort: medium
maxTurns: 10
memory: project
---

You are the deployment gatekeeper. Run these checks in order:

1. **Git Status** — Working tree must be clean. No uncommitted changes.
2. **Branch** — Must be on main or a release branch.
3. **Secret Scan** — Grep staged files for API keys, tokens, passwords.
4. **Dependencies** — Run pip-audit (Python) or npm audit (Node). Zero critical vulns.
5. **Tests** — Run the full test suite. All must pass.
6. **Build** — Run the build command. Must succeed without warnings.
7. **Environment** — Verify required env vars are documented.

Output:
| Check | Status | Details |
|-------|--------|---------|

Final verdict: **DEPLOY READY: YES** or **DEPLOY READY: NO — [blocking issues]**

If any check fails, stop and report. Don't continue checking — the first blocker is what needs fixing.

Multi-Agent Orchestration¶

Single agents are useful. Multiple agents working together are transformative. Here are the orchestration patterns.

Parallel Review¶

Run multiple specialists simultaneously on the same code:

You: "Review the auth module for security AND code quality"

Claude spawns both the security-reviewer (Opus) and code-reviewer (Sonnet) agents in parallel. Each examines the auth module through its lens. Results return independently, and Claude synthesizes them into a unified review.

This is faster than sequential review (both agents work concurrently) and produces deeper analysis (each agent focuses on its specialty without distraction).

Pipeline Orchestration¶

Agents feed their output into the next stage:

You: "Research the best approach to rate limiting, then implement it"

Stage 1: researcher agent investigates rate limiting approaches, compares token bucket vs. sliding window vs. fixed window, and recommends an approach.

Stage 2: Main session (or an implementation agent) takes the recommendation and implements it using the chosen approach.

The key is that stage 2 benefits from stage 1’s research without the user having to relay information between steps.

Debate Pattern¶

Two agents review the same code and their findings are compared:

You: "Have the security-reviewer and code-reviewer both examine the payment processing module, then synthesize their findings"

Both agents independently analyze the code. Where they agree, confidence is high. Where they disagree, Claude highlights the tension and presents both perspectives. This surfaces blind spots that a single reviewer might miss.

Agent Communication Patterns¶

How agents interact with the main session and each other:

One-Shot Delegation¶

The simplest pattern. Claude spawns an agent, waits for the result, and presents it.

Main session → spawns agent → agent investigates → agent returns findings → main session presents to user

This is the default for most tasks. The agent runs to completion (or until maxTurns), and its findings become part of the main conversation.

Background Agents¶

For long-running investigations, agents can run in the background while you continue working:

You: "In the background, have the security agent audit all files in src/api/"
You: [continue working on something else]
[Later: agent finishes, results appear]

The run_in_background capability lets agents work asynchronously. This is ideal for comprehensive audits, deep research, or large-scale code analysis that would otherwise block your workflow.

Result Aggregation¶

When multiple agents return results, the main session aggregates them:

Main session:
├── Spawns security-reviewer → returns 3 critical, 5 medium findings
├── Spawns code-reviewer → returns 1 critical, 8 suggestions
└── Aggregates: deduplicates, ranks by severity, presents unified report

The main session handles synthesis — merging overlapping findings, resolving conflicting recommendations, and presenting a coherent summary.

Cost Management for Agents¶

Each agent is a separate API session with its own token consumption. Without cost awareness, agents can become expensive.

Model Selection Per Agent¶

This is the single biggest cost lever. Assign models based on task criticality:

Task Model Why
Security audit Opus Mistakes here cost millions
Debugging production Opus Complex reasoning required
Code review Sonnet Good balance of quality/cost
Research Sonnet Web synthesis doesn’t need Opus
Documentation Sonnet Adequate for prose generation
Deploy checks Haiku Mechanical checks, no judgment
Formatting Haiku Trivial transformations

Turn Limits¶

Set maxTurns to prevent runaway investigations:

yaml
# Quick check — 5 turns is enough
maxTurns: 5

# Standard investigation — 10-15 turns
maxTurns: 15

# Deep dive — 20-25 turns max
maxTurns: 25

Without maxTurns, an Opus agent investigating a complex bug could run for 40+ turns, consuming significant tokens. Set limits based on the task’s expected complexity.

Monitoring¶

Use /cost during your session to check cumulative spend across all agents. If you notice an agent consuming disproportionate tokens, consider:

  • Reducing its maxTurns
  • Switching to a cheaper model
  • Narrowing its scope via the system prompt
  • Using a skill with fixed steps instead

Cost Estimation Rules of Thumb¶

  • Opus agent, 10 turns, reading ~5 files: approximately $0.50-1.00
  • Sonnet agent, 10 turns, reading ~5 files: approximately $0.10-0.25
  • Haiku agent, 5 turns, reading ~3 files: approximately $0.01-0.05
  • Parallel agents multiply these costs

Practical Tips for Effective Agents¶

Writing Good Descriptions¶

The description field is your agent’s resume. Claude reads it to decide delegation. Include:

  • What the agent does — one sentence summary
  • Trigger words — exact words the user might say
  • Negative triggers — “Do NOT use for general code review” to prevent false matches
yaml
# Effective description
description: >
  Investigates database performance issues including slow queries,
  missing indexes, N+1 patterns, and connection pool exhaustion.
  Use when user mentions "slow query", "database performance",
  "N+1", "query optimization", "missing index", "connection pool",
  or "database bottleneck". Do NOT use for schema design or migrations.

Keeping Agent Prompts Focused¶

An agent that tries to do everything does nothing well. Each agent should have:

  • A single area of expertise
  • A clear protocol (numbered steps)
  • A specific output format
  • Explicit boundaries on what it does NOT do

Testing Agents¶

Before relying on an agent in production work, test it:

  1. Ask Claude: “When would you use the security-reviewer agent?” — it will quote the description
  2. Give a prompt that should trigger the agent — verify it delegates
  3. Give a prompt that should NOT trigger the agent — verify it doesn’t
  4. Check the agent’s output quality on a known file

Evolving Agents Over Time¶

Agents improve with iteration. After using an agent:

  • Add trigger words it missed to the description
  • Remove trigger words that caused false matches
  • Refine the protocol based on what the agent got wrong
  • Adjust maxTurns based on actual usage patterns
  • Consider switching models if quality is too low or cost too high

Chapter Summary¶

  • Agents are specialized Claude instances with their own model, tools, and system prompt, defined as markdown files in .claude/agents/
  • The description field is the most important part of an agent definition — it determines when Claude delegates to the agent
  • Five core agents (security reviewer, code reviewer, debugger, researcher, doc writer) cover most development needs
  • Intent routing maps trigger words to workflows, enabling Claude to auto-detect what you want from short prompts
  • Domain-specific agents encode your project’s unique knowledge (database schema, test frameworks, deployment procedures)
  • Multi-agent orchestration patterns (parallel, pipeline, debate) combine specialists for deeper analysis
  • Cost management through model selection, turn limits, and monitoring keeps agents economical
  • The auto-execute protocol (decode, route, execute) eliminates back-and-forth by having Claude silently route to the right workflow and execute it fully

Chapter 22: Prompt Patterns That 10x Your Output¶ ~17 min read

The difference between a junior and senior Claude Code user isn’t the features they know — it’s the prompts they write. A junior types “make a login page.” A senior types “Read src/auth/login.ts and src/components/LoginForm.tsx. Then add rate limiting to the login endpoint: max 5 attempts per IP per 15 minutes, using the existing Redis connection in src/lib/redis.ts. Write a test first.”

Both get results. One gets the right result on the first try.

This chapter catalogs 20 prompt patterns that consistently produce better output from Claude Code. Each pattern includes when to use it, a complete example, what Claude does in response, a pro tip, and the anti-pattern to avoid. These aren’t theoretical — they’re distilled from hundreds of hours of professional Claude Code usage.

Pattern 1: The Constraint Pattern¶

When to use: You want a focused solution without unnecessary complexity. Claude’s default tendency is to reach for external libraries, add comprehensive error handling, and build for extensibility. Constraints channel that energy.

Example prompt:

Using only the Python standard library, implement a URL shortener 
that stores mappings in a JSON file. No classes — only functions. 
Keep it under 80 lines.

What Claude does: Strips away the instinct to import Flask, set up SQLAlchemy, and build a REST API. Instead, it produces a minimal, elegant solution using http.server, json, and hashlib.

Pro tip: Stack constraints for precision. “No new dependencies, no classes, under 100 lines, Python 3.11+” gives Claude four guardrails that collectively force a specific kind of solution.

Anti-pattern: Giving no constraints and then complaining about over-engineering. If you don’t want a class hierarchy, say so upfront.

Variations: - “Without adding any new dependencies…” - “In a single file, under 200 lines…” - “Using only functions, no OOP…” - “Compatible with Python 3.9+ standard library only…”

Pattern 2: The Architecture-First Pattern¶

When to use: Before any complex feature or system. Forces Claude to think about structure before writing code, preventing the common failure mode of building a perfectly-coded component that doesn’t fit the architecture.

Example prompt:

Before writing any code, show me:
1. Component diagram (which modules/files we need)
2. Data flow (how a request moves through the system)
3. File structure (what goes where)
4. Interface contracts (function signatures between components)

Then I'll approve and you can implement.

The feature: real-time notification system using WebSockets, 
supporting email and in-app notification channels.

What Claude does: Produces a Mermaid diagram, maps the data flow from event trigger through notification dispatch, lays out the file structure, and defines the interfaces between components. Implementation only begins after you approve the design.

Pro tip: Combine with /plan mode. Enter plan mode, use this pattern, review the plan, then let Claude execute. The plan persists as a reference throughout implementation.

Anti-pattern: “Build me a notification system” — Claude dives into code immediately, makes architectural choices you’d disagree with, and you spend more time undoing than building.

Pattern 3: The Test-First Pattern¶

When to use: Any new feature or bug fix. Tests define the specification in executable form. Claude naturally follows the red-green-refactor cycle when you prompt this way.

Example prompt:

Write failing tests for a password validator with these rules:
- Minimum 12 characters
- At least one uppercase, one lowercase, one digit, one special char
- Cannot contain the username
- Cannot be in the common passwords list (load from passwords.txt)

Then implement the validator to make all tests pass.

What Claude does: Writes 8-12 test cases covering valid passwords, each individual rule violation, edge cases (empty string, None, unicode), and the username-in-password check. Then implements the validator, runs the tests, and iterates until they all pass.

Pro tip: Be specific about edge cases in your test prompt. “Include tests for: empty string, None input, unicode characters, passwords that are exactly 12 characters” guides Claude to test boundaries you care about.

Anti-pattern: “Build a password validator and add tests” — the tests end up testing what the implementation does rather than what it should do, missing edge cases entirely.

Pattern 4: The Chain-of-Files Pattern¶

When to use: When the change depends on understanding multiple files. Loading context before requesting changes dramatically reduces hallucination — Claude can’t invent function signatures that don’t exist when it’s already read the actual code.

Example prompt:

Read these files in order:
1. src/models/user.py (the User model)
2. src/services/auth.py (the authentication service) 
3. src/middleware/rate_limit.py (the existing rate limiter)
4. src/routes/auth_routes.py (the auth endpoints)

Then add a login attempt tracker that:
- Records failed login attempts per IP in Redis
- Locks the account after 5 failures in 10 minutes
- Integrates with the existing rate limiter middleware
- Returns a 429 with retry-after header when locked

What Claude does: Reads all four files, understands the existing patterns (how models are defined, how services call repositories, how middleware chains), and implements the feature using the same patterns. The result fits seamlessly into the codebase because Claude has the full picture.

Pro tip: Order matters. Read the foundational files first (models, types) and the integration points last (routes, controllers). This mirrors how a human developer would onboard to the code.

Anti-pattern: “Add a login attempt tracker to the auth system” without loading any context. Claude guesses at your User model fields, invents service method names, and produces code that requires extensive editing.

Advanced variation:

Read all files matching src/auth/**/*.py, then add session 
invalidation when a user changes their password.

Pattern 5: The Progressive Refinement Pattern¶

When to use: Building anything with multiple layers of complexity. Each turn adds one concern to working code, rather than trying to handle everything at once.

Example prompt sequence:

Turn 1: "Build a basic REST API for a todo app with FastAPI. 
         Just CRUD endpoints, in-memory storage. No auth, 
         no validation, no error handling."

Turn 2: "Add Pydantic validation models for all endpoints.
