The missing rate limiting on the public invoice page was an oversight born from focusing on functionality. The missing ownership check on the PDF route was a classic authorization flaw. These are the kinds of bugs that developers miss because they test happy paths.

Run the security audit before every deployment. As discussed in Chapter 19, the audit skill checks against OWASP Top 10 patterns and catches issues that manual review misses.

6. Environment Variables Are the Last Mile Problem¶

Both deployment failures on Day 7 — Prisma binary targets and environment variable configuration — represent a category of bugs that only surface in production. The code works perfectly locally where the environment is implicit. Production exposes every assumption the developer made.

The fix: create .env.example on Day 1, not Day 7. Document every variable with where to get its value. Run /deploy-check on Day 5 to surface issues early, not just before the final deployment.

Summary¶

InvoiceFlow demonstrates that Claude Code is a production-grade development partner, not a snippet generator. The seven-day build produced a fully functional invoicing platform with authentication, Stripe payments, email notifications, PDF generation, and an analytics dashboard — approximately 15,000 lines of tested, security-audited code.

The key to success was not the AI itself but the developer’s orchestration: a precise CLAUDE.md established conventions on Day 1, prompts were scoped appropriately based on interdependency, security was audited before deployment, and /rewind was used strategically as a navigation tool rather than avoided as an admission of failure.

The total API cost of approximately $85 across 150 prompts represents a paradigm shift in what a single developer can accomplish in one week. The developer’s role shifts from writing code to directing code — from syntax to strategy, from typing to architecture. That shift is what this book has been building toward across every chapter.

In the next chapter, we collect the complete reference material: every command, every tool, every configuration option, and every environment variable — the comprehensive index for your daily work with Claude Code.

Chapter 26: Complete Command and Tool Reference¶ ~23 min read

Every previous chapter taught concepts through explanation and example. This chapter is different. It is a reference — the kind of chapter you bookmark and return to when you need the exact name of a command, the precise parameter a tool accepts, or the environment variable that controls a behavior you vaguely remember exists.

The tables here are comprehensive. They cover every built-in command, every tool, the complete configuration schema, and every environment variable that influences Claude Code’s behavior. Where a feature was covered in depth in an earlier chapter, we note the chapter number. Where a feature appears only here, we provide enough detail to use it immediately.

Keep this chapter open in a second tab.

Part 1: Built-in Commands¶

Built-in commands start with / and are entered directly in the Claude Code prompt. They control session behavior, navigation, configuration, and development workflows. Unlike tools (which Claude invokes programmatically), commands are user-initiated actions that modify the session state or produce output.

Session Management Commands¶

These commands control your current conversation session — clearing context, viewing costs, and managing session state.

Command Arguments Description
/help None Display all available commands with brief descriptions. The starting point when you forget a command name.
/clear None Reset the conversation context entirely. Use between unrelated tasks to prevent context pollution. Does not affect files on disk — only the in-memory conversation. Covered in Chapter 7.
/compact [focus-topic] Compress the conversation context, optionally preserving information related to a specific topic. Example: /compact authentication retains auth-related context while discarding the rest. Essential when approaching context limits. Covered in Chapter 7.
/context None Display a visual breakdown of current context window usage — how many tokens are consumed by system prompt, conversation history, tool results, and available capacity. Use this to decide whether to /compact or /clear.
/cost None Show the cost of the current session in dollars. Breaks down input tokens, output tokens, and cache hits. Useful for monitoring spend during long sessions.
/tokens None Display raw token counts for the current session — input, output, cache read, cache write. More granular than /cost.
/stats None Session statistics dashboard: token usage, cost, tool call counts, duration, and model information. Run at end of day for a summary.

Navigation Commands¶

These commands let you move through conversation history, resume previous sessions, and export transcripts.

Command Arguments Description
/rewind None Roll back the conversation to a previous point. Claude presents numbered checkpoints — pick one to restore. Everything after that point is discarded. Use when a line of investigation went wrong.
/resume None Resume the most recent conversation. When you restart Claude Code, your previous session is available for continuation.
/sessions None List all saved conversation sessions with timestamps and summaries. Navigate between sessions or resume any previous one.
/export [filename] Export the current conversation to a file. Default format is markdown. Example: /export debug-session.md. Useful for creating records of debugging sessions or architectural discussions.

Configuration Commands¶

Runtime configuration — change models, reasoning depth, and settings without editing files.

Command Arguments Description
/model [model-name] Switch the active model. Options include opus, sonnet, haiku, or full model IDs like claude-sonnet-4-6. Without arguments, displays the current model. Change persists for the session. Covered in Chapter 3.
/effort low \| medium \| high \| max Set reasoning depth. low = fast, shallow responses. high = deep analysis (default in most professional setups). max = extended thinking with maximum token budget. Covered in Chapter 7.
/fast None Toggle fast mode on/off. When enabled, Claude produces shorter responses with less deliberation. Same model, faster output. Good for simple file edits and quick questions.
/config None Open the interactive settings editor. View and modify permissions, hooks, model preferences, and other configuration without editing JSON files directly.

Development Commands¶

Commands that integrate with your development workflow — viewing diffs, creating commits, and planning multi-step work.

Command Arguments Description
/diff None Display uncommitted changes in an interactive diff viewer. Shows staged and unstaged modifications. Equivalent to a visual git diff within the Claude Code interface.
/commit None Create a git commit with Claude-generated message. Claude analyzes staged changes, drafts a commit message following conventional commit format, and includes the Co-Authored-By attribution line. Covered in Chapter 8.
/plan None Enter plan mode. Claude creates a structured plan before executing any changes. Useful for multi-file refactors or complex features where you want to review the approach before code is modified. Exit with /exitplan. Covered in Chapter 11.

Task Management Commands¶

Commands for managing background and parallel tasks. Covered in depth in Chapter 14.

Command Arguments Description
/tasks None List all active, pending, and completed tasks in the current session. Shows task IDs, status, and summaries.
/branch [name] Create a conversation branch for experimental work. If the experiment fails, discard the branch. If it succeeds, continue from it.

Advanced Commands¶

Power-user commands for parallel operations, recurring tasks, side conversations, and diagnostics.

Command Arguments Description
/batch "instruction" Execute an instruction across multiple files in parallel using isolated worktrees. Example: /batch "Update all API endpoints to return standardized error responses". Claude spawns parallel agents, each working in its own worktree. Covered in Chapter 14.
/loop [interval] [command] Run a command on a recurring interval. Example: /loop 5m check if CI pipeline passed. Default interval is 10 minutes. Useful for monitoring deployments, waiting for CI, or polling external services.
/btw [question] Ask a side question without contaminating the main conversation context. Example: /btw what does the --no-verify flag do?. Claude answers in an isolated context, then returns to the main thread.
/simplify None Review recently changed code for quality — identifies redundancy, unnecessary complexity, and opportunities for reuse. Runs automatically in some configurations.
/doctor None Diagnose your Claude Code installation. Checks: API key validity, model access, MCP server connectivity, hook execution, settings syntax, and environment variables. Run this first when something is not working.
/skills None List all available skills (both built-in and custom) with their trigger descriptions. Shows which skills are user-invocable vs. auto-activating.
/agents None List all configured subagents with their model, tools, and descriptions. Shows which agents are available for delegation.

Keyboard Shortcuts¶

These work in the Claude Code terminal interface at any time.

Shortcut Action
Esc Cancel the current generation. Claude stops producing output immediately. Partial output is retained in context.
Ctrl+C Interrupt the current operation. If Claude is running a tool (e.g., a long bash command), this cancels the tool execution.
Tab Autocomplete file paths after @ mentions. Also autocompletes slash commands after /.
/ + Tab Browse all available slash commands and skills in an autocomplete menu.
Up Arrow Cycle through previous prompts (prompt history).
Ctrl+L Clear the terminal display (does not clear conversation context — use /clear for that).
Ctrl+D Exit Claude Code (end the session).

Part 2: All Tools Reference¶

Tools are the functions Claude invokes programmatically to interact with your system. Unlike commands (which you type), tools are called by Claude in response to your requests. Each tool has defined parameters, return values, and restrictions.

The tools below are listed in functional groups. For each tool, we document: what it does, its parameters, what it returns, important notes, and restrictions.

File System Tools¶

Read¶

Reads file contents from the local filesystem.

Field Detail
Parameters file_path (string, required): Absolute path to the file. offset (integer, optional): Line number to start reading from. limit (integer, optional): Number of lines to read. pages (string, optional): Page range for PDFs, e.g., "1-5".
Returns File contents with line numbers (cat -n format). For images, returns visual content. For PDFs, returns text content of specified pages. For notebooks, returns all cells with outputs.
Notes Can read any file type: text, images (PNG, JPG), PDFs, Jupyter notebooks (.ipynb). Default reads up to 2000 lines. For large PDFs (>10 pages), the pages parameter is required. Cannot read directories — use ls via Bash for that.
Restrictions Files listed in settings.json deny rules (e.g., .env, credentials.json) are blocked. Path must be absolute, not relative.

Edit¶

Performs exact string replacements in files.

Field Detail
Parameters file_path (string, required): Absolute path. old_string (string, required): Exact text to find. new_string (string, required): Replacement text. replace_all (boolean, optional, default false): Replace all occurrences instead of just the first.
Returns Confirmation of the edit with before/after context.
Notes The old_string must be unique in the file unless replace_all is true. If it matches multiple locations and replace_all is false, the edit fails with an error. Always preserves exact indentation. The file must have been Read at least once in the conversation before editing.
Restrictions Cannot create new files — use Write for that. Fails if old_string is not found or is not unique (without replace_all).

Write¶

Creates or completely overwrites a file.

Field Detail
Parameters file_path (string, required): Absolute path. content (string, required): Complete file content.
Returns Confirmation that the file was written.
Notes Overwrites existing files entirely. For existing files, the file must have been Read first — Write will fail otherwise. Prefer Edit for modifications to existing files (sends only the diff, more efficient).
Restrictions Path must be absolute. The requirement to Read before Write prevents accidental overwrites.

NotebookEdit¶

Edits Jupyter notebook (.ipynb) cells.

Field Detail
Parameters notebook_path (string, required): Absolute path to .ipynb file. new_source (string, required): New cell content. cell_id (string, optional): Cell ID for positioning inserts. cell_type (string, optional): "code" or "markdown". edit_mode (string, optional): "replace", "insert", or "delete".
Returns Confirmation of the notebook edit.
Notes Cell numbering is 0-indexed. When inserting, the new cell appears after the cell specified by cell_id. When edit_mode is "insert", cell_type is required.
Restrictions Path must be absolute. Only operates on .ipynb files.

Search Tools¶

Glob¶

Fast file pattern matching.

Field Detail
Parameters pattern (string, required): Glob pattern, e.g., "**/*.py", "src/**/*.ts". path (string, optional): Directory to search in. Defaults to current working directory.
Returns List of matching file paths, sorted by modification time (most recent first).
Notes Works with any codebase size. Use for finding files by name or extension. For finding files by content, use Grep instead.
Restrictions None beyond filesystem permissions.

Grep¶

Content search powered by ripgrep.

Field Detail
Parameters pattern (string, required): Regex pattern. path (string, optional): File or directory to search. output_mode (string, optional): "files_with_matches" (default), "content", or "count". glob (string, optional): File filter, e.g., "*.js". type (string, optional): File type filter, e.g., "py", "js". -A, -B, -C (integer, optional): Lines after, before, or around matches. -i (boolean, optional): Case-insensitive. -n (boolean, optional): Show line numbers (default true). multiline (boolean, optional): Match across line boundaries. head_limit (integer, optional): Limit output entries (default 250). offset (integer, optional): Skip first N entries.
Returns Depends on output_mode: file paths, matching lines with context, or match counts.
Notes Uses ripgrep syntax, not grep. Literal braces need escaping: interface\\{\\} to find interface{}. For cross-line patterns, enable multiline: true. Prefer type over glob for standard file types (more efficient).
Restrictions The head_limit defaults to 250 to prevent context overflow. Pass 0 for unlimited (use sparingly).

Execution Tools¶

Bash¶

Executes shell commands.

Field Detail
Parameters command (string, required): The shell command to execute. description (string, optional): Human-readable description of what the command does. timeout (integer, optional): Timeout in milliseconds (max 600,000 = 10 minutes). Default is 120,000 (2 minutes). run_in_background (boolean, optional): Run asynchronously.
Returns Command stdout and stderr. Exit code information on failure.
Notes Working directory resets between calls — always use absolute paths. Shell state (variables, aliases) does not persist between calls. When run_in_background is true, the result is delivered asynchronously. Use && to chain dependent commands; use ; when order matters but failure does not.
Restrictions Subject to permission rules in settings.json (allow/deny lists). PreToolUse hooks fire before execution and can block commands (exit code 2). Commands matching deny patterns are rejected.

Agent¶

Spawns a subagent for complex, multi-step tasks.

Field Detail
Parameters prompt (string, required): The task description for the subagent. model (string, optional): Override the subagent’s model. allowed-tools (list, optional): Restrict which tools the subagent can use.
Returns The subagent’s complete response after it finishes its work.
Notes Subagents run in forked context — they cannot see or modify the parent conversation’s state. Useful for investigation tasks where you want to keep your main context clean. Custom agents defined in .claude/agents/ are available for specialized delegation. Covered in Chapter 13.
Restrictions Subagents inherit permission rules from settings. They cannot access tools not in their allowed-tools list.

Web Tools¶

WebFetch¶

Fetches and processes web page content.

Field Detail
Parameters url (string, required): Fully-formed URL (HTTP auto-upgrades to HTTPS). prompt (string, required): Instruction for processing the fetched content.
Returns AI-processed summary of the page content based on the prompt.
Notes Converts HTML to markdown before processing. Includes a 15-minute cache for repeated requests. If the URL redirects to a different host, returns the redirect URL instead of following it — make a second request with the new URL. Content may be summarized if very large.
Restrictions Fails for authenticated or private URLs (Google Docs, Confluence, Jira, private GitHub). For GitHub, use gh CLI via Bash instead. For authenticated sites, use MCP tools or browser automation.

WebSearch¶

Performs web searches and returns results.

Field Detail
Parameters query (string, required): Search query (minimum 2 characters). allowed_domains (list, optional): Only include results from these domains. blocked_domains (list, optional): Exclude results from these domains.
Returns Search results with titles, URLs, and snippets formatted as markdown.
Notes Results include links as markdown hyperlinks. Domain filtering supports both inclusion and exclusion. When using this tool, sources should be cited in the response.
Restrictions Web search availability may be region-dependent.

User Interaction Tools¶

AskUserQuestion¶

Prompts the user for input during execution.

Field Detail
Parameters question (string, required): The question to ask the user.
Returns The user’s text response.
Notes Use sparingly. The auto-execute philosophy (Chapter 5) prefers Claude to make reasonable decisions rather than asking. Reserve for genuinely ambiguous situations where proceeding without clarification would likely waste significant effort.
Restrictions None, but overuse degrades the user experience.

Plan Mode Tools¶

EnterPlanMode¶

Switches Claude into planning mode.

Field Detail
Parameters None.
Returns Confirmation that plan mode is active.
Notes In plan mode, Claude analyzes and plans without making changes. Produces a structured plan with steps, affected files, and risk assessment. Equivalent to the /plan command. Covered in Chapter 11.
Restrictions None.

ExitPlanMode¶

Exits planning mode and returns to normal execution.

Field Detail
Parameters None.
Returns Confirmation that plan mode has ended.
Notes After exiting, Claude can resume making file changes and running commands.
Restrictions Only meaningful if currently in plan mode.

Task Management Tools¶

TaskCreate¶

Creates a new background task.

Field Detail
Parameters description (string, required): What the task should accomplish. priority (string, optional): "low", "medium", "high".
Returns Task ID and confirmation.
Notes Tasks run as parallel subagents. Each task gets its own context and can use tools independently. Useful for parallelizing independent work items. Covered in Chapter 14.
Restrictions Subject to maximum concurrent task limits.

TaskList¶

Lists all tasks in the current session.

Field Detail
Parameters None.
Returns Table of tasks with IDs, status (pending/running/completed/failed), and summaries.
Notes Equivalent to the /tasks command.
Restrictions None.

TaskGet¶

Gets detailed status of a specific task.

Field Detail
Parameters task_id (string, required): The task identifier.
Returns Full task details: status, output, errors, duration.
Notes Use after TaskList to inspect a specific task’s results.
Restrictions Task must exist in the current session.

TaskUpdate¶

Updates an existing task’s parameters.

Field Detail
Parameters task_id (string, required): The task identifier. status (string, optional): New status. notes (string, optional): Additional context or instructions.
Returns Confirmation of the update.
Notes Can be used to cancel a running task or update priority.
Restrictions Task must exist and be in a modifiable state.

Worktree Tools¶

EnterWorktree¶

Creates an isolated git worktree for safe experimentation.

Field Detail
Parameters name (string, optional): Name for the worktree (kebab-case, max 64 characters). Auto-generated if omitted.
Returns Confirmation with worktree path and branch name.
Notes Creates a new worktree inside .claude/worktrees/ with a new branch based on HEAD. The session’s working directory switches to the worktree. All file operations happen in isolation — the main working tree is untouched. Used by /batch internally. Covered in Chapter 14.
Restrictions Must be in a git repository. Cannot enter a worktree if already in one. Only use when the user explicitly asks for a worktree.

ExitWorktree¶

Leaves a worktree session and returns to the original directory.

Field Detail
Parameters action (string, required): "keep" (leave worktree on disk) or "remove" (delete worktree and branch). discard_changes (boolean, optional): Required when removing a worktree with uncommitted changes.
Returns Confirmation of exit. If kept, returns the worktree path for later access.
Notes Only operates on worktrees created by EnterWorktree in the current session. With "keep", the worktree remains on disk for future use. With "remove", the worktree directory and branch are deleted. Refuses to remove worktrees with uncommitted changes unless discard_changes is true.
Restrictions No-op if not in a worktree session. Will not touch manually created worktrees or worktrees from previous sessions.

Scheduled Task Tools¶

CronCreate¶

Creates a recurring scheduled task.

Field Detail
Parameters schedule (string, required): Cron expression or interval string (e.g., "*/5 * * * *" or "every 5 minutes"). command (string, required): The command or prompt to execute on each run. name (string, optional): Human-readable name for the scheduled task.
Returns Cron job ID and confirmation with next run time.
Notes Tasks execute in the background. Each execution gets its own context. Useful for monitoring, periodic health checks, or recurring maintenance tasks.
Restrictions Maximum number of concurrent cron jobs may be limited.

CronDelete¶

Deletes a scheduled task.

Field Detail
Parameters cron_id (string, required): The cron job identifier from CronCreate.
Returns Confirmation of deletion.
Notes Stops all future executions. Currently running executions complete normally.
Restrictions Cron job must exist.

CronList¶

Lists all scheduled tasks.

Field Detail
Parameters None.
Returns Table of cron jobs with IDs, schedules, names, status, and last/next run times.
Notes Shows both active and paused cron jobs.
Restrictions None.

Skill Tool¶

Skill¶

Invokes a registered skill within the conversation.

Field Detail
Parameters skill (string, required): Skill name, e.g., "commit", "review-pr", "fix-issue". Can use fully qualified names like "my-extension:skill-name". args (string, optional): Arguments to pass to the skill.
Returns The skill’s output after execution.
Notes Skills are loaded from .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md. When a user types /skill-name, this tool is invoked internally. Skills can also be triggered automatically based on their description field matching the user’s intent. Covered in Chapter 10.
Restrictions Skill must be registered and available. Skills with disable-model-invocation: true can only be triggered by the user, not by Claude automatically.

Part 3: Configuration Reference¶

settings.json Complete Schema¶

The settings.json file (located at .claude/settings.json) controls permissions, hooks, and behavior. A .claude/settings.local.json file provides personal overrides that are not committed to git. Settings precedence: Local > Project > User > Managed.

Top-Level Fields¶

Field Type Description
permissions object Allow and deny rules for tool access.
permissions.allow string[] Glob patterns for auto-approved tool calls. Example: "Bash(git diff *)".
permissions.deny string[] Glob patterns for blocked tool calls. Example: "Read(./.env)". Evaluated before allow rules.
hooks object Maps lifecycle events to hook scripts.
hooks.SessionStart HookDef[] Hooks that run when a session begins.
hooks.PreToolUse HookDef[] Hooks that run before a tool executes. Can block (exit 2).
hooks.PostToolUse HookDef[] Hooks that run after a tool executes successfully.
hooks.Notification HookDef[] Hooks that run when Claude sends a notification.
hooks.UserPromptSubmit HookDef[] Hooks that run when the user submits a prompt.
hooks.Stop HookDef[] Hooks that run before Claude finishes responding.
hooks.SubagentStop HookDef[] Hooks that run when a subagent finishes.
hooks.TaskCompleted HookDef[] Hooks that run when a background task completes.
effortLevel string Default reasoning depth: "low", "medium", "high", or "max".
model string Default model ID, e.g., "claude-sonnet-4-6".
includeGitInstructions boolean Include git workflow instructions in system prompt. Default true.
autoMemoryEnabled boolean Enable auto-memory across sessions. Default false.
attribution.commit string Co-author line added to git commits.
attribution.pr string Attribution line added to PR descriptions.
disableAllHooks boolean Disable all hooks (useful for debugging). Default false.

HookDef Object¶

Field Type Description
matcher string Tool name this hook applies to, e.g., "Bash", "Edit", "Write". Omit for event-level hooks (SessionStart, Notification).
hooks HookConfig[] Array of hook configurations.

HookConfig Object¶

Field Type Description
type string Always "command".
command string Shell command or script path to execute.
timeout integer Maximum execution time in milliseconds. Default 10,000.
statusMessage string Message displayed while the hook runs.

Permission Rule Syntax¶

Permission rules use the format ToolName(pattern):

Pattern Meaning
Bash(git diff *) Allow any bash command starting with git diff
Bash(python -m pytest *) Allow running pytest
Read(./.env) Match reading the .env file
Read(./secrets/*) Match reading any file in secrets/
Bash(*) Match any bash command (use in local settings for unrestricted access)

Evaluation order: deny rules are checked first. First matching rule wins. If no rule matches, Claude asks for permission.

SKILL.md Frontmatter Fields¶

Skills are defined in .claude/skills/[name]/SKILL.md. The YAML frontmatter controls behavior. Covered in Chapter 10.

Field Type Required Description
name string Yes The slash command name. Becomes /name in the interface.
description string Yes When Claude should auto-load this skill. Include trigger phrases for reliable auto-activation.
argument-hint string No Shown in autocomplete. Example: "[issue-number]".
allowed-tools string No Comma-separated list of tools the skill can use without permission prompts. Example: "Read, Grep, Bash, Glob".
model string No Override model for this skill: "opus", "sonnet", "haiku", or full model ID.
effort string No Reasoning depth: "low", "medium", "high", "max".
context string No "fork" runs the skill in an isolated subagent context. Keeps main context clean.
agent string No Which subagent type to use when context: fork. Example: "general-purpose".
user-invocable boolean No If false, hidden from the / menu. Only Claude can trigger it. Default true.
disable-model-invocation boolean No If true, only the user can trigger this skill via slash command — Claude cannot auto-invoke it. Default false.
paths string[] No Glob patterns for auto-activation. When the user works on files matching these patterns, the skill loads automatically. Example: ["src/api/**", "**/routes/**"].

Dynamic Context Injection¶

Within SKILL.md content, the !`command` syntax executes shell commands before content reaches Claude:

markdown
## Context
- PR diff: !`gh pr diff $ARGUMENTS`
- Changed files: !`gh pr diff $ARGUMENTS --name-only`

The $ARGUMENTS variable contains whatever the user typed after the slash command.

Agent Definition Frontmatter Fields¶

Agents are defined in .claude/agents/[name].md. Covered in Chapter 13.

Field Type Required Description
name string Yes Agent identifier. Used for delegation references.
description string Yes When Claude should delegate to this agent. Include specific task types and trigger phrases.
tools string Yes Comma-separated tools the agent can access. Example: "Read, Grep, Glob, Bash".
model string No Model override: "opus" for critical analysis, "sonnet" for balanced work, "haiku" for fast tasks.
effort string No Reasoning depth: "low", "medium", "high", "max".
memory string No Memory scope: "user" (global), "project" (per-project), "local" (gitignored).
maxTurns integer No Maximum number of tool-use turns the agent can take before returning. Prevents runaway agents.
isolation string No "worktree" runs the agent in an isolated git worktree. Changes do not affect the main working tree until explicitly merged.

Rule Frontmatter Fields¶

Rules are defined in .claude/rules/[name].md. Covered in Chapter 9.

Field Type Required Description
paths string[] No Glob patterns that activate this rule. Example: ["**/*.py", "**/*.pyi"]. When a file matching the pattern is involved in the conversation, the rule loads automatically.

Rules without paths frontmatter are always loaded. Rules with paths are loaded only when relevant files are being worked on.

Rule Import Syntax¶

In CLAUDE.md, rules can be imported with the @ prefix:

markdown
@.claude/rules/python.md
@.claude/rules/security.md

Imported rules load every session regardless of their paths frontmatter. Path-scoped rules that are not @-imported load conditionally based on file context.

Part 4: Environment Variables¶

Environment variables control Claude Code’s behavior at the system level. They are set in your shell profile, in .claude/hooks/session-setup.sh, or passed directly when launching Claude Code.

Authentication & Provider Variables¶

Variable Description Default
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY API key for direct Anthropic API access. Required unless using Bedrock or Vertex. None
CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK Set to 1 to route requests through AWS Bedrock instead of the Anthropic API. Requires AWS credentials in environment. 0
CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX Set to 1 to route requests through Google Cloud Vertex AI. Requires GCP credentials. 0
ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL Custom base URL for the Anthropic API. Use for proxies or enterprise API gateways. https://api.anthropic.com
BEDROCK_AWS_REGION AWS region for Bedrock requests. us-east-1
BEDROCK_AWS_PROFILE AWS profile name for Bedrock authentication. default
VERTEX_PROJECT_ID Google Cloud project ID for Vertex AI. None
VERTEX_REGION Google Cloud region for Vertex AI. us-central1

Model & Behavior Variables¶

Variable Description Default
CLAUDE_MODEL Override the default model. Accepts full model IDs like claude-sonnet-4-6 or short names like sonnet. Takes precedence over settings.json model setting. Set by settings or plan tier
CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TURNS Maximum number of agentic turns (tool calls) Claude can take in a single response. Prevents runaway tool-use loops. 200
CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_TOKENS Maximum output tokens per response. Model default
CLAUDE_CODE_BUDGET_TOKENS Token budget for extended thinking when effort is set to max. Controls how much thinking Claude can do. Model default
CLAUDE_CODE_EFFORT Default effort level: low, medium, high, max. Overridden by /effort command or effortLevel in settings. high

Feature Control Variables¶

Variable Description Default
CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC Set to 1 to disable telemetry, analytics, and non-essential network requests. Recommended for air-gapped or high-security environments. 0
CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_CACHING Set to 1 to disable prompt caching. Forces fresh processing of every request. Increases cost but ensures no stale cache hits. 0
CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_HOOKS Set to 1 to skip all hook execution. Equivalent to disableAllHooks in settings but set at the environment level. 0
CLAUDE_CODE_GIT_INSTRUCTIONS Set to 0 to disable automatic git workflow instructions in the system prompt. 1
CLAUDE_CODE_AUTO_MEMORY Set to 1 to enable automatic memory persistence across sessions. 0

Path & Environment Variables¶

Variable Description Default
CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR Override the location of Claude Code’s configuration directory. ~/.claude
CLAUDE_CODE_PROJECTS_DIR Override the location of per-project memory and state. ~/.claude/projects
CLAUDE_CODE_TEMP_DIR Directory for temporary files created during tool execution. System temp dir
PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE Set to 1 in session-setup.sh to prevent Python from creating .pyc files in your project. Not set
PATH Often modified in session-setup.sh to include project-specific tool directories, virtual environments, or local bin paths. System PATH

MCP Server Variables¶

Variable Description Default
MCP_TIMEOUT Default timeout in milliseconds for MCP server responses. 30000
MCP_MAX_RETRIES Number of retry attempts when an MCP server call fails. 3

Proxy & Network Variables¶

Variable Description Default
HTTP_PROXY HTTP proxy URL for outbound requests. None
HTTPS_PROXY HTTPS proxy URL for outbound requests. None
NO_PROXY Comma-separated list of hosts to bypass the proxy. None
CLAUDE_CODE_HTTP_TIMEOUT Timeout in milliseconds for HTTP requests made by tools. 30000

Debug & Development Variables¶

Variable Description Default
CLAUDE_CODE_DEBUG Set to 1 to enable verbose debug logging. Outputs internal state, tool call details, and hook execution traces. 0
CLAUDE_CODE_LOG_LEVEL Logging verbosity: "error", "warn", "info", "debug", "trace". "info"
CLAUDE_CODE_LOG_FILE Path to a file for logging output. When set, logs are written to this file instead of stderr. None

Quick Lookup Index¶

For fast navigation, here is where each topic is covered in this chapter and in the broader book:

Topic This Chapter Section Detailed Chapter
Slash commands Part 1 Chapter 5 (workflow), Chapter 7 (context management)
Permission system Part 3 (settings.json) Chapter 6
Hooks Part 3 (HookDef) Chapter 12
Skills Part 2 (Skill tool), Part 3 (frontmatter) Chapter 10
Agents Part 2 (Agent tool), Part 3 (frontmatter) Chapter 13
Rules Part 3 (frontmatter) Chapter 9
Worktrees Part 2 (EnterWorktree/ExitWorktree) Chapter 14
Tasks Part 2 (Task tools) Chapter 14
MCP servers Part 2 (tool overview) Chapter 24
Git integration Part 1 (/commit, /diff) Chapter 8
Environment variables Part 4 Chapter 3 (setup), Chapter 6 (security)
Context management Part 1 (/clear, /compact, /context) Chapter 7
Extended thinking Part 4 (CLAUDE_CODE_BUDGET_TOKENS) Chapter 7
Browser automation — Chapter 15, Chapter 16
Memory system Part 4 (AUTO_MEMORY) Chapter 17

Summary¶

This chapter serves as the definitive reference for Claude Code’s interface surface area. The four parts cover:

  1. 32 built-in commands across seven categories (session, navigation, configuration, development, tasks, advanced) plus keyboard shortcuts.
  2. 23 tools that Claude invokes programmatically, from file operations (Read, Edit, Write) to search (Glob, Grep) to execution (Bash, Agent) to web access (WebFetch, WebSearch) to workflow management (Task, Worktree, Cron, Skill tools).
  3. Complete configuration schema for settings.json, SKILL.md frontmatter, agent definitions, and rule definitions — every field, its type, and its purpose.
  4. 30+ environment variables controlling authentication, model selection, feature flags, paths, networking, and debugging.

When you encounter a behavior you need to control, a tool you need to invoke, or a configuration option you half-remember, start here. The cross-reference index at the end maps each topic to both its reference table in this chapter and the detailed treatment in earlier chapters.

The next chapter completes Part VII with a glossary and troubleshooting guide — the final reference material for the handbook.

Chapter 27: Troubleshooting, FAQ, and Expert Tips¶ ~18 min read

Every tool has failure modes. Claude Code is no exception. The difference between a frustrated user and a productive one is knowing where to look when something breaks, having quick answers to recurring questions, and accumulating the small tricks that compound into dramatic efficiency gains.

This chapter is your reference guide for the rough edges. Part 1 walks through the ten most common issues with a strict Symptom-Cause-Fix format. Part 2 answers the twenty questions that come up in every team onboarding. Part 3 delivers twenty-five expert tips — the kind of knowledge that normally takes months of daily use to discover.

Part 1: Troubleshooting¶

Each issue follows the same structure: what you observe, why it happens, and how to fix it. No preamble.

Issue 1: “Claude Ignores My CLAUDE.md Rules”¶

Symptom: You added rules to CLAUDE.md — commit message format, coding standards, output preferences — but Claude doesn’t follow them. It formats code differently than specified, skips your preferred commit style, or ignores explicit instructions.

Cause: The most common culprit is file length. Once CLAUDE.md exceeds roughly 200 lines, Claude’s adherence to individual rules drops measurably. Rules buried below line 100 get less attention than rules at the top. Another cause: vague phrasing. “Try to use type hints” is weaker than “ALWAYS use type hints on every function signature.”

Fix:

  1. Keep CLAUDE.md under 100 lines. Move language-specific rules to .claude/rules/ files with paths: frontmatter so they load only when relevant.

  2. Put your most critical rules in the first 30 lines. Claude reads top-down, and early instructions carry more weight.

  3. Use the IMPORTANT: prefix for rules that must never be violated:

markdown
IMPORTANT: Never commit files matching *.env, *.pem, or credentials.json.
  1. Use @imports for domain rules instead of inlining them:
markdown
## Domain Rules
@.claude/rules/python.md
@.claude/rules/security.md
  1. Validate by asking Claude directly: “What rules from CLAUDE.md apply to this task?” If it can’t recite them, they’re buried too deep or phrased too weakly.

Issue 2: “Skill Doesn’t Trigger Automatically”¶

Symptom: You say “review this code for security issues” but Claude doesn’t load the security-audit skill. It gives a generic response instead of the structured OWASP/CWE output you built.

Cause: Claude decides whether to auto-load a skill based on its description field in the SKILL.md frontmatter. If the description doesn’t contain phrases that match your natural language, Claude won’t make the connection.

Fix:

  1. Open the skill’s SKILL.md and expand the description with more trigger phrases:
yaml
---
name: security-audit
description: >
  Performs security analysis on code. Use when user says "security audit",
  "check for vulnerabilities", "is this secure", "OWASP check", "find
  security bugs", "pentest this", "hardening review", "CVE scan",
  "injection check", "auth bypass", "security review".
---
  1. If you want the skill to trigger ONLY via /security-audit and never automatically, add:
yaml
disable-model-invocation: true
  1. Test by asking Claude: “When would you use the security-audit skill?” It will quote the description back. If it doesn’t mention your trigger phrases, they’re missing.

Issue 3: “Hook Blocks Everything”¶

Symptom: The block-destructive.sh hook rejects legitimate commands. You try to run rm -rf dist/ to clean a build directory, and the hook kills it. Or git push gets blocked because the regex matches too broadly.

Cause: Regex patterns in the hook are too greedy. A pattern like rm -rf catches every recursive force-delete, including safe ones. A pattern like git push.*force catches git push --force-with-lease, which is the safe variant.

Fix:

  1. Test the hook directly with the command that’s being blocked:
bash
echo '{"tool_input":{"command":"rm -rf dist/"}}' | .claude/hooks/block-destructive.sh
echo $?  # 0 = allowed, 2 = blocked
  1. Tighten the regex. Instead of blocking all rm -rf, block only rm -rf / (root) and rm -rf . (current directory):
bash
# Too broad — blocks legitimate cleanup
if [[ "${COMMAND}" =~ rm[[:space:]]+-rf ]]; then

# Better — blocks only catastrophic patterns
if [[ "${COMMAND}" =~ rm[[:space:]]+-rf[[:space:]]+(/|\.)[[:space:]]*$ ]]; then
  1. Add an allowlist for known-safe patterns that your workflow needs:
bash
# Allow build directory cleanup
if [[ "${COMMAND}" =~ rm[[:space:]]+-rf[[:space:]]+(dist|build|node_modules|__pycache__|\.pytest_cache) ]]; then
  exit 0
fi
  1. As a temporary bypass, disable hooks in settings.local.json:
json
{ "disableAllHooks": true }

Remember to re-enable them.

Issue 4: “Context Window Fills Up Too Fast”¶

Symptom: Mid-conversation, Claude starts losing track of earlier instructions. Responses become less accurate. You see warnings about context usage, or Claude asks questions you already answered.

Cause: Context is a finite resource. Common drains: reading large files without line limits, pasting entire stack traces or log dumps, accumulating tool call outputs from long debugging sessions, or working on many unrelated tasks in a single session.

Fix:

  1. Use /clear between unrelated tasks. This is the single highest-impact habit. Each new task should start with a clean context.

  2. Use /compact [focus-topic] when you’re mid-task and running low. The focus topic tells Claude what to preserve:

/compact authentication

This compresses the conversation history while keeping authentication-related context intact.

  1. Read files with limits instead of dumping entire files into context:
Read lines 50-120 of src/auth/middleware.py

  1. Use subagents for investigative work. When Claude delegates to a subagent, the subagent’s full investigation stays in its own context. Only the summary returns to your main conversation.

  2. Check context usage with /context to see how much headroom you have before hitting the limit.

Issue 5: “Claude Makes Wrong Tool Choices”¶

Symptom: You ask Claude to search for a pattern in the codebase and it reads files one by one instead of using Grep. Or you ask it to edit a file and it rewrites the entire file instead of making a targeted edit.

Cause: Ambiguous prompts. “Find where we handle auth” could mean search the codebase (Grep), read a specific file (Read), or explain the architecture (analysis). Claude guesses, and sometimes guesses wrong.

Fix:

  1. Be explicit about the tool you want:
# Vague — Claude guesses
"Find the auth handler"

# Explicit — Claude uses Grep
"Search the codebase for functions that handle authentication"

# Explicit — Claude uses Read
"Read src/auth/handler.py and explain the login flow"
  1. If Claude picks the wrong tool mid-conversation, correct it once. Claude learns from in-session corrections:
"Don't read each file individually. Use Grep to search across the codebase."
  1. In skills that execute multi-step workflows, specify the tools in the SKILL.md instructions:
markdown
## Step 1: Search
Use Grep to find all files containing the error pattern.

## Step 2: Analyze
Read the top 3 matching files to identify root cause.

Issue 6: “Settings Not Taking Effect”¶

Symptom: You changed a setting in settings.json — added a permission, modified a hook, changed the effort level — but Claude Code behaves as if nothing changed.

Cause: Three possibilities. First, invalid JSON: a missing comma or unclosed brace silently invalidates the entire file. Second, precedence: settings.local.json overrides settings.json, and a conflicting local setting wins. Third, the setting name is wrong — JSON keys are case-sensitive and must match the schema exactly.

Fix:

  1. Validate JSON syntax:
bash
python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('.claude/settings.json')); print('Valid')"
python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('.claude/settings.local.json')); print('Valid')"

  1. Check for conflicts between local and project settings. The precedence order is: Managed (enterprise) > Local > Project > User. If settings.local.json has "disableAllHooks": true, no hooks from settings.json will fire.

  2. Run the workspace validator:

bash
bash tools/scripts/validate-workspace.sh
  1. Check the schema for correct key names: https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json

Issue 7: “MCP Server Fails to Connect”¶

Symptom: You registered an MCP server in settings, but Claude Code can’t find its tools. You see errors like “server failed to start” or “command not found” in the logs.

Cause: MCP servers run as child processes spawned by Claude Code. If the server’s executable isn’t in the PATH, or if the server writes debug output to stdout (contaminating the JSON-RPC channel), or if the server crashes on startup, the connection fails silently.

Fix:

  1. Verify the command exists and runs:
bash
# If your MCP config says: "command": "node", "args": ["server.js"]
node server.js  # Does it start without errors?

# If it says: "command": "python3", "args": ["-m", "my_mcp_server"]
python3 -m my_mcp_server  # Does it start?
  1. Check that the server only writes JSON-RPC to stdout. Any print() or console.log() in the server’s main path will corrupt the protocol. Use stderr for debug logging:
python
# Wrong — breaks MCP
print("Server started")

# Right — stderr doesn't interfere
import sys
print("Server started", file=sys.stderr)
  1. Use absolute paths in the MCP configuration to avoid PATH issues:
json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "my-server": {
      "command": "/usr/local/bin/node",
      "args": ["/Users/me/mcp-servers/my-server/index.js"]
    }
  }
}
  1. Check for version mismatches. The MCP SDK evolves rapidly. Ensure your server uses a compatible version of @modelcontextprotocol/sdk or mcp (Python).

Issue 8: “Agent Tool Returns Unhelpful Results”¶

Symptom: You delegate to a subagent — the security reviewer or debugger — and the response is shallow, generic, or misses the point entirely.

Cause: Subagents inherit the prompt you give them, plus their system prompt from .claude/agents/. If your delegation prompt is vague (“check this code”), the agent lacks direction. Additionally, agents have limited tool access defined in their frontmatter, and may not have the tools needed for your specific task.

Fix:

  1. Be specific in your delegation:
# Vague — agent flounders
"Use the security-reviewer agent to check the code"

# Specific — agent delivers
"Use the security-reviewer agent to audit src/auth/oauth.py
for OWASP Top 10 issues, specifically injection and broken
access control. Check all user inputs that reach the database."
  1. Verify the agent has the right tools. If your security reviewer needs to run commands but only has Read, Grep, Glob, it can’t execute dynamic analysis:
yaml
# In .claude/agents/security-reviewer.md
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash  # Add Bash if needed
  1. Add maxTurns to prevent agents from spinning without progress:
yaml
maxTurns: 15  # Stop after 15 tool calls
  1. If the agent’s system prompt is too generic, add domain-specific instructions. The more context the agent has about what “good” looks like, the better its output.

Issue 9: “Claude Code Is Slow”¶

Symptom: Responses take 15-30 seconds. Tool calls feel sluggish. The overall interaction pace drags compared to what you’ve seen in demos or other sessions.

Cause: Three main factors. First, the Opus model is inherently slower than Sonnet — it’s doing more reasoning. Second, large context windows (approaching 200K tokens) increase latency on every turn. Third, complex tool chains with many sequential calls add round-trip time.

Fix:

  1. Switch to Sonnet for simple tasks:
/model sonnet

Use Opus for complex reasoning (debugging, security audits, architecture). Use Sonnet for straightforward tasks (file edits, simple searches, formatting).

  1. Use /effort low for trivial tasks:
/effort low
"Rename the variable from `usr` to `user` in auth.py"

  1. Use /fast to enable streaming optimizations without changing models.

  2. Reduce context size. /clear between tasks. /compact mid-task. Read files with line ranges instead of dumping entire files.

  3. Parallelize where possible. “Review security AND performance” spawns two agents simultaneously, faster than sequential reviews.

Issue 10: “Permission Denied Errors”¶

Symptom: Claude Code refuses to run a command or read a file, saying it’s not permitted. You didn’t intend to block that action.

Cause: The deny list in settings.json is too broad. A rule like Bash(rm *) blocks every command starting with rm, including safe ones. Or Read(./config/*) blocks all config files when you only meant to protect secrets.

Fix:

  1. Check what’s in the deny list:
bash
python3 -c "
import json
s = json.load(open('.claude/settings.json'))
for d in s.get('permissions', {}).get('deny', []):
    print(d)
"
  1. Use narrow, specific deny rules:
json
{
  "deny": [
    "Read(./.env)",
    "Read(./.env.production)",
    "Read(./secrets/*)",
    "Read(./credentials.json)"
  ]
}

  1. Remember the evaluation order: deny > ask > allow. A deny rule always wins over an allow rule for the same pattern.

  2. Use settings.local.json to override for your personal workflow without affecting the team:

json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": ["Bash(*)"]
  }
}

Part 2: Frequently Asked Questions¶

Twenty questions that come up in every onboarding, every team Slack channel, every “getting started” session. Concise answers.

Q1: How much does Claude Code cost per day?¶

It depends on usage, but a typical professional developer using Claude Code with the Opus model for 4-6 hours of active coding spends $15-40/day. Heavy users doing complex debugging, security audits, and multi-agent workflows can hit $50-80/day. Sonnet usage runs roughly 5-7x cheaper than Opus for the same token volume. Use /stats to see your actual usage.

Q2: Do I need my own API key?¶

If you’re using the Claude Code subscription plan (Max), your usage is included. If you’re using the API-key mode, yes — you need an Anthropic API key set as ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in your environment. Teams can also use centralized billing through Anthropic’s admin console.

Q3: Can I use Claude Code with AWS Bedrock or Google Vertex?¶

Yes. Set the appropriate environment variables:

bash
# For Bedrock
export CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1
export AWS_REGION=us-east-1

# For Vertex
export CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1
export CLOUD_ML_REGION=us-central1
export ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT_ID=my-project

Claude Code detects these and routes requests through your cloud provider instead of Anthropic’s API directly.

Q4: Does Claude Code have internet access?¶

Yes. Claude Code can use WebSearch and WebFetch tools to search the web and fetch URLs. It can also run curl, wget, or any network command via Bash. MCP servers can connect to external APIs. However, fetching authenticated pages (behind login walls) requires either an MCP server with credentials or the dev-browser tool for browser-based authentication.

Q5: How do I share my Claude Code setup with my team?¶

Commit these files to your repository:

  • .claude/settings.json — Shared permissions, hooks, guardrails
  • .claude/rules/ — Coding standards
  • .claude/skills/ — Workflow definitions
  • .claude/agents/ — Subagent configurations
  • CLAUDE.md — Project instructions

Keep .claude/settings.local.json in .gitignore for personal overrides. When a teammate clones the repo, Claude Code automatically picks up the shared configuration.

Q6: Can Claude Code edit multiple files at once?¶

Yes. Claude can edit as many files as needed in a single turn. For large-scale changes across many files, use /batch which spawns parallel worktrees — each making changes independently, then merging results. As discussed in Chapter 22, this is the fastest path for cross-cutting refactors.

Q7: How do I prevent Claude from reading sensitive files?¶

Add deny rules to .claude/settings.json:

json
"deny": [
  "Read(./.env)",
  "Read(./.env.*)",
  "Read(./secrets/*)",
  "Read(./**/*.pem)",
  "Read(./credentials.json)"
]

Deny rules are evaluated first and cannot be overridden by allow rules.

Q8: What’s the maximum file size Claude Code can read?¶

Claude Code can read files of any size, but it loads content into the context window, which has a finite token limit. Practically, files over 10,000 lines should be read with line ranges (offset and limit parameters). For binary files, images, and PDFs, the tools handle conversion automatically, but large PDFs should be read in page ranges of 20 or fewer.

Q9: Can I use Claude Code in CI/CD pipelines?¶

Yes. Claude Code runs in headless mode when there’s no TTY attached. Use the --print flag for non-interactive execution:

bash
claude -p "Review the diff in this PR and output a summary" --output-format json

Set CLAUDE_CODE_HEADLESS=1 for pipeline environments. This is commonly used for automated code review, changelog generation, and pre-merge security checks.

Q10: How do I understand why Claude made a particular decision?¶

Ask it. Claude Code retains the full conversation context. Simply ask: “Why did you choose that approach?” or “What alternatives did you consider?” For more systematic tracking, use a decisions.md file (see Expert Tip 9) or the memory system’s save_decision function.

Q11: Can Claude Code work offline?¶

No. Claude Code requires an internet connection to communicate with Anthropic’s API (or Bedrock/Vertex). The models run in the cloud. Local tools (file reads, grep, bash) work locally, but the reasoning engine itself is remote. There is no offline mode.

Q12: How do I reset Claude Code to factory defaults?¶

Remove your configuration files:

bash
rm -rf .claude/          # Project-level config
rm -rf ~/.claude/         # User-level config (careful — this includes memory)

For a lighter reset, just delete settings.local.json to remove personal overrides while keeping team config intact.

Q13: Can I use custom or fine-tuned models?¶

Claude Code supports model selection among Anthropic’s model family (Opus, Sonnet, Haiku). You can switch models per-session (/model sonnet), per-skill (in SKILL.md frontmatter), or per-agent (in agent frontmatter). Custom fine-tuned models are not currently supported through Claude Code.

Q14: How do I export a session?¶

Use the /export command:

/export session-debug-auth.txt

This saves the full conversation — your prompts, Claude’s responses, and tool outputs — to a text file. Useful for post-mortems, documentation, or sharing debugging sessions with teammates.

Q15: What data gets sent to Anthropic?¶

Your prompts, file contents that Claude reads, tool outputs, and conversation history are sent to Anthropic’s API for processing. Anthropic’s data retention policy applies — on the API tier, data is not used for training by default. Check your contract and Anthropic’s current privacy policy for specifics. For sensitive environments, use Bedrock or Vertex to keep data within your cloud account.

Q16: Can Claude Code run destructive commands?¶

By default, Claude Code asks for confirmation before running any command not in the allow list. The block-destructive.sh hook (Chapter 19) adds a hard block on patterns like rm -rf /, DROP TABLE, and git push --force main. You can further restrict or relax these in settings, but the defaults are designed to prevent irreversible mistakes.

Q17: How do I undo something Claude did?¶

For file changes: Claude Code edits files on disk. Use git diff to see what changed and git checkout -- <file> to revert. For commits: git reset HEAD~1 undoes the last commit while keeping changes staged. For multi-step changes: /rewind rolls the conversation back to an earlier state, though it doesn’t automatically revert file changes on disk.

Q18: Can I restrict Claude Code to specific directories?¶

Not directly through a single setting, but you can achieve this through deny rules:

json
"deny": [
  "Read(/etc/*)",
  "Read(/var/*)",
  "Bash(cd /etc*)",
  "Bash(cd /var*)"
]

For project isolation, use worktrees (Chapter 22) which confine Claude to a specific branch and directory.

Q19: What’s the cheapest way to use Claude Code?¶

Use Sonnet as your default model (/model sonnet). Use /effort low for simple tasks. Use /clear between tasks to avoid context bloat (large contexts cost more per turn). Use /compact to compress mid-session. Reserve Opus for complex reasoning tasks where the quality difference justifies the cost. A Sonnet-heavy workflow can run 5-7x cheaper than all-Opus.

Q20: What’s the difference between /fast and /effort low?¶

/fast enables streaming and output optimizations without reducing reasoning depth. Claude still thinks thoroughly but delivers results faster through output pipeline improvements. /effort low actually reduces reasoning depth — Claude spends less time deliberating, which is appropriate for trivial tasks (renaming variables, simple formatting) but produces shallower analysis for complex problems. Use /fast when you want speed without sacrificing quality. Use /effort low when the task genuinely doesn’t require deep thought.

Part 3: Expert Tips¶

Twenty-five tips from daily use. Each one earns its place.

Tip 1: Use /btw for side questions. When you’re mid-debugging and want to ask “what does this regex do?” without polluting your main context, prefix with /btw. Claude answers the side question in a fork that doesn’t affect your primary conversation flow.

Tip 2: Warm-start hooks with a session-setup script. The SessionStart hook runs once when Claude Code launches. Use it to set environment variables, check tool availability, and print a welcome message. This ensures every session starts in a known-good state:

bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
export PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1
export RUFF_CACHE_DIR="/tmp/ruff-cache"
command -v ruff >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo "Warning: ruff not installed" >&2

Tip 3: Build a /today skill. Create a skill that fetches your GitHub notifications, open PRs, assigned issues, and calendar (via MCP) in one command. Start every morning with /today instead of checking five different apps.

Tip 4: Use CronCreate patterns for polling. The /loop command runs a prompt on a recurring interval. Use it for deployment monitoring:

/loop 5m "Check if the deploy to staging succeeded using gh run list"

Tip 5: Build a /standup skill. A skill that reads your git log from the last 24 hours, summarizes what you did, checks your open PRs, and formats it for your team standup. Five seconds instead of five minutes of context-switching.

Tip 6: Use worktree isolation for risky experiments. When Claude is about to make sweeping changes, ask it to work in a worktree. The changes happen on a separate branch in a separate directory. If they fail, you discard the worktree with zero impact on your main branch. As covered in Chapter 22, this is essential for /batch operations.

Tip 7: Auto-format with PostToolUse hooks. The auto-lint-python.sh hook runs ruff after every Python file edit. Build equivalents for your stack:

json
{
  "matcher": "Edit|Write",
  "hooks": [{
    "type": "command",
    "command": "./.claude/hooks/auto-format-ts.sh",
    "timeout": 10000
  }]
}

This guarantees every file Claude touches is formatted correctly, without Claude spending tokens on formatting.

Tip 8: Use /compact with a focus topic. Don’t just run /compact — tell it what to keep:

/compact authentication flow

Claude compresses the conversation but preserves everything related to authentication. Without a focus topic, it guesses what’s important and sometimes guesses wrong.

Tip 9: Maintain a decisions.md file. For long-running projects, keep a file where Claude logs architectural decisions:

markdown
## 2026-03-15: Chose JWT over session cookies
**Context:** Need stateless auth for microservices
**Decision:** JWT with 15min expiry, refresh tokens in httpOnly cookies
**Trade-offs:** Revocation is harder, but avoids shared session store

Reference it in CLAUDE.md so Claude reads it every session and maintains consistency.

Tip 10: Mix models in a single session. Start with Sonnet for file exploration and understanding, then switch to Opus for the actual fix:

/model sonnet
"Read through src/auth/ and map out the authentication flow"

/model opus
"Now fix the race condition in the token refresh logic"

You get Sonnet’s speed for the exploratory phase and Opus’s reasoning depth for the surgical fix.

Tip 11: Use MCP servers for internal tools. If your team has internal APIs — deployment systems, feature flags, incident management — wrap them in MCP servers. Claude Code can then deploy, toggle flags, and check incident status without you writing bash commands or remembering API endpoints.

Tip 12: Deny rules for production credentials. Beyond denying .env files, deny access to production credential paths specifically:

json
"deny": [
  "Read(./.env.production)",
  "Read(./config/prod/*)",
  "Bash(*--env production*)",
  "Bash(*PROD_*)"
]

This creates a hard barrier even if you accidentally ask Claude to “check the production config.”

Tip 13: Use /batch with worktrees for cross-cutting changes. When you need to update 50 files with the same pattern change — renaming an API field, updating error handling, migrating to a new library — /batch parallelizes across worktrees. Each worktree handles a subset, and results merge automatically. A change that takes 30 minutes sequentially completes in 5.

Tip 14: Leverage auto-memory for cross-session continuity. Claude Code’s memory system persists insights across sessions. When you solve a tricky bug, the root cause and fix are remembered. Next time a similar issue appears, Claude recalls the pattern. Verify memory is enabled:

json
"autoMemoryEnabled": true

You can also manually save critical facts with the memory MCP tools.

Tip 15: Set maxTurns on agents to prevent runaway investigations. Without a limit, a subagent can burn through tokens chasing dead ends. Set a reasonable cap:

yaml
---
name: debugger
maxTurns: 20
---

If the agent can’t solve it in 20 turns, it reports what it found and returns control. You can then redirect with better context.

Tip 16: Use context: fork on expensive skills. Skills with context: fork run in an isolated subagent context. The main conversation only receives the final output, not every intermediate tool call. This keeps your primary context clean and reduces token consumption for investigation-heavy workflows.

Tip 17: Pin skill models for consistency. If your security-audit skill should always use Opus (because Sonnet misses subtle vulnerabilities), pin it in the frontmatter:

yaml
---
name: security-audit
model: opus
effort: high
---

This overrides whatever model the main session is using, ensuring critical workflows always get the reasoning depth they need.

Tip 18: Use dynamic context injection in skills. The !`command` syntax in SKILL.md runs shell commands before the skill loads, injecting their output as context:

markdown
## Current state
- Branch: !`git branch --show-current`
- Recent commits: !`git log --oneline -5`
- Failing tests: !`python -m pytest --tb=no -q 2>&1 | tail -5`

Claude starts with all this context loaded, saving 3-4 tool calls per invocation.

Tip 19: Use UserPromptSubmit hooks for input validation. If your team has conventions about how prompts should be structured (issue numbers must include #, file paths must be absolute), validate at the prompt level:

json
{
  "UserPromptSubmit": [{
    "hooks": [{
      "type": "command",
      "command": "./.claude/hooks/validate-prompt.sh"
    }]
  }]
}

The hook can reject malformed prompts before they waste a model turn.

Tip 20: Create project-specific .claude/ configs. Every repository can have its own .claude/ directory with rules, skills, and settings tailored to that project. When you cd into the repo, Claude Code loads that project’s configuration automatically. A Python microservice, a React frontend, and an infrastructure repo can each have completely different rules.

Tip 21: Use the Stop hook as a quality gate. The Stop event fires before Claude finalizes its response. Use it to verify that outputs meet your standards — for example, checking that code review findings include file:line references, or that commit messages follow conventional commits format.

Tip 22: Leverage --output-format json for toolchain integration. When using Claude Code in scripts or CI/CD, JSON output is parseable by downstream tools:

bash
result=$(claude -p "List all TODO comments in src/" --output-format json)
echo "$result" | jq '.todos[] | .file + ":" + .line'

This lets you build Claude Code into automated workflows where its output feeds into other systems.

Tip 23: Use paths: frontmatter to auto-activate skills. Instead of typing /api-conventions every time you edit an API file, make the skill auto-activate:

yaml
---
name: api-conventions
paths:
  - "src/api/**"
  - "**/routes/**"
  - "**/endpoints/**"
user-invocable: false
---

Claude loads the skill automatically when you touch matching files. Zero friction.

Tip 24: Keep a runbooks/ directory for operational procedures. Document your team’s recurring procedures — deployments, incident response, database migrations — as runbooks. Reference them in CLAUDE.md:

markdown
@docs/runbooks/deploy.md
@docs/runbooks/incident-response.md

When someone says “deploy to staging,” Claude follows the documented procedure instead of improvising.

Tip 25: Audit your setup quarterly. Run the workspace validator every few months:

bash
bash tools/scripts/validate-workspace.sh

Check for: skills that nobody uses (remove them to reduce cognitive load), hooks that fire too often (tighten matchers), rules that contradict each other (merge or prioritize), and deny rules that are too broad (relax to match actual threats). A lean configuration is a fast configuration.

Summary¶

This chapter is a reference, not a narrative. Return to it when:

  • Something breaks (Part 1 — ten common issues with direct fixes)
  • Someone asks a question you’ve heard before (Part 2 — twenty FAQ answers ready to share)
  • You want to level up (Part 3 — twenty-five tips that compound over time)

The difference between a Claude Code user and a Claude Code expert isn’t knowledge of features. It’s knowing what to do when features don’t work as expected, having answers ready for the questions that always come up, and accumulating the small workflow optimizations that turn a good tool into an indispensable one.

The next logical step: build these troubleshooting patterns and tips into your own skills and hooks, so they become automated rather than manual. The best debugging is the kind you never have to do twice.

Part VIII: Specialized CLI Systems

Chapter 28: Specializing Claude Code at the Source Code Level¶ ~22 min read

Every chapter in this book so far has worked within the boundaries that Claude Code gives you. CLAUDE.md files, skills, rules, hooks, agents, MCP servers — these are the official extension points. They are powerful. For most teams, they are enough.

But some teams hit a wall.

You need a tool that participates in the agentic loop differently than any built-in tool can. You need permission semantics that don’t map to allow/deny lists. You need to strip away 80% of Claude Code’s capabilities and ship a focused product to a non-engineering team. You need telemetry, billing gates, or compliance logic wired into the core execution path.

When you reach that wall, there is one more layer: forking and modifying Claude Code’s TypeScript source code itself.

This chapter is not about fine-tuning models. It is not about writing better CLAUDE.md files. It is about reading, understanding, and modifying the actual TypeScript codebase that powers Claude Code — building specialized CLI tools that share its DNA but serve entirely different purposes.

The Five Customization Layers¶

Before you touch source code, understand the full spectrum of customization available to you. Each layer goes deeper. Each layer trades convenience for control.

Layer 1: CLAUDE.md (Zero Code)¶

The project instruction file. Loaded into context at session start. Controls communication style, coding standards, workflow preferences. You write natural language and Claude follows it.

What it can do: Change how Claude talks, what conventions it follows, which patterns it prefers. Reference architecture docs, import rules.

What it cannot do: Change what tools exist, modify permission logic, alter the execution loop, add new capabilities.

Sufficient when: Your team wants consistent behavior from stock Claude Code across a project.

Layer 2: Skills + Rules + Hooks (Configuration)¶

Skills package reusable workflows as slash commands. Rules enforce path-scoped coding standards. Hooks run shell scripts at lifecycle events — blocking destructive commands, auto-linting on save, scanning for secrets before commits.

What it can do: Create repeatable workflows, enforce guardrails, automate pre/post actions on tool calls, gate specific operations.

What it cannot do: Add new tool types, modify how the agentic loop decides what to do next, change the permission model fundamentally.

Sufficient when: You need automated enforcement, repeatable workflows, and lifecycle-event automation — but the existing tools and loop behavior work for you.

Layer 3: Custom Agents (Agent Definitions)¶

Agent markdown files in .claude/agents/ define specialized sub-instances with their own model, tool restrictions, and system prompts. Claude delegates to them for specific task types.

What it can do: Create specialists with constrained tool access, different models, focused system prompts. Run parallel investigations.

What it cannot do: Add tools that don’t already exist. Change how delegation works at the protocol level. Modify the agent execution engine.

Sufficient when: You need role-based specialization — a security reviewer on Opus, a doc writer on Sonnet — using existing tools.

Layer 4: MCP Servers (External Capabilities)¶

As discussed in Chapter 24, MCP servers add entirely new tools via JSON-RPC over stdio. You write a server in any language, register it in settings, and Claude Code treats its tools like built-in ones.

What it can do: Add arbitrary new capabilities — database queries, API integrations, custom analysis. Tools participate in Claude’s planning like any built-in tool.

What it cannot do: Modify the core loop’s decision-making. Change how permissions are evaluated. Alter the system prompt injection. Control billing or cost gates. Remove or replace built-in tools.

Sufficient when: You need new capabilities exposed to Claude, and the standard tool invocation pattern (Claude decides to call → tool executes → result returns) works.

Layer 5: Source Code Modification (This Chapter)¶

Fork the Claude Code TypeScript repository. Modify the tool registry, the agentic loop, the permission engine, the CLI entry point, the system prompt assembly. Build and distribute a custom binary.

What it can do: Anything. Add tools that interact with the loop differently. Implement cost gates, compliance checks, telemetry. Strip features. Rebrand. Ship a focused product.

What it costs: You own a fork. You must merge upstream changes. You need TypeScript expertise and a build/distribution pipeline.

When you need it:

  • Your tool needs to influence the loop’s next decision (not just return a result)
  • You need permission semantics beyond allow/deny (role-based, time-based, budget-based)
  • You are building a product on top of Claude Code for a specific audience
  • You need to remove capabilities (compliance requirement: no web access, no file writes outside a sandbox)
  • You need deep telemetry or billing integration in the execution path

The rest of this chapter lives in Layer 5.

Claude Code’s Source Architecture¶

Claude Code is a TypeScript application built on Node.js. The open-source repository contains roughly 500K+ lines of TypeScript, organized into a clear modular structure. Understanding this structure is prerequisite to modifying it safely.

High-Level Directory Map¶

claude-code/
├── src/
│   ├── cli/                  # CLI entry point, argument parsing, REPL
│   ├── engine/               # The agentic loop — the brain
│   │   ├── AgentLoop.ts      # Main loop: prompt → plan → tool call → observe → repeat
│   │   ├── ToolExecutor.ts   # Dispatches tool calls to registered handlers
│   │   └── ContextManager.ts # Manages conversation context window
│   ├── tools/                # Built-in tool implementations
│   │   ├── registry.ts       # Tool registration and discovery
│   │   ├── ReadTool.ts       # File reading
│   │   ├── EditTool.ts       # File editing
│   │   ├── BashTool.ts       # Shell command execution
│   │   ├── GrepTool.ts       # Content search
│   │   ├── GlobTool.ts       # File pattern matching
│   │   ├── WebFetchTool.ts   # URL fetching
│   │   ├── WebSearchTool.ts  # Web search
│   │   └── ...               # ~50 more tools
│   ├── permissions/          # Permission evaluation engine
│   │   ├── PermissionEngine.ts
│   │   ├── rules.ts          # Allow/deny rule evaluation
│   │   └── modes.ts          # Permission mode definitions
│   ├── commands/             # Slash commands (/clear, /compact, etc.)
│   ├── hooks/                # Hook lifecycle management
│   ├── mcp/                  # MCP client implementation
│   ├── context/              # CLAUDE.md loading, rule injection
│   └── utils/                # Shared utilities
├── tests/                    # Test suite
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
└── build/                    # Build output

The Tool Registry Pattern¶

Every tool in Claude Code — built-in and MCP-provided — goes through a central registry. This is the single most important pattern to understand for customization.

typescript
// src/tools/registry.ts (simplified)

export interface ToolDefinition {
  name: string;
  description: string;
  parameters: JsonSchema;
  execute: (params: ToolInput, context: ExecutionContext) => Promise<ToolResult>;
  isAvailable?: (context: ExecutionContext) => boolean;
  metadata?: ToolMetadata;
}

class ToolRegistry {
  private tools: Map<string, ToolDefinition> = new Map();

  register(tool: ToolDefinition): void {
    if (this.tools.has(tool.name)) {
      throw new Error(`Tool "${tool.name}" is already registered`);
    }
    this.tools.set(tool.name, tool);
  }

  get(name: string): ToolDefinition | undefined {
    return this.tools.get(name);
  }

  list(context: ExecutionContext): ToolDefinition[] {
    return Array.from(this.tools.values())
      .filter(tool => !tool.isAvailable || tool.isAvailable(context));
  }
}

export const toolRegistry = new ToolRegistry();

Key observations:

  • ToolDefinition is the interface every tool implements. Name, description, JSON Schema parameters, and an async execute function.
  • ExecutionContext carries session state — current directory, permissions, settings, conversation history.
  • isAvailable is an optional gate. A tool can hide itself based on context (e.g., a database tool only appears when a connection string is configured).
  • toolRegistry is a singleton. All tools register here at startup.

When Claude decides to use a tool, the engine calls toolRegistry.get(toolName), checks permissions, then calls tool.execute(params, context).

The Agentic Loop¶

The loop is the core of Claude Code. It is what makes Claude Code an agent rather than a chatbot.

typescript
// src/engine/AgentLoop.ts (simplified)

class AgentLoop {
  async run(userMessage: string, context: ExecutionContext): Promise<void> {
    // Add user message to conversation
    context.conversation.addUser(userMessage);

    while (true) {
      // 1. Send conversation to Claude API
      const response = await this.callClaude(context);

      // 2. If Claude responded with text only, we're done
      if (!response.hasToolCalls()) {
        context.conversation.addAssistant(response.text);
        break;
      }

      // 3. Process each tool call
      for (const toolCall of response.toolCalls) {
        // 3a. Check permissions
        const permitted = await this.permissionEngine.check(
          toolCall, context
        );
        if (!permitted) {
          context.conversation.addToolResult(toolCall.id, {
            error: "Permission denied"
          });
          continue;
        }

        // 3b. Run PreToolUse hooks
        const hookResult = await this.hooks.runPre(toolCall, context);
        if (hookResult.blocked) {
          context.conversation.addToolResult(toolCall.id, {
            error: hookResult.reason
          });
          continue;
        }

        // 3c. Execute the tool
        const result = await this.toolExecutor.execute(toolCall, context);

        // 3d. Run PostToolUse hooks
        await this.hooks.runPost(toolCall, result, context);

        // 3e. Add result to conversation
        context.conversation.addToolResult(toolCall.id, result);
      }

      // 4. Loop back — Claude sees the tool results and decides next action
    }
  }
}

The loop is simple in structure but rich in extension points:

  1. Before calling Claude — you can modify the context, inject system prompts, add constraints
  2. After Claude responds but before tool execution — permission checks, hook execution, custom gates
  3. During tool execution — the tool itself runs
  4. After tool execution — post-hooks, telemetry, result transformation
  5. Loop continuation decision — Claude decides whether to call more tools or respond with text

Every modification in this chapter targets one of these five points.

Adding Custom Tools to the Registry¶

The most common source-level modification is adding tools that cannot be implemented as MCP servers — tools that need access to internal engine state, tools that need to influence the loop’s behavior, or tools that need to be deeply integrated for performance reasons.

Tool Interface Deep Dive¶

A complete tool implementation follows this structure:

typescript
// src/tools/DatabaseQueryTool.ts

import { ToolDefinition, ToolInput, ToolResult } from "./registry";
import { ExecutionContext } from "../engine/types";
import { Pool } from "pg";

let pool: Pool | null = null;

function getPool(context: ExecutionContext): Pool {
  if (!pool) {
    const connectionString = context.env.DATABASE_URL;
    if (!connectionString) {
      throw new Error(
        "DATABASE_URL environment variable is required for database queries"
      );
    }
    pool = new Pool({
      connectionString,
      max: 5,
      idleTimeoutMillis: 30000,
      connectionTimeoutMillis: 5000,
    });
  }
  return pool;
}

export const DatabaseQueryTool: ToolDefinition = {
  name: "DatabaseQuery",

  description:
    "Execute a read-only SQL query against the project's PostgreSQL database. " +
    "Use this to inspect schema, check data, debug issues, or answer questions " +
    "about application state. Only SELECT queries are permitted.",

  parameters: {
    type: "object",
    properties: {
      query: {
        type: "string",
        description:
          "The SQL query to execute. Must be a SELECT statement. " +
          "Use LIMIT to avoid returning excessive rows.",
      },
      maxRows: {
        type: "number",
        description: "Maximum rows to return. Defaults to 100.",
        default: 100,
      },
    },
